<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction** is the phenomenological constitutive equation that governs the rate of thermal energy transfer by molecular diffusion through a stationary medium (solid, fluid, or gas) under the influence of a temperature gradient. It is one of the three primary modes of heat transfer (along with convection and radiation) and serves as the foundational differential equation for the entire discipline of conduction heat transfer.

In its most general **three-dimensional vector form**, Fourier's Law is expressed as:

$$\vec{q} = -k \nabla T$$

where $\vec{q}$ is the **heat flux vector** (W/m²), $k$ is the **thermal conductivity** (W/m·K), and $\nabla T$ is the **temperature gradient** (K/m). The negative sign is the mathematical embodiment of the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**, asserting that heat flows spontaneously from regions of higher temperature to regions of lower temperature (i.e., down the temperature gradient).

For the simplified **one-dimensional, steady-state case** through a plane wall of thickness $L$, the law reduces to the integrated form:

$$Q = \frac{kA}{L}(T_1 - T_2) = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{th}}$$

where $Q$ is the rate of heat transfer (W), $A$ is the cross-sectional area perpendicular to heat flow (m²), and $R_{th} = L/(kA)$ is the **thermal resistance** (K/W).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Fourier's Law is the *cornerstone equation* of Module 1. It is a CO1 (Apply) and CO2 (Analyze) level topic. Mastery of this law is mandatory before proceeding to composite walls, cylinders, fins, or transient conduction problems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuitive Picture

Imagine a **water slide at a swimming pool**. Children (heat energy) always slide from the **top of the slide (hot region)** down to the **bottom (cold region)** — they never climb up. The steeper the slide (the larger the temperature difference), the faster the children arrive at the pool (the higher the heat flow rate).

In this analogy:
- **Children sliding** → heat energy in motion (Q)
- **Slope of the slide** → temperature gradient (dT/dx)
- **Friction/material of the slide** → thermal resistance (1/kA) — a smoother (more conductive) slide lets more children through
- **Width of the slide** → cross-sectional area (A)
- **Length of the slide** → wall thickness (L)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:**
> - Thermal conductivity of pure copper: **k ≈ 401 W/m·K** (excellent conductor)
> - Thermal conductivity of still air: **k ≈ 0.026 W/m·K** (excellent insulator)
> - Thermal conductivity of fiberglass insulation: **k ≈ 0.04 W/m·K**
> - Standard heat flux unit: **W/m²** (or **kW/m²** in industrial applications)

## 1.3 Historical Context

The law was formulated by **Joseph Fourier** in 1822 in his treatise *Théorie analytique de la chaleur*. Although it appears deceptively simple, it is an empirical observation — it cannot be derived from first principles alone. It is a *constitutive equation* valid for isotropic materials under the assumption of **local thermodynamic equilibrium**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "Why" and "How"

### Step 1: Molecular Mechanism
Heat conduction occurs through lattice vibrations (**phonons** in solids) and free-electron drift (dominant in metals). Fourier's Law is the macroscopic, averaged description of countless microscopic energy-exchange events.

### Step 2: Proportionality Principle
Experimental observation shows that the heat transfer rate $Q$ is:
- **Directly proportional** to the cross-sectional area $A$ (bigger pipe → more flow)
- **Directly proportional** to the temperature difference $\Delta T$ (bigger driving force → more flow)
- **Inversely proportional** to the thickness $L$ (shorter path → less resistance)

### Step 3: Material Property — Thermal Conductivity ($k$)
The proportionality constant $k$ is an intensive transport property that quantifies a material's intrinsic ability to conduct heat. It depends on:
- **Temperature** (often expressed as $k = k_0(1 + \beta T)$ or $k = k_{avg}$ over a range)
- **Pressure** (significant only for gases)
- **Composition and microstructure**

### Step 4: Directional Sign Convention
The **negative sign** mathematically enforces the physical reality that heat flows *down* the temperature gradient. If temperature increases in the +x direction ($dT/dx > 0$), then heat must flow in the −x direction, making $q_x$ negative.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula Symbol | Mathematical Expression | Description | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Differential form (1D) | $q_x = -k \dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | Local heat flux in x-direction | W/m² |
| Integrated form (1D) | $Q = -kA \dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | Heat transfer rate through area A | W |
| Plane wall equation | $Q = \dfrac{kA}{L}(T_1 - T_2)$ | Steady, 1D conduction, constant k | W |
| Thermal resistance | $R_{th} = \dfrac{L}{kA}$ | Conduction resistance | K/W |
| Thermal resistivity | $r = \dfrac{1}{k}$ | Inverse of conductivity | m·K/W |
| General vector form | $\vec{q} = -k \nabla T$ | 3D conduction | W/m² |
| Mean conductivity | $k_m = \dfrac{\int_{T_1}^{T_2} k(T)\, dT}{T_2 - T_1}$ | For variable $k(T)$ | W/m·K |
| Fourier number | $Fo = \dfrac{\alpha t}{L_c^2}$ | Dimensionless time for transient | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU TRICK:** In heat-transfer problems, the thermal-resistance network is *analogous* to an electrical-resistance network. **Voltage ↔ Temperature (ΔT)**, **Current ↔ Heat flow (Q)**, **Resistance ↔ L/kA**. This analogy allows KTU examiners to set composite-wall, series-parallel resistance problems that look intimidating but reduce to Ohm's law: $Q = \Delta T / R_{total}$.

## 2.3 Boundary Conditions Used with Fourier's Law

For the differential equation $\nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) = 0$ (steady state, no source), three classical boundary conditions appear in KTU questions:

1. **Prescribed temperature (Dirichlet):** $T(0) = T_s$
2. **Prescribed heat flux (Neumann):** $-k \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x}\Big\vert_{x=0} = q_s$
3. **Convection at surface (Robin):** $-k \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x}\Big\vert_{surface} = h(T_s - T_\infty)$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of Fourier's Law |
|---|---|
| Building insulation design | Sizing wall thickness to meet U-value codes |
| Heat exchangers | Tube-wall conduction loss analysis |
| Electronics cooling | Heat-spreader / TIM (thermal interface material) design |
| Furnace / boiler walls | Refractory lining thickness calculation |
| Nuclear reactor shielding | Composite lead/borated-polyethylene layers |
| Aerospace TPS | Re-entry vehicle thermal protection sizing |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Differential to Integrated Form for a Plane Wall

**Problem Setup:** Consider a homogeneous, isotropic plane wall of thickness $L$, area $A$, with constant thermal conductivity $k$. The inner surface at $x = 0$ is held at $T_1$ and the outer surface at $x = L$ is held at $T_2$, with $T_1 > T_2$. Find the heat transfer rate $Q$ and the temperature distribution $T(x)$.

**Step 1: Start with the differential form of Fourier's Law in 1D.**

$$q_x = -k \frac{dT}{dx}$$

Here, $q_x$ is the heat flux in the x-direction (W/m²), $k$ is the constant thermal conductivity (W/m·K), and $\frac{dT}{dx}$ is the temperature gradient (K/m).

**Step 2: Rearrange to separate variables.**

Move the temperature derivative to the left side to enable integration.

$$q_x \, dx = -k \, dT$$

**Step 3: Integrate across the wall from $x = 0$ (where $T = T_1$) to $x = L$ (where $T = T_2$).**

Since $q_x$ and $k$ are constants under the steady-state assumption with no internal heat generation, they can be brought outside the integral.

$$\int_{0}^{L} q_x \, dx = -\int_{T_1}^{T_2} k \, dT$$

**Step 4: Evaluate both integrals.**

Left side: $q_x [x]_{0}^{L} = q_x L$.
Right side: $-k [T]_{T_1}^{T_2} = -k(T_2 - T_1) = k(T_1 - T_2)$.

$$q_x L = k(T_1 - T_2)$$

**Step 5: Solve for the heat flux $q_x$.**

$$q_x = \frac{k(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$$

Since $T_1 > T_2$, $q_x$ is positive, confirming heat flows in the +x direction. This matches the Second Law.

**Step 6: Multiply by area $A$ to obtain the total heat transfer rate $Q$.**

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L} = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{th}}}$$

This is the canonical form used in 90% of KTU plane-wall problems.

**Step 7 (Bonus): Derive the temperature distribution $T(x)$.**

Integrate the differential form without applying the right boundary:

$$\int_{0}^{x} q_x \, dx' = -\int_{T_1}^{T(x)} k \, dT'$$

$$q_x x = k[T_1 - T(x)]$$

Substituting $q_x = k(T_1 - T_2)/L$:

$$\frac{k(T_1 - T_2)}{L} \cdot x = k[T_1 - T(x)]$$

Cancel $k$ and rearrange:

$$T(x) = T_1 - \left(\frac{T_1 - T_2}{L}\right) x$$

This is a **linear** temperature profile — a hallmark of 1D steady conduction with constant $k$ and no source.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Thermal Resistance Concept (Ohm's-Law Analogy)

**Step 1:** Write Fourier's integrated law as:

$$Q = \frac{\Delta T}{L/(kA)}$$

**Step 2:** Define thermal resistance $R_{th}$ such that $Q = \Delta T / R_{th}$.

$$\boxed{R_{th} = \frac{L}{kA} \quad \text{(units: K/W or °C/W)}}$$

**Step 3:** For series resistances (layers in a composite wall):

$$R_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{k_i A_i}$$

**Step 4:** For parallel resistances (e.g., wall with studs and insulation side-by-side):

$$\frac{1}{R_{total}} = \sum_{j} \frac{1}{R_j}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Variable Thermal Conductivity $k(T)$

When $k$ depends strongly on temperature (e.g., insulation materials, refractories), the wall is no longer linear. Define:

$$k_m = \frac{1}{T_2 - T_1}\int_{T_1}^{T_2} k(T)\, dT$$

Then the heat flow is:

$$Q = \frac{k_m A (T_1 - T_2)}{L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** If a problem gives $k = k_0(1 + \beta T)$, the examiner will award full marks **only if** you compute $k_m$ explicitly and substitute — using $k$ at the mean temperature is an approximation, not exact.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A furnace wall is made of fireclay brick ($k = 1.0$ W/m·K) of thickness $0.20$ m. The inner surface is at $1200$ K and the outer surface is at $350$ K. The wall area is $2.5$ m². Calculate: (a) the rate of heat loss through the wall, (b) the temperature at mid-thickness.

**Solution (a):**

$$Q = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L} = \frac{(1.0)(2.5)(1200 - 350)}{0.20}$$

$$Q = \frac{(1.0)(2.5)(850)}{0.20} = \frac{2125}{0.20} = 10625 \text{ W} \approx 10.625 \text{ kW}$$

**Solution (b):** Using $T(x) = T_1 - \left(\frac{T_1 - T_2}{L}\right) x$ at $x = 0.10$ m:

$$T(0.10) = 1200 - \frac{850}{0.20}(0.10) = 1200 - 425 = 775 \text{ K}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Typical KTU Problem Patterns

| Pattern Type | Givens | Find | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Plane wall (constant k) | $T_1, T_2, L, A, k$ | $Q$ or $T(x)$ | $Q = kA\Delta T / L$ |
| Variable k | $k(T)$ form, $T_1, T_2, L, A$ | $Q$ | $Q = k_m A \Delta T / L$ |
| Heat flux specified | $q'', T_1, k, A$ | $T_2$ or $L$ | $q'' = k\Delta T / L$ |
| Contact resistance | $h_{c}$, surface temps | Effective $R$ | $R_{total} = R_{cond} + 1/(h_c A)$ |
| Composite wall | Multi-layer $k_i, L_i$ | $Q$, interface $T$ | Series $R$ summation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Note

> [!NOTE]
> Per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol, all node identifiers are prefixed with letters and contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric labels — no markdown bold/italic/HTML tags inside node labels — to ensure clean Mermaid rendering.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Heat Flow Through a Composite Plane Wall

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[HOT FLUID T1] --> B[CONVECTIVE LAYER h1]
    B --> C[SOLID LAYER 1 k1 L1]
    C --> D[INTERFACE RESISTANCE Rcontact]
    D --> E[SOLID LAYER 2 k2 L2]
    E --> F[SOLID LAYER 3 k3 L3]
    F --> G[CONVECTIVE LAYER h2]
    G --> H[COLD FLUID T2]
    
    A1[FOURIER LAW APPLIED AT EACH LAYER] -.-> C
    A1 -.-> E
    A1 -.-> F
    
    B1[OHMS LAW ANALOGY: Q EQUALS DELTA T DIVIDED BY R TOTAL] -.-> A
    B1 -.-> H
    
    C1[R TOTAL EQUALS R1 PLUS R2 PLUS R3 PLUS Rconv1 PLUS Rconv2] -.-> B1
```

## 4.3 Schematic Representation — 1D Plane Wall Heat Conduction

```
   x = 0                                       x = L
   T = T₁ (Hot)                                T = T₂ (Cold)
   ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │◄────────── Heat Flow Direction (Q) ─────────────►│
   │                                                  │
   │      T(x)                                       │
   │   T₁ ●╲                                          │
   │       ╲╲                                         │
   │        ╲╲                                        │
   │         ╲╲                                       │
   │          ╲╲                                      │
   │           ╲╲                                     │
   │            ● T₂                                   │
   │                                                  │
   └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
          Linear Temperature Profile
         dT/dx = (T₂ - T₁)/L = -ΔT/L
```

## 4.4 Resistance Network Analogy (Electrical ↔ Thermal)

```
   ELECTRICAL CIRCUIT                    THERMAL ANALOG

    I ──[R1]──[R2]──[R3]──               T₁ ──[L1/k1A]──[L2/k2A]──[L3/k3A]── T₂
    ▲       ▲       ▲                   ▲                                  ▲
    V₁                              T₁ (hot)                            T₂ (cold)
    ▼                                    │                                  │
    V₂                                    └────── Q = ΔT / R_total ─────────┘

    ΔV = I · R                           ΔT = Q · R
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]
**State Fourier's law of heat conduction. Explain the significance of the negative sign.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Fourier's law of heat conduction states that the rate of heat transfer through a homogeneous medium is directly proportional to the area perpendicular to heat flow and the temperature gradient across it. Mathematically:

$$Q = -kA\frac{dT}{dx}$$

The negative sign indicates that heat flows in the direction of **decreasing temperature**, i.e., from the hotter region to the colder region, which is consistent with the Second Law of Thermodynamics. **(1 mark definition, 1 mark equation, 1 mark significance of negative sign)**

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define thermal conductivity. What are its SI units? Arrange the following in increasing order of thermal conductivity: Air, Copper, Asbestos, Water.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Thermal conductivity is the property of a material that quantifies its ability to conduct heat. It is defined as the rate of heat transfer per unit area per unit temperature gradient. **(1 mark)**
SI unit: **W/(m·K)** or **W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹** **(1 mark)**
Increasing order: **Air < Asbestos < Water < Copper** (Air ≈ 0.026, Asbestos ≈ 0.17, Water ≈ 0.6, Copper ≈ 401 W/m·K) **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**(a)** Derive the general one-dimensional Fourier's law of heat conduction for a plane wall. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A brick wall of thickness $0.30$ m, thermal conductivity $k = 0.7$ W/m·K, and area $15$ m² has its inner surface at $60°$C and outer surface at $20°$C. Calculate: (i) the rate of heat transfer through the wall, (ii) the temperature at a depth of $0.10$ m from the hot side. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Full derivation as in Section 3.1 of these notes.* 
- Statement of Fourier's law (1 mark)
- 1D differential form $q_x = -k dT/dx$ (1 mark)
- Assumptions (homogeneous, isotropic, constant k, steady state, no heat generation) (2 marks)
- Integration steps leading to $Q = kA\Delta T / L$ (2 marks)
- Final linear temperature profile (1 mark)

**(b) (i) Heat transfer rate:**

$$Q = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L} = \frac{(0.7)(15)(60 - 20)}{0.30}$$

$$Q = \frac{(0.7)(15)(40)}{0.30} = \frac{420}{0.30} = 1400 \text{ W} \approx 1.4 \text{ kW}$$

**(1 mark formula, 1 mark substitution, 1 mark answer)**

**(b) (ii) Temperature at $x = 0.10$ m:**

Using the linear temperature profile derived earlier:

$$T(x) = T_1 - \frac{(T_1 - T_2)}{L} x = 60 - \frac{40}{0.30}(0.10)$$

$$T(0.10) = 60 - 13.33 = 46.67°\text{C}$$

**(1 mark formula, 1 mark substitution, 1 mark answer with units)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a)** A furnace wall is constructed of three layers in series: a $0.10$ m thick firebrick layer ($k_1 = 1.4$ W/m·K), a $0.08$ m thick insulating brick layer ($k_2 = 0.35$ W/m·K), and a $0.05$ m thick outer layer of plaster ($k_3 = 0.7$ W/m·K). The inner wall temperature is $1400$ K and the outer surface temperature is $340$ K. The wall area is $10$ m². Calculate: (i) the rate of heat flow per unit area, and (ii) the interface temperatures between layers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat thermal-resistance network diagram, explain how Fourier's law is analogous to Ohm's law for steady-state 1D conduction. Define the concept of overall heat-transfer coefficient $U$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) (i) Total resistance:**

$$R_{total} = \frac{L_1}{k_1 A} + \frac{L_2}{k_2 A} + \frac{L_3}{k_3 A}$$

Per unit area, divide by $A$:

$$r_{total} = \frac{0.10}{1.4} + \frac{0.08}{0.35} + \frac{0.05}{0.7} = 0.0714 + 0.2286 + 0.0714 = 0.3714 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$q'' = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{r_{total}} = \frac{1400 - 340}{0.3714} = \frac{1060}{0.3714} = 2854 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**(a) (ii) Interface temperatures (working backward from cold side):**

Interface between plaster and insulating brick ($T_{23}$):

$$q'' = \frac{T_{23} - 340}{L_3/k_3} \Rightarrow T_{23} = 340 + q'' \cdot \frac{0.05}{0.7} = 340 + 2854 \times 0.0714 = 340 + 203.9 = 543.9 \text{ K}$$

Interface between insulating brick and firebrick ($T_{12}$):

$$q'' = \frac{T_{12} - T_{23}}{L_2/k_2} \Rightarrow T_{12} = 543.9 + 2854 \times \frac{0.08}{0.35} = 543.9 + 652.4 = 1196.3 \text{ K}$$

**Verification:** $q''$ from firebrick layer = $(1400 - 1196.3)/0.0714 = 2851$ W/m² ✓ (matches within rounding)

**(3 marks each sub-part)**

**(b) Ohm's law analogy:**

The thermal-resistance network for the composite wall is drawn as three series resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3$ between $T_1$ and $T_2$. The analogy is:

| Electrical | Thermal |
|---|---|
| Current $I$ (A) | Heat rate $Q$ (W) |
| Voltage $\Delta V$ (V) | Temperature difference $\Delta T$ (K) |
| Resistance $R$ ($\Omega$) | Thermal resistance $L/kA$ (K/W) |
| Ohm's law: $I = \Delta V / R$ | $Q = \Delta T / R_{th}$ |

**Overall heat-transfer coefficient** $U$ is defined such that:

$$Q = U A \Delta T \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \frac{1}{UA} = \sum \frac{L_i}{k_i A}$$

It represents the **combined conductance** of all layers per unit area. **(1 mark analogy table, 2 marks derivation, 2 marks $U$ definition, 2 marks series formula)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (Mark-Loss Zones):**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in $q_x = -k dT/dx$ — examiner deducts 1 mark if you only write $q_x = k dT/dx$.
> 2. **Mixing up heat flux $q''$ (W/m²) with heat rate $Q$ (W).** Always read the problem carefully. If "per unit area" is asked, divide $Q$ by $A$.
> 3. **Using $k$ at the wrong temperature** for variable-conductivity problems. KTU expects either $k_m$ (exact, using integration) or $k$ at the mean temperature (approximate).
> 4. **Linear temperature profile assumption breaks** when there is internal heat generation (e.g., electric heater inside the wall) or when $k$ varies strongly with $T$.
> 5. **Unit conversions** — temperatures in °C vs K give the same $\Delta T$, so don't add 273 unnecessarily, but if absolute $k$ values are given for gases, watch for K.
> 6. **Composite wall questions:** Always compute $R_{total}$ first, *then* find $Q = \Delta T / R_{total}$, *then* back-calculate interface temperatures. Students often skip the $R$ step and lose partial marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Fourier's Law (1D): $\boxed{Q = -kA \dfrac{dT}{dx}}$
- ✅ Plane wall (steady, constant k): $Q = \dfrac{kA \Delta T}{L}$
- ✅ Heat flux: $q'' = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{k \Delta T}{L}$
- ✅ Thermal resistance (conduction): $R_{th} = \dfrac{L}{kA}$
- ✅ **Sign convention:** negative sign ⇒ heat flows from hot → cold
- ✅ **Temperature profile** (constant k, no source): **linear**; slope $= -\dfrac{q''}{k}$
- ✅ **Variable k:** use mean conductivity $k_m = \dfrac{1}{\Delta T}\int_{T_1}^{T_2} k(T)\, dT$
- ✅ **Ohm's law analogy:** $Q = \Delta T / R$, series $R$ add, parallel $1/R$ add
- ✅ **Overall U-value:** $\dfrac{1}{UA} = \sum \dfrac{L_i}{k_i A}$
- ✅ **Typical $k$ order:** Metals (high) ≫ Non-metals ≫ Liquids ≫ Gases (low)
- ✅ **Assumptions:** homogeneous, isotropic, constant $k$, 1D, steady state, no generation
- ✅ **SI units:** $k$ in W/(m·K), $Q$ in W, $q''$ in W/m², $R_{th}$ in K/W
- ✅ **Fourier number** $Fo = \alpha t / L_c^2$ — used in transient conduction (advanced)
- ✅ **Master the integration trick:** $\int dT \Rightarrow$ boundary limits, **never** integrate indefinite

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Conductivity of Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT501 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermal Conductivity of Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Conductivity (k)** is defined as the **intensive transport property** that quantifies a material's intrinsic ability to transfer heat by **conduction** through its bulk under a steady-state temperature gradient. Mathematically, it is the proportionality constant in **Joseph Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction**.

From Fourier's Law, for one-dimensional steady-state conduction:

$$q_x = -k \frac{dT}{dx}$$

Where:
- $q_x$ = heat flux in the $x$-direction (W/m²)
- $k$ = thermal conductivity (W/m·K)
- $\frac{dT}{dx}$ = temperature gradient in the $x$-direction (K/m)

The **SI units** of thermal conductivity are **W/(m·K)** or equivalently **W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹**. The negative sign physically enforces the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**, indicating that heat flows spontaneously from a region of higher temperature to a region of lower temperature.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Highway of Heat:**
> Imagine thermal conductivity as the **"width and smoothness of a highway for heat energy."** 
> - **High-k materials** (e.g., copper, aluminum) are like a **wide, 10-lane expressway** — heat zips through them almost effortlessly. 
> - **Low-k materials** (e.g., fiberglass, aerogel) are like a **narrow, potholed village road** — heat creeps through slowly.
> - The **temperature gradient** is like the **"hill slope"** pushing the heat down. The steeper the slope (greater $\Delta T$ over a short distance), the faster the heat travels.

In one sentence: **Thermal conductivity answers the question — "If I create a temperature difference across a material, how willingly does it transport heat?"**

## 1.3 Physical Significance & Classification Metrics

The value of $k$ is **not a universal constant** — it varies dramatically (over **~5 orders of magnitude**) across different classes of materials. The following ranges are **standard KTU reference values**:

| Material Class | Typical $k$ Range (W/m·K) | Conduction Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Pure Metals (Ag, Cu) | 100 – 430 | Excellent Conductor |
| Alloys (Stainless Steel) | 10 – 50 | Good Conductor |
| Non-Metallic Solids (Ceramics) | 1 – 30 | Moderate Conductor |
| Building Materials (Concrete) | 0.5 – 1.5 | Poor Conductor |
| Insulating Materials (Fiberglass) | 0.03 – 0.05 | Insulator |
| Gases (Air at 25°C) | 0.025 | Very Poor Conductor |
| Vacuum | 0 (theoretical) | No Conduction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination Note:** Students are expected to know the **order-of-magnitude** values of $k$ for the above classes, not exact numbers. A question like *"Arrange Cu, water, air, and asbestos in increasing order of thermal conductivity"* is a classic KTU short-answer (3-mark) item.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Thermal Conduction — The "Why" Behind Heat Transfer

The microscopic mechanism of heat conduction differs fundamentally between material classes. Understanding *why* $k$ varies is essential for KTU application-based questions.

### 2.1.1 Conduction in Pure Metals
- Heat is transported primarily by **free (delocalized) electrons**.
- According to the **Wiedemann–Franz Law**, good electrical conductors are also good thermal conductors because the same electrons carry both charge and thermal energy.
- Contribution from **lattice vibrations (phonons)** is secondary.

$$k_{metal} \approx k_{electron} + k_{phonon}$$

### 2.1.2 Conduction in Alloys
- Alloying disrupts the regular lattice, scattering electrons and phonons.
- Result: $k_{alloy} \ll k_{pure\,metal}$ (e.g., stainless steel $\approx$ 16 W/m·K vs. iron $\approx$ 80 W/m·K).

### 2.1.3 Conduction in Non-Metallic Solids (Ceramics, Polymers)
- No free electrons → conduction occurs via **lattice vibrations (phonons)**.
- Defects, grain boundaries, and porosity **scatter phonons**, lowering $k$.

### 2.1.4 Conduction in Liquids
- Mechanism: molecular collisions and translational motion.
- Metals in liquid state (e.g., mercury, liquid Na) still conduct well because of free electrons, but non-metallic liquids (water, oil) are poor conductors.

### 2.1.5 Conduction in Gases
- Mechanism: random molecular collisions across the temperature gradient.
- At low densities, $k$ is very low (air = 0.026 W/m·K).
- This is precisely why **insulating materials are porous** — they trap air to leverage its low $k$.

## 2.2 Factors Affecting Thermal Conductivity

| Factor | Effect on $k$ | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | $k = k_0(1 + \beta T)$ for moderate ranges; $k \propto 1/T$ for gases | Design of furnace linings, cryogenic insulation |
| **Pressure** | Minimal effect on solids/liquids; gases show $k \propto P$ at low $P$ | Vacuum insulation panels |
| **Moisture Content** | Wet insulators have higher $k$ (water $k$ = 0.6 W/m·K) | Building thermal design |
| **Density / Porosity** | Lower density → more trapped air → lower $k$ | Aerogel manufacturing (densities as low as 3 kg/m³) |
| **Crystal Structure** | Single crystals > polycrystalline > amorphous (for same composition) | Semiconductor wafer processing |
| **Alloying / Impurities** | Disrupts lattice periodicity → reduces $k$ | Inconel superalloys for turbine blades |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **High-$k$** materials are used in **heat exchangers, heat sinks (CPU cooling), and cooking utensils** (Cu, Al).
> - **Low-$k$** materials are used in **furnace linings, building walls, aerospace thermal protection systems (TPS), and cryogenic storage**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table consolidates every high-yield formula you need for KTU 2024 ESE questions on Thermal Conductivity of Materials. Memorize these — they form the backbone of numerical problems.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Description / Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = -kA\dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | Fourier's Law (1-D, steady state) |
| 2 | $q = \dfrac{Q}{A} = -k\dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | Heat flux form |
| 3 | $R_{cond} = \dfrac{L}{kA}$ | Conductive thermal resistance (m²K/W) |
| 4 | $\dfrac{1}{U A} = \sum_i \dfrac{L_i}{k_i A_i}$ | Composite wall resistance (series) |
| 5 | $k_{eff} = \dfrac{L_{total}}{\sum_i \dfrac{L_i}{k_i}}$ | Effective $k$ for series composite wall |
| 6 | $k_{eff} = \dfrac{\sum_i k_i A_i}{\sum_i A_i}$ | Effective $k$ for parallel composite (heat flow perpendicular to layers) |
| 7 | $\bar{k} = \dfrac{1}{T_2 - T_1} \displaystyle\int_{T_1}^{T_2} k(T)\, dT$ | Mean thermal conductivity for variable $k$ |
| 8 | $k(T) = k_0(1 + \beta T)$ | Linear temperature dependence |
| 9 | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho c_p}$ | Thermal diffusivity (m²/s) — measures rate of temperature propagation |
| 10 | $\dfrac{k}{\sigma T} = L = 2.44 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W}\Omega/\text{K}^2$ | Wiedemann–Franz Law (for metals) |

**Variable Definitions:**
- $Q$ = heat transfer rate (W)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area perpendicular to heat flow (m²)
- $L$ = thickness (m)
- $R_{cond}$ = conductive thermal resistance (K/W)
- $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity (m²/s)
- $\rho$ = density (kg/m³)
- $c_p$ = specific heat (J/kg·K)
- $\sigma$ = electrical conductivity (S/m)
- $L$ (in #10) = Lorenz number

## 2.4 Boundary Conditions (KTU Standard Notation)

For **1-D steady conduction** problems, KTU expects familiarity with three canonical boundary condition pairs:

1. **Prescribed Surface Temperatures:** $T(0) = T_1$ and $T(L) = T_2$
2. **Prescribed Heat Flux:** $-k\dfrac{dT}{dx}\big\vert_{x=0} = q_0''$ (constant flux)
3. **Convection at Surface:** $-k\dfrac{dT}{dx}\big\vert_{x=0} = h\big[T_\infty - T(0)\big]$ (Newton's cooling)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $k$ matters in boundary conditions:** The flux at any surface is **always proportional to $k$**. Any uncertainty in $k$ directly translates to uncertainty in the predicted heat loss — this is why **accurate $k$ data is critical in the design of nuclear reactors, aerospace re-entry vehicles, and electronic cooling systems.**

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mean Thermal Conductivity for a Plane Wall with Variable $k$

### Problem Setup
A plane wall of thickness $L$ has a temperature-dependent thermal conductivity $k(T) = k_0(1 + \beta T)$. The inner surface is at $T_1$ and the outer surface is at $T_2$. Derive an expression for the steady-state heat flux and define the **mean thermal conductivity**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Start with Fourier's Law in 1-D.**

We write Fourier's law as a finite-difference form because $k$ varies:

$$Q = -k(T) A \frac{dT}{dx}$$

**Step 2: Separate variables** and integrate from $x = 0$ to $x = L$.

$$\int_0^L \frac{Q}{A}\, dx = -\int_{T_1}^{T_2} k(T)\, dT$$

The left side becomes $\dfrac{Q L}{A}$ because $Q$ is constant (steady state, no internal heat generation).

**Step 3: Substitute the linear form of $k(T)$.**

$$\frac{Q L}{A} = -\int_{T_1}^{T_2} k_0(1 + \beta T)\, dT$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the definite integral.**

$$\int_{T_1}^{T_2} k_0(1 + \beta T)\, dT = k_0 \big[(T_2 - T_1) + \frac{\beta}{2}(T_2^2 - T_1^2)\big]$$

**Step 5: Apply the negative sign** (reversing integration limits).

$$\frac{Q L}{A} = k_0 \big[(T_1 - T_2) + \frac{\beta}{2}(T_1^2 - T_2^2)\big]$$

**Step 6: Factor the temperature difference** using $T_1^2 - T_2^2 = (T_1 - T_2)(T_1 + T_2)$:

$$\frac{Q L}{A} = k_0 (T_1 - T_2) \left[1 + \frac{\beta}{2}(T_1 + T_2)\right]$$

**Step 7: Express $Q/A$ in the form of Fourier's Law with constant $k$.**

$$q = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{k_0 \left[1 + \frac{\beta}{2}(T_1 + T_2)\right]}{L} (T_1 - T_2)$$

**Step 8: Define the mean thermal conductivity** $\bar{k}$:

$$\boxed{\;\bar{k} = k_0 \left[1 + \frac{\beta}{2}(T_1 + T_2)\right]\;}$$

Or equivalently, in the integral form (valid for any $k(T)$):

$$\bar{k} = \frac{1}{T_2 - T_1} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} k(T)\, dT$$

### Physical Interpretation
The mean thermal conductivity $\bar{k}$ is simply the **arithmetic average of $k$ over the temperature interval**, weighted by the temperature span. For a wall, we can then write:

$$Q = \frac{\bar{k} A (T_1 - T_2)}{L}$$

This is the form students must use in **KTU numerical problems** when $k$ is given as a function of temperature.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effective Thermal Conductivity of a Composite Wall (Series Resistance)

### Problem Setup
A composite plane wall consists of three layers with thicknesses $L_1, L_2, L_3$ and thermal conductivities $k_1, k_2, k_3$, all with the same cross-sectional area $A$. Show that the effective conductivity is the harmonic-mean-weighted value.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Recognize the series resistance analogy.** Under steady state, the same heat $Q$ flows through all three layers, with temperatures $T_1 > T_2 > T_3 > T_4$ at the four interfaces.

**Step 2: Apply Fourier's Law to each layer:**

$$Q = \frac{k_1 A (T_1 - T_2)}{L_1} = \frac{k_2 A (T_2 - T_3)}{L_2} = \frac{k_3 A (T_3 - T_4)}{L_3}$$

**Step 3: Rewrite each as a temperature drop** $\Delta T_i$:

$$T_1 - T_2 = \frac{Q L_1}{k_1 A}, \quad T_2 - T_3 = \frac{Q L_2}{k_2 A}, \quad T_3 - T_4 = \frac{Q L_3}{k_3 A}$$

**Step 4: Add the three equations** (telescoping sum):

$$T_1 - T_4 = \frac{Q}{A} \left( \frac{L_1}{k_1} + \frac{L_2}{k_2} + \frac{L_3}{k_3} \right)$$

**Step 5: Define the effective conductivity** such that the same $Q$ flows through a single equivalent layer of thickness $L_{tot} = L_1 + L_2 + L_3$:

$$Q = \frac{k_{eff} A (T_1 - T_4)}{L_{tot}}$$

**Step 6: Equate the two expressions for $T_1 - T_4$:**

$$\frac{Q L_{tot}}{k_{eff} A} = \frac{Q}{A} \left( \frac{L_1}{k_1} + \frac{L_2}{k_2} + \frac{L_3}{k_3} \right)$$

**Step 7: Solve for $k_{eff}$:**

$$\boxed{\;k_{eff} = \frac{L_1 + L_2 + L_3}{\dfrac{L_1}{k_1} + \dfrac{L_2}{k_2} + \dfrac{L_3}{k_3}}\;}$$

### Physical Interpretation
For **series conduction** (layers stacked in the direction of heat flow), the **resistances add** ($R_i = L_i/k_iA$), and $k_{eff}$ is dominated by the layer with the **largest resistance** (smallest $k_i$ or largest $L_i$). This is the principle behind **multi-layer insulation**: the thinnest, lowest-$k$ material dictates the overall insulating performance.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thermal Diffusivity and Its Physical Meaning

### Starting from Fourier's Law + Energy Balance
Consider a small element of thickness $dx$ in a 1-D solid with no internal heat generation. The energy balance under transient conditions gives:

$$\rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x} \left( k \frac{\partial T}{\partial x} \right)$$

For constant $k$:

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \frac{k}{\rho c_p} \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$$

Defining **thermal diffusivity**:

$$\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho c_p}\quad (\text{m}^2/\text{s})$$

This non-dimensional grouping tells us:
- **High $\alpha$** (e.g., copper $\alpha \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-4}$ m²/s): heat propagates rapidly, material "responds" quickly to temperature changes.
- **Low $\alpha$** (e.g., plastic $\alpha \approx 10^{-7}$ m²/s): material has high thermal inertia.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 3-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A brick wall of thickness 0.25 m has thermal conductivity $k = 0.7$ W/m·K. The inner surface is at 35°C and the outer at 5°C. Calculate the heat loss per m² of wall area.

**Solution:**

$$q = \frac{k (T_1 - T_2)}{L} = \frac{0.7 \times (35 - 5)}{0.25} = \frac{0.7 \times 30}{0.25} = 84\ \text{W/m}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** Heat loss = **84 W/m²** (model answer format for 3-mark question).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]
**Define thermal conductivity. Explain how it varies with temperature for (a) metals, (b) gases, and (c) insulating materials.**

**Model Answer:**

> Thermal conductivity is the property of a material that quantifies its ability to conduct heat, defined as the constant of proportionality in Fourier's Law of heat conduction: $q = -k (dT/dx)$. Its SI unit is W/(m·K).

**(a) Metals:** For pure metals, $k$ **decreases** with increasing temperature. This is because increased lattice vibrations at higher temperatures scatter the free electrons that transport heat, reducing their mean free path.

**(b) Gases:** For gases, $k$ **increases** with increasing temperature. Higher temperatures increase the random molecular translational kinetic energy, leading to more energetic molecular collisions and faster energy transport across the gradient.

**(c) Insulating materials (e.g., fiberglass, foams):** $k$ **increases** with temperature, but remains very low (typically 0.025–0.05 W/m·K). The dominant mode is conduction through the trapped air pockets, plus radiation across voids at high T.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]
**State and explain Fourier's Law of heat conduction. What is the physical significance of the negative sign?**

**Model Answer:**

> Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction states that the rate of heat transfer by conduction through a homogeneous material is proportional to the area perpendicular to heat flow and the temperature gradient in the direction of flow:
> $$Q = -kA \frac{dT}{dx}$$
> The proportionality constant $k$ is the thermal conductivity of the material.

**Physical significance of the negative sign:** The negative sign ensures that heat always flows in the direction of **decreasing temperature**, consistent with the Second Law of Thermodynamics. If we define positive $x$ along the heat flow, then $\frac{dT}{dx}$ is negative (temperature drops in $+x$), and the product $-k(dT/dx)$ becomes positive, yielding a positive heat flux in the correct direction.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** are the #1 cause of mark loss. Always convert thickness from mm/cm to m, and temperatures from °C to K (differences are the same in both, but watch the absolute values for the mean k formula).
> 2. **Forgetting to average k** when $k$ depends on temperature is a very common mistake. If $k$ is a function of $T$, you MUST use the mean value $\bar{k}$, not the value at one surface.
> 3. **Series vs. parallel confusion** in composite walls costs full marks. Identify whether layers are stacked in the heat flow direction (series) or side-by-side (parallel) BEFORE writing the resistance equation.
> 4. **Sign convention sloppiness** — KTU evaluators deduct for heat flux written without the negative sign in derivations.

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**(a)** A composite wall consists of three layers in series: a 10 cm thick layer of fireclay brick ($k = 1.0$ W/m·K), a 5 cm thick layer of insulating brick ($k = 0.21$ W/m·K), and a 15 cm thick layer of red brick ($k = 0.69$ W/m·K). The inner and outer surface temperatures are 1200 K and 320 K, respectively. Calculate:
   (i) The effective thermal conductivity of the wall. (4 marks)
   (ii) The heat flux through the wall. (3 marks)

**(b)** A wall of thickness 0.4 m has a thermal conductivity that varies with temperature as $k(T) = 0.8(1 + 0.002T)$ W/m·K, where $T$ is in °C. If the two faces are at 200°C and 50°C, find the steady-state heat flux per unit area. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a)(i): Effective Thermal Conductivity**

$$k_{eff} = \frac{L_1 + L_2 + L_3}{\dfrac{L_1}{k_1} + \dfrac{L_2}{k_2} + \dfrac{L_3}{k_3}}$$

$$k_{eff} = \frac{0.10 + 0.05 + 0.15}{\dfrac{0.10}{1.0} + \dfrac{0.05}{0.21} + \dfrac{0.15}{0.69}}$$

$$k_{eff} = \frac{0.30}{0.10 + 0.2381 + 0.2174} = \frac{0.30}{0.5555}$$

$$\boxed{k_{eff} = 0.540\ \text{W/m·K}}$$

**Part (a)(ii): Heat Flux**

$$q = \frac{k_{eff}(T_1 - T_2)}{L_{total}} = \frac{0.540 \times (1200 - 320)}{0.30}$$

$$q = \frac{0.540 \times 880}{0.30} = \frac{475.2}{0.30}$$

$$\boxed{q = 1584\ \text{W/m}^2}$$

**Part (b): Heat Flux with Variable k**

Given: $k(T) = 0.8(1 + 0.002T)$ W/m·K; $T_1 = 200$°C; $T_2 = 50$°C; $L = 0.4$ m.

**Step 1: Compute mean thermal conductivity** (using the derived formula):

$$\bar{k} = k_0 \left[1 + \frac{\beta}{2}(T_1 + T_2)\right] = 0.8 \left[1 + \frac{0.002}{2}(200 + 50)\right]$$

$$\bar{k} = 0.8 \left[1 + 0.001 \times 250\right] = 0.8 \times 1.25 = 1.0\ \text{W/m·K}$$

**Step 2: Compute heat flux:**

$$q = \frac{\bar{k}(T_1 - T_2)}{L} = \frac{1.0 \times (200 - 50)}{0.4} = \frac{150}{0.4}$$

$$\boxed{q = 375\ \text{W/m}^2}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** A furnace wall is constructed of 0.10 m thick stainless steel plate ($k = 16$ W/m·K) lined on the inner side with 0.08 m of insulating material ($k = 0.04$ W/m·K). The inside of the furnace is at 800 K and the outer surface of the steel is at 350 K. Calculate:
   (i) The rate of heat loss per m² of wall. (3 marks)
   (ii) The temperature at the interface between steel and insulation. (4 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **Wiedemann–Franz Law** and use it to estimate the thermal conductivity of copper at 300 K, given that its electrical conductivity is $5.8 \times 10^7$ S/m. The Lorenz number is $2.44 \times 10^{-8}$ W·Ω/K². (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a)(i): Rate of Heat Loss**

This is a two-layer series wall. Use the resistance network approach.

$$q = \frac{T_1 - T_3}{\dfrac{L_1}{k_1} + \dfrac{L_2}{k_2}} = \frac{800 - 350}{\dfrac{0.10}{16} + \dfrac{0.08}{0.04}}$$

$$q = \frac{450}{0.00625 + 2.0} = \frac{450}{2.00625}$$

$$\boxed{q = 224.3\ \text{W/m}^2}$$

**Part (a)(ii): Interface Temperature**

The heat flux is the same through both layers. The temperature drop across the stainless steel is:

$$\Delta T_{steel} = q \times \frac{L_1}{k_1} = 224.3 \times \frac{0.10}{16} = 1.40\ \text{K}$$

Therefore, the interface temperature is:

$$T_{interface} = T_1 - \Delta T_{steel} = 800 - 1.40$$

$$\boxed{T_{interface} = 798.6\ \text{K}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical insight:** The temperature drop across the steel is negligible (1.4 K) because steel has very high $k$ compared to insulation. Almost all the thermal resistance is in the insulation layer. This is why the **insulation thickness is the design-critical parameter** in furnace walls.

**Part (b): Wiedemann–Franz Law Application**

**Explanation:** The Wiedemann–Franz Law states that for metals at not-too-low temperatures, the ratio of thermal conductivity to electrical conductivity is proportional to absolute temperature, with the proportionality constant being the **Lorenz number**:

$$\frac{k}{\sigma T} = L \quad \text{or} \quad k = L \sigma T$$

This law works for metals because **the same free electrons transport both heat and charge**. As temperature increases, electron scattering by lattice vibrations (phonons) increases, raising electrical resistivity and (counterintuitively at first) but the relation $k/\sigma T$ remains constant.

**Numerical calculation:**

$$k = L \times \sigma \times T = (2.44 \times 10^{-8}) \times (5.8 \times 10^7) \times 300$$

$$k = 2.44 \times 10^{-8} \times 5.8 \times 10^7 \times 300$$

Compute step by step:
- $2.44 \times 5.8 = 14.152$
- $14.152 \times 10^{-8+7} = 14.152 \times 10^{-1} = 1.4152$
- $1.4152 \times 300 = 424.56$

$$\boxed{k_{Cu} \approx 424.6\ \text{W/m·K}}$$

This is in excellent agreement with the experimentally measured value of ~**401 W/m·K** for pure copper at 300 K, validating the Wiedemann–Franz law.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 1, Topic: Thermal Conductivity of Materials**

- **Fourier's Law** is the foundational equation: $Q = -kA(dT/dx)$. The negative sign enforces heat flow from hot to cold.
- **Units of k:** W/(m·K). Always present in W per meter per Kelvin.
- **Order of magnitude for k:** Metals $\sim$ 10², ceramics $\sim$ 10⁰, insulators $\sim$ 10⁻², gases $\sim$ 10⁻².
- **Metals vs. non-metals:** Metals conduct via **free electrons**; non-metals via **phonons (lattice vibrations)**.
- **Wiedemann–Franz Law** ($k = L\sigma T$) applies to metals and links thermal & electrical conductivity.
- **Mean thermal conductivity** must be used when $k = k(T)$. Formula: $\bar{k} = \frac{1}{T_2 - T_1} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} k(T)\,dT$.
- **Composite walls (series):** Resistances add, $k_{eff} = L_{tot} / \sum (L_i/k_i)$.
- **Composite walls (parallel):** Conductances add, $k_{eff} = \sum(k_i A_i) / \sum A_i$.
- **Thermal diffusivity** $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$: high $\alpha$ = fast thermal response (e.g., metals); low $\alpha$ = high thermal inertia (e.g., plastics).
- **Insulating materials** work primarily by **trapping air** (low $k \approx 0.026$ W/m·K).
- **Factors affecting k:** Temperature, pressure, moisture, density, crystal structure, alloying.
- **Common pitfalls:** Unit conversion errors (mm to m!), forgetting to average variable $k$, mixing up series vs. parallel composite wall formulas, dropping the negative sign in derivations.
- **Real-world applications to remember:** Heat exchangers (high $k$), furnace linings (low $k$), aerospace TPS (very low $k$), CPU heat sinks (high $k$), cryogenic storage (very low $k$).

---

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Steady-state unidirectional heat conduction** refers to the transport of thermal energy through a stationary medium (solid, liquid, or gas) in which the temperature at any spatial point does **not change with time** (∂T/∂t = 0) and heat flows along a **single spatial coordinate** (x, r, or θ). Under this regime, the temperature field is governed solely by geometry and the thermal conductivity distribution of the medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** The governing differential equation reduces from the general heat equation to the Laplace equation, $\nabla^{2}T = 0$ in Cartesian, cylindrical, or spherical coordinates, depending on geometry.

**Thermal conductivity (k)** is the intrinsic property of a material that quantifies its ability to conduct heat, with units of **W/(m·K)** in the SI system.

## 1.2 Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction (The Governing Postulate)

The rate of heat transfer by conduction is directly proportional to the area perpendicular to the heat flow direction and the temperature gradient along that direction:

$$q = -kA \frac{dT}{dx}$$

where:
- $q$ = rate of heat transfer (**W**)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area perpendicular to heat flow (**m²**)
- $\frac{dT}{dx}$ = temperature gradient in the direction of heat flow (**K/m**)
- $k$ = thermal conductivity (**W/m·K**)

The **negative sign** is a thermodynamic necessity: heat spontaneously flows from regions of higher temperature to regions of lower temperature (Second Law of Thermodynamics).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Through Pipe" Model

Imagine thermal energy as **water** flowing through a pipe:
- **Temperature difference (ΔT)** acts as the **pressure difference** (driving force).
- **Thermal conductivity (k)** is the **ease of flow** (high k = wide pipe, low k = narrow pipe).
- **Thickness (L)** is the **length of pipe** (longer pipe = more resistance).
- **Cross-sectional area (A)** is the **pipe diameter** (wider = more flow).

The **heat flow rate** corresponds to the **volumetric flow rate of water** — it is maximized when the driving force (ΔT) is large, the pipe is short and fat, and the material is highly conductive.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Physical Constants & Typical Values (Memorize These for KTU):**
> - Copper: k ≈ 385 **W/m·K** (excellent conductor)
> - Stainless Steel: k ≈ 16 **W/m·K**
> - Firebrick insulation: k ≈ 1.0 **W/m·K**
> - Asbestos/Cork: k ≈ 0.04 – 0.1 **W/m·K** (excellent insulator)

## 1.4 Steady vs. Unsteady State

| State | Time Dependence | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|
| **Steady State** | $\partial T/\partial t = 0$ | $\nabla^2 T = 0$ (Laplace) |
| **Unsteady State** | $\partial T/\partial t \neq 0$ | $\nabla^2 T = (1/\alpha)(\partial T/\partial t)$ (Poisson) |

The word "**unidirectional**" restricts heat flow to a single axis — for slabs this is the x-axis, for cylinders/spheres this is the radial (r) direction only, with no angular or axial variation.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logic Pipeline (Why → How → Result)

**Step 1 — Start with Fourier's Law** (1D form):
$q_x = -kA \frac{dT}{dx}$

**Step 2 — Impose Steady-State and Constant k:**
At steady state, q is constant along the path. Integrating between the two bounding surfaces ($T_1$ at x = 0 and $T_2$ at x = L) yields the rate equation.

**Step 3 — Introduce the Thermal Resistance Analogy (Ohm's Law of Heat Transfer):**
Just as electrical current I = ΔV/R, heat flow Q = ΔT/R_th. The total resistance of composite walls is the sum of individual resistances (for resistances in series).

> [!TIP]
> The resistance concept is the **single most important tool** in KTU board exams. It applies uniformly to slabs, cylinders, and spheres — only the formula for R changes with geometry.

## 2.2 Comprehensive KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **NOTATION STANDARD:** Subscript 1 denotes the inner (hot) surface; Subscript 2 denotes the outer (cold) surface. All formulas assume heat flows from surface 1 → surface 2.

### A. Single Layer — Constant Thermal Conductivity

| Geometry | Heat Rate Equation | Thermal Resistance R_th | Log Mean Area (where applicable) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Plane Slab (Wall)** | $Q = \dfrac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$ | $R = \dfrac{L}{kA}$ | $A = A_1 = A_2$ (constant) |
| **Hollow Cylinder** | $Q = \dfrac{2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ | $R = \dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}$ | $A_{lm} = \dfrac{2\pi L (r_2 - r_1)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$ |
| **Hollow Sphere** | $Q = \dfrac{4\pi k (T_1 - T_2)}{\left(\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2}\right)}$ | $R = \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2}\right)}{4\pi k}$ | $A_1 = 4\pi r_1^2,\ A_2 = 4\pi r_2^2$ |

### B. Single Layer — Variable Thermal Conductivity (k = f(T))

When k depends on temperature (e.g., $k = k_0(1 + \beta T)$ or $k = k_0 + \beta T$), integration must be performed **over the thermal conductivity function**:

| Geometry | Integrated Heat Rate Equation |
|---|---|
| **Plane Slab** | $Q = \dfrac{A \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}{\displaystyle \int_0^L \dfrac{dx}{k(T)}}$ |
| **Hollow Cylinder** | $Q = \dfrac{2\pi L \cdot (T_1 - T_2)}{\displaystyle \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \dfrac{dr}{r \cdot k(T)}}$ |
| **Hollow Sphere** | $Q = \dfrac{4\pi (T_1 - T_2)}{\displaystyle \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \dfrac{dr}{r^2 \cdot k(T)}}$ |

### C. Multiple (Composite) Layers — Resistances in Series

For n layers stacked in the direction of heat flow:

$$Q = \dfrac{T_1 - T_{n+1}}{\displaystyle \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i}$$

The interface temperatures are unknown and must be found by continuity of heat flux: $Q$ is identical through every layer.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Boiler/Furnace Walls (Slab):** Designing refractory linings to minimize heat loss.
- **Steam Pipes / Insulated Process Lines (Cylinder):** Lagging thickness design to prevent burns and energy loss.
- **Reactor Vessels / Spherical Pressure Tanks (Sphere):** Storage of cryogenic LNG, hot thermic fluid tanks.
- **Aerospace Nose Cones (Variable k):** Materials exposed to extreme temperature gradients where k changes appreciably.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Variable k:** A common linear approximation is $k = k_0(1 + \beta T)$, where $\beta$ is the temperature coefficient of thermal conductivity. For most metals, $\beta < 0$ (k decreases with T); for many insulators, $\beta > 0$.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Single Layer Plane Slab (Constant k)

**Setup:** Heat flows through a slab of thickness L, area A, between T₁ (x = 0) and T₂ (x = L). Steady state, constant k.

**Step 1 — Apply Fourier's Law:**

$$Q = -kA \frac{dT}{dx}$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables and integrate:**

At steady state, Q is constant. Rearranging:

$$Q \, dx = -kA \, dT$$

Integrating from $x = 0, T = T_1$ to $x = L, T = T_2$:

$$\int_0^L Q \, dx = -kA \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT$$

$$Q \cdot L = kA(T_1 - T_2)$$

**Step 3 — Final Equation:**

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L}}$$

**Temperature Profile:** $T(x) = T_1 - \dfrac{Q}{kA} \cdot x$ → **Linear** in x for constant k.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Single Layer Hollow Cylinder (Constant k)

**Setup:** Heat flows radially through a cylindrical wall of length L, inner radius r₁, outer radius r₂, with surface temperatures T₁ > T₂. Steady state, constant k.

**Step 1 — Express Fourier's Law in cylindrical coordinates:**
At radius r, the area normal to heat flow is $A = 2\pi r L$.

$$Q = -k(2\pi r L) \frac{dT}{dr}$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables:**

$$Q \frac{dr}{r} = -2\pi k L \, dT$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from r₁ to r₂ and T₁ to T₂:**

$$Q \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \frac{dr}{r} = -2\pi k L \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT$$

$$Q \ln\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) = 2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)$$

**Step 4 — Final Equation:**

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}}$$

**Temperature Profile:** $T(r) = T_1 - \dfrac{Q}{2\pi k L} \ln\left(\dfrac{r}{r_1}\right)$ → **Logarithmic** in r.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Insight:** The logarithmic mean area $A_{lm} = 2\pi L (r_2 - r_1)/\ln(r_2/r_1)$ allows one to rewrite the cylinder equation in the form $Q = k A_{lm} \Delta T / (r_2 - r_1)$, structurally identical to the slab equation. This is why the **thermal resistance approach** works so elegantly.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Single Layer Hollow Sphere (Constant k)

**Setup:** Heat flows radially through a spherical shell, inner radius r₁, outer radius r₂, with T₁ > T₂. Steady state, constant k.

**Step 1 — Apply Fourier's Law:**
Area at radius r: $A = 4\pi r^2$.

$$Q = -k(4\pi r^2) \frac{dT}{dr}$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables:**

$$Q \frac{dr}{r^2} = -4\pi k \, dT$$

**Step 3 — Integrate:**

$$Q \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \frac{dr}{r^2} = -4\pi k \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT$$

$$Q \left[-\frac{1}{r}\right]_{r_1}^{r_2} = 4\pi k (T_1 - T_2)$$

$$Q \left(\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2}\right) = 4\pi k (T_1 - T_2)$$

**Step 4 — Final Equation:**

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{4\pi k (T_1 - T_2)}{\left(\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r_2}\right)}}$$

**Temperature Profile:** $T(r) = T_1 - \dfrac{Q}{4\pi k}\left(\dfrac{1}{r_1} - \dfrac{1}{r}\right)$ → **Hyperbolic (1/r)** in r.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Hollow Cylinder with Variable k (Most Frequently Tested in KTU)

**Setup:** $k = k_0(1 + \beta T)$, where $k_0$ is the conductivity at 0°C (or reference) and $\beta$ is a constant.

**Step 1 — Apply Fourier's Law with variable k:**

$$Q = -2\pi r L \cdot k_0(1 + \beta T) \frac{dT}{dr}$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables:**

$$Q \frac{dr}{r} = -2\pi L k_0 (1 + \beta T) dT$$

**Step 3 — Integrate:**

$$Q \ln\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) = -2\pi L k_0 \int_{T_1}^{T_2} (1 + \beta T) dT$$

$$Q \ln\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) = -2\pi L k_0 \left[(T_2 - T_1) + \frac{\beta}{2}(T_2^2 - T_1^2)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Simplify using $(T_1^2 - T_2^2) = (T_1 - T_2)(T_1 + T_2)$:**

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{2\pi L k_0 \left[(T_1 - T_2) + \dfrac{\beta}{2}(T_1^2 - T_2^2)\right]}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}}$$

**Step 5 — Alternative compact form using mean temperature $\bar{T} = (T_1 + T_2)/2$:**

$$Q = \frac{2\pi L k_0 (1 + \beta \bar{T})(T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mean Conductivity Trick:** When k varies linearly with T, evaluate it at the **arithmetic mean temperature** $\bar{T} = (T_1 + T_2)/2$ to get an exact result. This is a high-yield shortcut for board problems.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Plane Slab with Variable k

**Setup:** $k = k_0(1 + \beta T)$ through a wall of thickness L, area A, T₁ at x = 0, T₂ at x = L.

**Step 1 — Apply Fourier's Law:**

$$Q = -k_0(1 + \beta T) A \frac{dT}{dx}$$

**Step 2 — Separate and integrate:**

$$Q \int_0^L dx = -k_0 A \int_{T_1}^{T_2} (1 + \beta T) dT$$

$$Q L = k_0 A \left[(T_1 - T_2) + \frac{\beta}{2}(T_1^2 - T_2^2)\right]$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{k_0 A \left[(T_1 - T_2) + \dfrac{\beta}{2}(T_1^2 - T_2^2)\right]}{L}}$$

**Temperature Profile:** **Non-linear (parabolic)** in x for variable k.

---

## 3.6 Composite Wall with Three Layers (Resistance Method)

For a 3-layer slab in series with surface temperatures T₁ (left of layer 1) and T₄ (right of layer 3):

$$Q = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{\frac{L_1}{k_1 A} + \frac{L_2}{k_2 A} + \frac{L_3}{k_3 A}}$$

The interface temperatures T₂ and T₃ are found using continuity of heat flux:

$$Q = \frac{k_1 A (T_1 - T_2)}{L_1} = \frac{k_2 A (T_2 - T_3)}{L_2} = \frac{k_3 A (T_3 - T_4)}{L_3}$$


# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Define thermal conductivity. State Fourier's law of heat conduction and explain the significance of the negative sign.

**Model Answer:**

**Thermal Conductivity (k):** It is defined as the rate of heat transfer by conduction per unit area per unit temperature gradient. Units: **W/(m·K)**.

**Fourier's Law:**

$$Q = -kA \frac{dT}{dx}$$

**Significance of negative sign:** It indicates that heat flows in the direction of **decreasing temperature**, which is a consequence of the Second Law of Thermodynamics. Mathematically, if T decreases with increasing x (dT/dx < 0), the negative sign ensures Q is positive (in the +x direction).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** State the electrical analogy for steady-state heat conduction and write the thermal resistance expressions for a plane wall, a hollow cylinder, and a hollow sphere.

**Model Answer:**

The electrical analogy treats heat flow Q as analogous to electric current I, temperature difference ΔT as analogous to voltage difference ΔV, and thermal resistance R_th as analogous to electrical resistance R.

| Geometry | Thermal Resistance R_th |
|---|---|
| Plane Wall | $R = \dfrac{L}{kA}$ |
| Hollow Cylinder | $R = \dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}$ |
| Hollow Sphere | $R = \dfrac{(1/r_1 - 1/r_2)}{4\pi k}$ |

The governing equation becomes: $Q = \Delta T / R_{th}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) Derive the expression for steady-state heat conduction through a hollow cylinder with constant thermal conductivity, starting from Fourier's law. State the temperature distribution. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply Fourier's law in radial form (area at radius r is $2\pi r L$):

$$Q = -k(2\pi r L) \frac{dT}{dr}$$

**Step 2:** Separate variables:

$$Q \frac{dr}{r} = -2\pi k L \, dT$$

**Step 3:** Apply boundary conditions: at $r = r_1$, $T = T_1$; at $r = r_2$, $T = T_2$:

$$Q \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \frac{dr}{r} = -2\pi k L \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate:

$$Q \ln\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) = 2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)$$

**Step 5:** Final equation:

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}}$$

**Temperature Distribution:** Integrating only up to radius r:

$$T(r) = T_1 - \frac{Q}{2\pi k L} \ln\left(\frac{r}{r_1}\right)$$

This is a **logarithmic (semi-log)** temperature profile in r.

---

**(b) A composite wall consists of three layers in perfect thermal contact. The data is given below:**

| Layer | Thickness (m) | k (W/m·K) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 (Refractory brick) | 0.10 | 1.05 |
| 2 (Insulating brick) | 0.08 | 0.18 |
| 3 (Building brick) | 0.15 | 0.69 |

The inner and outer surface temperatures are 1200°C and 60°C respectively. Calculate: (i) the rate of heat loss per m², and (ii) the interface temperatures T₂ and T₃. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate thermal resistances per unit area:**

$$R_1 = \frac{L_1}{k_1} = \frac{0.10}{1.05} = 0.0952\ \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$R_2 = \frac{L_2}{k_2} = \frac{0.08}{0.18} = 0.4444\ \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$R_3 = \frac{L_3}{k_3} = \frac{0.15}{0.69} = 0.2174\ \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**Step 2 — Total resistance:**

$$R_{total} = 0.0952 + 0.4444 + 0.2174 = 0.7570\ \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$$

**Step 3 — Heat flux:**

$$q = \frac{T_1 - T_4}{R_{total}} = \frac{1200 - 60}{0.7570} = 1505.94\ \text{W/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{q \approx 1506\ \text{W/m}^2}$$

**Step 4 — Interface temperature T₂ (after layer 1):**

$$T_2 = T_1 - q \cdot R_1 = 1200 - (1505.94)(0.0952) = 1200 - 143.36 = 1056.64\ °C$$

**Step 5 — Interface temperature T₃ (after layer 2):**

$$T_3 = T_2 - q \cdot R_2 = 1056.64 - (1505.94)(0.4444) = 1056.64 - 669.24 = 387.40\ °C$$

**Verification:** $T_4 = T_3 - q R_3 = 387.40 - (1505.94)(0.2174) = 387.40 - 327.39 = 60.01\ °C$ ✓

$$\boxed{T_2 \approx 1056.6\ °C,\quad T_3 \approx 387.4\ °C}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) Derive the expression for steady-state heat conduction through a hollow cylinder when the thermal conductivity varies linearly with temperature as k = k₀(1 + βT). (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply Fourier's law with variable k in radial coordinates:

$$Q = -2\pi r L \cdot k_0(1 + \beta T) \frac{dT}{dr}$$

**Step 2:** Separate variables:

$$Q \frac{dr}{r} = -2\pi L k_0 (1 + \beta T) dT$$

**Step 3:** Apply boundary conditions: $r = r_1, T = T_1$; $r = r_2, T = T_2$:

$$Q \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \frac{dr}{r} = -2\pi L k_0 \int_{T_1}^{T_2} (1 + \beta T) dT$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate integrals:

$$Q \ln\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) = 2\pi L k_0 \left[(T_1 - T_2) + \frac{\beta}{2}(T_1^2 - T_2^2)\right]$$

**Step 5:** Final expression:

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{2\pi L k_0 \left[(T_1 - T_2) + \dfrac{\beta}{2}(T_1^2 - T_2^2)\right]}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}}$$

**Equivalent form (Mean Conductivity):** Let $\bar{T} = (T_1 + T_2)/2$; then $\bar{k} = k_0(1 + \beta \bar{T})$ and:

$$Q = \frac{2\pi L \bar{k}(T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The mean-conductivity trick is valid **only for linear k(T) dependence**. This is the form examiners prefer in board valuation for speed and accuracy.

---

**(b) A steam pipe of inner radius 5 cm and outer radius 5.5 cm is covered with a 4 cm thick layer of insulation (k = 0.20 W/m·K). The thermal conductivity of the steel pipe is 45 W/m·K. The inner surface temperature of the pipe is 300°C, and the outer surface of the insulation is 40°C. Calculate: (i) the rate of heat loss per metre length, and (ii) the temperature at the pipe-insulation interface. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify geometry and parameters:**

Steel pipe: $r_1 = 0.05$ m, $r_2 = 0.055$ m, $k_{steel} = 45$ W/m·K
Insulation: $r_2 = 0.055$ m, $r_3 = 0.055 + 0.04 = 0.095$ m, $k_{ins} = 0.20$ W/m·K
Surface temperatures: $T_1 = 300$°C (inner), $T_3 = 40$°C (outer)

**Step 2 — Calculate thermal resistances per metre length:**

$$R_{steel} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_{steel} L} = \frac{\ln(0.055/0.05)}{2\pi (45)(1)} = \frac{\ln(1.1)}{282.74} = \frac{0.0953}{282.74} = 3.37 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m·K/W}$$

$$R_{ins} = \frac{\ln(r_3/r_2)}{2\pi k_{ins} L} = \frac{\ln(0.095/0.055)}{2\pi (0.20)(1)} = \frac{\ln(1.7273)}{1.2566} = \frac{0.5469}{1.2566} = 0.4353\ \text{m·K/W}$$

**Step 3 — Total resistance:**

$$R_{total} = 3.37 \times 10^{-4} + 0.4353 = 0.4356\ \text{m·K/W}$$

**Step 4 — Heat loss per metre:**

$$Q = \frac{T_1 - T_3}{R_{total}} = \frac{300 - 40}{0.4356} = \frac{260}{0.4356}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 596.9\ \text{W per m length}}$$

**Step 5 — Interface temperature T₂:**

$$\Delta T_{steel} = Q \cdot R_{steel} = 596.9 \times 3.37 \times 10^{-4} = 0.201\ °C$$

$$T_2 = T_1 - \Delta T_{steel} = 300 - 0.201 \approx 299.8\ °C$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The temperature drop across the steel pipe is negligible (~0.2°C) because steel has very high conductivity. Almost all thermal resistance comes from the insulation, which is precisely why we insulate!

$$\boxed{T_2 \approx 299.8\ °C}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Top 5 Mark-Deduction Traps in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the ln(r₂/r₁) in the cylinder formula** — students often write the slab formula. The cylinder equation is logarithmic; the slab equation is linear.
> 2. **Mixing up r₁ and r₂** — always define r₁ < r₂ with r₁ being the inner (hot) radius. Reversing the order in the logarithm gives a negative Q.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency** — temperatures must be in **°C differences** (which equal K differences) and thicknesses in **metres** to get Q in **watts**.
> 4. **Forgetting the 2πL factor in cylinder resistance** — R = ln(r₂/r₁) / (2πkL), NOT ln(r₂/r₁)/k.
> 5. **Neglecting to convert cm to m** — this is the #1 source of wrong numerical answers. Always convert at the start of the problem.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 HIGH-YIELD POINTS — Memorize for KTU 2024:**

- **Steady state** = no time dependence → ∂T/∂t = 0 → Laplace equation.
- **Fourier's Law** is the foundation: $Q = -kA(dT/dx)$; the negative sign is mandatory.
- **Plane slab** has a **linear** T-profile; resistance $R = L/(kA)$.
- **Hollow cylinder** has a **logarithmic** T-profile; resistance $R = \ln(r_2/r_1)/(2\pi kL)$.
- **Hollow sphere** has a **hyperbolic (1/r)** T-profile; resistance $R = (1/r_1 - 1/r_2)/(4\pi k)$.
- **Series resistances add:** $R_{total} = \sum R_i$; **identical Q flows through each layer**.
- **Interface temperatures** are found by subtracting $Q \cdot R_i$ from the previous surface temperature.
- **Variable k = k₀(1 + βT):** use mean conductivity $\bar{k} = k_0(1 + \beta \bar{T})$ at $\bar{T} = (T_1 + T_2)/2$ as a shortcut, OR integrate directly.
- **Typical k values:** Copper ≈ 385, Steel ≈ 45, Insulator ≈ 0.04–0.2, all in W/m·K.
- **Always convert dimensions to SI units (metres) before calculating.**
- **Critical radius of insulation** (advanced) for cylinders: $r_c = k/h$ — below this, adding insulation actually *increases* heat loss (not in this module, but related).
- **Composite wall problem-solving mantra:** Compute each R → sum → apply ΔT/R → back-calculate interface T's.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solution of Steady State One-Dimensional Heat Conduction with Heat Generation in Slabs, Cylinders, and Spheres
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT501 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solution of Steady State One-Dimensional Heat Conduction with Heat Generation in Slabs, Cylinders, and Spheres

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Steady-state, one-dimensional heat conduction with heat generation** refers to a class of conduction problems in which thermal energy is *internally produced* within a solid body (via chemical reaction, nuclear decay, electrical resistance heating, or radioactive decay) at a uniform volumetric rate $\dot{q}$ (units: **W/m³**) and conducted outward to the bounding surface, which is held at a known surface temperature $T_s$ or subjected to a convective boundary condition.

Mathematically, it is the solution of the **General Heat Diffusion Equation** under the constraints:
- **Steady state:** $\partial T / \partial t = 0$ (temperature does not vary with time)
- **One-dimensional:** temperature varies along a single spatial coordinate ($x$ or $r$)
- **Constant thermal conductivity:** $k = \text{const}$ (typical KTU assumption)
- **Uniform internal heat generation:** $\dot{q} = \text{const}$ throughout the volume

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This is a high-weightage topic in Module 1 because it forms the analytical foundation for **finned surfaces, nuclear fuel rods, electrical heating elements, and chemical reactor walls**. Mastery of the governing ODE and its boundary conditions is mandatory.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English Explanation)

Imagine a thick, **flat slab of butter** sitting on a hot griddle. Heat flows in from below. Now suppose the *butter itself* is also generating heat — like a slab of **toast with an embedded heating wire** that warms from within. 

- The **wire in the middle** is the *heat source* (heat generation $\dot{q}$).
- The **outer surfaces** are kept cool — perhaps by air convection or a cold fluid.
- Heat flows **outward** in all directions, but in 1D problems, it only flows along one axis (slab → x-direction; cylinder → radial $r$; sphere → radial $r$).

The **temperature is highest at the center** (where heat has the longest path to escape) and **lowest at the surface** (where heat leaves the body). This temperature "dome" or "bowl" shape is the **hallmark** of internal heat generation problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Intuition:** A sphere has the *lowest* center-to-surface temperature rise because its surface area (per unit volume) is largest. A slab has the *highest* rise because of its poor surface-to-volume ratio. This is why **nuclear fuel pellets** are made as small spheres rather than large slabs.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard SI Unit |
|:------:|:---------|:----------------:|
| $\dot{q}$ | Volumetric rate of heat generation | **W/m³** |
| $k$ | Thermal conductivity | **W/(m·K)** |
| $T$ | Temperature | **K** or **°C** |
| $R$ | Outer radius (cylinder/sphere) | **m** |
| $L$ | Half-thickness (slab) | **m** |
| $T_s$ | Surface temperature | **K** |
| $T_0$ | Centerline (maximum) temperature | **K** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Governing Differential Equation — Why It Looks the Way It Does

Starting from the **general 3-D heat diffusion equation**:

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \nabla^2 T + \frac{\dot{q}}{\rho c_p}$$

Applying the **KTU Module 1 simplifications** ($\partial T/\partial t = 0$, 1-D, constant $k$, uniform $\dot{q}$):

$$\nabla^2 T + \frac{\dot{q}}{k} = 0$$

This single equation, written in the appropriate coordinate system, governs **all three** geometries. The challenge is choosing the right Laplacian $\nabla^2$:

- **Slab** (Cartesian): $\nabla^2 T = \dfrac{d^2 T}{dx^2}$
- **Cylinder** (Cylindrical, radial only): $\nabla^2 T = \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\dfrac{dT}{dr}\right)$
- **Sphere** (Spherical, radial only): $\nabla^2 T = \dfrac{1}{r^2}\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left(r^2\dfrac{dT}{dr}\right)$

## 2.2 Standard Boundary Conditions (KTU Convention)

For all three geometries, two boundary conditions are required:

1. **Surface condition:** $T(R) = T_s$ (or convective: $-k\,\partial T/\partial n\big\vert_R = h(T_s - T_\infty)$)
2. **Centerline / symmetry condition:** $\dfrac{dT}{dr}\bigg\vert_{r=0} = 0$ (no heat flux through the center — the temperature gradient must vanish by symmetry; otherwise the heat flux would be infinite)

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mastery Rule:** Memorize the coefficient $\dfrac{\dot{q}R^2}{nk}$ pattern. Notice the **denominator is $2k$, $4k$, $6k$** for slab, cylinder, sphere respectively — this pattern is *guaranteed* to appear in KTU exams.

| Geometry | Governing ODE | Integrated Solution $T(r) - T_s$ | Centerline Rise $T_0 - T_s$ | Surface Heat Flux $q''$ |
|:--------:|:-------------:|:--------------------------------:|:--------------------------:|:----------------------:|
| **Plane Slab** (symmetric, $\pm L$) | $\dfrac{d^2T}{dx^2} + \dfrac{\dot{q}}{k} = 0$ | $\dfrac{\dot{q}}{2k}(L^2 - x^2)$ | $\dfrac{\dot{q}L^2}{2k}$ | $\dot{q}L$ (at $x=L$) |
| **Solid Cylinder** (radius $R$) | $\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\dfrac{dT}{dr}\right) + \dfrac{\dot{q}}{k} = 0$ | $\dfrac{\dot{q}}{4k}(R^2 - r^2)$ | $\dfrac{\dot{q}R^2}{4k}$ | $\dfrac{\dot{q}R}{2}$ |
| **Solid Sphere** (radius $R$) | $\dfrac{1}{r^2}\dfrac{d}{dr}\!\left(r^2\dfrac{dT}{dr}\right) + \dfrac{\dot{q}}{k} = 0$ | $\dfrac{\dot{q}}{6k}(R^2 - r^2)$ | $\dfrac{\dot{q}R^2}{6k}$ | $\dfrac{\dot{q}R}{3}$ |
| **Asymmetric Slab** ($0$ to $L$, $x=0$ fixed, $x=L$ insulated) | $\dfrac{d^2T}{dx^2} + \dfrac{\dot{q}}{k} = 0$ | $\dfrac{\dot{q}}{2k}(2Lx - x^2)$ | $\dfrac{\dot{q}L^2}{2k}$ (at $x=L$) | $0$ (at $x=L$) |

**Total heat generation rate** (energy balance check):

| Geometry | Total Heat Generated $Q$ |
|:--------:|:------------------------:|
| Slab ($A$ = area) | $\dot{q} \cdot A \cdot 2L$ |
| Cylinder ($L$ = length) | $\dot{q} \cdot \pi R^2 L$ |
| Sphere | $\dot{q} \cdot \dfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3$ |

## 2.4 Convective Boundary Extension (Frequently Asked)

When the surface is exposed to a fluid at $T_\infty$ with convection coefficient $h$, the surface temperature is no longer given — it must be solved from:

$$-k \frac{\partial T}{\partial r}\bigg\vert_{R} = h(T_s - T_\infty)$$

The total centerline-to-fluid temperature difference becomes:

$$T_0 - T_\infty = \frac{\dot{q}R^2}{nk} + \frac{\dot{q}R}{m h}$$

where $n = 2, 4, 6$ for slab, cylinder, sphere, and $m = 1, 2, 3$ respectively.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Nuclear reactor fuel rods:** Uranium dioxide pellets generate heat by fission; the centerline temperature must be computed to avoid melting.
- **Electrical heating elements:** Resistive heating in cartridge heaters requires max-temperature prediction.
- **Chemical reactors:** Exothermic reactions in packed-bed reactors need temperature control to prevent runaway.
- **Curie-point magnetic materials:** Self-heating in induction heating processes.
- **Concrete curing:** Heat of hydration in massive concrete pours (e.g., dam construction) — temperature rise can crack the structure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Symmetric Plane Slab with Heat Generation

**Setup:** A slab of half-thickness $L$ extends from $x = -L$ to $x = +L$. The surfaces at $x = \pm L$ are held at $T_s$. Uniform heat generation $\dot{q}$ occurs throughout the volume.

### Step 1: Write the governing equation

$$\frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} + \frac{\dot{q}}{k} = 0$$

### Step 2: First integration (multiply by $dx$ and integrate)

$$\frac{dT}{dx} = -\frac{\dot{q}\,x}{k} + C_1$$

### Step 3: Second integration (integrate once more)

$$T(x) = -\frac{\dot{q}\,x^2}{2k} + C_1 x + C_2$$

### Step 4: Apply boundary conditions

**BC 1 (symmetry at center):** $\dfrac{dT}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=0} = 0$

$$0 = -\frac{\dot{q}(0)}{k} + C_1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; C_1 = 0$$

**BC 2 (surface temperature):** $T(L) = T_s$

$$T_s = -\frac{\dot{q} L^2}{2k} + 0 + C_2 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; C_2 = T_s + \frac{\dot{q}L^2}{2k}$$

### Step 5: Final temperature distribution

$$T(x) = T_s + \frac{\dot{q}}{2k}\left(L^2 - x^2\right)$$

### Step 6: Centerline (maximum) temperature at $x = 0$

$$T_0 = T_s + \frac{\dot{q} L^2}{2k}$$

### Step 7: Surface heat flux at $x = L$ (Fourier's law)

$$q'' = -k \frac{dT}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=L} = -k \cdot \left(-\frac{\dot{q}L}{k}\right) = \dot{q}L$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy-balance check:** Total heat generated per unit area = $\dot{q} \cdot 2L$. Heat lost from both surfaces = $2 \cdot \dot{q}L = 2\dot{q}L$. ✓ Matches exactly. This is the **mandatory verification step** KTU examiners expect.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Solid Cylinder with Heat Generation

**Setup:** Solid cylinder of radius $R$ and length $L$, surface at $T_s$, uniform generation $\dot{q}$.

### Step 1: Governing ODE in cylindrical coordinates (radial only)

$$\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\frac{dT}{dr}\right) + \frac{\dot{q}}{k} = 0$$

### Step 2: Multiply through by $r$ to clean the equation

$$\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r\frac{dT}{dr}\right) = -\frac{\dot{q} r}{k}$$

### Step 3: Integrate once with respect to $r$

$$r\frac{dT}{dr} = -\frac{\dot{q} r^2}{2k} + C_1$$

### Step 4: Divide by $r$ to isolate $dT/dr$

$$\frac{dT}{dr} = -\frac{\dot{q} r}{2k} + \frac{C_1}{r}$$

### Step 5: Integrate a second time

$$T(r) = -\frac{\dot{q} r^2}{4k} + C_1 \ln(r) + C_2$$

### Step 6: Apply BCs

**BC 1 (finite gradient at centerline):** $\dfrac{dT}{dr}\bigg\vert_{r=0}$ must be finite. The term $C_1 / r \to \pm\infty$ as $r \to 0$ **unless** $C_1 = 0$.

$$C_1 = 0$$

**BC 2 (surface temperature):** $T(R) = T_s$

$$T_s = -\frac{\dot{q} R^2}{4k} + 0 + C_2 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; C_2 = T_s + \frac{\dot{q}R^2}{4k}$$

### Step 7: Final radial temperature distribution

$$T(r) = T_s + \frac{\dot{q}}{4k}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)$$

### Step 8: Centerline temperature

$$T_0 = T_s + \frac{\dot{q}R^2}{4k}$$

### Step 9: Surface heat flux

$$q'' = -k\frac{dT}{dr}\bigg\vert_{r=R} = -k \cdot \left(-\frac{\dot{q}R}{2k}\right) = \frac{\dot{q}R}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy-balance check:** Heat generated per unit length = $\dot{q}\pi R^2$. Heat lost through lateral surface = $q'' \cdot 2\pi R = \dfrac{\dot{q}R}{2} \cdot 2\pi R = \dot{q}\pi R^2$. ✓ Perfectly consistent.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Solid Sphere with Heat Generation

**Setup:** Solid sphere of radius $R$, surface at $T_s$, uniform heat generation $\dot{q}$.

### Step 1: Governing ODE in spherical coordinates (radial only)

$$\frac{1}{r^2}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r^2\frac{dT}{dr}\right) + \frac{\dot{q}}{k} = 0$$

### Step 2: Multiply by $r^2$ to remove the singularity coefficient

$$\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r^2\frac{dT}{dr}\right) = -\frac{\dot{q} r^2}{k}$$

### Step 3: Integrate once

$$r^2\frac{dT}{dr} = -\frac{\dot{q} r^3}{3k} + C_1$$

### Step 4: Divide by $r^2$

$$\frac{dT}{dr} = -\frac{\dot{q} r}{3k} + \frac{C_1}{r^2}$$

### Step 5: Integrate a second time

$$T(r) = -\frac{\dot{q} r^2}{6k} - \frac{C_1}{r} + C_2$$

### Step 6: Apply BCs

**BC 1 (finite temperature at center):** As $r \to 0$, the term $-C_1/r$ diverges unless $C_1 = 0$.

$$C_1 = 0$$

**BC 2 (surface temperature):** $T(R) = T_s$

$$T_s = -\frac{\dot{q}R^2}{6k} + C_2 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; C_2 = T_s + \frac{\dot{q}R^2}{6k}$$

### Step 7: Final radial temperature distribution

$$T(r) = T_s + \frac{\dot{q}}{6k}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)$$

### Step 8: Centerline temperature

$$T_0 = T_s + \frac{\dot{q}R^2}{6k}$$

### Step 9: Surface heat flux

$$q'' = -k\frac{dT}{dr}\bigg\vert_{r=R} = -k \cdot \left(-\frac{\dot{q}R}{3k}\right) = \frac{\dot{q}R}{3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy-balance check:** Total heat generated = $\dot{q} \cdot \dfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3$. Heat lost through surface = $q'' \cdot 4\pi R^2 = \dfrac{\dot{q}R}{3} \cdot 4\pi R^2 = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3 \dot{q}$. ✓ Perfectly consistent.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Asymmetric Slab with One Insulated Face

**Setup:** A slab extends from $x = 0$ (held at $T_s$) to $x = L$ (insulated). Uniform generation $\dot{q}$.

### Step 1: Governing ODE (same as before)

$$\frac{d^2T}{dx^2} + \frac{\dot{q}}{k} = 0$$

### Step 2: General solution

$$T(x) = -\frac{\dot{q}x^2}{2k} + C_1 x + C_2$$

### Step 3: Apply BCs

**BC 1:** $T(0) = T_s \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; C_2 = T_s$

**BC 2:** $\dfrac{dT}{dx}\bigg\vert_{x=L} = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; 0 = -\dfrac{\dot{q}L}{k} + C_1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; C_1 = \dfrac{\dot{q}L}{k}$

### Step 4: Final temperature profile

$$T(x) = T_s + \frac{\dot{q}}{2k}\left(2Lx - x^2\right)$$

### Step 5: Maximum temperature at the insulated face $x = L$

$$T_0 = T_s + \frac{\dot{q}L^2}{2k}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual equivalence:** This result is identical in form to the *symmetric* slab with half-thickness $L$ because an insulated plane is a symmetry plane for the temperature profile. KTU often tests this equivalence — be prepared.

---
