<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Q1] [3 Marks]**
> **Q: Define the Nusselt Number. What is its physical significance in forced convection?**

**Model Answer**:
The **Nusselt Number** is a dimensionless group defined as the ratio of convective to conductive heat transfer across a fluid boundary layer:

$$Nu = \frac{h L_c}{k}$$

**Physical Significance**:
- It quantifies the enhancement of heat transfer due to convection over pure conduction.
- $Nu = 1$ implies pure conduction (no convection).
- $Nu \gg 1$ indicates convection dominates (e.g., turbulent flow over plate).
- It is the **dependent variable** in all empirical convection correlations: $Nu = f(Re, Pr)$.

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Q2] [3 Marks]**
> **Q: Why is the exponent on $Pr$ taken as 1/3 in most forced convection correlations?**

**Model Answer**:
The exponent **1/3** arises from the **Pohlhausen integral analysis** of the thermal boundary layer, where a fourth-order polynomial velocity profile is assumed to approximate the Blasius solution. Mathematically, when applying the Buckingham Pi theorem to the boundary layer energy equation and integrating the wall temperature gradient, the similarity variable $\eta = y \sqrt{u_\infty / \nu x}$ combined with the assumed velocity profile yields:

$$\left(\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial \eta}\right)_{\eta=0} \propto Pr^{1/3}$$

For liquids ($Pr >> 1$), a thin thermal layer exists, justifying the 1/3 law. For gases ($Pr \approx 0.7$), the 1/3 exponent is also valid. For liquid metals ($Pr << 1$), different correlations apply (e.g., $Nu \propto Pr^{0.5}$).

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Module 2 Question]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks) - Internal Choice Option 1**

**(a) Derive the local Nusselt number correlation for laminar flow over a flat plate, starting from the boundary layer energy equation. State the assumptions clearly. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution**:

**Assumptions**:
1. Steady, incompressible, 2-D flow.
2. Constant fluid properties.
3. Boundary layer approximation valid.
4. Negligible viscous dissipation.
5. No internal heat generation.
6. $Pr \geq 0.6$ (thermal BL thinner than velocity BL).

**Derivation**:

The boundary layer energy equation for a flat plate is:

$$u \frac{\partial T}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial T}{\partial y} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial y^2}$$

Define the non-dimensional temperature:

$$\theta = \frac{T - T_\infty}{T_s - T_\infty}$$

Define the similarity variable (Pohlhausen):

$$\eta = y \sqrt{\frac{u_\infty}{\nu x}}, \quad \text{and} \quad f'(\eta) = \frac{u}{u_\infty}$$

The governing ODE (from Pohlhausen analysis) gives:

$$\frac{d^3 f}{d\eta^3} + \frac{1}{2} f \frac{d^2 f}{d\eta^2} = 0 \quad \text{(Blasius)}$$

For the thermal profile, the wall temperature gradient is:

$$\left(\frac{\partial \theta}{\partial y}\right)_{y=0} = -\frac{(T_s - T_\infty)}{x} \sqrt{\frac{u_\infty}{\nu x}} \cdot 0.332 \, Pr^{1/3}$$

Using Newton's law of cooling:

$$h_x = \frac{-k (\partial T/\partial y)_{y=0}}{T_s - T_\infty} = \frac{0.332 \, k}{x} Re_x^{0.5} Pr^{1/3}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{Nu_x = 0.332 \, Re_x^{0.5} Pr^{1/3}}$$

**(b) Engine oil at 60°C enters a 5 cm diameter tube with a mean velocity of 2 m/s. The tube wall is maintained at a constant temperature of 20°C by cooling water on the outside. Estimate the heat transfer coefficient using an appropriate correlation. Given: for engine oil at 40°C, $\rho = 876$ kg/m³, $k = 0.144$ W/m·K, $\mu = 0.21$ N·s/m², $c_p = 1964$ J/kg·K. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1: Identify flow regime**
Calculate $Re_D$:
$$Re_D = \frac{\rho u_m D}{\mu} = \frac{876 \times 2 \times 0.05}{0.21} = 417.14$$

Since $Re_D = 417.14 < 2300$, the flow is **LAMINAR**.

**Step 2: Determine Prandtl Number**
$$Pr = \frac{c_p \mu}{k} = \frac{1964 \times 0.21}{0.144} = 2864.17$$

**Step 3: Check entry length**
Thermal entry length: $L_t = 0.05 Re_D Pr D = 0.05 \times 417.14 \times 2864.17 \times 0.05 = 2987$ m
Hydrodynamic entry length: $L_h = 0.05 Re_D D = 0.05 \times 417.14 \times 0.05 = 1.04$ m

If the tube is short, use **Sieder-Tate for entry region**:
$$Nu = 1.86 \left( \frac{Re_D Pr D}{L} \right)^{1/3} \left( \frac{\mu_b}{\mu_s} \right)^{0.14}$$

If the tube is long (> 2987 m), flow is **thermally fully developed**:
$$Nu = 3.66 \quad \text{(constant wall temp)}$$

**Step 4: Assume a 3 m long tube (typical)**:
$$\frac{Re_D Pr D}{L} = \frac{417.14 \times 2864.17 \times 0.05}{3} = 19910$$

Since this is > 10, Sieder-Tate applies.

For viscosity correction at film temp: $T_f = (60+20)/2 = 40°C$. 
Assume $\mu_b \approx \mu_s$ (properties given at 40°C ≈ film temp):
$$\left(\frac{\mu_b}{\mu_s}\right)^{0.14} \approx 1$$

$$Nu = 1.86 \times (19910)^{1/3} \times 1 = 1.86 \times 27.17 = 50.53$$

**Step 5: Calculate $h$**:
$$h = \frac{Nu \cdot k}{D} = \frac{50.53 \times 0.144}{0.05} = 145.5 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**Final Answer**: $h \approx 145.5$ W/m²·K

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) - Internal Choice Option 2**

**(a) With a neat sketch, explain the development of hydrodynamic and thermal boundary layers in flow through a circular tube. Define hydrodynamic entry length and thermal entry length. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer**:

**Sketch**:
```
|  |
|  |  _______________
|  | |               |  <-- Uniform Velocity u_m
|  | |               |
|  | |  ______       |
|  | | |      |      |  <-- Velocity Profile Forming
|  | | |  →   |      |
|  | | |______|      |
|  | |               |
|  | |  ___________  |
|  | | |           | |  <-- Fully Developed Parabolic Profile
|  | | |    →→     | |
|  | | |___________| |
|__|_|_______________|___
   |   |   |   |   |
  Entry  Develop  Fully
        Region  Developed
```

**Definitions**:

1. **Hydrodynamic Entry Length ($L_h$)**: The distance from the tube entrance required for the velocity boundary layer to merge at the centerline, after which the flow becomes **hydrodynamically fully developed**.
$$L_h = 0.05 \, Re_D \, D \quad \text{(laminar)}$$

2. **Thermal Entry Length ($L_t$)**: The distance required for the thermal boundary layer to reach the tube centerline, after which the **thermal profile is fully developed**.
$$L_t = 0.05 \, Re_D \, Pr \, D \quad \text{(laminar)}$$

**Key Concepts**:
- For $L < L_h$: Velocity profile is **developing** (uniform at entry → parabolic).
- For $L > L_h$: Velocity profile is **fully developed** (parabolic, no change in shape).
- For **constant wall temperature** ($T_s$): Nusselt number is 3.66.
- For **constant heat flux** ($q''_s$): Nusselt number is 4.36.

**(b) Air at 27°C flows over a flat plate of length 2 m with a free stream velocity of 3 m/s. The plate is maintained at 67°C. Determine: (i) the location of transition from laminar to turbulent flow, (ii) the total drag force on one side of the plate, and (iii) the average heat transfer coefficient. [7 Marks]**

**Given**: Air at 27°C: $\nu = 15.89 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, $Pr = 0.707$, $k = 0.02624$ W/m·K.

**Step 1: Critical location**
$$x_{cr} = \frac{Re_{cr} \cdot \nu}{u_\infty} = \frac{5 \times 10^5 \times 15.89 \times 10^{-6}}{3} = 2.648 \text{ m}$$

Since $L = 2$ m $< x_{cr} = 2.648$ m, the **flow is fully laminar** over the entire plate.

**Step 2: Average $h$ (laminar)**:
$$Re_L = \frac{u_\infty L}{\nu} = \frac{3 \times 2}{15.89 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.776 \times 10^5$$
$$Nu_L = 0.664 \times (3.776 \times 10^5)^{0.5} \times (0.707)^{1/3}$$
$$Nu_L = 0.664 \times 614.5 \times 0.8907 = 363.4$$
$$h = \frac{Nu_L \cdot k}{L} = \frac{363.4 \times 0.02624}{2} = 4.77 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**Step 3: Total drag force** (using average friction coefficient):
$$C_D = \frac{1.328}{\sqrt{Re_L}} = \frac{1.328}{\sqrt{3.776 \times 10^5}} = \frac{1.328}{614.5} = 0.00216$$
$$F_D = C_D \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho u_\infty^2 \cdot A$$

For air, $\rho = 1.177$ kg/m³ (at 27°C). Assuming plate width $W = 1$ m (per unit width):
$$F_D = 0.00216 \times 0.5 \times 1.177 \times 3^2 \times (2 \times 1) = 0.0224 \text{ N}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Property Evaluation Temperature**: Always evaluate fluid properties at the **film temperature** ($T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2$) unless the problem explicitly states otherwise. Using $T_\infty$ properties when the problem wants film properties is a **2-mark penalty** in 14-mark questions.
> 2. **Forgetting the Viscosity Correction Ratio** $(\mu_b/\mu_s)^{0.14}$ in Sieder-Tate: This ratio accounts for property variation and is a **favorite KTU question**. Skipping it loses 2-3 marks.
> 3. **Mixing Up $Nu$ Constants**: $3.66$ is for constant wall temperature, $4.36$ is for constant heat flux. Confusing them is a 1-mark error.
> 4. **The "+2" in Sphere Correlations**: When asked "why 2?", students must explain it's the **conduction limit** ($Nu \to 2$ as $Re \to 0$), corresponding to pure conduction in an infinite medium. Not explaining this loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Mixing Up $Re$ Definition**: Always use $Re_L = u_\infty L / \nu$ for plates and $Re_D = \rho u_m D / \mu$ for tubes. Substituting diameter for length or vice versa is a 2-mark error.
> 6. **Laminar vs Turbulent Selection**: Always compare $Re$ with critical values ($5 \times 10^5$ for plate, $2300$ for tube). Not checking this is a 3-mark error.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST**

- [ ] **Nusselt Number** definition: $Nu = hL_c / k$ — always required as the dependent variable.
- [ ] **Critical $Re$ for flat plate** = $5 \times 10^5$; for tube = $2300$ (laminar-turbulent transition).
- [ ] **Laminar plate local**: $Nu_x = 0.332 Re_x^{0.5} Pr^{1/3}$
- [ ] **Laminar plate average**: $Nu_L = 0.664 Re_L^{0.5} Pr^{1/3}$
- [ ] **Turbulent plate local**: $Nu_x = 0.0296 Re_x^{0.8} Pr^{1/3}$
- [ ] **Turbulent plate average**: $Nu_L = 0.037 Re_L^{0.8} Pr^{1/3}$
- [ ] **Mixed boundary layer** (laminar + turbulent on same plate): $Nu_L = (0.037 Re_L^{0.8} - 870) Pr^{1/3}$
- [ ] **Dittus-Boelter** (heating): $Nu = 0.023 Re^{0.8} Pr^{0.4}$; **Cooling**: $Pr^{0.3}$
- [ ] **Sieder-Tate** (with property variation): $Nu = 0.027 Re^{0.8} Pr^{1/3} (\mu_b/\mu_s)^{0.14}$
- [ ] **Entry region** Sieder-Tate: $Nu = 1.86(Re Pr D/L)^{1/3} (\mu_b/\mu_s)^{0.14}$
- [ ] **Fully developed laminar** — $Nu = 3.66$ (const $T_s$); $Nu = 4.36$ (const $q''_s$).
- [ ] **Ranz-Marshall sphere**: $Nu = 2 + 0.6 Re^{0.5} Pr^{1/3}$ — the "2" is the conduction limit.
- [ ] **Whitaker sphere**: $Nu = 2 + (0.4 Re^{0.5} + 0.06 Re^{2/3}) Pr^{0.4} (\mu_\infty/\mu_s)^{1/4}$ — more accurate.
- [ ] **Churchill-Bernstein cylinder**: Most general — valid across all $Re$ ranges.
- [ ] **Hilpert cylinder**: $Nu = C Re^m$ — table of $C$ and $m$ per $Re$ range.
- [ ] Always evaluate properties at **film temperature** $T_f$ or **bulk mean temperature** $T_b$ — state the assumption.
- [ ] $Pr^{1/3}$ exponent arises from Pohlhausen 4th-order polynomial velocity profile assumption.
- [ ] $Pr^{0.4}$ or $Pr^{0.3}$ in Dittus-Boelter differentiates heating (n=0.4) from cooling (n=0.3).
- [ ] For tubes, **hydrodynamic entry length**: $L_h = 0.05 Re D$ (laminar).
- [ ] For tubes, **thermal entry length**: $L_t = 0.05 Re Pr D$ (laminar).
- [ ] Mixed convection: dominant mechanism decided by comparing $Gr/Re^2$ with 0.1 and 10.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Film Concept of Heat Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT501 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Film Concept of Heat Transfer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🌡️ FILM CONCEPT OF HEAT TRANSFER

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

The **Film Concept of Heat Transfer** is a classical hydrodynamic and thermal model proposed to describe the mechanism of **convective heat transfer** between a solid surface and a fluid flowing over it. According to this concept, a thin, relatively stagnant layer of fluid (called the **thermal boundary layer** or **film**) exists immediately adjacent to the solid surface. The entire temperature difference between the wall and the bulk fluid stream is assumed to be concentrated across this thin film.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme - Module 2):** The film concept forms the foundation of understanding the **individual heat transfer coefficient (h)** and the derivation of **Newton's Law of Cooling**. It is the conceptual bridge between conduction and convection.

Mathematically, the heat transfer rate based on the film concept is:

$$q = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = \frac{k_f}{\delta_f} \cdot A \cdot (T_w - T_b)$$

where **h** is the individual/film heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K), **k_f** is the thermal conductivity of the fluid (W/m·K), and **δ_f** is the effective film thickness (m).

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Building Intuition)

Imagine you are standing in a **fast-flowing river**. The water rushing past you feels cold and uniform. Now, place your hand on a **large flat rock** lying in the middle of the stream. The rock surface feels comparatively warmer (or cooler). 

Between the fast-moving water and the rock, there is an **invisible thin layer of nearly-still water** hugging the rock's surface. This is the "film." 

- The temperature **jump** from the rock's surface to the river's bulk happens almost entirely across this thin film.
- Outside this film, the water is well-mixed and at a single bulk temperature.
- The thinner this stagnant layer, the more vigorous the heat exchange — because heat has to "punch through" less insulating fluid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Definition:** The film concept treats convective heat transfer as if it were a **conduction process** across a fictitious thin, stagnant layer of fluid stuck to the wall, while ignoring complex fluid motion outside this layer.

## 1.3 Why the Film Concept Matters in Engineering

- It provides a **simple, physically intuitive** way to define the convective heat transfer coefficient **h**.
- It allows engineers to **decouple fluid mechanics** (complex turbulent eddies) from heat transfer (treated as steady conduction).
- All empirical correlations (Dittus-Boelter, Sieder-Tate, etc.) are essentially **corrections to the simple film picture**.
- It is the basis for deriving **overall heat transfer coefficients (U)** used in exchanger design.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Range/Value | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **h** | Film (convective) coefficient | 5 – 25,000 | **W/m²·K** |
| **k_f** | Fluid thermal conductivity | 0.02 – 0.7 | **W/m·K** |
| **δ_f** | Effective film thickness | 0.1 – 10 | **mm** |
| **T_w** | Wall temperature | Variable | **K** or **°C** |
| **T_b** | Bulk fluid temperature | Variable | **K** or **°C** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Underlying Physical Picture

The film concept rests on **three foundational assumptions**:

1. **Existence of a Stagnant Film:** A thin, laminar layer of fluid adheres to the heat transfer surface due to viscous forces (no-slip condition).
2. **Conduction Across the Film:** Heat transfer across this film occurs **purely by molecular conduction** (Fourier's Law), since the fluid is essentially motionless.
3. **No Temperature Gradient Outside the Film:** Beyond the film, the fluid is perfectly mixed at the **bulk temperature (T_b)**. The convective eddies instantaneously equalize the temperature in the bulk.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Buildup

- **Step 1 — Temperature Profile:** Near a heated wall, fluid temperature drops sharply from **T_w** (at the wall) to **T_b** (in the bulk) across a short distance **y = δ_f**.
- **Step 2 — Fourier's Law Application:** Since conduction governs the film, the heat flux is given by:

$$q'' = -k_f \cdot \frac{dT}{dy}$$

- **Step 3 — Linearization:** Assuming a linear temperature profile across the thin film (valid for small δ_f and constant k_f):

$$\frac{dT}{dy} \approx \frac{T_w - T_b}{\delta_f}$$

- **Step 4 — Definition of h:** Equating the conductive flux to Newton's Law of Cooling:

$$h = \frac{q''}{T_w - T_b} = \frac{k_f}{\delta_f}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Insight:** The film coefficient **h** is **inversely proportional** to the film thickness. This is why high-velocity flows (which reduce δ_f via turbulence) yield higher **h** values and more efficient heat exchangers.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $q = h \cdot A \cdot (T_w - T_b)$ | Newton's Law of Cooling (film-based) | **W** |
| 2 | $h = \frac{k_f}{\delta_f}$ | Definition of film coefficient via conductivity | **W/m²·K** |
| 3 | $q'' = \frac{k_f \cdot (T_w - T_b)}{\delta_f}$ | Heat flux through film (Fourier analogy) | **W/m²** |
| 4 | $\delta_f = \frac{k_f}{h}$ | Effective film thickness | **m** |
| 5 | $\frac{h \cdot L}{k_f} = \frac{L}{\delta_f} = Nu$ | Nusselt number as film-thickness ratio | dimensionless |
| 6 | $R_{film} = \frac{1}{h \cdot A}$ | Thermal resistance of the film | **K/W** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In practice, **δ_f is fictitious** — we never measure it directly. Instead, we use experimental data to back-calculate **h**, and that **h** is plugged into exchanger design equations. The film is a *conceptual tool*, not a measurable boundary.

## 2.4 Limitations of the Film Concept

- It is a **simplified model**; in reality, the thermal boundary layer has a gradual transition, not a sharp cutoff.
- It assumes constant **k_f**; in truth, **k_f** varies with temperature.
- It does not directly account for **turbulence intensity** — empirical corrections (e.g., Nusselt correlations) handle this.
- The "stagnant film" is an idealization; in turbulent flow, only a very thin **viscous sublayer** is truly laminar.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL FRAMEWORK

## 3.1 Derivation: Linking the Film Concept to Newton's Law of Cooling

### Starting Premise (Fourier's Law of Conduction in the y-direction)

By Fourier's Law, the heat flux conducted across a stationary fluid layer is:

$$q'' = -k_f \cdot \frac{dT}{dy}$$

### Step 1 — Boundary Conditions for the Film

At the wall-solid interface (y = 0): the fluid temperature equals the wall temperature.

$$T(y=0) = T_w$$

At the edge of the film (y = δ_f): the fluid reaches the bulk temperature.

$$T(y=\delta_f) = T_b$$

### Step 2 — Integration Across the Film

Separating variables and integrating the Fourier equation from the wall to the film edge:

$$q'' \int_0^{\delta_f} dy = -k_f \int_{T_w}^{T_b} dT$$

This yields:

$$q'' \cdot \delta_f = -k_f \cdot (T_b - T_w)$$

### Step 3 — Rearranging for Heat Flux

Pulling out the sign by reversing the temperature difference:

$$q'' = \frac{k_f \cdot (T_w - T_b)}{\delta_f}$$

### Step 4 — Comparison with Newton's Law

Newton's Law of Cooling states:

$$q'' = h \cdot (T_w - T_b)$$

### Step 5 — Defining the Film Coefficient

By direct comparison of the two expressions:

$$h \cdot (T_w - T_b) = \frac{k_f \cdot (T_w - T_b)}{\delta_f}$$

Cancelling the common temperature difference term:

$$h = \frac{k_f}{\delta_f}$$

### Step 6 — Closed-Form Result

The final closed-form relationship between the film coefficient, fluid conductivity, and effective film thickness is:

$$\boxed{\;h = \frac{k_f}{\delta_f}\;}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

> **Problem:** Air at **50 °C** flows over a flat plate maintained at **100 °C**. The effective film thickness is **2 mm**, and the thermal conductivity of air at the mean film temperature is **0.028 W/m·K**. The heat transfer area is **0.5 m²**. Calculate: (a) the film heat transfer coefficient, (b) the heat transfer rate.

### Given Data
- $T_w = 100\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- $T_b = 50\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- $\delta_f = 2\ \text{mm} = 0.002\ \text{m}$
- $k_f = 0.028\ \text{W/m·K}$
- $A = 0.5\ \text{m}^2$

### Part (a): Film Coefficient **h**

Apply the film concept formula:

$$h = \frac{k_f}{\delta_f}$$

Substitute the values:

$$h = \frac{0.028\ \text{W/m·K}}{0.002\ \text{m}}$$

Perform the division:

$$h = 14\ \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$$

### Part (b): Heat Transfer Rate **q**

Apply Newton's Law of Cooling:

$$q = h \cdot A \cdot (T_w - T_b)$$

Substitute the values:

$$q = 14\ \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K} \times 0.5\ \text{m}^2 \times (100 - 50)\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

$$q = 14 \times 0.5 \times 50$$

$$q = 350\ \text{W}$$

### Final Result
- The film coefficient is **h = 14 W/m²·K**.
- The heat transfer rate is **q = 350 W**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** A typical value of **h = 14 W/m²·K** falls well within the expected range for **natural convection of gases** (5–25 W/m²·K), confirming the film-based prediction is physically consistent.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How Film Concept Links to Other HTO Modules

| Concept from Film Theory | Connects To | Engineering Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Effective film thickness δ_f | Reynolds number, Prandtl number | Predicting h in laminar/turbulent flow |
| Nusselt number Nu = hL/k | Dimensionless analysis | Scale-up of laboratory data to plant scale |
| Thermal resistance 1/(hA) | Overall heat transfer coefficient U | Heat exchanger design (LMTD, ε-NTU methods) |
| Temperature gradient at wall | Boiling & condensation correlations | Reboiler and condenser sizing |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define the film concept of heat transfer. What is meant by the "effective film thickness"?**

**Model Answer:**

The film concept of heat transfer states that during convection between a solid surface and a moving fluid, a thin, relatively stagnant layer of fluid (called the *film* or *thermal boundary layer*) exists adjacent to the solid surface. The entire temperature difference between the wall and the bulk fluid is assumed to occur across this film, and heat is transferred through it purely by molecular conduction (Fourier's Law).

The **effective film thickness (δ_f)** is the hypothetical thickness of this stagnant layer such that if the heat were transferred by conduction alone through a fluid layer of thermal conductivity **k_f**, it would yield the same heat flux as the actual convective process. It is defined by the relation:

$$h = \frac{k_f}{\delta_f}$$

It is not a directly measurable quantity but a conceptual parameter used to visualize and quantify convective resistance.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**State Newton's Law of Cooling. How is the film heat transfer coefficient defined?**

**Model Answer:**

**Newton's Law of Cooling** states that the rate of heat transfer by convection is directly proportional to the temperature difference between the solid surface and the bulk fluid, and to the area of heat transfer.

Mathematically:

$$q = h \cdot A \cdot (T_w - T_b)$$

The **film heat transfer coefficient (h)** is defined as the **proportionality constant** in Newton's Law. Physically, it represents the heat flux per unit temperature difference per unit area. From the film concept, it is related to the fluid thermal conductivity and effective film thickness by:

$$h = \frac{k_f}{\delta_f}$$

Its units are **W/m²·K**, and its magnitude depends on fluid properties, flow regime (laminar/turbulent), geometry, and driving force.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the relationship between the **individual heat transfer coefficient (h)**, the **fluid thermal conductivity (k_f)**, and the **effective film thickness (δ_f)** from first principles using the film concept. State clearly the assumptions made.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A metal pipe of outer diameter **50 mm** carries steam at **150 °C**. It is exposed to ambient air at **30 °C** with a film coefficient of **15 W/m²·K**. If the pipe surface temperature is **140 °C** and the length of pipe considered is **5 m**, calculate:
   1. The heat loss per unit time.
   2. The effective film thickness, given the thermal conductivity of air as **0.03 W/m·K**.

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation

**Assumptions of the Film Concept:**

1. A thin, stagnant film of fluid of thickness **δ_f** exists adjacent to the heat transfer surface.
2. Heat transfer across this film occurs **purely by molecular conduction** (Fourier's Law).
3. Beyond the film, the fluid is perfectly mixed and at a single bulk temperature **T_b** (no temperature gradient).
4. The thermal conductivity **k_f** is constant across the film.

**Derivation:**

Fourier's Law of conduction in the y-direction (perpendicular to wall):

$$q'' = -k_f \cdot \frac{dT}{dy}$$

**Boundary Conditions:**

- At the wall surface: $T(y=0) = T_w$
- At the film edge: $T(y=\delta_f) = T_b$

**Integration across the film:**

$$q'' \int_0^{\delta_f} dy = -k_f \int_{T_w}^{T_b} dT$$

$$q'' \cdot \delta_f = -k_f \cdot (T_b - T_w)$$

$$q'' = \frac{k_f \cdot (T_w - T_b)}{\delta_f}$$

**Comparison with Newton's Law:**

$$q'' = h \cdot (T_w - T_b)$$

**Equating the two:**

$$\boxed{\;h = \frac{k_f}{\delta_f}\;}$$

This completes the derivation, showing that **h** is inversely proportional to the film thickness and directly proportional to fluid conductivity.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- Outer diameter $D = 50\ \text{mm} = 0.05\ \text{m}$
- Steam temperature $T_{\text{steam}} = 150\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- Ambient air temperature $T_\infty = 30\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- Film coefficient $h = 15\ \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$
- Wall surface temperature $T_w = 140\ ^\circ\text{C}$
- Pipe length $L = 5\ \text{m}$
- Air thermal conductivity $k_f = 0.03\ \text{W/m·K}$

**Step 1 — Calculate the heat transfer area:**

$$A = \pi \cdot D \cdot L = \pi \times 0.05 \times 5$$

$$A = 0.7854\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the heat loss rate:**

$$q = h \cdot A \cdot (T_w - T_\infty)$$

$$q = 15 \times 0.7854 \times (140 - 30)$$

$$q = 15 \times 0.7854 \times 110$$

$$q = 1295.9\ \text{W} \approx 1.296\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the effective film thickness:**

$$\delta_f = \frac{k_f}{h} = \frac{0.03}{15}$$

$$\delta_f = 0.002\ \text{m} = 2\ \text{mm}$$

**Final Results:**
- Heat loss rate: **q ≈ 1.296 kW**
- Effective film thickness: **δ_f = 2 mm**

---

### 📘 Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **film theory of convection** with a neat temperature profile sketch. Discuss its **limitations** in real engineering systems.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A vertical hot plate at **80 °C** is cooled by natural convection in air at **20 °C**. The film coefficient is **8 W/m²·K**, and the plate area is **2 m²**. The thermal conductivity of air is **0.026 W/m·K**.
   1. Compute the rate of heat removal.
   2. Find the effective film thickness.
   3. If a fan is used to increase **h** to **40 W/m²·K**, by what factor does the heat removal rate increase?

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

#### Part (a) — Theory + Limitations

The **film theory of convection** postulates that:
- A thin, stagnant film of fluid of thickness **δ_f** clings to the heat transfer surface.
- The entire temperature drop from wall (**T_w**) to bulk fluid (**T_b**) occurs across this film.
- Heat transfer across the film is by **molecular conduction** alone.
- The bulk fluid is at uniform temperature **T_b** (well-mixed region).

The temperature profile is therefore **linear** within the film and **flat** in the bulk region, as illustrated in the schematic in Section 4.2.

**Limitations:**
1. The film thickness is **fictitious**; it cannot be measured directly.
2. The model assumes constant **k_f**, ignoring temperature-dependent variation.
3. The sharp transition from film to bulk is unrealistic; in practice, the thermal boundary layer grows gradually.
4. It does not account for **turbulence intensity** explicitly — empirical corrections like Nusselt correlations are required.
5. For high-Prandtl fluids (oils), the thermal boundary layer is much thinner than the hydrodynamic one, contradicting the film's implicit one-to-one correspondence.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $T_w = 80\ ^\circ\text{C}$, $T_b = 20\ ^\circ\text{C}$, $h_1 = 8\ \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$, $A = 2\ \text{m}^2$, $k_f = 0.026\ \text{W/m·K}$, $h_2 = 40\ \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$

**1. Rate of heat removal (natural convection):**

$$q_1 = h_1 \cdot A \cdot (T_w - T_b) = 8 \times 2 \times (80 - 20) = 8 \times 2 \times 60 = 960\ \text{W}$$

**2. Effective film thickness:**

$$\delta_f = \frac{k_f}{h_1} = \frac{0.026}{8} = 0.00325\ \text{m} = 3.25\ \text{mm}$$

**3. Heat removal with forced convection ($h_2 = 40$ W/m²·K):**

$$q_2 = h_2 \cdot A \cdot (T_w - T_b) = 40 \times 2 \times 60 = 4800\ \text{W}$$

**4. Factor of increase:**

$$\text{Factor} = \frac{q_2}{q_1} = \frac{4800}{960} = 5$$

**Final Results:**
- Natural convection heat removal: **q₁ = 960 W**
- Effective film thickness: **δ_f = 3.25 mm**
- Increase factor with fan: **5×** (heat removal rises from 960 W to 4800 W)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often **forget to convert mm to m** when computing film thickness. A common error is writing **δ_f = 0.026 / 8 = 0.00325** but leaving the unit as "m" without the millimeter conversion check. Always verify dimensional consistency: $k_f$ is in W/m·K and $h$ is in W/m²·K, so $\delta_f$ **must be in meters**. Additionally, in 14-mark derivations, examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if the **assumptions of the film concept are not explicitly listed** before starting the integration.

---

## 5.3 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ The **film concept** assumes a thin, stagnant fluid layer (film) of thickness **δ_f** adjacent to the heat transfer surface.
- ✅ The entire temperature difference **T_w − T_b** is assumed to drop linearly across this film.
- ✅ Heat transfer through the film is by **pure conduction (Fourier's Law)**, not convection.
- ✅ The **film heat transfer coefficient** is defined as $h = \dfrac{k_f}{\delta_f}$.
- ✅ The **effective film thickness** is a **fictitious, conceptual quantity** — never directly measured.
- ✅ **Newton's Law of Cooling** is: $q = h \cdot A \cdot (T_w - T_b)$.
- ✅ The **Nusselt number** is the dimensionless film thickness ratio: $Nu = \dfrac{h \cdot L}{k_f} = \dfrac{L}{\delta_f}$.
- ✅ **Higher h** ⟹ **smaller δ_f** ⟹ **thinner film** ⟹ **more efficient** heat transfer.
- ✅ **Forced convection** typically yields much higher **h** (50–25,000 W/m²·K) than **natural convection** (5–25 W/m²·K) for gases.
- ✅ The film concept is the **conceptual foundation** for deriving the **overall heat transfer coefficient U** in multi-layer systems.
- ✅ The model has **limitations**: no turbulence, no property variation, fictitious film — but remains a powerful pedagogical and engineering tool.
- ✅ **Always state assumptions** before deriving from the film concept in KTU exams to avoid losing marks.
- ✅ **Watch unit conversions**: mm → m for film thickness; °C differences are equivalent to K differences.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Individual and Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT501 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Individual and Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Individual Heat Transfer Coefficient ($h$)

The **Individual Heat Transfer Coefficient** (also called the **convective heat transfer coefficient** or **film coefficient**) is a proportionality constant that quantifies the rate of heat transfer between a solid surface and a fluid moving over it, driven solely by **convection** at that single interface.

The defining rate equation is Newton's Law of Cooling:

$$
q = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T
$$

where:
- $q$ = rate of heat transfer (W)
- $h$ = individual heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = heat transfer area (m²)
- $\Delta T$ = temperature difference between the surface and the bulk fluid (K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Syllabus Standard):** The individual heat transfer coefficient $h$ represents the conductance of heat transfer across a *single fluid film boundary* — it does **not** account for conduction through the wall itself or convection on the other side. Typical values range from **5–25 W/m²·K for natural convection of gases**, **100–1000 W/m²·K for forced convection of liquids**, and **5000–25,000 W/m²·K for condensing steam or boiling water**.

## 1.2 Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient ($U$)

The **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient** bundles the *entire* thermal resistance path — inner convection, wall conduction (possibly through multiple layers), and outer convection — into a **single coefficient**, allowing a clean one-line rate equation:

$$
q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{overall}
$$

where $\Delta T_{overall}$ is the temperature difference between the **two bulk fluids** (hot fluid and cold fluid), not between any wall surface and a fluid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Overall" Exists:** In real equipment (heat exchangers, reactor walls, insulated pipes), heat passes through a *chain* of resistances. Instead of writing $q = \Delta T_{total}/R_{total}$, engineers prefer $q = U A \Delta T_{overall}$, where $U$ conveniently absorbs all the resistances. This is the form used in 90% of chemical engineering heat-exchanger design.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Electrical Circuit

Imagine electricity flowing through a circuit made of resistors. The current $I$ equals voltage divided by total resistance: $I = V/R_{total}$. Each resistor adds a hurdle.

Now substitute:
- **Current** → **Heat flux** $q$
- **Voltage drop** → **Temperature drop** $\Delta T$
- **Electrical resistance** → **Thermal resistance** $R_{th}$

Then $q = \Delta T / R_{th,total}$. The **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient** is simply the reciprocal of the *total* thermal resistance, evaluated per unit area:

$$
\frac{1}{U \cdot A} = R_{th,total}
$$

Just like in a circuit, resistances can be **in series** (plane composite wall) or **in parallel** (cylindrical or finned surfaces with multiple heat paths). This thermal-electrical analogy is the **single most powerful tool** for solving composite heat-transfer problems in KTU exams.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Box:** Think of a sandwich wall. Heat must cross the inside air film (resistance $1/h_i$), then bread layer 1 (resistance $x_1/k_1$), then cheese (resistance $x_2/k_2$), then bread layer 2 ($x_3/k_3$), then the outside air film ($1/h_o$). All in **series** — like a chain of resistors. The smallest resistance dominates; if the inside air film has very low $h$, it bottlenecks the entire flow even if the wall is highly conductive.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Thermal Resistance Concept

Every mode of heat transfer offers a *resistance* to the flow of heat. The greater the resistance, the smaller the heat flux for a given driving temperature difference.

| Mode | Driving Force | Resistance Form (per unit area) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Convection (fluid film) | $\Delta T$ between surface and bulk fluid | $R_{conv} = 1/h$ | m²·K/W |
| Conduction (plane slab) | $\Delta T$ across thickness $x$ | $R_{cond} = x/k$ | m²·K/W |
| Conduction (cylindrical, length $L$) | $\Delta T$ between radii $r_1$ and $r_2$ | $R_{cond} = \ln(r_2/r_1)/(2\pi k L)$ | K/W |
| Radiation | $\Delta T^4$ | $R_{rad} = 1/h_r$ | m²·K/W |

## 2.2 Resistances in Series — Plane Composite Wall

For a flat wall composed of multiple solid layers with fluids on both sides:

$$
q = \frac{T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}}{\dfrac{1}{h_i A} + \dfrac{x_1}{k_1 A} + \dfrac{x_2}{k_2 A} + \cdots + \dfrac{x_n}{k_n A} + \dfrac{1}{h_o A}}
$$

For a unit-area expression, divide numerator and denominator by $A$:

$$
\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{x_j}{k_j} + \frac{1}{h_o}
$$

where:
- $h_i$ = inside (hot-side) convective coefficient
- $h_o$ = outside (cold-side) convective coefficient
- $x_j, k_j$ = thickness and thermal conductivity of the $j^{th}$ solid layer
- $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient based on area $A$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Controlling Resistance:** The largest term in the sum determines the dominant resistance. In many practical problems, one of the convective films contributes the most — adding wall layers beyond a certain thickness becomes wasteful because $1/h$ already controls the heat flow.

## 2.3 Resistances in Series — Cylindrical (Pipe) Wall

For a cylindrical pipe with insulation layers, the heat-transfer rate depends on the **logarithmic mean area** because area changes with radius:

$$
q = \frac{T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}}{\dfrac{1}{h_i (2\pi r_i L)} + \dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_1 L} + \dfrac{\ln(r_3/r_2)}{2\pi k_2 L} + \cdots + \dfrac{1}{h_o (2\pi r_o L)}}
$$

This is most cleanly expressed via an **overall coefficient based on the inner area**:

$$
\frac{1}{U_i} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{r_i}{k_1}\ln\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) + \frac{r_i}{k_2}\ln\!\left(\frac{r_3}{r_2}\right) + \cdots + \frac{r_i}{r_o}\cdot\frac{1}{h_o}
$$

Equivalently on the outer area:

$$
\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{r_o}{r_i}\cdot\frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{r_o}{k_1}\ln\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) + \frac{r_o}{k_2}\ln\!\left(\frac{r_3}{r_2}\right) + \cdots + \frac{1}{h_o}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $U$ depends on which area you choose:** $q$ is a single physical quantity (in W), but $U \cdot A$ must equal $q$. Therefore if you redefine area from $A_i$ to $A_o$, $U$ must scale accordingly: $U_i A_i = U_o A_o$. KTU examiners frequently test this conversion.

## 2.4 Fouling Factor ($R_f$)

Real equipment accumulates dirt, scale, rust, and biological films on heat-transfer surfaces. These add *extra* thermal resistance, called the **fouling resistance** or **fouling factor**:

$$
\frac{1}{U_{dirty}} = \frac{1}{U_{clean}} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}
$$

Typical values of $R_f$:
- Cooling water: $0.0002$ to $0.0006$ m²·K/W
- Sea water: $0.0003$ to $0.001$ m²·K/W
- Oil streams: $0.0004$ to $0.002$ m²·K/W

## 2.5 Critical Radius of Insulation

A counterintuitive but exam-favorite result: adding insulation to a *bare* cylinder or wire can **increase** the heat loss if the outer radius $r$ is less than a critical value:

$$
r_c = \frac{k_{ins}}{h_o}
$$

where $k_{ins}$ is the thermal conductivity of the insulation and $h_o$ is the outside convective coefficient. This occurs because while insulation adds conduction resistance, it also increases the outer surface area where convection occurs. The two effects compete, and below $r_c$ the area-increase effect dominates.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Quantity | Plane Wall | Cylindrical Wall |
|---|---|---|
| Overall resistance per unit area (inner basis) | $\dfrac{1}{U_i} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \sum \dfrac{x_j}{k_j} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$ | $\dfrac{1}{U_i} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \sum \dfrac{r_i \ln(r_{j+1}/r_j)}{k_j} + \dfrac{r_i}{r_o}\cdot\dfrac{1}{h_o}$ |
| Overall resistance per unit area (outer basis) | same (plane geometry) | $\dfrac{1}{U_o} = \dfrac{r_o}{r_i}\cdot\dfrac{1}{h_i} + \sum \dfrac{r_o \ln(r_{j+1}/r_j)}{k_j} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$ |
| Critical radius of insulation | not applicable | $r_c = k_{ins}/h_o$ |
| Fouling adjusted | $1/U = 1/U_{clean} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$ | same form |
| Heat rate | $q = U A \Delta T$ | $q = U_i A_i \Delta T = U_o A_o \Delta T$ |
| Conversion | $U A = \text{const}$ | $U_i r_i = U_o r_o$ (per unit length) |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Heat Exchanger Design:** $U$ values are tabulated for shell-and-tube exchangers handling specific fluid pairs (e.g., water-to-water ≈ 1500 W/m²·K, condensing steam to oil ≈ 300 W/m²·K). Used in LMTD and effectiveness-NTU methods.
- **Building Insulation:** Wall $U$-values (or $R$-values in the US) dictate heating/cooling load. KTU students may see Building Science analogues.
- **Reactor Design:** Jacketed chemical reactors use $U$-values to size cooling coils.
- **Process Piping:** Insulating steam lines requires checking $r_c$ to avoid the *increase* in heat loss on thin pipes — a frequent industrial pitfall.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient for a Plane Composite Wall

**Setup:** A plane wall has inside fluid at $T_{h,i}$ with coefficient $h_i$, three solid layers with thicknesses $x_1, x_2, x_3$ and conductivities $k_1, k_2, k_3$, and outside fluid at $T_{c,o}$ with coefficient $h_o$. Temperatures at the interfaces are $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$ (see Section 4 for diagram).

**Step 1: Heat flux through each resistance in series must be equal (steady state).**

For each sub-region we apply Fourier's law or Newton's law of cooling. Writing per unit area:

$$
q'' = h_i (T_{h,i} - T_1) \quad \text{(inner convection)}
$$

$$
q'' = \frac{k_1}{x_1}(T_1 - T_2) \quad \text{(layer 1 conduction)}
$$

$$
q'' = \frac{k_2}{x_2}(T_2 - T_3) \quad \text{(layer 2 conduction)}
$$

$$
q'' = \frac{k_3}{x_3}(T_3 - T_4) \quad \text{(layer 3 conduction)}
$$

$$
q'' = h_o (T_4 - T_{c,o}) \quad \text{(outer convection)}
$$

**Step 2: Express each temperature drop as $q'' \times$ resistance.**

Rearrange each equation so the temperature difference equals $q''$ multiplied by the appropriate thermal resistance:

$$
T_{h,i} - T_1 = \frac{q''}{h_i}
$$

$$
T_1 - T_2 = \frac{q'' \cdot x_1}{k_1}
$$

$$
T_2 - T_3 = \frac{q'' \cdot x_2}{k_2}
$$

$$
T_3 - T_4 = \frac{q'' \cdot x_3}{k_3}
$$

$$
T_4 - T_{c,o} = \frac{q''}{h_o}
$$

**Step 3: Sum all temperature drops.**

By the additive property of a series of linear equations (the interface temperatures $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$ cancel):

$$
(T_{h,i} - T_1) + (T_1 - T_2) + (T_2 - T_3) + (T_3 - T_4) + (T_4 - T_{c,o}) = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}
$$

Substituting the right-hand side expressions:

$$
T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = q'' \left[ \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x_1}{k_1} + \frac{x_2}{k_2} + \frac{x_3}{k_3} + \frac{1}{h_o} \right]
$$

**Step 4: Identify the overall coefficient.**

We want the form $q'' = U \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_{c,o})$. Comparing, the bracket is exactly $1/U$:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x_1}{k_1} + \frac{x_2}{k_2} + \frac{x_3}{k_3} + \frac{1}{h_o}\;}
$$

**Step 5: Generalize to $n$ layers.** By induction, the same pattern holds for any number of layers $n$:

$$
\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{x_j}{k_j} + \frac{1}{h_o}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient for a Composite Cylinder

**Setup:** A pipe of inner radius $r_1$, outer radius $r_2$, conductivity $k_1$, carries hot fluid at $T_{h,i}$ with inside coefficient $h_i$. It is wrapped with insulation of outer radius $r_3$ and conductivity $k_2$. The outside ambient is at $T_{c,o}$ with coefficient $h_o$. Steady state, length $L$.

**Step 1: Heat rate through the inner fluid film.**

$$
q = h_i \cdot (2\pi r_1 L) \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_{r_1})
$$

Solving for the temperature drop:

$$
T_{h,i} - T_{r_1} = \frac{q}{h_i (2\pi r_1 L)} = \frac{q}{2\pi L}\cdot\frac{1}{h_i r_1}
$$

**Step 2: Heat rate through the pipe wall (cylindrical conduction).**

Fourier's law for a cylinder:

$$
q = \frac{2\pi k_1 L (T_{r_1} - T_{r_2})}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}
$$

Rearranged:

$$
T_{r_1} - T_{r_2} = \frac{q \ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_1 L}
$$

**Step 3: Heat rate through the insulation layer.**

$$
T_{r_2} - T_{r_3} = \frac{q \ln(r_3/r_2)}{2\pi k_2 L}
$$

**Step 4: Heat rate through the outer fluid film.**

$$
q = h_o \cdot (2\pi r_3 L) \cdot (T_{r_3} - T_{c,o})
$$

$$
T_{r_3} - T_{c,o} = \frac{q}{2\pi L}\cdot\frac{1}{h_o r_3}
$$

**Step 5: Sum the four temperature drops.**

Interface temperatures $T_{r_1}, T_{r_2}, T_{r_3}$ cancel:

$$
T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = \frac{q}{2\pi L}\left[ \frac{1}{h_i r_1} + \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k_1} + \frac{\ln(r_3/r_2)}{k_2} + \frac{1}{h_o r_3} \right]
$$

**Step 6: Define $U_o$ on outer-area basis.** Recognizing that $q = U_o (2\pi r_3 L) (T_{h,i} - T_{c,o})$, we get:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{1}{U_o\, r_3} = \frac{1}{h_i r_1} + \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{k_1} + \frac{\ln(r_3/r_2)}{k_2} + \frac{1}{h_o r_3}\;}
$$

or equivalently, defining $R_3 = r_3$ for compactness:

$$
\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{r_3}{r_1 h_i} + \frac{r_3 \ln(r_2/r_1)}{k_1} + \frac{r_3 \ln(r_3/r_2)}{k_2} + \frac{1}{h_o}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Critical Radius of Insulation

**Setup:** A bare cylinder of radius $r_1$, conductivity $k$ (this is the *pipe* itself, treated as the inner material) is being insulated. The insulation has outer radius $r_2$ and conductivity $k_{ins}$. We want to find the radius $r_2$ at which **adding more insulation begins to reduce heat loss** (i.e., where heat loss $q$ is maximum).

**Step 1: Total resistance per unit length (combining pipe wall + insulation + outer film).** Treat the inner pipe as a perfect conductor (assume $T_{r_1}$ is essentially the pipe outer temperature for simplicity — or include pipe resistance as constant). For thin insulation analysis, we lump and consider:

$$
R_{tot} = \frac{1}{2\pi r_1 L h_i} + \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_{ins} L} + \frac{1}{2\pi r_2 L h_o}
$$

The first term is constant (independent of $r_2$). The variable part is:

$$
R_{var}(r_2) = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_{ins} L} + \frac{1}{2\pi r_2 L h_o}
$$

**Step 2: Heat loss is $q = \Delta T / R_{tot}$.** Maximum $q$ corresponds to **minimum** $R_{var}$. Differentiate $R_{var}$ with respect to $r_2$ and set to zero:

$$
\frac{d R_{var}}{d r_2} = \frac{1}{2\pi k_{ins} L}\cdot\frac{1}{r_2} - \frac{1}{2\pi h_o L}\cdot\frac{1}{r_2^{2}} = 0
$$

**Step 3: Solve the resulting equation.**

$$
\frac{1}{2\pi k_{ins} L}\cdot\frac{1}{r_2} = \frac{1}{2\pi h_o L}\cdot\frac{1}{r_2^{2}}
$$

Multiplying both sides by $2\pi L r_2^{2}$:

$$
r_2 = \frac{k_{ins}}{h_o}
$$

Thus the **critical radius of insulation** is:

$$
\boxed{\;r_c = \frac{k_{ins}}{h_o}\;}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** If the bare pipe radius $r_1 < r_c$, adding insulation *first* increases heat loss (because the outer area grows faster than the conductive resistance rises). Heat loss reaches a maximum at $r_2 = r_c$, then decreases as more insulation is added. For $r_1 > r_c$, heat loss decreases monotonically with insulation — the expected behavior.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Pattern)

**Problem:** A furnace wall consists of three layers: a firebrick layer ($k_1 = 1.4$ W/m·K, thickness $x_1 = 0.10$ m), an insulating brick layer ($k_2 = 0.35$ W/m·K, thickness $x_2 = 0.075$ m), and a red-brick layer ($k_3 = 0.7$ W/m·K, thickness $x_3 = 0.10$ m). The inside convective coefficient is $h_i = 80$ W/m²·K (hot gases at $T_{h,i} = 1500$ °C) and outside is $h_o = 15$ W/m²·K (ambient air at $T_{c,o} = 30$ °C). Calculate the overall heat transfer coefficient based on unit area, the heat flux, and the temperature at the outer surface of the furnace.

**Step 1: Calculate the overall resistance per unit area.**

$$
\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x_1}{k_1} + \frac{x_2}{k_2} + \frac{x_3}{k_3} + \frac{1}{h_o}
$$

Compute each term:

$$
\frac{1}{h_i} = \frac{1}{80} = 0.01250 \; \text{m}^2\text{·K/W}
$$

$$
\frac{x_1}{k_1} = \frac{0.10}{1.4} = 0.07143 \; \text{m}^2\text{·K/W}
$$

$$
\frac{x_2}{k_2} = \frac{0.075}{0.35} = 0.21429 \; \text{m}^2\text{·K/W}
$$

$$
\frac{x_3}{k_3} = \frac{0.10}{0.7} = 0.14286 \; \text{m}^2\text{·K/W}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{h_o} = \frac{1}{15} = 0.06667 \; \text{m}^2\text{·K/W}
$$

Sum:

$$
\frac{1}{U} = 0.01250 + 0.07143 + 0.21429 + 0.14286 + 0.06667 = 0.50774 \; \text{m}^2\text{·K/W}
$$

$$
U = \frac{1}{0.50774} = 1.969 \; \text{W/m}^2\text{·K}
$$

**Step 2: Heat flux through the wall.**

$$
q'' = U (T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}) = 1.969 \times (1500 - 30) = 1.969 \times 1470 = 2894.7 \; \text{W/m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Temperature at the outer surface.** The outer surface is between layer 3 and the outer air film. The temperature drop across the outer film is:

$$
\Delta T_{outer} = \frac{q''}{h_o} = \frac{2894.7}{15} = 192.98 \; \text{K}
$$

So:

$$
T_{outer\;surface} = T_{c,o} + \Delta T_{outer} = 30 + 192.98 = 222.98 \; ^\circ\text{C} \approx 223 \; ^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 4: Sanity check — controlling resistance.** The largest single resistance is $x_2/k_2 = 0.21429$ m²·K/W (the insulating brick), contributing about $42\%$ of the total resistance. Removing it would nearly double the heat loss. This confirms the value of using low-$k$ insulating bricks in furnace design.

> [!TIP]
> **Answer:** $U \approx 1.97$ W/m²·K, $q'' \approx 2895$ W/m², $T_{outer\;surface} \approx 223$ °C.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Thermal Resistance Network — Plane Composite Wall

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["Hot fluid T_hi"]:::hot -->|R_conv_i = 1/hiA| node2["Inner surface T1"]
    node2 -->|R_cond_1 = x1/k1A| node3["Interface T2"]
    node3 -->|R_cond_2 = x2/k2A| node4["Interface T3"]
    node4 -->|R_cond_3 = x3/k3A| node5["Outer surface T4"]
    node5 -->|R_conv_o = 1/hoA| node6["Cold fluid T_co"]:::cold

    classDef hot fill:#ffe2e2,stroke:#b30000,color:#000
    classDef cold fill:#e2f0ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
```

The above is a **series** network. Heat flows from hot fluid → wall stack → cold fluid, encountering five resistors in a chain. The temperature drops *stepwise* at each interface.

## 4.2 Temperature Profile Through the Wall

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["T_hi = 1500 C"] --> B["T1 inner surface"]
    B --> C["T2 interface"]
    C --> D["T3 interface"]
    D --> E["T4 outer surface"]
    E --> F["T_co = 30 C"]

    B -.- Bx["slope = hi"]
    C -.- Cx["slope = k1/x1"]
    D -.- Dx["slope = k2/x2"]
    E -.- Ex["slope = k3/x3"]
    F -.- Fx["slope = ho"]
```

The **steeper** the temperature drop across a layer, the **larger** that layer's resistance. In our numerical example, the steepest drop occurs across the insulating brick ($x_2/k_2$ is the dominant term).

## 4.3 Thermal Resistance Network — Cylindrical Pipe with Insulation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1["Hot fluid T_hi"]:::hot -->|1 over hi 2 pi r1 L| p2["Pipe inner wall r1"]
    p2 -->|ln r2 over r1 over 2 pi k1 L| p3["Pipe outer wall r2"]
    p3 -->|ln r3 over r2 over 2 pi k2 L| p4["Insulation outer r3"]
    p4 -->|1 over ho 2 pi r3 L| p5["Cold ambient T_co"]:::cold

    classDef hot fill:#ffe2e2,stroke:#b30000,color:#000
    classDef cold fill:#e2f0ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
```

Notice the resistances here are **per unit length** of pipe, expressed in K/W (not m²·K/W). Each resistance is multiplied by $L$ to give total resistance.

## 4.4 Critical Radius Behavior — Heat Loss vs. Insulation Outer Radius

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Bare pipe radius r1"] --> B["Insulation thickness increases"]
    B --> C{"Compare r1 with r_critical"}
    C -->|"r1 greater than r_c"| D["Heat loss decreases monotonically"]
    C -->|"r1 less than r_c"| E["Heat loss first INCREASES then decreases"]
    E --> F["Maximum heat loss at r2 = r_c = k_ins over ho"]
    F --> G["Heat loss falls below bare value beyond r_c"]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Industrial Pitfall:** A ½-inch copper refrigerant line ($r_1 \approx 6.4$ mm) with $k_{ins} \approx 0.04$ W/m·K insulation and $h_o \approx 10$ W/m²·K in still air gives $r_c \approx 4$ mm — **smaller than the bare pipe**! Wrapping such a line with thin insulation can paradoxically *increase* frost formation on the cold line. Always check $r_c$ before insulating small-diameter cold pipes.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define the term **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient**. For a plane wall with two solid layers and fluids on both sides, write the expression for $\dfrac{1}{U}$ in terms of individual coefficients and conductivities.

**Model Answer:**
The **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient** $U$ is defined as the heat flux that would be transferred per unit temperature difference between the two bulk fluids if all thermal resistances (convective films + conductive layers) were lumped into a single coefficient:

$$
q = U A (T_{hot} - T_{cold})
$$

For a plane wall with two solid layers ($k_1, x_1$ and $k_2, x_2$) and inside/outside convective coefficients $h_i$ and $h_o$:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x_1}{k_1} + \frac{x_2}{k_2} + \frac{1}{h_o}\;}
$$

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Distinguish between **Individual** and **Overall** heat transfer coefficients. What is the significance of the **controlling resistance**?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Individual $h$ | Overall $U$ |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Heat transfer across a *single* fluid film by convection | Heat transfer across the *entire* resistance chain (films + walls) |
| Driving $\Delta T$ | Surface to bulk fluid of *one* fluid | Bulk *hot* fluid to bulk *cold* fluid |
| Magnitude | Typically 5–25,000 W/m²·K depending on flow regime | Smaller than smallest individual $h$ in the chain |
| Use | Local heat-transfer correlations (Nusselt number based) | Heat-exchanger sizing, design $q = U A \Delta T_{lm}$ |

**Controlling Resistance:** The single largest term in the sum $1/U$ dominates the heat flow. Reducing smaller resistances has negligible effect on $U$ — the design effort must target the dominant term. In a furnace wall, often the insulating layer or one of the convective films is controlling.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Comprehensive Composite Wall & Fouling Problem

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2, Question Choice A]**

A furnace wall is constructed from the following layers in series from inside to outside:

1. Firebrick: $k_1 = 1.5$ W/m·K, $x_1 = 100$ mm
2. Insulating brick: $k_2 = 0.25$ W/m·K, $x_2 = 80$ mm
3. Red brick: $k_3 = 0.70$ W/m·K, $x_3 = 100$ mm

The hot gases inside the furnace are at $T_{h,i} = 1400$ °C with a convective coefficient $h_i = 100$ W/m²·K. The outside ambient air is at $T_{c,o} = 25$ °C with $h_o = 12$ W/m²·K.

After a few months of operation, fouling deposits on both the hot and cold sides give fouling factors $R_{f,i} = 0.0008$ m²·K/W and $R_{f,o} = 0.0004$ m²·K/W.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
Calculate **(i)** the clean overall heat transfer coefficient, **(ii)** the heat flux through the wall, and **(iii)** the temperature at the outer surface of the wall. Identify the **controlling resistance**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
Calculate **(i)** the dirty overall heat transfer coefficient (including fouling) and **(ii)** the percentage reduction in heat loss due to fouling.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1: Compute clean overall resistance (per unit area).**

$$
\frac{1}{U_{clean}} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x_1}{k_1} + \frac{x_2}{k_2} + \frac{x_3}{k_3} + \frac{1}{h_o}
$$

Numerically:

$$
\frac{1}{h_i} = \frac{1}{100} = 0.01000 \; \text{m}^2\text{·K/W}
$$

$$
\frac{x_1}{k_1} = \frac{0.100}{1.5} = 0.06667 \; \text{m}^2\text{·K/W}
$$

$$
\frac{x_2}{k_2} = \frac{0.080}{0.25} = 0.32000 \; \text{m}^2\text{·K/W}
$$

$$
\frac{x_3}{k_3} = \frac{0.100}{0.70} = 0.14286 \; \text{m}^2\text{·K/W}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{h_o} = \frac{1}{12} = 0.08333 \; \text{m}^2\text{·K/W}
$$

Summing:

$$
\frac{1}{U_{clean}} = 0.01000 + 0.06667 + 0.32000 + 0.14286 + 0.08333 = 0.62286 \; \text{m}^2\text{·K/W}
$$

$$
U_{clean} = \frac{1}{0.62286} = 1.605 \; \text{W/m}^2\text{·K}
$$

**Step 2: Heat flux.**

$$
q''_{clean} = U_{clean} \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}) = 1.605 \times (1400 - 25) = 1.605 \times 1375 = 2207.6 \; \text{W/m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Outer surface temperature.** The temperature drop across the outer film:

$$
\Delta T_{outer} = \frac{q''}{h_o} = \frac{2207.6}{12} = 183.97 \; \text{K}
$$

$$
T_{outer\;surface} = T_{c,o} + \Delta T_{outer} = 25 + 183.97 \approx 209 \; ^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 4: Identify controlling resistance.** Compute fractional contributions:

- $1/h_i$: $0.01000 / 0.62286 = 1.6\%$
- $x_1/k_1$: $0.06667 / 0.62286 = 10.7\%$
- $x_2/k_2$: $0.32000 / 0.62286 = 51.4\%$ ← **controlling**
- $x_3/k_3$: $0.14286 / 0.62286 = 22.9\%$
- $1/h_o$: $0.08333 / 0.62286 = 13.4\%$

The **insulating brick** ($x_2/k_2$) controls at about $51\%$ of total resistance.

#### Part (b) Solution

**Step 1: Add fouling resistance.**

$$
\frac{1}{U_{dirty}} = \frac{1}{U_{clean}} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o} = 0.62286 + 0.0008 + 0.0004 = 0.62406 \; \text{m}^2\text{·K/W}
$$

$$
U_{dirty} = \frac{1}{0.62406} = 1.602 \; \text{W/m}^2\text{·K}
$$

**Step 2: Percentage reduction in heat loss.**

$$
\% \text{ reduction} = \frac{U_{clean} - U_{dirty}}{U_{clean}} \times 100 = \frac{1.605 - 1.602}{1.605} \times 100 = 0.187\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part (a):** Many students write the heat-flux equation as $q'' = U(T_{h,i} - T_{c,o})$ but then mistakenly use $T_{outer}$ where $T_{c,o}$ should appear, or compute $T_{outer}$ from the *wrong* film. Always remember: the outer surface temperature is found by adding the temperature drop *across the outer film only* (not all films) to the ambient temperature.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):** A common error is to *multiply* fouling factors instead of *adding* them to $1/U$. Fouling factors are *resistances* and must add to the resistance sum. Watch units — fouling factors must be in m²·K/W, not W/m²·K.

---

### Question B — Cylindrical Pipe with Insulation and Critical Radius

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, Question Choice B]**

A steam pipe of outer diameter 80 mm and wall thermal conductivity $k_{pipe} = 45$ W/m·K carries saturated steam at $T_{h,i} = 200$ °C. The pipe is lagged (insulated) with a material of conductivity $k_{ins} = 0.06$ W/m·K. The inner steam-side convective coefficient is $h_i = 5000$ W/m²·K, and the outside air is at $T_{c,o} = 25$ °C with $h_o = 10$ W/m²·K.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
If the insulation outer radius is $r_3 = 70$ mm, calculate the **overall heat transfer coefficient based on the outer surface** ($U_o$) and the **heat loss per metre of pipe length**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
Determine the **critical radius of insulation** and explain with a sketch whether wrapping this pipe (outer radius $= 40$ mm) will reduce heat loss monotonically.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) Solution

**Given:** $r_1 = 40$ mm = 0.040 m, $r_2 = r_1$ (pipe outer = insulation inner), $r_3 = 70$ mm = 0.070 m, $L = 1$ m.

**Step 1: Compute each resistance per unit length.**

$$
R_{i} = \frac{1}{h_i \cdot 2\pi r_1 L} = \frac{1}{5000 \times 2\pi \times 0.040 \times 1} = \frac{1}{1256.6} = 7.958 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{K/W}
$$

Negligible — steam condensation gives huge $h_i$.

$$
R_{pipe} = \frac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k_{pipe} L}
$$

Since the pipe is treated as having negligible radial thickness for this problem (we treat $r_2 = r_1$ — the pipe wall is thin metal, so $\ln(1) = 0$): $R_{pipe} \approx 0$.

$$
R_{ins} = \frac{\ln(r_3/r_1)}{2\pi k_{ins} L} = \frac{\ln(70/40)}{2\pi \times 0.06 \times 1} = \frac{\ln(1.75)}{0.3770} = \frac{0.5596}{0.3770} = 1.484 \; \text{K/W}
$$

$$
R_o = \frac{1}{h_o \cdot 2\pi r_3 L} = \frac{1}{10 \times 2\pi \times 0.070 \times 1} = \frac{1}{4.398} = 0.2274 \; \text{K/W}
$$

**Step 2: Total resistance.**

$$
R_{tot} = 7.958 \times 10^{-4} + 1.484 + 0.2274 = 1.7122 \; \text{K/W}
$$

**Step 3: Heat loss per metre.**

$$
q' = \frac{T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}}{R_{tot}} = \frac{200 - 25}{1.7122} = \frac{175}{1.7122} = 102.2 \; \text{W/m}
$$

**Step 4: Overall coefficient $U_o$ based on outer area $A_o = 2\pi r_3 L$.**

$$
U_o = \frac{1}{R_{tot} \cdot A_o} = \frac{1}{1.7122 \times 2\pi \times 0.070 \times 1} = \frac{1}{0.7529} = 1.328 \; \text{W/m}^2\text{·K}
$$

Check using $U_o (2\pi r_3)(T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}) = 1.328 \times 0.4398 \times 175 = 102.2$ W/m ✓

#### Part (b) Solution

**Critical radius:**

$$
r_c = \frac{k_{ins}}{h_o} = \frac{0.06}{10} = 0.006 \; \text{m} = 6 \; \text{mm}
$$

**Comparison with bare pipe radius:** $r_1 = 40$ mm $\gg r_c = 6$ mm.

Since $r_1 > r_c$, the bare pipe radius is **already beyond the critical value**. Therefore, adding insulation to this pipe will reduce heat loss **monotonically** from the very first millimetre of insulation thickness. There is no initial increase in heat loss, so wrapping is unconditionally beneficial.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part (a):** A very common error is to omit the inner radius area factor when computing $R_i$. The convective resistance on the inner side is $1/(h_i \cdot A_i)$ where $A_i = 2\pi r_1 L$ — **not** $1/h_i$. The radius must be included. Likewise for the outer side.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part (b):** Students often answer "Yes, heat loss will increase first" without comparing $r_1$ with $r_c$. The correct comparison is *always* between the *bare* (un-insulated) radius and the critical radius. If $r_1 > r_c$, insulation always helps; only if $r_1 < r_c$ does the counterintuitive increase occur.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Individual coefficient $h$** applies to *one* fluid film; **Overall $U$** lumps *all* resistances in the heat flow path.
- For a **plane wall**, the overall resistance per unit area is the **direct sum** of each layer's resistance: $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \sum \dfrac{x_j}{k_j} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$.
- For a **cylindrical wall**, areas differ at each interface, so resistances are in series on a *length basis* (K/W), and the overall $U$ depends on which area ($A_i$ or $A_o$) you choose as the reference.
- **Fouling** adds *resistances* $R_f$, so they *add* to $1/U$, never multiply.
- **Critical radius of insulation** $r_c = k_{ins}/h_o$. Below $r_c$, adding insulation *increases* heat loss (often tested in Part A as a conceptual question).
- The **controlling resistance** is the largest term in the sum — design improvements should target it, not smaller terms.
- For plane walls, $U$ is the same regardless of which face's area you use. For cylinders, $U_i A_i = U_o A_o$, and a common conversion formula is $U_i r_i = U_o r_o$ per unit length.
- Always include **units** when reporting $U$ — examiners frequently deduct a mark if you write only a numerical value without W/m²·K.
- **Sanity-check** at the end: compute each term's percentage of $1/U$ to identify the controlling resistance — this is a free mark-saver in long problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LMTD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT501 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** LMTD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LMTD (Log Mean Temperature Difference)** is defined as the *logarithmic mean* of the temperature differences between the hot and cold streams at the **two terminal ends** (inlet and outlet) of a heat exchanger. It represents the **effective average driving force** for heat transfer when the temperature difference between the two fluids varies along the length of the exchanger.

Mathematically, for a heat exchanger operating under steady-state conditions:

$$\text{LMTD} = \Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}$$

where $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ are the terminal temperature differences evaluated at the two ends of the exchanger, depending on the flow arrangement (parallel or counter-current).

The **fundamental heat transfer equation** that couples LMTD with the area of a heat exchanger is:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$$

where:
- $Q$ = Total heat transferred (**W** or **kW**)
- $U$ = Overall heat transfer coefficient (**W/m²·K**)
- $A$ = Heat transfer area (**m²**)
- $\Delta T_{lm}$ = Log mean temperature difference (**K** or **°C**)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Way Street of Heat"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition:** Imagine two conveyor belts running next to each other — one carrying hot roasted coffee beans, the other carrying cold milk. The hot beans release heat to the cold milk as they pass each other.
> 
> - At the **start** of the journey, the beans are scorching hot (say 90°C) and milk is ice-cold (5°C) → **Big temperature gap** (ΔT₁ = 85°C).
> - At the **end** of the journey, beans have cooled to 60°C and milk has warmed to 40°C → **Small temperature gap** (ΔT₂ = 20°C).
> 
> The heat transfer rate is **not simply** (85 + 20)/2 = 52.5°C because the rate of heat transfer depends on the *instantaneous* gap, which changes exponentially along the path. A simple **arithmetic mean would overestimate** the driving force.
> 
> The **logarithmic mean** (≈ 44.2°C in this example) is the *true* average driving force that correctly accounts for this exponential variation.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> **Key Engineering Metric — The LMTD Correction Factor (F-factor):**
> In multi-pass shell-and-tube exchangers (e.g., 1-2, 2-4 passes), pure counter-flow is not achieved everywhere. The actual mean temperature difference is:
> $$\Delta T_{m} = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,cf}$$
> 
> where $F$ (typically between **0.75 and 1.0**) corrects for flow geometry deviations. **F < 0.75** often indicates uneconomical design.

---

## 1.4 Why LMTD Matters in Chemical Engineering

LMTD is the **backbone of heat exchanger rating and sizing** calculations in:
- **Process industries**: oil refineries, petrochemical plants
- **Power plants**: condensers, feedwater heaters
- **Pharmaceutical & food processing**: pasteurizers, sterilizers
- **HVAC systems**: chillers, cooling coils

It directly enables engineers to determine the **required heat transfer area** for a given duty, or conversely, to predict the **outlet temperatures** for an existing exchanger.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Assumptions for LMTD Validity

The LMTD equation is rigorously valid only when **ALL** the following assumptions hold:

1. **Steady-state operation** — no temperature variation with time.
2. **Constant overall heat transfer coefficient (U)** along the entire length of the exchanger.
3. **Constant mass flow rates** of both fluids (no phase change, or accounted for separately).
4. **No heat loss to the surroundings** — all heat from hot fluid transfers to cold fluid.
5. **No axial conduction** along the exchanger walls or fluids.
6. **Pure parallel or pure counter-current flow** — for multi-pass arrangements, an F-correction factor must be applied.

> [!WARNING]
> If any fluid undergoes **phase change** (e.g., condensation at constant temperature), the temperature profile becomes a **straight horizontal line**, and LMTD reduces to a simple arithmetic mean of the two terminal temperature differences. The logarithmic mean degenerates correctly when one stream is isothermal.

---

## 2.2 Identification of Terminal ΔT for Different Flow Arrangements

| Flow Arrangement | $\Delta T_1$ (Hot-End Inlet) | $\Delta T_2$ (Cold-End Outlet) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Parallel (Co-current) Flow** | $T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$ | $T_{h,out} - T_{c,out}$ |
| **Counter-current Flow** | $T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ | $T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In **counter-current flow**, both $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ use temperatures from **opposite ends** of the exchanger, which is why counter-current operation achieves a **higher** LMTD and is thermodynamically more efficient.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU heat transfer problem.**

| # | Equation | Description | Standard Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$ | Core heat exchanger design equation | W, W/m²·K, m², K |
| 2 | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log Mean Temperature Difference | K or °C |
| 3 | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_2 - \Delta T_1}{\ln(\Delta T_2 / \Delta T_1)}$ | Alternative form (when $\Delta T_2 > \Delta T_1$) | K or °C |
| 4 | $Q = \dot{m}_h \cdot C_{p,h} \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})$ | Heat duty from hot fluid stream | W |
| 5 | $Q = \dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c} \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$ | Heat duty from cold fluid stream | W |
| 6 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot \lambda$ | Latent heat during phase change | W |
| 7 | $\Delta T_{m} = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,cf}$ | Corrected MTD for multi-pass exchangers | K |
| 8 | $P = \dfrac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}}$ | Cold-fluid thermal effectiveness ratio | dimensionless |
| 9 | $R = \dfrac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}$ | Capacity rate ratio | dimensionless |
| 10 | $A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}}$ | Area calculation (rearranged design equation) | m² |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** When the two $\Delta T$ values are close (ratio < 2), the LMTD can be approximated by the **arithmetic mean** with negligible error. Always verify $\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2 < 2$ before applying the shortcut.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Heat Exchanger Sizing (Design Mode):** Given $Q$, $U$, and the four terminal temperatures, calculate required area $A$.
- **Heat Exchanger Rating (Performance Mode):** Given existing area $A$ and $U$, calculate outlet temperatures or heat duty.
- **Energy Audit & Pinch Analysis:** LMTD analysis identifies the minimum approach temperature ($\Delta T_{min}$), which is critical for optimal network design in process integration.
- **Process Control:** Real-time LMTD monitoring helps detect fouling (a decrease in actual LMTD vs. design value indicates loss of performance).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of LMTD for a Parallel-Flow Heat Exchanger

### Setup

Consider a **parallel-flow (co-current)** heat exchanger where both hot and cold fluids flow in the **same direction**. Take a **differential element** of area $dA$ at a distance $x$ from the inlet.

Let:
- $T_h$ = Temperature of hot fluid at location $x$
- $T_c$ = Temperature of cold fluid at location $x$
- $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$ at location $x$
- $U$ = Overall heat transfer coefficient (constant)

### Step 1 — Heat Transfer Across Differential Element

The rate of heat transferred through the differential element $dA$ is:

$$dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot (T_h - T_c) = U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T$$

### Step 2 — Energy Balance on Hot Fluid (Cooling)

The hot fluid loses heat as it flows. Let $\dot{m}_h$ and $C_{p,h}$ be its mass flow rate and specific heat:

$$dQ = -\dot{m}_h \cdot C_{p,h} \cdot dT_h$$

The negative sign indicates that as area increases ($dA > 0$), the hot fluid temperature **decreases** ($dT_h < 0$).

### Step 3 — Energy Balance on Cold Fluid (Heating)

The cold fluid gains heat as it flows:

$$dQ = +\dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c} \cdot dT_c$$

### Step 4 — Subtracting the Two Energy Balances

$$dQ \cdot \left(\frac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}} - \frac{1}{(-\dot{m}_h C_{p,h})}\right) = dT_c + dT_h$$

Rewriting carefully:

$$-\frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} = dT_h \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}} = dT_c$$

Adding these two equations:

$$\frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}} - \frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}} = dT_c - dT_h = -d(T_h - T_c) = -d(\Delta T)$$

Therefore:

$$dQ = -\frac{d(\Delta T)}{\dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}} + \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}}}$$

Let the constant denominator be denoted:

$$\frac{1}{K'} = \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{p,c}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{p,h}}$$

So:

$$dQ = -K' \cdot d(\Delta T)$$

### Step 5 — Equating with Step 1

$$U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T = -K' \cdot d(\Delta T)$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -\frac{U}{K'} \cdot dA$$

### Step 6 — Integration Across the Entire Exchanger

Integrate from inlet (where $A = 0$, $\Delta T = \Delta T_1$) to outlet (where $A = A$, $\Delta T = \Delta T_2$):

$$\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -\frac{U}{K'} \int_0^A dA$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -\frac{U \cdot A}{K'}$$

### Step 7 — Solving for Total Heat Transfer $Q$

From Step 4, integrating across the entire exchanger:

$$Q = -K' \cdot (\Delta T_2 - \Delta T_1) = K' \cdot (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)$$

Substituting the value of $K'$ from Step 6:

$$K' = -\frac{U \cdot A}{\ln(\Delta T_2 / \Delta T_1)} = \frac{U \cdot A}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned} Q &= \frac{U \cdot A}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} \cdot (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) \end{aligned}$$

### Step 8 — Final Form

$$\boxed{Q = U \cdot A \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Note on Derivation:** The same derivation holds for **counter-current flow** as well. The only difference is in **how $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ are defined at the terminals** (refer to Section 2.2 table). The logarithmic mean formula itself remains **identical**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A counter-current heat exchanger cools oil from 150°C to 80°C using water that enters at 25°C and leaves at 60°C. Calculate the LMTD.

**Step 1 — Identify Terminal Temperature Differences**

For **counter-current flow**:
- $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 150 - 60 = 90°C$
- $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 80 - 25 = 55°C$

**Step 2 — Verify Ratio**

$$\frac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2} = \frac{90}{55} = 1.636$$

Since the ratio is less than 2, the arithmetic mean approximation would be reasonably accurate, but we'll use the exact logarithmic mean.

**Step 3 — Apply LMTD Formula**

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{90 - 55}{\ln(90/55)} = \frac{35}{\ln(1.636)}$$

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{35}{0.4925} = 71.07°C$$

**Step 4 — Cross-Check with Arithmetic Mean**

$$\Delta T_{am} = \frac{90 + 55}{2} = 72.5°C$$

The error is only $\approx 2\%$ — confirming the validity of the arithmetic shortcut for close ratios.

**Final Answer:** $\Delta T_{lm} = 71.07°C$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison: Parallel vs Counter-Current

| Parameter | Parallel Flow | Counter-Current Flow |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| LMTD (for same inlet/outlet temps) | **Lower** | **Higher** |
| Required area for same duty | **Larger** | **Smaller** |
| Cold fluid outlet temperature | **Limited** by hot outlet | Can exceed hot outlet |
| Temperature cross-over possible? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Preferred in industry? | Rarely | **Yes** (preferred) |
| Sensitivity to flow rate changes | Higher | Lower |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Temperature Cross-Over:** In a **counter-current exchanger**, it is theoretically possible for $T_{c,out} > T_{h,out}$. This is **impossible** in parallel flow because both fluids approach the same equilibrium temperature. Counter-current design allows the cold outlet to be hotter than the hot outlet — a major efficiency advantage.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — LMTD Calculation Methodology

The following flowchart captures the systematic procedure for solving any heat exchanger problem using the LMTD method:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[START: Given Q or A?] --> condA{Determine Mode}
    condA --> mode1[Rating Mode: Given A, find Q or outlet temps]
    condA --> mode2[Design Mode: Given Q, find A]

    mode1 --> stepB1[Step 1: Identify flow arrangement]
    mode2 --> stepB1

    stepB1 --> stepB2[Step 2: List all 4 terminal temperatures]
    stepB2 --> stepB3[Step 3: Compute delta T1 and delta T2]

    stepB3 --> stepC1{Delta T1 greater than delta T2?}
    stepC1 --> yes1[Assign delta T1 to larger value]
    stepC1 --> no1[Assign delta T1 to smaller value]

    yes1 --> stepD1[Step 4: Apply LMTD formula]
    no1 --> stepD1

    stepD1 --> stepD2[Step 5: Compute Q = U times A times LMTD]
    stepD2 --> stepE1{Need area?}
    stepE1 --> yes2[Rearrange: A = Q divided by U times LMTD]
    stepE1 --> no2[Final Answer Q or outlet temperature]

    yes2 --> finalA[END: Report area in m squared]
    no2 --> finalB[END: Report heat duty in W]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Heat Exchanger Functional Architecture (Flow Paths)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Parallel["PARALLEL FLOW ARRANGEMENT"]
        HI1[Hot Fluid IN: T_h_in] --> HE1[Heat Exchanger Core]
        CI1[Cold Fluid IN: T_c_in] --> HE1
        HE1 --> HO1[Hot Fluid OUT: T_h_out]
        HE1 --> CO1[Cold Fluid OUT: T_c_out]
    end

    subgraph Counter["COUNTER-CURRENT FLOW ARRANGEMENT"]
        HI2[Hot Fluid IN: T_h_in] --> HE2[Heat Exchanger Core]
        CO2[Cold Fluid OUT: T_c_out] --> HE2
        HE2 --> HO2[Hot Fluid OUT: T_h_out]
        HE2 --> CI2[Cold Fluid IN: T_c_in]
    end

    style Parallel fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000
    style Counter fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366
```

## 4.3 ASCII Schematic — Temperature Profiles (Visual Aid)

### Parallel Flow Profile

```
Temperature
   ^
T_h,in |*\
       |  \
       |    \
       |      *--- T_h,out  (Both approach same T)
       |       \      
T_c,in |--------\*     
       |          \    
       |           \*  T_c,out
       |____________\____________> Length L
              (ΔT_1)   (ΔT_2)
```

### Counter-Current Flow Profile

```
Temperature
   ^
T_h,in |*\
       |  \
       |    \
       |      \  T_c,out  (Can exceed T_h,out!)
       |       \*
T_c,in |        |\
       |        | \
       |        |  * T_h,out
       |________|___\_______> Length L
                ΔT_2   ΔT_1
```

> [!NOTE]
> In **parallel flow**, both curves converge towards a common asymptote (the equilibrium temperature). In **counter-current flow**, the temperature profiles remain separated throughout, yielding a **larger area under the curve** = **larger LMTD** = **more heat transferred** for the same area.

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Multi-Pass Exchanger with F-Correction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input1[Hot fluid enters Shell side] --> pass1[Pass 1: Left to Right]
    pass1 --> baffle[Baffle reverses flow]
    baffle --> pass2[Pass 2: Right to Left]
    pass2 --> output1[Hot fluid exits Shell]

    input2[Cold fluid enters Tube inlet] --> tube1[Tubes 1 to N: Counter to Shell Pass 1]
    tube1 --> tube2[Tubes continue through Pass 2]
    tube2 --> output2[Cold fluid exits Tube outlet]

    pass1 --> calcL[Calculate LMTD assuming pure counter flow]
    calcL --> calcF[Lookup F from P and R chart]
    calcF --> calcM[True MTD = F times LMTD]
    calcM --> final[Final Area = Q divided by U times MTD]

    style calcF fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
*Define Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD). Why is the logarithmic mean used instead of the arithmetic mean of temperature differences?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
