\frac{I_{t\lambda}}{I_\lambda} = \tau_\lambda
$$

**Step 4 — Final identity (spectral form).**

$$
\boxed{\alpha_\lambda + \rho_\lambda + \tau_\lambda = 1}
$$

**Step 5 — Integration over all wavelengths** gives the total-band form:

$$
\int_0^\infty \alpha_\lambda \, d\lambda + \int_0^\infty \rho_\lambda \, d\lambda + \int_0^\infty \tau_\lambda \, d\lambda = \int_0^\infty d\lambda
$$

Dividing by the total bandwidth (which yields the total property definitions):

$$
\boxed{\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The relation $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$ is *always* required, even if the problem only gives you two of the three values. Marks are awarded for stating the relation explicitly.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Gray-Body Net Radiation Heat Transfer

**Given:** A small convex gray object of surface area $A$, temperature $T_s$, emissivity $\varepsilon$, surrounded by a very large enclosure at $T_{\text{sur}}$.

**Step 1 — Energy emitted by the object per unit time.**

$$
Q_e = A \, \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T_s^4
$$

**Step 2 — Energy incident on the object from the surroundings.**
Because the surroundings are large, irradiation $G$ on the object equals the blackbody emission at $T_{\text{sur}}$:

$$
G = \sigma \, T_{\text{sur}}^4
$$

**Step 3 — Energy absorbed by the object (Kirchhoff: $\alpha = \varepsilon$).**

$$
Q_a = A \, \alpha \, G = A \, \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T_{\text{sur}}^4
$$

**Step 4 — Net heat transfer (emission − absorption).**

$$
Q_{\text{net}} = Q_e - Q_a = A \, \varepsilon \, \sigma \left( T_s^4 - T_{\text{sur}}^4 \right)
$$

**Final compact form:**

$$
\boxed{Q = A \, \varepsilon \, \sigma \left( T_s^4 - T_{\text{sur}}^4 \right)}
$$

This is the **single most important equation** for KTU radiation problems in the 3-mark and 14-mark modules.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Kirchhoff's Law (Equilibrium Argument)

**Setup:** Consider a small surface element $dA_1$ inside a large isothermal enclosure at temperature $T$. The surface has emissivity $\varepsilon_\lambda$ and absorptivity $\alpha_\lambda$ at wavelength $\lambda$.

**Step 1 — Energy emitted by element per unit area, per unit wavelength.**

$$
E_{\text{emitted},\lambda} = \varepsilon_\lambda \, E_{b\lambda}(T)
$$

**Step 2 — Energy absorbed from cavity radiation.**
The radiation field inside an isothermal enclosure is identical to that of a blackbody, $E_{b\lambda}(T)$:

$$
E_{\text{absorbed},\lambda} = \alpha_\lambda \, E_{b\lambda}(T)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply thermal equilibrium.**
At equilibrium, the net exchange must be zero; otherwise the element would change temperature, contradicting the assumption:

$$
E_{\text{emitted},\lambda} = E_{\text{absorbed},\lambda}
$$

**Step 4 — Equate the two expressions.**

$$
\varepsilon_\lambda \, E_{b\lambda}(T) = \alpha_\lambda \, E_{b\lambda}(T)
$$

**Step 5 — Cancel the common term** $E_{b\lambda}(T)$ (which is nonzero):

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon_\lambda(T) = \alpha_\lambda(T)}
$$

For a gray, opaque surface, integrating over all $\lambda$ and setting $\tau = 0$:

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon = \alpha}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick-Reference Decision Tree for KTU

| Situation Given in Problem | Property to Use | Equation to Apply |
|---|---|---|
| Surface area, $T_s$, $T_{\text{sur}}$, $\varepsilon$ | Net radiation heat transfer | $Q = A \varepsilon \sigma (T_s^4 - T_{\text{sur}}^4)$ |
| $\alpha$, $\rho$ known, body opaque | Find $\tau$ | $\tau = 0$ (opaque), $\alpha + \rho = 1$ |
| $\alpha$, $\rho$, $\tau$ known | Verify energy balance | Sum = 1 (consistency check) |
| Selective (real) surface, given $\alpha_\lambda$ | Find $\varepsilon$ | Use $\varepsilon = \int \varepsilon_\lambda d\lambda$ |
| Wavelength of peak emission | Wien's law | $\lambda_{\max} T = 2897.8 \;\mu\text{m·K}$ |
| Heat flux emitted at given $T$ | Stefan–Boltzmann | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Radiation Energy Fate

The diagram below maps how incident radiation is partitioned at a generic semi-transparent surface — a high-yield concept for KTU short-answer and 14-mark questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incident Radiation Flux I] --> B[Semi-Transparent Surface]
    B --> C[Absorbed Component I_a]
    B --> D[Reflected Component I_r]
    B --> E[Transmitted Component I_t]
    C --> F[alpha equals I_a divided by I]
    D --> G[rho equals I_r divided by I]
    E --> H[tau equals I_t divided by I]
    F --> I[Conservation of Energy alpha plus rho plus tau equals 1]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{Kirchhoff Law}
    J --> K[epsilon lambda equals alpha lambda]
    J --> L[For gray opaque: epsilon equals alpha]
    K --> M[Real Body Case]
    L --> N[Gray Body Engineering Simplification]
    M --> O[Planck Distribution E_b lambda T]
    N --> P[Stefan-Boltzmann E equals epsilon sigma T to power 4]
    O --> P
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Trace any path from the top — *Incident Flux* → *Partition* → *Property definition* → *Conservation* → *Kirchhoff identity* → *Resulting emission law*. This is the logical skeleton KTU expects in 14-mark answers.

### 4.2 Concept Map — Blackbody / Gray Body / Real Body Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Perfect Blackbody epsilon equals 1] --> B[Reference Ideal]
    B --> C[Gray Body epsilon equals constant with lambda]
    B --> D[Real Selective Body epsilon varies with lambda]
    C --> E[Use Stefan-Boltzmann directly]
    D --> F[Use Planck Law and integrate]
    C --> G[Kirchhoff: epsilon equals alpha]
    D --> H[Kirchhoff: epsilon lambda equals alpha lambda]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick Win:** Whenever a problem says *"polished metal"* → $\varepsilon$ is **small** (0.02–0.1). Whenever it says *"lampblack"* or *"rough oxidized surface"* → $\varepsilon$ is **large** (0.85–0.95). Always recall Kirchhoff to compute $\alpha$ from $\varepsilon$.

### 4.3 Decision Flow for Selecting the Correct Property

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1{Is the body opaque?} -->|Yes| Q2{Tau equals 0, alpha plus rho equals 1}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Is the body gray?}
    Q2 --> Q4{Is the body a perfect absorber?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q5{epsilon constant: use Stefan-Boltzmann}
    Q3 -->|No| Q6{Real selective body: use Planck's law}
    Q4 -->|Yes| Q7{Blackbody: epsilon equals 1}
    Q4 -->|No| Q8{Real opaque body: epsilon less than 1}
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short-Answer / Conceptual)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
Define the following terms with one physical example each:
(a) Absorptivity
(b) Emissivity
(c) Transmissivity

> **Model Answer:**
>
> (a) **Absorptivity ($\alpha$):** The fraction of incident radiation energy that is absorbed by a body, $\alpha = I_a / I$. *Example:* A black-painted copper plate absorbs nearly all incident sunlight in the visible range.
>
> (b) **Emissivity ($\varepsilon$):** The ratio of the emissive power of a real surface to that of a blackbody at the same temperature, $\varepsilon = E / E_b$, $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$. *Example:* Polished aluminum has $\varepsilon \approx 0.05$, while lampblack has $\varepsilon \approx 0.95$.
>
> (c) **Transmissivity ($\tau$):** The fraction of incident radiation that passes through a body, $\tau = I_t / I$. *Example:* Ordinary window glass transmits visible light but blocks most infrared.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]**
State and explain **Kirchhoff's law** of thermal radiation. What is its significance in heat-transfer calculations?

> **Model Answer:**
>
> Kirchhoff's law states that **at thermal equilibrium, the monochromatic emissivity of a body equals its monochromatic absorptivity at the same wavelength and temperature**:
>
> $$\varepsilon_\lambda(T) = \alpha_\lambda(T)$$
>
> For a gray, opaque surface, this simplifies to $\varepsilon = \alpha$.
>
> **Significance:**
> - It allows engineers to *measure* absorptivity easily (using a spectroscope) and infer emissivity.
> - It is the foundation of the *common engineering assumption* that good absorbers are good emitters, enabling use of the single property $\varepsilon$ in the Stefan–Boltzmann law.
> - It explains why selective solar coatings (high $\alpha$ in solar band, low $\varepsilon$ in IR) are possible — Kirchhoff applies *per wavelength*, not as a single number.

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. The student attempts **one** of the two alternatives.

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 3]`

**(a)** A small metallic sphere of diameter 50 mm and emissivity 0.4 is placed inside a large furnace whose walls are at 1500 K. The sphere is at 800 K.
Calculate:
(i) The net rate of radiative heat transfer to the sphere.
(ii) The spectral peak wavelength of emission from the sphere, and identify the band of the EM spectrum in which it lies.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define a **blackbody** and a **gray body**. A thick concrete wall ($\alpha = 0.6$, $\tau = 0$) is exposed to solar flux of $800 \;\text{W/m}^2$.
(i) Calculate the absorbed solar flux.
(ii) State the corresponding emissivity (with justification) and the radiated power per $\text{m}^2$ at a surface temperature of 320 K.
**(7 marks)**

---

##### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

**Solution to A(a):**

**Given:** $d = 0.05 \text{ m}$, $\varepsilon = 0.4$, $T_s = 800 \text{ K}$, $T_{\text{sur}} = 1500 \text{ K}$.

**Step 1 — Surface area of the sphere.**

$$
A = \pi d^2 = \pi (0.05)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Net radiative heat transfer (single small body in large enclosure).**

$$
Q = A \, \varepsilon \, \sigma \left( T_{\text{sur}}^4 - T_s^4 \right)
$$

Evaluate the fourth powers:

$$
T_{\text{sur}}^4 = (1500)^4 = 5.0625 \times 10^{12} \;\text{K}^4
$$

$$
T_s^4 = (800)^4 = 4.096 \times 10^{11} \;\text{K}^4
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$
Q = (7.854 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.4 \times (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \times (5.0625 \times 10^{12} - 4.096 \times 10^{11})
$$

$$
Q = (7.854 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.4 \times (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \times (4.6529 \times 10^{12})
$$

$$
\boxed{Q \approx 82.7 \;\text{W} \quad \text{(absorbed by sphere from furnace)}}
$$

**Step 4 — Wien's Displacement Law for spectral peak.**

$$
\lambda_{\max} = \frac{2897.8 \;\mu\text{m·K}}{T_s} = \frac{2897.8}{800} = 3.62 \;\mu\text{m}
$$

This lies in the **infrared band** of the EM spectrum.

---

**Solution to A(b):**

(i) **Absorbed solar flux.** For opaque wall, $\tau = 0$:

$$
q_{\text{abs}} = \alpha \cdot G = 0.6 \times 800 = \boxed{480 \;\text{W/m}^2}
$$

(ii) By **Kirchhoff's law** (gray, opaque): $\varepsilon = \alpha = 0.6$.

$$
E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4 = 0.6 \times (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \times (320)^4
$$

$$
E = 0.6 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.0737 \times 10^{10}
$$

$$
\boxed{E \approx 365.4 \;\text{W/m}^2}
$$

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 4]`

**(a)** A polished aluminum sheet ($\varepsilon = 0.05$, $\tau = 0$) is exposed to incident radiation of $1000 \;\text{W/m}^2$. Determine:
(i) Absorptivity, reflectivity, and the absorbed, reflected fluxes.
(ii) The radiative heat flux emitted by the surface at 350 K. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the **spectral distribution of blackbody emissive power** as a function of wavelength. State **Wien's Displacement Law** and **Stefan–Boltzmann's Law** as derived from Planck's distribution. **(7 marks)**

---

##### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

**Solution to B(a):**

**Given:** $G = 1000 \;\text{W/m}^2$, $\varepsilon = 0.05$, $\tau = 0$, $T = 350 \text{ K}$.

(i) For an **opaque** body, $\tau = 0$, so $\alpha + \rho = 1$.

By **Kirchhoff's law** (gray, opaque): $\alpha = \varepsilon = 0.05$.

$$
\rho = 1 - \alpha = 1 - 0.05 = 0.95
$$

**Absorbed flux:**

$$
q_{\text{abs}} = \alpha \cdot G = 0.05 \times 1000 = \boxed{50 \;\text{W/m}^2}
$$

**Reflected flux:**

$$
q_{\text{ref}} = \rho \cdot G = 0.95 \times 1000 = \boxed{950 \;\text{W/m}^2}
$$

(ii) **Radiative emission from the surface:**

$$
E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4 = 0.05 \times (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \times (350)^4
$$

$$
E = 0.05 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.5006 \times 10^{10}
$$

$$
\boxed{E \approx 425.4 \;\text{W/m}^2}
$$

---

**Solution to B(b):**

**Spectral distribution:** The blackbody monochromatic emissive power $E_{b\lambda}$ is given by **Planck's Law**:

$$
E_{b\lambda}(T) = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left[ e^{C_2/(\lambda T)} - 1 \right]}
$$

where $C_1 = 2 \pi h c_0^2$ and $C_2 = h c_0 / k$.

**Qualitative Sketch Description (verbal schematic):**

```
E_bλ (W/m²·µm)
   |            *
   |         *     *
   |       *         *
   |      *           *
   |     *             *
   |    *               *
   |   *                  *
   |  *                       *
   |_*_________________________*____
   0        λ_max              Wavelength λ
```

- At $T_1 = 1500 \text{ K}$: peak is near 2 µm, curve is high and shifted left.
- At $T_2 = 1000 \text{ K}$: peak is near 3 µm, lower magnitude.
- At $T_3 = 500 \text{ K}$: peak is near 6 µm, even lower and broader.

**Wien's Displacement Law** — obtained by differentiating $E_{b\lambda}$ and setting $dE_{b\lambda}/d\lambda = 0$:

$$
\boxed{\lambda_{\max} T = 2897.8 \;\mu\text{m·K}}
$$

**Stefan–Boltzmann Law** — obtained by integrating $E_{b\lambda}$ over all $\lambda$ from 0 to $\infty$:

$$
E_b = \int_0^\infty E_{b\lambda}(T) \, d\lambda = \sigma T^4
$$

with $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert to Kelvin:** Temperatures in Stefan–Boltzmann equations must be in **Kelvin**. Using °C gives absurdly wrong answers.
> 2. **Mixing up absorptivity and emissivity:** For *non-gray* surfaces $\alpha \neq \varepsilon$. For gray + opaque surfaces, $\alpha = \varepsilon$ is valid. KTU problems frequently test whether you choose the right identity.
> 3. **Sign error in $Q$:** Heat *to* the sphere uses $T_{\text{sur}}^4 - T_s^4$, not the reverse. Always verify physical sense (energy flows hot → cold).
> 4. **Skipping units in the final answer:** Examiner deducts 0.5–1 mark for unit-less or unit-mismatched answers.
> 5. **Not stating Kirchhoff's assumption:** When you write $\alpha = \varepsilon$, declare *"by Kirchhoff's law for a gray, opaque surface"*. Marks are awarded for the **principle cited**, not just the calculation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Thermal radiation** is energy transfer via EM waves, no medium needed, propagates at $c_0 = 2.998 \times 10^8 \;\text{m/s}$.
- The four key properties are $\alpha, \rho, \tau, \varepsilon$, all **dimensionless** and in $[0, 1]$.
- **Energy balance:** $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$; for opaque body $\alpha + \rho = 1$.
- **Stefan–Boltzmann law:** $E_b = \sigma T^4$, with $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$.
- **Planck's distribution** describes the spectral variation; integrate it to get Stefan–Boltzmann.
- **Wien's displacement law:** $\lambda_{\max} T = 2897.8 \;\mu\text{m·K}$ — hotter bodies emit at shorter wavelengths.
- **Kirchhoff's law:** $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$ at equilibrium; reduces to $\varepsilon = \alpha$ for gray, opaque surfaces.
- A **blackbody** absorbs all and emits maximum possible radiation at every $\lambda$.
- A **gray body** has constant $\varepsilon_\lambda = \varepsilon$ across all $\lambda$.
- A **selective (real) body** has $\varepsilon_\lambda$ that varies with $\lambda$ — Kirchhoff applies *per wavelength*.
- Net radiation for a small body in a large enclosure: $Q = A\varepsilon\sigma(T_s^4 - T_{\text{sur}}^4)$ — the *most-tested* 14-mark equation.
- Always use **Kelvin** in all temperature-dependent radiation formulas.
- Polished metals have **low** $\varepsilon$ (good reflectors, poor emitters); oxidized/rough surfaces have **high** $\varepsilon$.
- For KTU, the **gray-opaque-diffuse** assumption is the default unless stated otherwise.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Absorptivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBT501 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Absorptivity # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Radiation Heat Transfer (Module 3)**, the term **Absorptivity** is a fundamental **radiative surface property** that quantifies the efficiency with which a real surface absorbs thermal radiation incident upon it. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):** > **Absorptivity (α)** is defined as the ratio of the **radiant energy absorbed** by a surface to the **total radiant energy incident** upon that surface, evaluated over a specified spectral range and direction. > > Symbol: **α** (Greek letter *alpha*) > > Dimensionless quantity: $0 \le \alpha \le 1$ Mathematically, for the **monochromatic (spectral) absorptivity** at a particular wavelength $\lambda$: $$ \alpha_\lambda = \frac{E_{\lambda,\text{absorbed}}}{E_{\lambda,\text{incident}}} $$ For the **total (hemispherical) absorptivity**, integrating over all wavelengths from $0$ to $\infty$: $$ \alpha = \frac{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} \alpha_\lambda \, E_{\lambda,i} \, d\lambda}{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} E_{\lambda,i} \, d\lambda} $$ where $E_{\lambda,i}$ is the spectral incident radiation flux (W/m²·μm). > [!NOTE] > The total absorptivity is a **weighted average** of the spectral absorptivities, weighted by the spectral distribution of the **incoming** radiation. This is why absorptivity is not a property of the surface alone — it also depends on the spectrum of the source. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are wearing a **black cotton t-shirt on a sunny summer afternoon**. The shirt feels extremely hot because almost all the sunlight that falls on it is **absorbed** by the dark fabric. Now replace it with a **shiny silver jacket**. The same sunlight falls on it, but you barely feel warmth because most of the energy is **reflected**, not absorbed. - The black t-shirt has a **high absorptivity** ($\alpha \to 1$). - The silver jacket has a **low absorptivity** ($\alpha \to 0$). **Absorptivity answers the question:** *"Out of all the thermal radiation that strikes a surface, what fraction does the surface actually capture and convert into internal energy (heat)?"* It is a number between **0 and 1**: - $\alpha = 1$ → **perfect absorber** (a blackbody) - $\alpha = 0$ → **perfect reflector** (ideal mirror) - $0 < \alpha < 1$ → **real engineering surface** ## 1.3 Physical Interpretation & Boundary Values | Surface Behavior | Absorptivity Value | Engineering Example | |---|---|---| | Perfect Blackbody | $\alpha = 1$ | Lampblack coating, cavity interior | | Highly Polished Metal | $\alpha \approx 0.05$ | Polished aluminium, mirror | | Painted White Surface | $\alpha \approx 0.2$ | White roof paint | | Matte Concrete | $\alpha \approx 0.6 - 0.7$ | Building wall | | Solar Absorber Plate | $\alpha \approx 0.95$ | Selective black coating | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examination Note:** When a numerical problem says *"a black surface"* without further qualification, the examiner expects $\alpha = 1$, $\rho = 0$, $\tau = 0$. Always state this explicitly in the first line of your answer to secure **1 mark** for assumptions. ## 1.4 Classification of Absorptivity There are **two principal classifications** used across Module 3 of PCBBT501: 1. **Spectral (Monochromatic) Absorptivity — $\alpha_\lambda$**: Absorptivity at a single wavelength $\lambda$. Required for non-gray, wavelength-dependent analysis. 2. **Total (Hemispherical) Absorptivity — $\alpha$**: Absorptivity integrated over the entire electromagnetic spectrum incident on the surface. A surface is called a **GRAY BODY** if $\alpha_\lambda = \text{constant} = \alpha$ for **all** wavelengths in the relevant spectral range. This is the most common engineering assumption used to simplify radiation calculations. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The Energy Balance at a Surface When a beam of monochromatic radiation of intensity $G_\lambda$ strikes a real surface (semi-transparent or opaque), three things happen simultaneously: 1. A fraction $\alpha_\lambda$ is **absorbed** — converted into internal energy of the body. 2. A fraction $\rho_\lambda$ is **reflected** — bounced back into the surroundings. 3. A fraction $\tau_\lambda$ is **transmitted** — passed through the body (for thin or semi-transparent media like glass). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conservation of Radiative Energy (per unit wavelength):** > > $$\alpha_\lambda + \rho_\lambda + \tau_\lambda = 1$$ > > This is the **fundamental radiative balance equation**. It applies at every wavelength and is a direct consequence of the **First Law of Thermodynamics** (energy cannot be destroyed). For most **opaque engineering surfaces** (metals, painted solids, ceramics), transmission is negligible: $$ \tau_\lambda \approx 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \alpha_\lambda + \rho_\lambda = 1 $$ ## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Absorptivity — Physical Mechanism At the microscopic level, absorptivity depends on: - **Electronic band structure** of the material (free electrons in metals reflect strongly). - **Surface roughness** (rough surfaces absorb more due to multiple internal reflections). - **Wavelength of incident radiation** (coatings can be designed to absorb short wavelengths but emit long ones — the principle behind **selective solar absorbers**). - **Temperature** (slight temperature dependence, but assumed constant in most KTU problems). ## 2.3 Kirchhoff's Law — The Deep Link Between Absorption and Emission Kirchhoff's Law of thermal radiation is the single most important theoretical result that connects absorptivity with another radiative property — **emissivity (ε)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Kirchhoff's Identity (KTU 2024 Module 3 High-Yield):** > > At **thermal equilibrium**, for a surface exposed to radiation from a blackbody at the same temperature: > > $$\alpha_\lambda(T) = \varepsilon_\lambda(T)$$ > > In words: **the spectral absorptivity of a surface equals its spectral emissivity at the same wavelength and temperature**. **Generalized (gray-body) form:** $$ \alpha = \varepsilon \quad \text{(for gray surfaces)} $$ This is **why polished metals** (which are poor absorbers) are also **poor emitters** — and conversely, **black surfaces** are **good emitters**. This identity is essential for solving **radiation network problems with enclosures**. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Conditions / Remarks | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Monochromatic Absorptivity | $\alpha_\lambda = \dfrac{E_{\lambda,\text{abs}}}{E_{\lambda,\text{inc}}}$ | Defined at wavelength $\lambda$, dimension-less | | 2 | Total Absorptivity (spectral weighted average) | $\alpha = \dfrac{\int_0^\infty \alpha_\lambda E_{\lambda,i}\, d\lambda}{\int_0^\infty E_{\lambda,i}\, d\lambda}$ | Depends on **source spectrum** | | 3 | Energy Balance (semi-transparent) | $\alpha_\lambda + \rho_\lambda + \tau_\lambda = 1$ | Per unit wavelength | | 4 | Energy Balance (opaque surface) | $\alpha_\lambda + \rho_\lambda = 1$ | Most engineering solids | | 5 | Kirchhoff's Law (spectral) | $\alpha_\lambda(T) = \varepsilon_\lambda(T)$ | At thermal equilibrium | | 6 | Kirchhoff's Law (gray body) | $\alpha = \varepsilon$ | Independent of wavelength | | 7 | Absorbed Radiation Flux | $G_{\text{abs}} = \alpha \cdot G$ | $G$ = incident irradiation (W/m²) | | 8 | Gray-body net radiation exchange (small object in large enclosure) | $q = \varepsilon \sigma (T_s^4 - T_{\text{sur}}^4)$ | Uses $\varepsilon = \alpha$ for gray body | | 9 | Blackbody Absorptivity | $\alpha_{bb} = 1$ | Reference maximum | > [!NOTE] > **Avoid LaTeX pipe errors:** Whenever you write expressions involving absolute value or size differentials like $|\Delta T|$ inside table cells, write them as $\vert \Delta T \vert$ or $\lvert \Delta T \rvert$ to prevent markdown table corruption. ## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Solar thermal collectors**: Selective coatings achieve $\alpha \approx 0.95$ for solar spectrum (0.3–2.5 μm) but very low $\varepsilon \approx 0.05$ for infrared emission — to maximize heat capture and minimize radiative loss. - **Building energy design**: Light-colored roofs ($\alpha \approx 0.2$) reduce cooling loads in hot climates (cool roof technology). - **Thermography & pyrometry**: Infrared cameras rely on Kirchhoff's law — knowing $\varepsilon$ gives $\alpha$ directly. - **Spacecraft thermal control**: Multi-layer insulation (MLI) has $\alpha \approx 0.1$ to reject solar radiation and prevent overheating in space. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Definition to Total Absorptivity (Weighted Mean Form) We begin with the spectral definition and integrate across the spectrum to obtain the *total* absorptivity. This derivation is **commonly asked in KTU Part B (14-mark) problems**. ### Starting Point The monochromatic absorptivity at wavelength $\lambda$: $$ \alpha_\lambda = \frac{G_{\lambda,\text{abs}}(\lambda)}{G_{\lambda,\text{inc}}(\lambda)} $$ where $G_{\lambda,\text{inc}}$ is the spectral incident irradiation at wavelength $\lambda$. ### Step 1 — Multiply both sides by the incident spectral flux $$ \alpha_\lambda \cdot G_{\lambda,\text{inc}}(\lambda) = G_{\lambda,\text{abs}}(\lambda) $$ *Conversion logic*: This expresses the **spectral absorbed flux** as the product of spectral absorptivity and incident spectral flux. ### Step 2 — Integrate both sides over the entire spectrum $$ \int_{0}^{\infty} \alpha_\lambda \, G_{\lambda,\text{inc}}(\lambda) \, d\lambda = \int_{0}^{\infty} G_{\lambda,\text{abs}}(\lambda) \, d\lambda $$ *Conversion logic*: Because integration is a linear operator, we can sum the contributions of every infinitesimal wavelength band $d\lambda$ to obtain the total absorbed flux $G_{\text{abs}}$. ### Step 3 — Recognize the total incident flux on the right-hand side (RHS) The total incident irradiation $G_{\text{inc}}$ is by definition: $$ G_{\text{inc}} = \int_{0}^{\infty} G_{\lambda,\text{inc}}(\lambda) \, d\lambda $$ ### Step 4 — Define total absorptivity as a ratio The total absorptivity $\alpha$ is the ratio of the **total absorbed flux** to the **total incident flux**: $$ \alpha \;\equiv\; \frac{G_{\text{abs}}}{G_{\text{inc}}} = \frac{\int_{0}^{\infty} \alpha_\lambda \, G_{\lambda,\text{inc}}(\lambda) \, d\lambda}{\int_{0}^{\infty} G_{\lambda,\text{inc}}(\lambda) \, d\lambda} $$ *Conversion logic*: This is exactly a **weighted arithmetic mean** of the spectral absorptivities, with the spectral incident flux $G_{\lambda,\text{inc}}$ acting as the weighting function. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight for Examiners:** Because the weighting function depends on the *source spectrum*, $\alpha$ is **not a unique material property** — it changes if the source spectrum changes. This is the reason a surface behaves differently under sunlight (high-temperature source) versus a furnace flame (different spectrum). ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Kirchhoff's Law from Thermodynamic Equilibrium This is a high-yield derivation often expected in the **Part B compulsory question** on radiation. ### Setup Consider a small surface $A$ at temperature $T$ enclosed in a much larger isothermal enclosure at the same temperature $T$. Both surfaces are opaque ($\tau = 0$). ### Step 1 — Net heat exchange must be zero at equilibrium Because the entire system is at the same temperature, by the **Zeroth and Second Laws of Thermodynamics**, the net radiative heat transfer must vanish: $$ q_{\text{net}} = 0 $$ ### Step 2 — Express $q_{\text{net}}$ in terms of properties The small surface $A$ emits at rate $A\varepsilon \sigma T^4$ and absorbs at rate $A\alpha \sigma T^4$ (since the enclosure acts as a blackbody at temperature $T$): $$ q_{\text{net}} = A\varepsilon \sigma T^4 - A\alpha \sigma T^4 = 0 $$ ### Step 3 — Simplify Cancel the common factor $A \sigma T^4$: $$ \varepsilon - \alpha = 0 $$ ### Step 4 — Conclude Kirchhoff's Identity $$ \boxed{\varepsilon = \alpha} $$ with the generalized spectral form $\varepsilon_\lambda(T) = \alpha_\lambda(T)$ following by the same argument applied to a single wavelength. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Solved in KTU Board Exam Style > **Problem:** A flat opaque surface at 600 K receives radiation from a furnace at 1500 K. The surface has spectral absorptivity $\alpha_\lambda = 0.9$ for $\lambda \le 2\ \mu\text{m}$ and $\alpha_\lambda = 0.2$ for $\lambda > 2\ \mu\text{m}$. Assuming the incident flux from the furnace follows Wein's displacement with peak at $\lambda_{\max} \approx 1.93\ \mu\text{m}$, compute the **total absorptivity** if 80% of the incident flux lies in $\lambda \le 2\ \mu\text{m}$ and 20% lies beyond. ### Solution **Step 1 — Identify the weighted average formula** $$ \alpha = \frac{\int_0^\infty \alpha_\lambda G_{\lambda,\text{inc}}\, d\lambda}{\int_0^\infty G_{\lambda,\text{inc}}\, d\lambda} = f_1 \alpha_1 + f_2 \alpha_2 $$ where $f_1, f_2$ are fractional contributions to incident flux. **Step 2 — Substitute values** $$ \alpha = (0.80)(0.9) + (0.20)(0.2) $$ **Step 3 — Compute** $$ \alpha = 0.72 + 0.04 = 0.76 $$ **Step 4 — State the final answer** $$ \boxed{\alpha = 0.76} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Marks allocation in KTU valuation:** Setup of weighted average formula = 4 marks, substitution = 3 marks, arithmetic & unit-less final answer with statement = 3 marks. Total = 10 marks (typical sub-part weightage). ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Absorptivity vs Other Radiative Properties | Property | Symbol | Definition | Range | Relation to α | |---|---|---|---|---| | Absorptivity | $\alpha$ | Fraction of incident radiation absorbed | $0$ to $1$ | Self | | Reflectivity | $\rho$ | Fraction of incident radiation reflected | $0$ to $1$ | $\rho = 1 - \alpha$ (opaque) | | Transmissivity | $\tau$ | Fraction of incident radiation transmitted | $0$ to $1$ | $\alpha + \tau \le 1$ (general) | | Emissivity | $\varepsilon$ | Effectiveness of emitting radiation vs blackbody | $0$ to $1$ | $\varepsilon = \alpha$ (gray, Kirchhoff) | | Radiosity | $J$ | Total radiation leaving a surface (W/m²) | $\ge 0$ | $J = \varepsilon \sigma T^4 + \rho G$ | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Radiative Energy Balance at an Opaque Surface The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how incident radiation $G$ is split into **absorbed**, **reflected**, and (for completeness, in the general case) **transmitted** components at a real opaque surface. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Incident Radiation G
from source at Ts]:::source B{Opaque Surface
at temperature T}:::surface C[Absorbed Fraction
alpha times G]:::absorb D[Reflected Fraction
rho times G]:::reflect E[Net Energy Gain
q absorbed equals alpha times G]:::output A --> B B --> C B --> D C --> E classDef source fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef surface fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef absorb fill:#2E8B57,stroke:#013220,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF classDef reflect fill:#4682B4,stroke:#0B243B,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF classDef output fill:#CD5C5C,stroke:#400000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Conceptual Linkage via Kirchhoff's Law This flowchart maps the **logical chain** between surface temperature, absorptivity, and emissivity — a high-yield concept for **14-mark derivations**. ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Thermal Equilibrium
System at uniform T]:::eq P2[Apply Second Law
Net heat transfer must equal zero]:::law P3[Small Surface in
Large Enclosure]:::geom P4[Energy Balance:
A epsilon sigma T4 minus A alpha sigma T4 equals 0]:::balance P5[Cancel common terms]:::cancel P6[Result:
epsilon equals alpha]:::result P7[Generalized Spectral Form:
epsilon lambda T equals alpha lambda T]:::general P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> P4 P4 --> P5 P5 --> P6 P6 --> P7 classDef eq fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef law fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef geom fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef balance fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cancel fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef result fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF classDef general fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Architecture: Absorptivity in a Solar Collector Adapting the physical schematic into a block-level functional flow (as per the **diagram-fallback rule** for complex physical drawings): ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Sun Source
High Temperature
Ts approx 5778 K]:::sun S2[Selective Absorber
Coating on Plate]:::coat S3[Working Fluid
Water or Oil]:::fluid S4[Storage Tank]:::tank S1 -- Spectrum 0.3 to 2.5 micrometre --> S2 S2 -- Absorbed Heat Q equals alpha times G --> S3 S3 -- Convected Energy --> S4 classDef sun fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef coat fill:#2F4F4F,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFFFFF classDef fluid fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#000080,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF classDef tank fill:#A0522D,stroke:#3B1F0E,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram-reading tip for KTU answers:** Even when not explicitly asked, sketching a simple energy-balance diagram at the start of a radiation problem earns **1–2 easy marks** for organized presentation. Examiners reward visual clarity. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define absorptivity. State the relationship between absorptivity, reflectivity, and transmissivity for (i) an opaque surface and (ii) a general semi-transparent surface.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1 mark):** Absorptivity $\alpha$ is the ratio of the radiant energy absorbed by a surface to the total radiant energy incident upon it. It is a dimensionless quantity lying in the range $0 \le \alpha \le 1$. > > **(i) Opaque surface (1 mark):** Transmission is zero, hence > $$\alpha + \rho = 1$$ > > **(ii) Semi-transparent surface (1 mark):** All three fractions must sum to unity: > $$\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$$ --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **State Kirchhoff's law of thermal radiation. Why is it significant in engineering heat-transfer problems?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Statement (2 marks):** Kirchhoff's law states that at thermal equilibrium, the spectral emissivity of a surface equals its spectral absorptivity at the same wavelength and temperature: > $$\varepsilon_\lambda(T) = \alpha_\lambda(T)$$ > For a gray surface, this reduces to $\varepsilon = \alpha$. > > **Significance (1 mark):** It allows engineers to determine the **emissivity** of a surface (which is difficult to measure directly) by simply measuring its **absorptivity** (which can be obtained spectroscopically). It also justifies the use of polished metal shields (low $\alpha$ → low $\varepsilon$) to reduce radiative heat loss in cryogenic and aerospace applications. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - Students often **lose 2 marks** by writing $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$ without specifying the spectral ($\lambda$) subscript, which makes the equation dimensionally incomplete for non-gray analysis. Always write $\alpha_\lambda + \rho_\lambda + \tau_\lambda = 1$. > - In Kirchhoff's-law derivations, students skip the **Second Law justification** (net heat transfer must be zero at equilibrium) and lose the conceptual marks. > - Marks are deducted for **incorrect unit handling** in absorbed-flux problems — always state the final answer in W/m² with correct significant figures. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Compulsory-style with internal sub-parts** > **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified per 2024 Scheme]** > > **(a)** Define monochromatic absorptivity $\alpha_\lambda$ and total absorptivity $\alpha$. Derive the relationship that expresses total absorptivity as a weighted average of spectral absorptivities. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A polished copper plate ($\alpha = 0.15$) of area $0.5\ \text{m}^2$ is placed inside a furnace whose walls are at $1200\ \text{K}$. The plate surface temperature is steady at $400\ \text{K}$. Treating the plate as a gray body in a large enclosure, compute (i) the absorbed radiative flux (W/m²), and (ii) the net radiative heat **lost** by the plate (W). **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **(7 marks)** **Step 1 — Definitions (2 marks)** Monochromatic absorptivity at wavelength $\lambda$: $$ \alpha_\lambda = \frac{G_{\lambda,\text{abs}}(\lambda)}{G_{\lambda,\text{inc}}(\lambda)} $$ Total (hemispherical) absorptivity: $$ \alpha = \frac{G_{\text{abs}}}{G_{\text{inc}}} = \frac{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} G_{\lambda,\text{abs}}(\lambda)\, d\lambda}{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} G_{\lambda,\text{inc}}(\lambda)\, d\lambda} $$ **Step 2 — Derivation (5 marks)** From the monochromatic definition: $$ G_{\lambda,\text{abs}}(\lambda) = \alpha_\lambda \, G_{\lambda,\text{inc}}(\lambda) $$ Substitute into the total-integral expression: $$ \alpha = \frac{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} \alpha_\lambda \, G_{\lambda,\text{inc}}(\lambda)\, d\lambda}{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} G_{\lambda,\text{inc}}(\lambda)\, d\lambda} $$ This is the **weighted mean** of $\alpha_\lambda$ weighted by $G_{\lambda,\text{inc}}$. Therefore total absorptivity depends on both the **surface property** $\alpha_\lambda$ and the **source spectrum**. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **(7 marks)** **Given (1 mark):** - $A = 0.5\ \text{m}^2$ - $\alpha = 0.15$, $\varepsilon = \alpha = 0.15$ (gray body, Kirchhoff) - $T_{\text{sur}} = 1200\ \text{K}$, $T_s = 400\ \text{K}$ - $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ **Step 1 — Compute irradiation on plate from furnace (1 mark)** Treating furnace as large blackbody enclosure: $$ G = \sigma T_{\text{sur}}^{4} = (5.67 \times 10^{-8})(1200)^{4} $$ Compute $(1200)^4$: $$ 1200^2 = 1{,}440{,}000 = 1.44 \times 10^{6} $$ $$ 1200^4 = (1.44 \times 10^{6})^2 = 2.0736 \times 10^{12} $$ Therefore: $$ G = (5.67 \times 10^{-8})(2.0736 \times 10^{12}) = 1.175 \times 10^{5}\ \text{W/m}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Absorbed flux (1 mark)** $$ G_{\text{abs}} = \alpha \cdot G = (0.15)(1.175 \times 10^{5}) = 1.763 \times 10^{4}\ \text{W/m}^2 $$ **Step 3 — Net radiative heat transfer (small body in large enclosure formula, 3 marks)** $$ Q_{\text{net}} = A \varepsilon \sigma (T_s^4 - T_{\text{sur}}^4) $$ Compute $T_s^4 = 400^4 = 2.56 \times 10^{10}\ \text{K}^4$ and $T_{\text{sur}}^4 = 2.0736 \times 10^{12}\ \text{K}^4$: $$ T_s^4 - T_{\text{sur}}^4 = 2.56 \times 10^{10} - 2.0736 \times 10^{12} = -2.048 \times 10^{12}\ \text{K}^4 $$ $$ Q_{\text{net}} = (0.5)(0.15)(5.67 \times 10^{-8})(-2.048 \times 10^{12}) $$ $$ Q_{\text{net}} = (0.075)(5.67 \times 10^{-8})(-2.048 \times 10^{12}) $$ $$ Q_{\text{net}} = (4.2525 \times 10^{-9})(-2.048 \times 10^{12}) $$ $$ Q_{\text{net}} \approx -8.71 \times 10^{3}\ \text{W} = -8.71\ \text{kW} $$ **Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark)** The negative sign indicates the plate **loses** heat to the furnace by net radiation, since it is colder. Magnitude: $$ \boxed{Q_{\text{net}} \approx 8.71\ \text{kW (heat lost by plate)}} $$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice** > **Q4. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified per 2024 Scheme]** > > **(a)** State and prove Kirchhoff's law for a small gray surface enclosed in a large isothermal blackbody cavity. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A solar flat-plate collector has a selective absorber coating. For solar radiation (effective blackbody source at $5800\ \text{K}$), the absorptivity is $\alpha_s = 0.92$. For thermal re-radiation at the collector's operating temperature ($350\ \text{K}$), the effective emissivity is $\varepsilon_t = 0.12$. Compute the **net useful heat gain per m²** by the collector when it is exposed to a solar irradiation of $G_{\text{solar}} = 800\ \text{W/m}^2$ and the surroundings (sky + ground) are at $T_{\text{sur}} = 300\ \text{K}$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **(7 marks)** **Statement (1 mark):** At thermal equilibrium, the emissivity of a surface equals its absorptivity. **Proof (6 marks):** Consider a small surface of area $A$ at temperature $T$ inside a large isothermal black enclosure also at temperature $T$. - Energy emitted by the small surface per unit time: $A\varepsilon\sigma T^4$ - Irradiation on small surface from black enclosure: $G = \sigma T^4$ - Energy absorbed by small surface: $A\alpha G = A\alpha\sigma T^4$ By the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**, at thermal equilibrium the net heat exchange must equal zero: $$ A\varepsilon\sigma T^4 - A\alpha\sigma T^4 = 0 $$ Dividing throughout by $A\sigma T^4$: $$ \varepsilon - \alpha = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \boxed{\varepsilon = \alpha} $$ By repeating the argument for a narrow band of wavelengths $d\lambda$ around $\lambda$, we obtain the **spectral** form $\varepsilon_\lambda(T) = \alpha_\lambda(T)$. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **(7 marks)** **Given (1 mark):** - $\alpha_s = 0.92$ (solar-spectrum absorptivity) - $\varepsilon_t = 0.12$ (infrared emissivity at 350 K) - $G_{\text{solar}} = 800\ \text{W/m}^2$ - $T_s = 350\ \text{K}$, $T_{\text{sur}} = 300\ \text{K}$ - $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ **Step 1 — Solar heat absorbed (2 marks)** $$ q_{\text{abs,solar}} = \alpha_s \cdot G_{\text{solar}} = (0.92)(800) = 736\ \text{W/m}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Thermal re-radiation loss to surroundings (2 marks)** $$ q_{\text{emit}} = \varepsilon_t \sigma (T_s^4 - T_{\text{sur}}^4) $$ Compute $T_s^4 = 350^4 = 1.5006 \times 10^{10}\ \text{K}^4$ and $T_{\text{sur}}^4 = 300^4 = 8.1 \times 10^{9}\ \text{K}^4$: $$ T_s^4 - T_{\text{sur}}^4 = 1.5006 \times 10^{10} - 0.81 \times 10^{10} = 0.6906 \times 10^{10}\ \text{K}^4 $$ $$ q_{\text{emit}} = (0.12)(5.67 \times 10^{-8})(0.6906 \times 10^{10}) $$ $$ q_{\text{emit}} = (0.12)(391.6) \approx 47\ \text{W/m}^2 $$ **Step 3 — Net useful heat gain (2 marks)** $$ q_{\text{net}} = q_{\text{abs,solar}} - q_{\text{emit}} = 736 - 47 = 689\ \text{W/m}^2 $$ $$ \boxed{q_{\text{net}} \approx 689\ \text{W/m}^2} $$ --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Absorptivity** - **Symbol:** $\alpha$ (dimensionless, $0 \le \alpha \le 1$). - **Definition:** $\alpha = \dfrac{\text{energy absorbed}}{\text{energy incident}}$. - **Energy balance:** $\alpha_\lambda + \rho_\lambda + \tau_\lambda = 1$ (general); $\alpha_\lambda + \rho_\lambda = 1$ (opaque). - **Total α:** Weighted mean of $\alpha_\lambda$, weighted by the **source spectrum** $G_{\lambda,\text{inc}}$. - **Kirchhoff's Law:** $\varepsilon_\lambda(T) = \alpha_\lambda(T)$ (spectral); $\varepsilon = \alpha$ (gray body). - **Gray-body assumption:** $\alpha_\lambda = \alpha$ for all $\lambda$ — required to use $\alpha = \varepsilon$ without spectral integration. - **Blackbody limit:** $\alpha = 1$, $\rho = 0$, $\tau = 0$. - **Engineering insight:** Polished metals have **low α → low ε** (good thermal shields); black surfaces have **high α → high ε** (good emitters/absorbers). - **Selectivity:** Real surfaces can have $\alpha_s \gg \alpha_t$ (e.g., solar absorbers); this is exploited in solar collector design. - **Always state assumptions:** Opaque? Gray? Equilibrium temperature? Surface temperature? In KTU answers, **assumption statements carry 1–2 marks**. - **Common pitfall:** Forgetting the spectral subscript $\lambda$ in the energy-balance equation. - **Common pitfall 2:** Treating absorptivity as a *pure material property* — it is not; it depends on the **incident spectrum**.

topics.title: Reflectivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT501 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reflectivity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Reflectivity (ρ)** is defined as the fraction of the total incident radiation flux (or intensity) that is reflected by a surface, when radiation strikes that surface. It is one of the three fundamental **radiative surface properties**, alongside **absorptivity (α)** and **transmissivity (τ)**.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\rho = \frac{\text{Radiation reflected}}{\text{Total incident radiation}}$$

where $\rho$ is a dimensionless quantity (a fraction, hence $0 \le \rho \le 1$).

The **complementary energy balance** for any semi-transparent surface that radiation encounters is given by the conservation principle:

$$\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$$

For an **opaque surface** (τ = 0), this simplifies to:

$$\alpha + \rho = 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 - Radiation):** The radiative properties — absorptivity, reflectivity, and transmissivity — form the foundational basis for solving **blackbody, gray-body, and real-surface radiation problems** in the KTU Heat Transfer syllabus. Mastering these properties is mandatory for solving radiation network and radiation shield problems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a dark room and you shine a powerful flashlight onto a wall. Three things can happen to that light:

1. **Absorption** — The wall "drinks in" some of the light energy, converting it to a tiny bit of heat.
2. **Reflection** — The wall "bounces back" some of the light, like a mirror.
3. **Transmission** — A small fraction of the light passes *through* the wall (only possible if the wall is glass or thin film).

**Reflectivity (ρ)** is simply the "bouncing-back fraction." If $\rho = 0.9$, the surface is like a high-quality **mirror** — it sends back 90% of the incoming radiation. If $\rho = 0.05$, the surface is **matte black** — it barely reflects anything.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Stefan–Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$
- **Spectral reflectivity** $\rho_\lambda$: Reflectivity at a specific wavelength $\lambda$.
- **Total (overall) reflectivity** $\rho$: Integrated reflectivity over all wavelengths.
- **Directional reflectivity** $\rho_\theta$: Reflectivity at a specific angle $\theta$ of incidence.

> [!NOTE]
> The term **"reflectance"** is often used interchangeably with reflectivity in KTU exam answers, but strictly speaking, *reflectance* refers to a measured experimental value (which includes both specular and diffuse components), while *reflectivity* is the intrinsic property of a perfectly clean, smooth material.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the Concept

**Why does reflection happen at all?**
When electromagnetic radiation (e.g., thermal IR waves) strikes a surface, the oscillating electric field of the incoming wave interacts with the electrons in the material. These electrons are forced to oscillate, re-radiating the energy. The portion of energy that re-radiates *back* into the original medium constitutes **reflection**.

**How is reflectivity classified?**

1. **Specular Reflection (Mirror-like)**
   - Angle of incidence = Angle of reflection.
   - Occurs on **smooth, polished surfaces** (polished metals: ρ ≈ 0.95 for Al, Ag).
   - Image is preserved.

2. **Diffuse Reflection (Lambertian)**
   - Radiation is scattered uniformly in all directions.
   - Occurs on **rough, matte surfaces** (brick, cloth, snow, white paint).
   - Image is destroyed.

3. **Mixed Reflection**
   - Real engineering surfaces (e.g., slightly tarnished aluminum) display a combination of both.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Condition | Units |
|---|---------|------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$ | Energy balance at a semi-transparent surface | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\alpha + \rho = 1$ | Energy balance for **opaque** surface (τ = 0) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\rho = \dfrac{\text{Reflected flux}}{\text{Incident flux}}$ | Definition of total reflectivity | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\rho = \int_0^\infty \rho_\lambda \, I_{\lambda,i}(\lambda) \, d\lambda \Big/ \int_0^\infty I_{\lambda,i}(\lambda) \, d\lambda$ | Spectral integration for **monochromatic incidence** | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\rho_\lambda = 1 - \alpha_\lambda$ | Opaque, monochromatic relation | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $\rho = 1 - \alpha$ (for gray body) | Opaque, gray-body relation | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $E_\lambda(\lambda, T) = \varepsilon_\lambda(\lambda, T) \, E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T)$ | Spectral emissivity definition (Kirchhoff basis) | W/m³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** The relation $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$ is the *radiative equivalent of the First Law of Thermodynamics applied to a control volume of zero thickness* (the surface). It is a **conservation-of-energy statement**, not an empirical rule. Examiners frequently test this directly.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Solar thermal collectors:** Low-reflectivity (anti-reflective) coatings (ρ ≈ 0.05) are used on solar absorber plates to maximize absorbed energy.
- **Building insulation:** Low-e (low-emissivity) window glass uses a thin Ag coating with $\rho \approx 0.9$ to reflect IR radiation back into the room.
- **Radiation shields (KTU Module 3 favorite topic):** Highly polished surfaces (high ρ, low ε) are used in multi-shield enclosures to drastically reduce net radiation heat transfer.
- **Thermos flasks:** Inner surfaces are silvered ($\rho \approx 0.97$) to reflect radiation back into the contents.
- **Astronomy/Spacecraft:** Multi-layer insulation (MLI) blankets use alternating high-ρ polymer films to minimize radiative heat loss in vacuum.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Energy Balance at a Radiative Surface

Consider a thin surface element of area $A$ exposed to incident radiation $G$ (W/m²).

**Step 1 — Identify the incident energy flux.**
Let $G$ be the total irradiation (incident flux) on the surface in W/m².

The total incident power on area $A$ is:
$$Q_{\text{inc}} = G \cdot A$$

**Step 2 — Identify the three possible fates of incident energy.**
Upon striking the surface, the radiation is partitioned into:
- Absorbed part: $\alpha \, G \, A$
- Reflected part: $\rho \, G \, A$
- Transmitted part: $\tau \, G \, A$

**Step 3 — Apply conservation of energy (no energy is created or destroyed at the surface).**
$$G \cdot A = \alpha G A + \rho G A + \tau G A$$

**Step 4 — Divide both sides by $G \cdot A$.**
$$1 = \alpha + \rho + \tau$$

This is the **universal radiative energy balance** for any surface.

**Step 5 — Specialize to an opaque surface.**
An **opaque** surface (such as a metal plate) is defined as one that does not allow radiation to pass through. Therefore:
$$\tau = 0$$

Substituting into the energy balance:
$$\alpha + \rho = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho = 1 - \alpha$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Relationship Between Spectral Reflectivity and Monochromatic Absorptivity

For an **opaque** surface at a specific wavelength $\lambda$:

**Step 1 — Start with the spectral energy balance.**
At wavelength $\lambda$, the energy balance becomes:
$$\alpha_\lambda + \rho_\lambda = 1$$

**Step 2 — Solve for spectral reflectivity.**
$$\rho_\lambda = 1 - \alpha_\lambda$$

**Step 3 — Connect to Kirchhoff's Law.**
By **Kirchhoff's Law**, for a surface in thermal equilibrium, the monochromatic emissivity equals the monochromatic absorptivity:
$$\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$$

**Step 4 — Combine the two results.**
Substituting:
$$\rho_\lambda = 1 - \varepsilon_\lambda$$

This crucial relationship tells us that **a surface that emits strongly absorbs strongly, and therefore reflects weakly**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Directional-Spectral Reflectivity (Fresnel Equations)

For a *perfectly smooth* (specular) surface, reflectivity depends on the angle of incidence and the material's complex refractive index. For a **dielectric non-conducting material** (e.g., glass), the **Fresnel equations** give:

**For polarization parallel to the plane of incidence:**
$$\rho_{\lambda,\parallel} = \left[ \frac{n_2 \cos\theta_i - n_1 \cos\theta_t}{n_2 \cos\theta_i + n_1 \cos\theta_t} \right]^2$$

**For polarization perpendicular to the plane of incidence:**
$$\rho_{\lambda,\perp} = \left[ \frac{n_1 \cos\theta_i - n_2 \cos\theta_t}{n_1 \cos\theta_i + n_2 \cos\theta_t} \right]^2$$

where:
- $n_1, n_2$ are the refractive indices of the two media.
- $\theta_i$ is the angle of incidence.
- $\theta_t$ is the angle of transmission (from Snell's law: $n_1 \sin\theta_i = n_2 \sin\theta_t$).

**Step 5 — Special case: Normal incidence ($\theta_i = 0$).**
At $\theta_i = 0$, both polarizations give the same result:
$$\rho_{\lambda,n} = \left( \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2} \right)^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** You will **not** be required to derive Fresnel equations in the KTU exam for Heat Transfer Operations, but knowing the *form* of the equation for $\rho_{\lambda,n}$ is useful for solving radiation problems involving glass covers and selective surfaces.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Reflectivity of Common Engineering Materials

| Material | Surface Condition | Typical ρ (Total) | Behavior |
|----------|------------------|------------------|----------|
| Polished Aluminum | Mirror | **0.95** | Specular, near-IR reflective |
| Polished Silver | Mirror | **0.97** | Best overall IR reflector |
| Polished Copper | Mirror | **0.93** | IR reflective |
| White Paint | Matte | **0.80** | Diffuse |
| Snow, fresh | Matte | **0.85** | Diffuse |
| Brick, red | Matte | **0.55** | Diffuse |
| Concrete | Rough | **0.30** | Diffuse |
| Asphalt | Rough | **0.10** | Diffuse |
| Black lacquer | Matte | **0.05** | Diffuse, almost black |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Radiation-Surface Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incoming Radiation G]:::input
    B[Surface Element dA]:::surface
    C[Absorbed Flux alpha times G]:::absorb
    D[Reflected Flux rho times G]:::reflect
    E[Transmitted Flux tau times G]:::transmit
    F[Conservation Equation: alpha plus rho plus tau equals 1]:::equation
    G[Opaque Surface Branch: tau equals 0, alpha plus rho equals 1]:::opaque
    H[Polished Metal: rho near 0.95]:::polish
    I[Matte Black: rho near 0.05]:::matte
    J[Kirchhoff Result: rho equals 1 minus epsilon]:::kirch

    A --> B
    B --> C
    B --> D
    B --> E
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    G --> I
    G --> J

    classDef input fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef surface fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    classDef absorb fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    classDef reflect fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000
    classDef transmit fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6,color:#000
    classDef equation fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#d79b00,color:#000
    classDef opaque fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000
    classDef polish fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#666666,color:#000
    classDef matte fill:#1f1f1f,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
    classDef kirch fill:#fad7ac,stroke:#b46504,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Specular vs Diffuse Reflection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Incident Ray at angle theta_i]:::ray
    P1[Polished Surface]:::polished
    P2[Rough Surface]:::rough
    R1[Reflected Ray at angle theta_r equals theta_i]:::ray
    R2[Scattered Rays in Many Directions]:::ray

    A1 --> P1
    P1 --> R1
    A1 --> P2
    P2 --> R2

    classDef ray fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000
    classDef polished fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    classDef rough fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Use of Reflectivity in a Radiation Shield

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Hot Plate Temperature Th]:::hot
    S2[Shield 1 rho1 high epsilon1 low]:::shield
    S3[Shield 2 rho2 high epsilon2 low]:::shield
    S4[Cold Plate Temperature Tc]:::cold
    OUT[Reduced Net Radiation Q net]:::result

    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S2 --> OUT
    S3 --> OUT

    classDef hot fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000
    classDef cold fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef shield fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The radiation shield diagram above is a *functional map* (not a literal geometric drawing). It shows that inserting a **high-reflectivity (low-emissivity) shield** between a hot and a cold surface reduces the net radiative heat flux — this is a direct application of the $\rho + \varepsilon = 1$ relationship.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "reflectivity" as applied to thermal radiation. State the energy balance equation that relates absorptivity, reflectivity, and transmissivity.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Reflectivity (ρ)** is defined as the ratio of the radiation flux reflected by a surface to the total radiation flux incident upon it. It is a dimensionless quantity ranging from 0 to 1.

$$\rho = \frac{\text{Reflected flux}}{\text{Incident flux}}$$

The energy balance at any surface, based on the conservation of incident energy, is:

$$\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$$

where $\alpha$ is the absorptivity, $\rho$ is the reflectivity, and $\tau$ is the transmissivity of the surface.

*(Breakdown: 1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark explanation of symbols = 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State Kirchhoff's Law. Show that for an opaque, gray body surface, the emissivity and reflectivity are related by $\varepsilon + \rho = 1$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Kirchhoff's Law** states that at thermal equilibrium, the monochromatic emissivity of a surface equals its monochromatic absorptivity at the same wavelength and temperature:
$$\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$$

For a **gray body**, this holds across all wavelengths, so $\varepsilon = \alpha$. For an **opaque** surface, $\tau = 0$, so from the energy balance:
$$\alpha + \rho = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \varepsilon + \rho = 1$$

*(Breakdown: 1 mark Kirchhoff statement + 1 mark opaque condition + 1 mark final relation = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE guidelines**, Part B questions carry 14 marks and typically have an **internal choice**. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]`

**(a)** A small surface element of area $A = 0.05 \text{ m}^2$ is exposed to an incident radiation flux of $G = 1200 \text{ W/m}^2$. The surface has an absorptivity $\alpha = 0.35$ and a transmissivity $\tau = 0.10$.
**(i)** Calculate the reflectivity $\rho$ of the surface.
**(ii)** Determine the absorbed, reflected, and transmitted power.
**(iii)** If the surface is coated with a thin opaque aluminum layer ($\alpha = 0.07$), what is the new reflectivity?

**(b)** Derive the relation $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$ starting from the energy balance at a radiative surface. State the simplified form for an opaque surface and explain its physical significance with a real-world example.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a)(i) — Find reflectivity (2 marks):**
From the energy balance:
$$\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho = 1 - \alpha - \tau$$

Substituting:
$$\rho = 1 - 0.35 - 0.10 = 0.55$$

**(a)(ii) — Power breakdown (3 marks):**

- Absorbed power: $Q_{\text{abs}} = \alpha \, G \, A = 0.35 \times 1200 \times 0.05 = 21.0 \text{ W}$
- Reflected power: $Q_{\text{ref}} = \rho \, G \, A = 0.55 \times 1200 \times 0.05 = 33.0 \text{ W}$
- Transmitted power: $Q_{\text{trans}} = \tau \, G \, A = 0.10 \times 1200 \times 0.05 = 6.0 \text{ W}$

**Check (energy conservation):** $21.0 + 33.0 + 6.0 = 60.0 \text{ W} = G \times A = 1200 \times 0.05 = 60.0 \text{ W}$ ✓

**(a)(iii) — New reflectivity (2 marks):**
Aluminum coating is opaque, so $\tau = 0$:
$$\rho_{\text{new}} = 1 - \alpha = 1 - 0.07 = 0.93$$

**(b) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Let $G$ be the incident radiation flux (W/m²) on a surface of area $A$. The total incident energy rate is $G \cdot A$.

**Step 2:** The incident energy splits into three parts based on the surface properties:
- Absorbed: $\alpha G A$
- Reflected: $\rho G A$
- Transmitted: $\tau G A$

**Step 3:** By conservation of energy (no energy is created, destroyed, or stored at the infinitesimally thin surface):
$$G A = \alpha G A + \rho G A + \tau G A$$

**Step 4:** Dividing by $G A$:
$$1 = \alpha + \rho + \tau$$

**Step 5 — Opaque surface simplification:** For an opaque surface (e.g., metals, wood, concrete, paints), no radiation is transmitted, so $\tau = 0$:
$$\alpha + \rho = 1$$

**Step 6 — Physical significance with real-world example:** This relation means a *good absorber is a poor reflector* and vice versa. For example, a polished silver surface used in a **thermos flask** has $\rho \approx 0.97$, so its absorptivity is only $\alpha \approx 0.03$, making it an excellent IR reflector that minimizes radiative heat loss.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Internal Choice

**(a)** Two parallel large plates are separated by a vacuum gap. Plate 1 (hot, at $T_1 = 800 \text{ K}$) has $\varepsilon_1 = 0.20$. Plate 2 (cold, at $T_2 = 300 \text{ K}$) has $\varepsilon_2 = 0.05$. The plates are opaque.
**(i)** Determine the reflectivity of each plate.
**(ii)** Explain what the difference in reflectivity implies about the surface coating.

**(b)** A polished aluminum surface has $\rho = 0.95$ in the infrared region. Determine its absorptivity and emissivity. If the same surface is exposed to a directional solar source (parallel rays) at an angle of incidence $\theta_i = 30°$ from the normal, and the refractive index of aluminum oxide layer is $n = 1.7$, calculate the **normal-incidence spectral reflectivity** using the Fresnel equation. State one assumption.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a)(i) — Reflectivities (4 marks):**
For opaque surfaces, $\alpha + \rho = 1$. From Kirchhoff's law for gray bodies, $\varepsilon = \alpha$, hence:
$$\varepsilon + \rho = 1$$

- Plate 1: $\rho_1 = 1 - 0.20 = 0.80$
- Plate 2: $\rho_2 = 1 - 0.05 = 0.95$

**(a)(ii) — Surface coating inference (3 marks):**
Plate 1 (ρ = 0.80) reflects moderately — likely an unpolished metal or a metallic-oxide-coated surface. Plate 2 (ρ = 0.95) reflects strongly — likely **highly polished silver or aluminum**, designed to be a low-emissivity surface (such as a low-e window coating or a cryogenic radiation shield).

**(b) — Aluminum properties (4 marks):**
- Absorptivity: $\alpha = 1 - \rho = 1 - 0.95 = 0.05$
- Emissivity (Kirchhoff): $\varepsilon = \alpha = 0.05$ (gray body assumption)

**Fresnel reflectivity at normal incidence (3 marks):**

$$\rho_{\lambda,n} = \left( \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2} \right)^2$$

Taking $n_1 = 1.0$ (air) and $n_2 = 1.7$ (oxide layer):
$$\rho_{\lambda,n} = \left( \frac{1.0 - 1.7}{1.0 + 1.7} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{-0.7}{2.7} \right)^2 = ( -0.2593 )^2 = 0.0672 \approx 6.72\%$$

**Assumption:** The oxide layer is treated as a smooth, dielectric (non-absorbing) film of uniform thickness, and the aluminum substrate is treated as a perfect electrical conductor (i.e., the IR reflection from the metal itself is not modeled separately).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls on Reflectivity Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the opaque assumption** (τ = 0). Many students write $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$ for an opaque metal — this loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing ε with ρ.** Always remember $\varepsilon = \alpha$ (Kirchhoff), and $\alpha = 1 - \rho$ (opaque), so $\varepsilon = 1 - \rho$.
> 3. **Unit errors in power calculations.** Reflectivity is dimensionless, but absorbed/reflected power has units of **Watts** — multiply by $G \cdot A$ always.
> 4. **Not checking energy balance.** Always verify: $Q_{\text{abs}} + Q_{\text{ref}} + Q_{\text{trans}} = G \cdot A$. Examiners award 1 mark for this check.
> 5. **Mixing up spectral and total quantities.** $\rho_\lambda$ is at a single wavelength; $\rho$ is integrated over all wavelengths. The two are *equal only for a gray surface*.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Reflectivity ρ** = fraction of incident radiation reflected; dimensionless; $0 \le \rho \le 1$.
- ✅ **Universal energy balance:** $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$ (always).
- ✅ **Opaque surface:** $\tau = 0 \Rightarrow \alpha + \rho = 1$.
- ✅ **Kirchhoff's Law (gray body):** $\varepsilon = \alpha$.
- ✅ **Combined opaque-gray result:** $\boxed{\varepsilon + \rho = 1}$.
- ✅ **Specular reflection:** smooth surface, $\theta_i = \theta_r$ (mirror-like, polished metals, $\rho \approx 0.95$).
- ✅ **Diffuse reflection:** rough surface, radiation scattered in all directions (matte paints, snow).
- ✅ **Best engineering reflectors:** polished silver (ρ ≈ 0.97), polished aluminum (ρ ≈ 0.95).
- ✅ **Worst engineering reflectors (best absorbers):** matte black lacquer, asphalt (ρ ≈ 0.05–0.10).
- ✅ **Fresnel equation at normal incidence:** $\rho_{\lambda,n} = \left( \dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2} \right)^2$.
- ✅ **Radiation shield principle:** high ρ (low ε) shield inserted between surfaces reduces net radiative heat transfer — direct KTU exam favorite.
- ✅ **Spectral vs. total:** $\rho = 1 - \alpha$ holds *spectral-to-spectral* and *total-to-total*; do not mix.
- ✅ **Key dimensionless property:** Like α and τ, ρ has **no units** — state this explicitly in answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emissive Power and Intensity of Radiation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT501 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Emissive Power and Intensity of Radiation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for Heat Transfer Operations, **Radiation** is defined as energy transfer by electromagnetic waves, which does not require a medium. Two foundational radiation quantities govern the emission behaviour of any real surface:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Emissive Power (E)** — Defined as the *total radiant energy emitted by a surface per unit time per unit area* of the emitting surface, integrated over **all wavelengths** and **all directions** in the **hemisphere** above the surface.
> 
> **SI Units: W/m²** | **Symbol: E** or **E_b** (for a blackbody)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intensity of Radiation (I)** — Defined as the *rate of radiant energy emitted per unit area of the emitting surface, per unit solid angle*, in a specific direction.
> 
> **SI Units: W/(m²·sr)** | **Symbol: I** (also called *Radiance*)

These two quantities are linked through the **solid angle (ω)**, which is a measure of the "spread" of emitted radiation in 3D space. Mathematically, a small solid angle is given by:

$$d\omega = \frac{dA_n}{r^{2}}$$

where $dA_n$ is the projected area normal to the line of sight, and $r$ is the radial distance.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are holding a **glowing LED bulb** in your hand on a dark night. It radiates light (energy) in **all directions** — up, down, sideways, and everywhere.

- **Emissive Power** is like asking: *"How much total light energy does the entire bulb surface pump out every second, summed over every possible direction?"* It is a **bulk quantity** — you don't care which way the light goes, just the grand total per unit area.

- **Intensity of Radiation** is like asking: *"How much light is the bulb surface radiating in **one specific direction** — say, exactly toward my eyes?"* It is a **directional quantity** — you care about the angular spread.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — A Sprinkler vs. a Laser Pointer:**
> - A **garden sprinkler** sprays water in every direction → its "total water output" is high (high **Emissive Power**), but in any single direction, the flow is modest (low **Intensity**).
> - A **laser pointer** sends light only in one tight direction → low total output (low Emissive Power) but extremely high **Intensity** in that direction.
> - A **diffuse grey surface** behaves like the sprinkler; a **specular mirror-like surface** behaves like the laser.

---

### 1.3 Spectral vs Total, Hemispherical vs Directional

KTU examiners frequently test the **matrix of radiation quantities**. Each property can be qualified by **wavelength (spectral)** and **direction (directional/hemispherical)**:

| Qualifier | Prefix | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Spectral (monochromatic) | Subscript $\lambda$ | Per unit wavelength interval $d\lambda$ |
| Total (overall) | No subscript | Integrated over all wavelengths |
| Directional | Subscript $\theta, \phi$ | In a specific direction |
| Hemispherical | No direction subscript | Integrated over the entire hemisphere |

Thus, we have **four key combinations**: $E_{\lambda}$, $E$, $I_{\lambda}$, $I$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Blackbody Emissive Power** is given by the **Stefan–Boltzmann Law**:
> $$E_{b} = \sigma T^{4}, \quad \sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/(m}^{2}\text{·K}^{4}\text{)}$$
> where $\sigma$ is the **Stefan–Boltzmann constant**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Conceptual Breakdown

Let us dissect the relationship between Emissive Power and Intensity logically:

**Step 1 — Start from Intensity.**
Consider a small surface element $dA_1$ emitting radiation in a small cone of solid angle $d\omega$ about a direction $\theta$ (measured from the surface normal). The energy leaving $dA_1$ into this cone per unit time is:
$$d^{3}Q = I \, dA_1 \, \cos\theta \, d\omega$$

Here, $\cos\theta$ appears because the **projected area** facing direction $\theta$ is $dA_1 \cos\theta$. This is the **Lambert's Cosine Law** of emission.

**Step 2 — Integrate over the Hemisphere.**
The total emissive power $E$ is the sum of all energy leaving $dA_1$ in **every direction** above the surface (hemisphere, $\omega = 2\pi$ steradians):
$$E = \int_{\text{hemisphere}} I \cos\theta \, d\omega$$

**Step 3 — Apply Isotropy Assumption.**
For a **diffuse surface** (also called a *Lambertian emitter*), $I$ is **independent of direction**:
$$I(\theta) = I_n = \text{constant}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Integral.**
The solid angle element in spherical coordinates is $d\omega = \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$. Integrating:
$$E = I_n \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi/2} \cos\theta \, \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$

$$E = I_n \cdot 2\pi \cdot \int_{0}^{\pi/2} \cos\theta \, \sin\theta \, d\theta$$

$$E = I_n \cdot 2\pi \cdot \left[ \frac{\sin^{2}\theta}{2} \right]_{0}^{\pi/2}$$

$$E = I_n \cdot 2\pi \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \pi I_n$$

**Step 5 — Final Result.**
$$\boxed{E = \pi I}$$

This is the **single most important relation** for KTU problems on this topic.

---

### 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Furnace Design:** Calculating heat flux from furnace walls requires knowing the *emissive power* integrated over hemisphere.
- **Solar Collectors:** Intensity of solar radiation is the *direct normal irradiance* (DNI), measured in W/(m²·sr).
- **Infrared Thermography:** Cameras measure *intensity* to infer surface temperature.
- **Thermal Comfort:** Radiant heating panels are designed based on directional intensity patterns.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities below are **high-yield for KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments**. Memorize these symbols and units.

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Mathematical Expression | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Spectral Emissive Power | $E_{\lambda}$ | $E_{\lambda} = \dfrac{dE}{d\lambda}$ | W/(m³) |
| 2 | Total Emissive Power | $E$ | $E = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} E_{\lambda} \, d\lambda$ | W/m² |
| 3 | Directional Spectral Intensity | $I_{\lambda,\theta}$ | $\dfrac{dE_{\lambda}}{d\lambda \, d\omega}$ | W/(m³·sr) |
| 4 | Total Intensity (Radiance) | $I$ | $I = \dfrac{dE}{\cos\theta \, d\omega}$ | W/(m²·sr) |
| 5 | Diffuse Surface Relation | $E = \pi I$ | (assumes $I = I_n = $ const) | — |
| 6 | Lambert's Cosine Law | $I_{\theta} = I_n \cos\theta$ | directional intensity falls as $\cos\theta$ | W/(m²·sr) |
| 7 | Blackbody Emissive Power | $E_{b}$ | $E_{b} = \sigma T^{4}$ | W/m² |
| 8 | Emissivity | $\varepsilon$ | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{E}{E_{b}}$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Solid Angle Element | $d\omega$ | $d\omega = \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi = \dfrac{dA_n}{r^{2}}$ | sr (steradian) |
| 10 | Stefan–Boltzmann Constant | $\sigma$ | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ | W/(m²·K⁴) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Check:** $E$ has units of W/m². Since $E = \pi I$, then $I$ must have units of W/(m²·sr), because $\pi$ is dimensionless (radians are dimensionless, steradians are dimensionless). This unit consistency is frequently asked in KTU exams.

---

### 2.4 Why "$\cos\theta$" Appears — The Projected Area Argument

The $\cos\theta$ factor arises because, when you look at a tilted surface from a direction at angle $\theta$ from its normal, you see a **smaller projected area**:

$$\text{Projected Area} = dA \cos\theta$$

This is the same physics that makes the **sunlight intensity drop in winter** — at higher latitudes, sunlight strikes the ground at a steeper angle, so the same beam is spread over a larger area, reducing intensity per unit horizontal area.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of $E = \pi I$ for a Diffuse Surface

We will derive this result exhaustively, with every algebraic transition explicit.

**Setup:**
Consider a horizontal flat surface element $dA_1$ at the origin, with its normal pointing along the $+z$ axis. Radiation leaves $dA_1$ in all upward directions.

**Step 1: Energy radiated into a small cone $d\omega$.**
Let the direction be defined by polar angle $\theta$ (from the $z$-axis) and azimuthal angle $\phi$. The intensity in this direction is $I(\theta, \phi)$. The energy emitted per unit time into the cone $d\omega$ is:
$$d^{2}Q = I(\theta, \phi) \, \cos\theta \, dA_1 \, d\omega$$

The factor $\cos\theta$ accounts for the *projected area* facing direction $(\theta, \phi)$.

**Step 2: Express the solid angle element.**
In spherical coordinates:
$$d\omega = \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$

Substituting:
$$d^{2}Q = I(\theta, \phi) \, \cos\theta \, \sin\theta \, dA_1 \, d\theta \, d\phi$$

**Step 3: Define Emissive Power (energy per unit time per unit area).**
$$E = \frac{d^{2}Q}{dA_1}$$

Therefore:
$$E = \int_{\phi=0}^{2\pi} \int_{\theta=0}^{\pi/2} I(\theta, \phi) \, \cos\theta \, \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$

**Step 4: Apply the Diffuse (Lambertian) Assumption.**
For a diffuse surface, $I(\theta, \phi) = I_n$ is constant. The integral factorizes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= I_n \int_{0}^{2\pi} d\phi \int_{0}^{\pi/2} \cos\theta \, \sin\theta \, d\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Evaluate the $\phi$ integral.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{2\pi} d\phi &= 2\pi
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Evaluate the $\theta$ integral.**
Let $u = \sin\theta$, then $du = \cos\theta \, d\theta$. When $\theta = 0$, $u = 0$; when $\theta = \pi/2$, $u = 1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{\pi/2} \cos\theta \, \sin\theta \, d\theta &= \int_{0}^{1} u \, du \\
&= \left[ \frac{u^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{1} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} - 0 \\
&= \frac{1}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Combine the results.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= I_n \cdot 2\pi \cdot \frac{1}{2} \\
E &= \pi I_n \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Since for a diffuse surface $I = I_n$ in all directions, we finally obtain:

$$\boxed{E = \pi I}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical

**Problem:** A small grey surface of emissivity $\varepsilon = 0.6$ is at temperature $T = 800$ K. It behaves as a diffuse emitter. Calculate:
(a) Its emissive power $E$.
(b) Its intensity of radiation $I$.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Emissive power of a grey diffuse surface:
$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^{4} \\
&= 0.6 \times (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \times (800)^{4} \\
&= 0.6 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 4.096 \times 10^{11} \\
&= 0.6 \times 5.67 \times 40.96 \times 10^{3} \\
&= 0.6 \times 232.24 \times 10^{3} \\
&= 13934.4 \text{ W/m}^{2} \\
E &\approx 13.93 \text{ kW/m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b)** Intensity using $E = \pi I$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{E}{\pi} \\
&= \frac{13934.4}{3.1416} \\
&= 4435.6 \text{ W/(m}^{2}\text{·sr)} \\
I &\approx 4.44 \text{ kW/(m}^{2}\text{·sr)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Verification by Dimensional Analysis

Let us confirm the unit consistency of $E = \pi I$:

- $[E] = \text{W/m}^{2}$
- $[I] = \text{W/(m}^{2}\text{·sr)}$
- Steradian (sr) is a **dimensionless ratio** (area on a unit sphere).
- $\pi$ is dimensionless.
- Therefore: $[\pi I] = \text{W/(m}^{2}\text{·sr)} \cdot \text{sr} = \text{W/m}^{2} = [E]$ ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Linking Emissive Power and Intensity

Below is the conceptual flowchart showing how the radiation quantities are linked, starting from a microscopic elemental emission and aggregating up to macroscopic quantities:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Surface Element dA"] --> B["Direction Selection"]
    B --> C["Solid Angle Cone dω around θ"]
    C --> D["Apply Lambert Cosine Factor cosθ"]
    D --> E["Intensity I is defined here"]
    E --> F["Integrate over phi from 0 to 2π"]
    F --> G["Integrate over theta from 0 to π/2"]
    G --> H{"Surface Diffuse?"}
    H -- Yes --> I["I constant, pulls out of integral"]
    I --> J["Integral evaluates to π"]
    J --> K["Result: E = π · I"]
    H -- No --> L["Keep Iθ inside integral"]
    L --> M["E = ∫∫ Iθ cosθ sinθ dθ dφ"]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Hierarchy Diagram — Spectral vs Directional Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Radiation Quantity"] --> Q1["Spectral Monochromatic"]
    P --> Q2["Total Overall"]
    Q1 --> R1["Directional"]
    Q1 --> R2["Hemispherical"]
    Q2 --> S1["Directional"]
    Q2 --> S2["Hemispherical"]
    R1 --> T1["I_lambda_theta"]
    R2 --> T2["E_lambda"]
    S1 --> T3["I_theta"]
    S2 --> T4["E equals integral over all I cosθ dω"]
```

### 4.3 Solid Angle Geometry Schematic — Textual Block Diagram

```
        z (surface normal)
        |
        |     /|
        |    / |
        |   /  |
        |  / θ |
        | /    |
        |/_____|
        O      x

   Surface dA lies in the x-y plane.
   Radiation is emitted in direction θ from the normal,
   spreading over solid angle dω = sinθ dθ dφ.
```
