```
   Projected area visible from this direction = dA · cosθ.
   Total hemispheric solid angle = 2π steradians.
```

### 4.4 Functional Mapping — Where These Quantities Are Used

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    App1["Furnace Wall Heat Flux"] --> Use1["Need Emissive Power E"]
    App2["Solar Collector Design"] --> Use2["Need Intensity I"]
    App3["IR Camera Imaging"] --> Use3["Need Radiance I"]
    App4["Re-entry Vehicle Shielding"] --> Use4["Need E and I together"]
    App5["Heat Sink Radiation Cooling"] --> Use5["Need E equals pi I for diffuse"]

    Use1 --> Def["E = ∫ I cosθ dω over hemisphere"]
    Use2 --> Def
    Use3 --> Def
    Use4 --> Def
    Use5 --> Def
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the terms: (i) Emissive Power, (ii) Intensity of Radiation. State the relation between them for a diffuse surface.**

**Model Answer:**

**Emissive Power (E):** It is defined as the total amount of radiation energy emitted per unit time per unit area from the surface, integrated over all wavelengths and over the entire hemisphere above the surface.

$$E = \int_{0}^{\infty} E_{\lambda} \, d\lambda \quad \text{Units: W/m}^{2}$$

**Intensity of Radiation (I):** It is defined as the rate of radiation energy emitted per unit area of the emitting surface per unit solid angle in a given direction. Also called *radiance*.

$$I = \frac{d^{2}Q}{dA \, \cos\theta \, d\omega} \quad \text{Units: W/(m}^{2}\text{·sr)}$$

**Relation for Diffuse Surface:** For a Lambertian (diffuse) emitter, the intensity is independent of direction, and the relation is:

$$\boxed{E = \pi I}$$

---

#### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain Lambert's Cosine Law. Why does the $\cos\theta$ term appear in the relation for emissive power?**

**Model Answer:**

**Lambert's Cosine Law** states that the intensity of radiation leaving a diffuse surface is proportional to the cosine of the angle between the direction of radiation and the surface normal:

$$I_{\theta} = I_{n} \cos\theta$$

The $\cos\theta$ term appears because, when radiation is observed at angle $\theta$ from the normal, only the **projected component of the surface area** $dA \cos\theta$ is "visible" to that direction. Therefore, the energy radiated into a solid angle $d\omega$ in direction $\theta$ is reduced by a factor of $\cos\theta$ compared to radiation in the normal direction.

**Physical Reasoning:** A surface element tilted at angle $\theta$ presents a smaller effective area to that direction, so it emits less energy into that direction. At $\theta = 0$ (normal direction), emission is maximum. At $\theta = 90°$ (grazing), emission becomes zero because the projected area vanishes.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU ESE 2024, Module 3 questions typically appear as a single Part B question worth 14 marks with internal choice between Question A and Question B.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the relation $E = \pi I$ for a diffuse (Lambertian) grey surface. Explain each step clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A small heating element is at $T = 1200$ K with emissivity $\varepsilon = 0.8$. It radiates as a diffuse surface into a hemisphere. Determine:
  (i) Total emissive power $E$.
  (ii) Intensity of radiation $I$.
  (iii) Spectral emissive power at $\lambda = 2 \, \mu$m using Wien's displacement approximation if peak wavelength is $2.898 \, \mu$m·K. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

Refer to **Section 3.1** for the exhaustive derivation. Key steps:

1. Consider elemental area $dA$ emitting into solid angle $d\omega$ at angle $\theta$.
2. Energy emitted: $d^{2}Q = I \cos\theta \, dA \, d\omega$.
3. Divide by $dA$: $dE = I \cos\theta \, d\omega$.
4. Express $d\omega = \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$.
5. For diffuse surface, $I = I_n$ = constant.
6. Integrate over hemisphere:
   $$E = I_n \int_{0}^{2\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi/2} \cos\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi = \pi I_n$$
7. Hence $\boxed{E = \pi I}$.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Emissive Power:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^{4} \\
&= 0.8 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1200)^{4} \\
&= 0.8 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.0736 \times 10^{12} \\
&= 0.8 \times 117574.2 \\
E &= 94059.4 \text{ W/m}^{2} \approx 94.06 \text{ kW/m}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Intensity:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{E}{\pi} = \frac{94059.4}{3.1416} \\
I &\approx 29940.4 \text{ W/(m}^{2}\text{·sr)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Spectral Emissive Power at $\lambda = 2 \, \mu$m:**
First, compute $\lambda T = 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 1200 = 2.4 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K.

For a blackbody, using the Wien approximation of Planck's law:
$$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{2\pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \exp\left(-\frac{hc}{\lambda k T}\right)$$

Constants: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s, $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.

Compute $\dfrac{hc}{\lambda k T}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{hc}{\lambda k T} &= \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \times 1200} \\
&= \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{3.3144 \times 10^{-26}} \\
&= 5.997 \\
&\approx 6.0
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $\dfrac{2\pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}}$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
2\pi h c^{2} &= 2\pi \times 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 9 \times 10^{16} \\
&= 2\pi \times 5.9634 \times 10^{-17} \\
&= 3.7470 \times 10^{-16} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda^{5} &= (2 \times 10^{-6})^{5} = 32 \times 10^{-30} = 3.2 \times 10^{-29} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{2\pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} &= \frac{3.7470 \times 10^{-16}}{3.2 \times 10^{-29}} = 1.171 \times 10^{13}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\exp(-6.0) &= 0.002479 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{b\lambda} &= 1.171 \times 10^{13} \times 0.002479 \\
&= 2.903 \times 10^{10} \text{ W/(m}^{3}\text{)} \\
E_{b\lambda} &\approx 2.90 \times 10^{10} \text{ W/(m}^{3}\text{)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

For grey surface:
$$E_{\lambda} = \varepsilon \, E_{b\lambda} = 0.8 \times 2.90 \times 10^{10} = 2.32 \times 10^{10} \text{ W/m}^{3}$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper — Alternative]`

**(a)** Define the following terms with units and physical significance:
  (i) Spectral emissive power
  (ii) Hemispherical emissive power
  (iii) Directional emissive power
  (iv) Solid angle **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A diffuse grey surface of area $0.05 \text{ m}^{2}$ is maintained at $1500$ K with emissivity $0.75$. Calculate:
  (i) Total radiation emitted per second.
  (ii) The intensity of radiation.
  (iii) The fraction of total radiation emitted in the angular range $\theta = 0$ to $\theta = 30°$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**(i) Spectral (Monochromatic) Emissive Power $E_{\lambda}$:** Rate of radiation energy emitted per unit area per unit wavelength interval at a particular wavelength $\lambda$.
$$E_{\lambda} = \frac{dE}{d\lambda} \quad \text{Units: W/m}^{3}$$

**(ii) Hemispherical Emissive Power $E$:** Total radiation energy emitted per unit area per unit time, integrated over all wavelengths and over the entire hemisphere above the surface.
$$E = \int_{0}^{\infty} E_{\lambda} \, d\lambda \quad \text{Units: W/m}^{2}$$

**(iii) Directional Emissive Power $E_{\theta}$:** Radiation emitted per unit area per unit time in a particular direction $\theta$ from the normal.
$$E_{\theta} = \frac{dE}{d\omega} \quad \text{Units: W/(m}^{2}\text{·sr)}$$

**(iv) Solid Angle $\omega$:** The two-dimensional angle subtended by a 3D cone, equal to the area on a unit sphere enclosed by the cone.
$$d\omega = \frac{dA_{n}}{r^{2}} \quad \text{Units: steradian (sr)}$$

Total solid angle of a hemisphere = $2\pi$ sr.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Total Radiation Emitted Per Second:**

Total energy rate $Q = E \times A = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4} A$.
$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 0.75 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1500)^{4} \\
&= 0.75 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 5.0625 \times 10^{12} \\
&= 0.75 \times 287040.4 \\
&= 215280.3 \text{ W/m}^{2} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= E \times A = 215280.3 \times 0.05 \\
Q &= 10764.0 \text{ W} \approx 10.76 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Intensity of Radiation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{E}{\pi} = \frac{215280.3}{3.1416} \\
I &\approx 68524.6 \text{ W/(m}^{2}\text{·sr)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Fraction in $\theta = 0°$ to $\theta = 30°$:**

For a diffuse surface, fraction of energy emitted within a cone of half-angle $\theta_0$ from the normal is:
$$F = \sin^{2}\theta_0$$

Derivation: The fraction is the ratio of energy emitted into the cone to total hemispheric energy.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \frac{\int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\theta_0} I_n \cos\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi}{\int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi/2} I_n \cos\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi} \\
&= \frac{\sin^{2}\theta_0 - \sin^{2}(0)}{\sin^{2}(\pi/2) - \sin^{2}(0)} \\
&= \sin^{2}\theta_0
\end{aligned}
$$

For $\theta_0 = 30°$:
$$F = \sin^{2}(30°) = (0.5)^{2} = 0.25$$

So **25%** of the total radiation is emitted within the $0°$ to $30°$ cone — a KTU examiner favourite result!

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Emissive Power & Intensity Problems:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting units of intensity.** Students frequently write $I$ in W/m² instead of W/(m²·sr). Always include **steradians**!
> 
> 2. **Confusing Emissive Power with Irradiation.** $E$ is *emitted*, $G$ is *received*. They are not the same unless the surface is a blackbody in equilibrium with its surroundings.
> 
> 3. **Applying $E = \pi I$ to non-diffuse surfaces.** This relation is **strictly for diffuse (Lambertian) emitters only**. For directional emitters, you must keep $I(\theta)$ inside the integral.
> 
> 4. **Mixing up Stefan–Boltzmann Law ($E = \sigma T^4$) with Newton's Law of Cooling.** Stefan–Boltzmann is for radiation, requires $T$ in Kelvin, and gives energy emitted (not net).
> 
> 5. **Dropping the emissivity factor.** For a *grey* surface, always multiply by $\varepsilon$. A grey surface has constant $\varepsilon(\lambda)$ across wavelengths.
> 
> 6. **Solid angle hemisphere value.** A full sphere is $4\pi$ sr; a hemisphere is $2\pi$ sr. Don't confuse the two in integration limits.
> 
> 7. **Cosine Law trap.** When the surface is tilted, the *projected area* facing a fixed direction changes. Always draw a clear free-body-style diagram with $\theta$ measured from the *normal*.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Emissive Power** $E$ = total energy emitted per unit area per unit time over the entire hemisphere (W/m²).
- ✅ **Intensity (Radiance)** $I$ = energy emitted per unit area per unit time per unit solid angle in a given direction (W/(m²·sr)).
- ✅ **Diffuse surface relation:** $E = \pi I$ — *the single most important formula on this topic*.
- ✅ **Lambert's Cosine Law:** $I_\theta = I_n \cos\theta$ for diffuse emitters.
- ✅ **Blackbody:** $E_b = \sigma T^4$, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴).
- ✅ **Grey surface:** $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$, where $\varepsilon$ is constant across wavelengths.
- ✅ **Solid angle of a full sphere:** $4\pi$ sr. **Hemisphere:** $2\pi$ sr.
- ✅ **Solid angle element in spherical coordinates:** $d\omega = \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$.
- ✅ **Spectral vs Total:** $E = \int_0^\infty E_\lambda \, d\lambda$.
- ✅ **Fraction of radiation within cone half-angle $\theta_0$:** $F = \sin^2\theta_0$ (for diffuse surfaces).
- ✅ **Units check trick:** If your intensity has no "per steradian," you have lost a mark.
- ✅ **Spectral Intensity $I_\lambda$** is to $I$ what $E_\lambda$ is to $E$ — per unit wavelength.
- ✅ **Always** quote $T$ in Kelvin in any $\sigma T^4$ calculation.
- ✅ **Diffuse = Lambertian = isotropic intensity** — three names, same meaning.
- ✅ **Irradiation $G \neq$ Emissive Power $E$** in general — the former is incident, the latter is emitted.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Black Body Radiation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT501 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Black Body Radiation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Black Body Radiation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Black Body (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A **black body** is an idealized physical body that absorbs **all incident electromagnetic radiation**, regardless of frequency or angle of incidence. It is a perfect absorber (absorptivity $\alpha = 1$) and, when in thermal equilibrium, also a perfect emitter (emissivity $\varepsilon = 1$). The thermal radiation emitted by such a body is termed **Black Body Radiation**.

The term "black" refers to the body's property of absorbing all wavelengths — not its visual color. At equilibrium temperature, a black body emits radiation whose spectral distribution is determined solely by its absolute temperature, following well-defined physical laws.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Cavity with a Pinhole"**
> Imagine a hollow metal sphere coated black on the inside, with a tiny pinhole. Any light ray entering the pinhole bounces around inside the cavity and is essentially never reflected back out — it gets absorbed by the rough black interior. From outside, the pinhole appears perfectly black, no matter what light shines on it. Conversely, when this cavity is heated to a uniform temperature, the radiation escaping through the pinhole is the purest, most intense thermal radiation possible. This is essentially a **laboratory-grade black body**.

Think of a black body as a **"perfect thermodynamic sponge"** for radiation:
- It soaks up *every* photon that hits it.
- When hot, it radiates *the maximum possible energy* at every wavelength.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

| Symbol | Constant | Value | Unit |
|--------|----------|-------|------|
| $\sigma$ | Stefan-Boltzmann Constant | $5.670 \times 10^{-8}$ | $\text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ |
| $h$ | Planck's Constant | $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ | $\text{J}\cdot\text{s}$ |
| $k_B$ | Boltzmann Constant | $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ | $\text{J/K}$ |
| $c$ | Speed of Light | $2.998 \times 10^{8}$ | $\text{m/s}$ |
| $c_1$ | First Radiation Constant | $3.743 \times 10^{-16}$ | $\text{W}\cdot\text{m}^2$ |
| $c_2$ | Second Radiation Constant | $1.439 \times 10^{-2}$ | $\text{m}\cdot\text{K}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The constants $c_1 = 2\pi h c^2$ and $c_2 = \frac{hc}{k_B}$ are extensively used in Planck's distribution problems. Memorize their numerical values — they appear in nearly every numerical question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Laws of Black Body Radiation

### A. Planck's Law (Spectral Distribution)

Planck's law describes the spectral emissive power of a black body as a function of wavelength and temperature. It is the **foundational equation** of black body radiation.

**Key Logic Steps:**
1. Radiation is emitted in discrete quanta (photons) of energy $E = h\nu$.
2. The cavity contains standing electromagnetic waves (modes).
3. The average energy per mode follows Bose-Einstein statistics.
4. The spectral distribution yields the wavelength-dependent emissive power.

### B. Stefan-Boltzmann Law (Total Emissive Power)

The total energy radiated per unit area per unit time by a black body is proportional to the **fourth power** of its absolute temperature.

$$E_b = \sigma T^4$$

### C. Wien's Displacement Law

The wavelength at which the spectral emissive power is maximum is inversely proportional to the absolute temperature.

$$\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}\cdot\text{K}$$

### D. Kirchhoff's Law

At thermal equilibrium, the emissivity of a body equals its absorptivity for each wavelength:

$$\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$$

This establishes the link between a material's ability to **absorb** and **emit** radiation.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Law / Concept | Formula | Description | Units |
|---------------|---------|-------------|-------|
| Planck's Spectral Emissive Power | $E_{b\lambda} = \frac{c_1 \lambda^{-5}}{e^{c_2/\lambda T} - 1}$ | Spectral radiation per unit wavelength | $\text{W/m}^3$ |
| Planck's Frequency Form | $E_{b\nu} = \frac{2\pi h \nu^3}{c^2} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{h\nu/k_BT} - 1}$ | Spectral radiation per unit frequency | $\text{W/m}^2\text{Hz}$ |
| Stefan-Boltzmann Law | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | Total hemispherical emissive power | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| Wien's Displacement Law | $\lambda_{\max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ | Peak wavelength relation | $\text{m}\cdot\text{K}$ |
| Emissivity Definition | $\varepsilon = \frac{E}{E_b}$ | Ratio of actual to black body emission | Dimensionless |
| Kirchhoff's Law | $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$ | Equilibrium emission-absorption relation | Dimensionless |
| Irradiation from Black Body | $G = \sigma T^4$ | Incoming radiation flux | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| Net Heat Exchange (Two Surfaces) | $q_{12} = \sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ | Net radiation between black bodies | $\text{W/m}^2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL — Markdown Table Rule:** All absolute value bars in formulas have been written using vertical-line characters outside the table's structural pipes. When writing $E_b = \sigma T^4$, do not write $\vert T \vert^4$ inside a table — it breaks the markdown parser. Use the LaTeX-form $T^4$ directly.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Furnace Design:** Calculation of maximum possible heat transfer from combustion gases to furnace walls.
- **Solar Energy Engineering:** The sun (~5800 K) is approximated as a black body; solar collectors are designed using black body spectrum.
- **Thermal Imaging / IR Cameras:** Wien's law determines the optimal detector wavelength range.
- **Aerospace (Re-entry Vehicles):** High-temperature surface heating during re-entry requires black body radiation estimates.
- **Nuclear Reactor Cooling:** Estimation of thermal radiation losses from hot reactor surfaces.
- **Glass Furnaces & Incandescent Lamps:** Filament temperatures and radiative heat fluxes use Stefan-Boltzmann calculations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Wien's Displacement Law from Planck's Law

**Objective:** Find the wavelength $\lambda_{\max}$ at which $E_{b\lambda}$ is maximum.

**Step 1 — Write Planck's spectral distribution:**

$$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{c_1}{\lambda^5} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{c_2/\lambda T} - 1}$$

**Step 2 — Take the derivative with respect to $\lambda$ and set it to zero:**

$$\frac{dE_{b\lambda}}{d\lambda} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate using quotient rule:**

Let $f(\lambda) = \frac{1}{\lambda^5 (e^{c_2/\lambda T} - 1)}$.

Then:

$$\frac{dE_{b\lambda}}{d\lambda} = c_1 \cdot \frac{d}{d\lambda}\left[\frac{1}{\lambda^5 (e^{c_2/\lambda T} - 1)}\right] = 0$$

**Step 4 — Apply the differentiation:**

Using $\frac{d}{d\lambda}\left[\frac{u}{v}\right] = \frac{u'v - uv'}{v^2}$ where $u = 1$ and $v = \lambda^5(e^{c_2/\lambda T} - 1)$:

$$0 = -\left[5\lambda^4(e^{c_2/\lambda T} - 1) + \lambda^5 \cdot e^{c_2/\lambda T} \cdot \left(-\frac{c_2}{\lambda^2 T}\right)\right]$$

**Step 5 — Simplify by dividing by $-\lambda^4$:**

$$0 = 5(e^{c_2/\lambda T} - 1) - \frac{c_2}{\lambda T} e^{c_2/\lambda T}$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to isolate the exponential:**

$$5e^{c_2/\lambda T} - 5 - \frac{c_2}{\lambda T} e^{c_2/\lambda T} = 0$$

$$e^{c_2/\lambda T}\left(5 - \frac{c_2}{\lambda T}\right) = 5$$

**Step 7 — Define the variable $x = \frac{c_2}{\lambda_{\max} T}$:**

$$e^x(5 - x) = 5$$

**Step 8 — Solve the transcendental equation numerically:**

The solution is $x \approx 4.9651$.

**Step 9 — Substitute back:**

$$\frac{c_2}{\lambda_{\max} T} = 4.9651 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_{\max} T = \frac{c_2}{4.9651} = \frac{1.4388 \times 10^{-2}}{4.9651}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{\max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}\cdot\text{K}}$$

This is **Wien's Displacement Law**.

## 3.2 Derivation of Stefan-Boltzmann Law by Integrating Planck's Law

**Objective:** Integrate $E_{b\lambda}$ over all wavelengths from $0$ to $\infty$ to obtain $E_b$.

**Step 1 — Set up the integration:**

$$E_b = \int_0^\infty E_{b\lambda} \, d\lambda = \int_0^\infty \frac{c_1 \lambda^{-5}}{e^{c_2/\lambda T} - 1} \, d\lambda$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $x = \frac{c_2}{\lambda T}$, so $dx = -\frac{c_2}{\lambda^2 T} d\lambda$, and $d\lambda = -\frac{c_2}{x^2 T} dx$:**

$$E_b = \int_\infty^0 \frac{c_1 (xT/c_2)^5}{e^x - 1} \cdot \left(-\frac{c_2}{x^2 T}\right) dx$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$E_b = \frac{c_1 T^5}{c_2^5} \cdot \frac{c_2}{T} \int_0^\infty \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} dx$$

$$E_b = \frac{c_1 T^4}{c_2^4} \int_0^\infty \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} dx$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral using the standard result:**

$$\int_0^\infty \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} dx = \frac{\pi^4}{15} \approx 6.4939$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$E_b = \frac{3.743 \times 10^{-16}}{(1.4388 \times 10^{-2})^4} \cdot \frac{\pi^4}{15} \cdot T^4$$

$$E_b = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \cdot T^4$$

**Step 6 — Define the Stefan-Boltzmann constant:**

$$\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$$

$$\boxed{E_b = \sigma T^4}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Black Body | Real (Gray) Body |
|----------|------------|------------------|
| Absorptivity $\alpha$ | $= 1$ | $< 1$ |
| Emissivity $\varepsilon$ | $= 1$ | $< 1$ |
| Spectral Distribution | Planckian | Planckian scaled by $\varepsilon$ |
| Reflectivity $\rho$ | $= 0$ | $> 0$ |
| Transmissivity $\tau$ | $= 0$ | $\geq 0$ |
| Color Appearance | "Perfectly black" | Material-dependent |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Radiation Laws Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Planck's Law"] --> nodeB["Spectral Distribution E_b lambda"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Integrate over all wavelengths"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Differentiate and set to zero"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["Stefan-Boltzmann Law E_b = sigma T4"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Wien's Displacement Law"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Peak Wavelength Relation"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["Total Hemispherical Emissive Power"]
    nodeA --> nodeI["Kirchhoff's Law"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Equilibrium: emissivity equals absorptivity"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Real Surface Radiation Analysis"]
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL["Engineering Design Applications"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of Black Body Radiation Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["Input: Temperature T and wavelength lambda"] --> stepB["Apply Planck Spectral Formula"]
    stepB --> stepC["Output: Spectral Emissive Power E_b lambda"]
    stepC --> stepD["Decision: Maximum or Total Energy"]
    stepD --> stepE["Apply Wien's Displacement Law"]
    stepD --> stepF["Integrate Planck over all wavelengths"]
    stepE --> stepG["Find Peak Wavelength"]
    stepF --> stepH["Compute Total Emissive Power"]
    stepH --> stepI["Use Stefan-Boltzmann E_b = sigma T4"]
    stepG --> stepJ["Final Output: Radiation Parameters"]
    stepI --> stepJ
```

## 4.3 Schematic Description: Cavity Black Body (Text Representation)

```
        +-------------------+
        |    WALL (HOT)     |
        |   T = constant    |
        +-------------------+
        |                   |
        |    |       |      |
        |    |       |      |   <-- Inner surface absorbs and re-emits
        |    |       |      |
        |    +-------+      |
        |         \         |   <-- Pinhole exit
        +----------\--------+
                    \
                  (ε=1, α=1)
                  Black Body Radiation
                  escapes here
```

**Working Principle:**
1. The cavity walls are held at uniform temperature $T$.
2. Radiation entering the pinhole undergoes multiple internal reflections.
3. With each reflection, the radiation intensity diminishes due to wall absorption.
4. After many bounces, essentially no radiation escapes back out — the pinhole is a perfect absorber.
5. When heated, the cavity emits thermal radiation through the pinhole with **black body spectral characteristics**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "design a laboratory black body," describe the **isothermal cavity with a small aperture**. This is the most common 3-mark question on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a black body. Why is it considered a perfect absorber and emitter?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **black body** is an idealized surface that absorbs all incident radiation at all wavelengths, directions, and temperatures, irrespective of the spectral distribution of incoming radiation.

- **Perfect Absorber:** Its **absorptivity $\alpha = 1$** for all wavelengths, meaning no radiation is reflected or transmitted.
- **Perfect Emitter:** By Kirchhoff's law, since $\alpha = 1$, its **emissivity $\varepsilon = 1$**, meaning it radiates the maximum possible energy at every wavelength for a given temperature.

A practical approximation is an **isothermal cavity with a small pinhole** — radiation entering the pinhole undergoes multiple internal reflections and is almost entirely absorbed. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and explain Wien's Displacement Law. Mention its significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Wien's Displacement Law** states that the wavelength at which the spectral emissive power of a black body is maximum is inversely proportional to its absolute temperature:

$$\lambda_{\max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}\cdot\text{K}$$

**Significance:**
- It enables determination of the surface temperature of a star or hot body from its spectral peak.
- In **thermal imaging**, the optimal detector wavelength is chosen based on this law.
- As temperature rises, the peak shifts to **shorter wavelengths** (e.g., red-hot to white-hot). **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Derive the Stefan-Boltzmann law starting from Planck's spectral distribution. (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Planck's Spectral Distribution:**

$$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{c_1 \lambda^{-5}}{e^{c_2/\lambda T} - 1}$$

**Step 2 — Total emissive power is the integral over all wavelengths:**

$$E_b = \int_0^\infty E_{b\lambda} \, d\lambda = \int_0^\infty \frac{c_1 \lambda^{-5}}{e^{c_2/\lambda T} - 1} \, d\lambda$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $x = \frac{c_2}{\lambda T}$:**

$$E_b = \frac{c_1 T^4}{c_2^4} \int_0^\infty \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} dx$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the standard integral:**

$$\int_0^\infty \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} dx = \frac{\pi^4}{15}$$

**Step 5 — Final form:**

$$E_b = \sigma T^4 \quad \text{where} \quad \sigma = \frac{2\pi^5 k_B^4}{15 c^2 h^3} = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$$

This is the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) The sun has a surface temperature of 5780 K. Treating it as a black body, calculate: (i) the total emissive power, (ii) the wavelength at which emission is maximum, and (iii) the spectral emissive power at $\lambda = 500$ nm. (7 marks)**

**Given:** $T = 5780 \text{ K}$, $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$, $c_1 = 3.743 \times 10^{-16} \text{ W}\cdot\text{m}^2$, $c_2 = 1.439 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}\cdot\text{K}$.

**(i) Total Emissive Power:**

$$E_b = \sigma T^4 = (5.670 \times 10^{-8})(5780)^4$$

$$E_b = (5.670 \times 10^{-8})(1.117 \times 10^{15})$$

$$\boxed{E_b = 6.33 \times 10^{7} \text{ W/m}^2}$$

**(ii) Peak Wavelength (Wien's Law):**

$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{5780} = 5.014 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{\max} \approx 501 \text{ nm}}$$

**(iii) Spectral Emissive Power at 500 nm:**

$$\lambda = 500 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}, \quad \frac{c_2}{\lambda T} = \frac{1.439 \times 10^{-2}}{(5 \times 10^{-7})(5780)} = 4.979$$

$$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{(3.743 \times 10^{-16})}{(5 \times 10^{-7})^5 (e^{4.979} - 1)}$$

$$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{3.743 \times 10^{-16}}{3.125 \times 10^{-33} \cdot (144.9 - 1)}$$

$$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{3.743 \times 10^{-16}}{4.497 \times 10^{-31}}$$

$$\boxed{E_{b\lambda} = 8.32 \times 10^{14} \text{ W/m}^3}$$

**(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) State and prove Kirchhoff's law. Discuss its physical significance in radiation heat transfer. (7 marks)**

**Statement:** At thermal equilibrium, the ratio of emissive power to absorptivity for any given wavelength and direction is the same for all bodies, and equals the emissive power of a black body at that temperature.

**Proof (Cavity Argument):**

Consider two large parallel surfaces inside an adiabatic enclosure at temperature $T$. Let surface 1 be any real surface and surface 2 be a black body. At equilibrium, the net heat exchange between them must be zero.

$$E_1 + \alpha_1 G - G = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_1 = G(1 - \alpha_1)$$

For the black body (surface 2):

$$E_b + (1)G_b - G_b = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_b = G_b$$

By symmetry of radiation fields in the enclosure, $G = G_b$. Therefore:

$$\frac{E_1}{\alpha_1} = E_b$$

Generalizing across all surfaces:

$$\boxed{\frac{E_1}{\alpha_1} = \frac{E_2}{\alpha_2} = \cdots = E_b \quad \Rightarrow \quad \varepsilon = \alpha}$$

**Physical Significance:**
- A good absorber is a good emitter.
- A polished (reflective) surface is a poor emitter.
- Forms the basis for selecting **low-emissivity coatings** in thermal insulation. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) A furnace wall at 1500 K is constructed of a refractory material with emissivity 0.75. The surroundings are at 500 K, and the effective surface area is 8 m². Calculate the net radiative heat loss from the furnace wall. (7 marks)**

**Given:** $T_1 = 1500 \text{ K}$, $T_2 = 500 \text{ K}$, $\varepsilon_1 = 0.75$, $A = 8 \text{ m}^2$, $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$.

**Assumption:** Treat surroundings as a black body ($\varepsilon_2 = 1$).

**Step 1 — Net heat transfer formula for a small body in a large enclosure:**

$$Q_{net} = A \varepsilon_1 \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$$

**Step 2 — Compute $T_1^4$ and $T_2^4$:**

$$T_1^4 = (1500)^4 = 5.0625 \times 10^{12}$$

$$T_2^4 = (500)^4 = 6.25 \times 10^{10}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$Q_{net} = 8 \times 0.75 \times (5.670 \times 10^{-8}) \times (5.0625 \times 10^{12} - 6.25 \times 10^{10})$$

$$Q_{net} = 6 \times 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \times 5.0 \times 10^{12}$$

$$Q_{net} = 6 \times 5.670 \times 5.0 \times 10^{4}$$

$$Q_{net} = 6 \times 28.35 \times 10^{4}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{net} = 1.701 \times 10^{6} \text{ W} = 1701 \text{ kW}}$$

**(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the emissivity factor:** Many students write $Q = \sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4) \cdot A$ and lose **3 marks** when the surface is non-black. Always multiply by $\varepsilon$.
> 2. **Unit errors in spectral emissive power:** $E_{b\lambda}$ has units of $\text{W/m}^3$ (per unit wavelength per unit area), not $\text{W/m}^2$. Examiners deduct marks for this.
> 3. **Confusing $\lambda_{\max}$ with the wavelength of maximum spectral radiance from the sun:** Some students use 500 nm blindly. Always apply Wien's law correctly.
> 4. **Mixing up the Stefan-Boltzmann constant:** Use $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}$ in SI units. Do not use cgs.
> 5. **Sign convention in net heat transfer:** Heat *flowing out* of a hot body is positive. Always state the direction explicitly.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Black Body:** Perfect absorber ($\alpha = 1$) and perfect emitter ($\varepsilon = 1$). A practical model is an isothermal cavity with a pinhole.
- **Planck's Law:** $E_{b\lambda} = \frac{c_1 \lambda^{-5}}{e^{c_2/\lambda T} - 1}$ — the most fundamental equation. Constants: $c_1 = 3.743 \times 10^{-16}$, $c_2 = 1.439 \times 10^{-2}$.
- **Stefan-Boltzmann Law:** $E_b = \sigma T^4$ where $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$.
- **Wien's Displacement Law:** $\lambda_{\max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}\cdot\text{K}$. Numerical problems require $\lambda_{\max}$ to be calculated via this constant.
- **Kirchhoff's Law:** $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$ at thermal equilibrium — a body that absorbs strongly emits strongly.
- **Emissivity:** $\varepsilon = E/E_b$ — dimensionless, between 0 and 1.
- **Net Radiation for a Gray Body in a Black Enclosure:** $Q = A \varepsilon \sigma (T_{\text{body}}^4 - T_{\text{env}}^4)$.
- **Spectral Units:** Watch out — $E_{b\lambda}$ is per unit wavelength, units are $\text{W/m}^3$.
- **Common KTU numerical problems:** Peak wavelength from temperature, total emissive power from temperature, spectral emissive power at a given wavelength, net heat loss from a furnace.
- **Engineering applications:** Furnace design, solar collectors, IR thermal imaging, incandescent lamp filaments, re-entry vehicle thermal analysis.
- **Remember the constants:** Memorize $c_1$, $c_2$, $\sigma$, and $h$, $k_B$, $c$ — they appear in nearly every numerical question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gray Body Radiation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBBT501 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Gray Body Radiation** ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition A **Gray Body** is defined as a surface whose **monochromatic emissivity** $\varepsilon_\lambda$ is **constant and independent of wavelength** across the entire spectrum of thermal radiation. In other words, the ratio of the spectral emissive power of a real surface to the spectral emissive power of a blackbody at the same wavelength and temperature remains uniform for all wavelengths: $$\varepsilon_\lambda = \varepsilon = \text{constant} \quad \text{for all } \lambda \in (0, \infty)$$ A gray body is therefore characterized by a single, wavelength-independent value of **emissivity** $\varepsilon$, where $0 < \varepsilon < 1$. The total emissive power of a gray body is simply the blackbody emissive power scaled by this constant: $$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A "Gray Body" is an *idealization* used in engineering. No real surface is perfectly gray, but many engineering materials (oxidized metals, paints, ceramics) approximate gray body behavior closely enough for accurate design calculations. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **perfectly black sponge** (blackbody) that absorbs **100%** of every drop of colored ink that falls on it — red, blue, green, yellow, all of it. Now imagine a **light gray sponge** (gray body) that absorbs the same *fraction* of every color of ink, say 70% of each. The key point is that the absorption fraction (emissivity) is the *same* for every wavelength (color) of incident radiation. This is unlike a **real colored surface** (say a red cloth), which absorbs 90% of red light but only 10% of blue light — its emissivity changes with wavelength. - **Emissivity** $\varepsilon$ : Fraction of blackbody emission that the surface actually radiates. Dimensionless, between **0 and 1**. - **Stefan–Boltzmann constant** $\sigma$ : $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$. - **Absorptivity** $\alpha$ : Fraction of incident radiation absorbed. For a gray body, $\alpha = \varepsilon$ at every wavelength. > [!NOTE] > **Kirchhoff's Law (Recap for Gray Bodies):** For a body in thermal equilibrium with its surroundings, the emissivity equals its absorptivity. For a *gray* body, this means $\alpha = \varepsilon = \text{constant}$ for the entire spectrum. This single property makes the gray body the *workhorse model* for almost all real engineering radiation problems. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why We Need the Gray Body Model The exact radiation exchange between two real surfaces requires integrating spectral properties over the entire wavelength range — a calculation that is mathematically intractable for practical engineering. The **gray body approximation** simplifies this by: 1. Replacing wavelength-dependent emissivity $\varepsilon_\lambda$ with a single constant $\varepsilon$. 2. Allowing use of the **view factor algebra** with closed-form solutions. 3. Enabling the **radiation network analogy** (electrical resistance network) for arbitrarily complex enclosures. ### 2.2 Radiation Heat Transfer Between Two Gray Surfaces (Two-Body Enclosure) For two diffuse, gray, opaque surfaces forming a closed enclosure, the net rate of radiation heat transfer from surface 1 to surface 2 is: $$Q_{1 \to 2} = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} + \dfrac{1}{A_1 F_{1\to 2}} + \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2}}$$ This is the **fundamental two-surface radiation formula** for gray bodies. ### 2.3 View Factor (Shape Factor) Fundamentals - **Definition:** $F_{i \to j}$ is the fraction of total radiation leaving surface $i$ that strikes surface $j$ directly. - **Summation Rule:** $\sum_{j=1}^{N} F_{i \to j} = 1$ - **Reciprocity Rule:** $A_i F_{i \to j} = A_j F_{j \to i}$ - **For two-surface enclosure:** $F_{1 \to 2} = F_{2 \to 1} = 1$ (if all radiation leaving one surface strikes the other) ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---------|---------|----------------| | 1 | Gray body total emissive power | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | W/m² | | 2 | Emissivity–Absorptivity (gray) | $\alpha = \varepsilon$ | Dimensionless | | 3 | Two gray surface net heat transfer | $Q_{1\to 2} = \dfrac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} + \dfrac{1}{A_1 F_{1\to 2}} + \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2}}$ | Watts | | 4 | Equivalent surface resistance (i) | $R_i = \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_i}{\varepsilon_i A_i}$ | m⁻² | | 5 | Geometric (space) resistance | $R_{1\to 2} = \dfrac{1}{A_1 F_{1\to 2}}$ | m⁻² | | 6 | View factor reciprocity | $A_i F_{i\to j} = A_j F_{j\to i}$ | m² | | 7 | Blackbody net exchange (ref) | $Q_{1\to 2, \text{blk}} = A_1 F_{1\to 2} \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ | Watts | | 8 | Radiation shield factor | $Q_{\text{with shield}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{without}}}{1 + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_s}\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2}\right)^{-1}}$ | Watts | | 9 | N identical parallel shields | $Q_N = \dfrac{Q_0}{1 + N\left(\dfrac{2}{\varepsilon} - 1\right)}$ (for $\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2 = \varepsilon_s = \varepsilon$) | Watts | | 10 | Stefan–Boltzmann constant | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ | W/m²K⁴ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** Gray body radiation analysis is critical in the design of **furnaces, boilers, solar collectors, spacecraft thermal control, nuclear reactor shields, and high-temperature process equipment**. The radiation shield principle (formula 8) is used to drastically cut heat loss from insulated pipes carrying hot fluids. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Two-Gray-Surface Radiation Formula **Starting Point:** Consider two diffuse, gray, opaque surfaces (1 and 2) forming a closed two-surface enclosure, with $F_{1\to 2} = 1$. **Step 1 — Define radiosity and irradiation.** - **Radiosity** $J_i$ = total radiation leaving surface $i$ per unit area = $E_i + \rho_i G_i$ (emitted + reflected). - **Irradiation** $G_i$ = total radiation incident on surface $i$ per unit area. - For opaque gray body: $\rho_i = 1 - \varepsilon_i$ (reflectivity). - For diffuse, gray emitter: $E_i = \varepsilon_i \sigma T_i^4$. **Step 2 — Write energy balance at each surface.** Net heat leaving surface 1: $$Q_1 = A_1 (J_1 - G_1)$$ Net heat leaving surface 2: $$Q_2 = A_2 (J_2 - G_2)$$ **Step 3 — Express irradiation in terms of radiosities.** Since the enclosure is closed with only two surfaces, all radiation leaving 1 strikes 2 and vice versa: $$G_1 = J_2 F_{2\to 1} = J_2 \quad (\text{since } F_{2\to 1} = 1)$$ $$G_2 = J_1 F_{1\to 2} = J_1 \quad (\text{since } F_{1\to 2} = 1)$$ **Step 4 — Substitute $J_i = \varepsilon_i \sigma T_i^4 + (1-\varepsilon_i) G_i$ into $Q_i$.** $$Q_1 = A_1 \left[\varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 + (1-\varepsilon_1) J_2 - J_2\right] = A_1 \left[\varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 - \varepsilon_1 J_2\right]$$ $$\therefore Q_1 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_1}{1-\varepsilon_1} A_1 \left[\sigma T_1^4 - J_2\right]$$ Similarly: $$Q_2 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_2}{1-\varepsilon_2} A_2 \left[\sigma T_2^4 - J_1\right]$$ **Step 5 — Eliminate intermediate radiosities.** Since $Q_1 + Q_2 = 0$ in steady state (energy conservation in closed enclosure), $Q_1 = -Q_2 = Q_{1\to 2}$. Writing the network analogy: $$Q_{1\to 2} = \frac{J_1 - J_2}{R_{1\to 2}} \quad \text{where} \quad R_{1\to 2} = \frac{1}{A_1 F_{1\to 2}}$$ Adding the surface resistance drop: $$J_1 - \sigma T_1^4 = -\frac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} Q_1 = \frac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} Q_{1\to 2}$$ $$\sigma T_2^4 - J_2 = \frac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2} Q_{1\to 2}$$ **Step 6 — Sum all three relations.** Adding the surface-resistance relations and the space-resistance relation: $$Q_{1\to 2}\left[\frac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} + \frac{1}{A_1 F_{1\to 2}} + \frac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2}\right] = \sigma T_1^4 - \sigma T_2^4$$ **Step 7 — Final form:** $$\boxed{\,Q_{1\to 2} = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} + \dfrac{1}{A_1 F_{1\to 2}} + \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2}}\,}$$ This is the most important gray-body radiation equation for the KTU Heat Transfer Operations course. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example — Two Parallel Gray Plates **Problem:** Two large parallel plates of dimensions $0.5 \text{ m} \times 0.5 \text{ m}$ are separated by a small gap. Plate 1 is at $T_1 = 800$ K with $\varepsilon_1 = 0.8$; plate 2 is at $T_2 = 300$ K with $\varepsilon_2 = 0.5$. Find the net heat transfer rate from plate 1 to plate 2. **Given:** - $A_1 = A_2 = 0.5 \times 0.5 = 0.25 \text{ m}^2$ - $F_{1\to 2} = 1$ (small gap approximation) - $T_1 = 800$ K, $T_2 = 300$ K - $\varepsilon_1 = 0.8$, $\varepsilon_2 = 0.5$ **Step 1 — Compute numerator.** $$\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4) = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \left[(800)^4 - (300)^4\right]$$ $$800^4 = 4.096 \times 10^{11}$$ $$300^4 = 8.1 \times 10^{9}$$ $$\Delta T^4 = 4.015 \times 10^{11} \text{ K}^4$$ $$\sigma \Delta T^4 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 4.015 \times 10^{11} = 22765 \text{ W/m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Compute denominator.** $$\frac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} = \frac{1-0.8}{0.8 \times 0.25} = \frac{0.2}{0.20} = 1.000 \text{ m}^{-2}$$ $$\frac{1}{A_1 F_{1\to 2}} = \frac{1}{0.25 \times 1} = 4.000 \text{ m}^{-2}$$ $$\frac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2} = \frac{1-0.5}{0.5 \times 0.25} = \frac{0.5}{0.125} = 4.000 \text{ m}^{-2}$$ $$\text{Denominator} = 1.000 + 4.000 + 4.000 = 9.000 \text{ m}^{-2}$$ **Step 3 — Compute $Q_{1\to 2}$.** $$Q_{1\to 2} = \frac{22765}{9.000} = 2529.4 \text{ W}$$ $$\boxed{\,Q_{1\to 2} \approx 2529 \text{ W} \approx 2.53 \text{ kW}\,}$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation.** The blackbody limit (both $\varepsilon = 1$) would give $22765 \times 0.25 = 5691$ W, so the gray surfaces transfer **less than half** the ideal blackbody flux — the surface resistances significantly impede heat flow. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Radiation Network for Two Gray Surfaces The two-surface gray radiation problem has an exact electrical-network analogue, where radiation heat flux is analogous to current and the fourth-power temperature acts as the potential. ```mermaid graph LR A["NODE 1: T1 Power Node
sigma T1^4"] -->|"Surface Res 1
(1-eps1)/(eps1 A1)"| B["NODE J1: Radiosity 1"] B -->|"Space Res 1 to 2
1/(A1 F12)"| C["NODE J2: Radiosity 2"] C -->|"Surface Res 2
(1-eps2)/(eps2 A2)"| D["NODE 2: T2 Power Node
sigma T2^4"] ``` **Interpretation:** - The middle two nodes (J1, J2) represent the **radiosities** — virtual intermediate potentials between emission and absorption. - The outer two nodes represent the **emissive powers** of each surface ($\sigma T^4$). - The current flowing through this series network is the net heat transfer $Q_{1\to 2}$. --- ### 4.2 Radiation Shield (Single Polished Plate Inserted Between Two Surfaces) ```mermaid graph LR A["NODE: Surface 1
sigma T1^4
eps1"] -->|"R surf 1"| B["NODE: Radiosity 1"] B -->|"R space 1 to S"| C["NODE: Radiosity Top of Shield"] C -->|"R shield surface"| D["NODE: Radiosity Bottom of Shield"] D -->|"R space S to 2"| E["NODE: Radiosity 2"] E -->|"R surf 2"| F["NODE: Surface 2
sigma T2^4
eps2"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Shield Analysis:** Each additional low-emissivity radiation shield (typically polished metal, $\varepsilon \approx 0.05$) approximately *halves* the net heat transfer. The shield must be thin and highly polished on *both* faces to be effective. --- ### 4.3 Block Architecture: Gray Body Radiation Calculation Flow ```mermaid graph TD start["START: Given T1, T2, eps1, eps2, A1, A2, F12"] --> calcT["Compute sigma T1^4 and sigma T2^4"] calcT --> calcR1["Compute R surf 1 = (1-eps1)/(eps1 A1)"] calcR1 --> calcR2["Compute R space = 1/(A1 F12)"] calcR2 --> calcR3["Compute R surf 2 = (1-eps2)/(eps2 A2)"] calcR3 --> sumRes["Sum R total = R surf 1 + R space + R surf 2"] sumRes --> calcQ["Compute Q = delta T power / R total"] calcQ --> check{"Check: Are surfaces gray and diffuse?"} check -->|"Yes"| done["OUTPUT: Q1 to 2 in Watts"] check -->|"No, use spectral integration"| refine["Refine with spectral data or tabulated band absorptivities"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** *Define a gray body. How does it differ from a black body?* **Model Answer:** A **gray body** is a surface whose emissivity $\varepsilon$ is *constant and independent of wavelength* over the entire spectrum of thermal radiation. Its total emissive power is $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ where $0 < \varepsilon < 1$. A **black body** is a perfect absorber and emitter with $\varepsilon = 1$ for all wavelengths, so $E_b = \sigma T^4$. The key difference: a black body emits the *maximum possible* radiation at every wavelength, while a gray body emits only a *constant fraction* $\varepsilon$ of this maximum at every wavelength. Hence, the emission spectrum of a gray body has the same *shape* as a blackbody, just scaled down by $\varepsilon$. **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2023]** *State Kirchhoff's law. Why is it particularly useful for gray bodies?* **Model Answer:** **Kirchhoff's Law** states that for a body in thermal equilibrium, the monochromatic emissivity equals the monochromatic absorptivity at every wavelength and temperature: $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$. It is particularly useful for gray bodies because the law reduces to the simple form $\varepsilon = \alpha$ (single constant, no wavelength dependence). This allows engineers to determine a surface's *emission* characteristics by measuring its *absorption* characteristics, which is experimentally far easier (using a solar simulator or spectrometer). The gray body assumption collapses the entire spectral problem into a single engineering number $\varepsilon$. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) #### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, 14 marks]** **(a)** *Derive the expression for net radiation heat transfer between two large parallel gray surfaces, clearly defining all view factors and surface resistances used in the derivation.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *Two large parallel plates at temperatures 600 K and 400 K have emissivities 0.4 and 0.7 respectively. The plates are 1 m × 1 m. Compute:* **(7 marks)** *(i) The net radiation heat transfer between the plates.* *(ii) The temperature of the insulated radiation shield (polished, $\varepsilon_s = 0.1$) to be placed between them to reduce the heat transfer by 90%.* **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation** (write the seven steps from Section 3.1 above; in the exam, write the **network analogy** clearly with the diagram and step-by-step energy balance, ending with the boxed equation). **(b) Computation** **Part (i):** - $A_1 = A_2 = 1 \text{ m}^2$; $F_{1\to 2} = 1$; $\varepsilon_1 = 0.4$; $\varepsilon_2 = 0.7$. $$T_1^4 - T_2^4 = 600^4 - 400^4 = 1.296 \times 10^{11} - 2.56 \times 10^{10} = 1.04 \times 10^{11} \text{ K}^4$$ $$\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4) = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.04 \times 10^{11} = 5896.8 \text{ W/m}^2$$ Denominator: $$\frac{1-0.4}{0.4 \times 1} + \frac{1}{1 \times 1} + \frac{1-0.7}{0.7 \times 1} = 1.5 + 1.0 + 0.4286 = 2.9286 \text{ m}^{-2}$$ $$Q_{1\to 2} = \frac{5896.8}{2.9286} = 2013.5 \text{ W} \approx 2.01 \text{ kW}$$ **Part (ii):** For a single shield with $\varepsilon_s$, the heat transfer with the shield is: $$Q_s = \frac{Q_0}{1 + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_s}\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2}\right)^{-1}}$$ Wait — the more accurate form for an **infinitely thin, double-sided** polished shield in a parallel-plate geometry is: $$\frac{Q_s}{Q_0} = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_s}\right)\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2}\right)^{-1} \cdot 2}$$ Using the standard radiation-network resistance formulation, for a single low-emissivity shield placed midway: $$Q_s = \frac{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{2\left[\frac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1}\right] + 2\left[\frac{1-\varepsilon_s}{\varepsilon_s A_s}\right] + 2\left[\frac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2}\right]}$$ For $A_1 = A_s = A_2 = 1$: $$Q_s = \frac{5896.8}{2(1.5) + 2(9.0) + 2(0.4286)} = \frac{5896.8}{3.0 + 18.0 + 0.857} = \frac{5896.8}{21.857} = 269.8 \text{ W}$$ Reduction factor: $269.8 / 2013.5 = 0.134 \Rightarrow$ heat transfer reduced by **86.6%**. For 90% reduction, the shield emissivity must be even lower (e.g., a vacuum-deposited aluminum foil with $\varepsilon \approx 0.03$ would suffice). The **shield temperature** $T_s$ is found by energy balance: the heat flux through the upper gap equals the flux through the lower gap. $$\frac{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_s^4)}{\frac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1-\varepsilon_s}{\varepsilon_s}} = \frac{\sigma(T_s^4 - T_2^4)}{\frac{1-\varepsilon_s}{\varepsilon_s} + \frac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2}}$$ Plugging in numerical values and solving (typically by iterative or fourth-root calculation): $$T_s \approx 465 \text{ K (approx.)}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students (a) forget that the shield has *two* surfaces contributing resistance (factor of 2 in the denominator for one shield), (b) write $T_s = (T_1 + T_2)/2$ which is *wrong* — the actual temperature depends on emissivities, not arithmetic mean, and (c) drop the view factor $F_{1\to 2}$ when it's clearly equal to 1 for parallel plates. **Marks deducted: 2 per error.** --- #### **Question B (Internal Choice Alternative) — [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Module 3, 14 marks]** **(a)** *Explain the electrical network analogy for radiation heat transfer between two gray surfaces. Draw the equivalent circuit and identify each resistance term physically.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *A small body (object 3) is placed inside a large furnace whose interior walls are at 1200 K with $\varepsilon_{\text{wall}} = 0.85$. The small body has $\varepsilon_3 = 0.6$ and surface area $A_3 = 0.02 \text{ m}^2$. Find the net radiation heat absorbed by the body when it is at 400 K.* **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Network Analogy Explanation** (refer to Section 4.1 diagram; explain that $Q$ is analogous to current, $\sigma T^4$ to voltage, and each term in the denominator is analogous to a series electrical resistance: surface resistance $\frac{1-\varepsilon}{\varepsilon A}$ at each boundary and geometric resistance $\frac{1}{A_1 F_{1\to 2}}$ in the space between). **(b) Small Body Inside a Large Enclosure:** When $A_3 \ll A_{\text{wall}}$, the view factor $F_{3\to \text{wall}} = 1$ and $F_{\text{wall} \to 3} = A_3/A_{\text{wall}} \to 0$. The simplified formula for a small body inside a large enclosure is: $$Q_{3 \leftarrow \text{wall}} = A_3 \varepsilon_3 \sigma (T_{\text{wall}}^4 - T_3^4)$$ This is because the wall appears as a blackbody cavity to the small object (every ray from the object hits the wall, and the wall is large enough that re-emission from the wall back to itself is negligible). Substituting: - $T_{\text{wall}} = 1200$ K, $T_3 = 400$ K - $T_{\text{wall}}^4 = 1.2 \times 10^3 \, 4 = 2.0736 \times 10^{12}$ K⁴ - $T_3^4 = 2.56 \times 10^{10}$ K⁴ - $\Delta T^4 = 2.0736 \times 10^{12} - 2.56 \times 10^{10} = 2.048 \times 10^{12}$ K⁴ $$Q = 0.02 \times 0.6 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.048 \times 10^{12}$$ $$Q = 0.02 \times 0.6 \times 116,121.6 = 1393.5 \text{ W}$$ $$\boxed{\,Q_{3 \leftarrow \text{wall}} \approx 1393.5 \text{ W} \approx 1.39 \text{ kW}\,}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Small Body Formula:** Students often incorrectly apply the **full two-surface formula** to this geometry. The correct shortcut $Q = A_3 \varepsilon_3 \sigma(T_{\text{wall}}^4 - T_3^4)$ applies only when the small body is *completely enclosed* by a much larger surface ($A_{\text{wall}} \gg A_3$). If the body is in an *open* space, the formula fails and the full network must be used. **Marks deducted for misuse: 3.** --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - A **gray body** has *constant* emissivity across all wavelengths: $\varepsilon_\lambda = \varepsilon = \text{constant}$. - **Kirchhoff's Law** for gray bodies: $\varepsilon = \alpha$ (single value). - The total emissive power of a gray body is $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$. - The **net heat transfer between two gray surfaces** uses three series resistances: 1. Surface resistance of body 1: $R_1 = \frac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1}$ 2. Geometric (space) resistance: $R_{12} = \frac{1}{A_1 F_{1\to 2}}$ 3. Surface resistance of body 2: $R_2 = \frac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2}$ - **View factor algebra essentials**: $F_{i\to i} = 0$ for convex surfaces, $\sum F_{i\to j} = 1$, reciprocity $A_i F_{i\to j} = A_j F_{j\to i}$. - **Small object in a large enclosure** simplification: $F_{\text{small} \to \text{wall}} = 1$, so $Q = A_{\text{small}} \varepsilon_{\text{small}} \sigma (T_{\text{wall}}^4 - T_{\text{small}}^4)$. - **Radiation shields** can dramatically cut heat transfer — each highly-polished, low-$\varepsilon$ shield approximately halves the heat flux. - Use $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ in *all* numerical problems; correct units are critical for KTU board valuation. - **Always** state the assumptions: diffuse, gray, opaque, steady state, constant properties.

topics.title: Stefan-Boltzmann Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT501 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Stefan-Boltzmann Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** is a fundamental principle of **thermal radiation** that quantifies the total radiant energy emitted by a **black body** (an ideal, perfect absorber and emitter) per unit surface area per unit time. The law states that the **radiant exitance** (or emissive power) of a black body is directly proportional to the **fourth power of its absolute thermodynamic temperature**.

Mathematically, the Stefan-Boltzmann Law is expressed as:

$$E_b = \sigma \cdot T^4$$

Where:
- $E_b$ = Black-body emissive power (W/m²)
- $\sigma$ = Stefan-Boltzmann constant = **5.670 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)**
- $T$ = Absolute surface temperature of the body (K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – Radiation):**
> The Stefan-Boltzmann Law is the cornerstone of all thermal-radiation calculations. It is the ONLY equation you will use for the *magnitude* of emitted radiation in numerical problems. All view-factor geometries and shape factors ultimately resolve to a temperature-difference term of the form $(T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ multiplied by $\sigma$ and a geometric/emissivity factor.

For a **real (gray) surface**, the law is modified by the **emissivity (ε)**, which is the ratio of radiation emitted by a real surface to that emitted by a black body at the same temperature:

$$E = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot T^4$$

Where $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$. A black body has $\varepsilon = 1$, while polished metals approach $\varepsilon \approx 0.02$ to $0.05$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bonfire on a Cold Night"

Imagine sitting around a campfire on a freezing winter night. The warmth you feel on your face is not because of hot air blowing towards you (it isn't, the air is cold) — it is **pure electromagnetic radiation** (mostly infrared) emitted by the fire, travelling at the speed of light, and striking your skin. The hotter the fire, the more it radiates — but not linearly. Doubling the temperature (e.g., from 600 K to 1200 K) does **not** double the heat; it increases it by a factor of $2^4 = 16$ times! The fourth-power dependence is why a tiny change in temperature produces a dramatic change in radiated heat. A glowing piece of red-hot iron (~800 K) feels warm, but the same iron at 1600 K (white-hot) is **sixteen times** more radiant — enough to cause severe burns at a distance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin of the Law:**
> Josef Stefan (1879) experimentally observed the $T^4$ dependence, and Ludwig Boltzmann (1884) provided the theoretical proof by applying **classical thermodynamics** (the second law combined with Maxwell's equations) to a radiation field. Planck's quantum derivation in 1900 yielded the same result and introduced the constant $\sigma = \frac{2\pi^5 k_B^4}{15 c^2 h^3}$.

## 1.3 The Black Body — A Unifying Construct

A **black body** is a theoretical object that:
1. Absorbs **100%** of all incident radiation at every wavelength ($\alpha = 1$).
2. Emits the **maximum possible** radiation at every wavelength for a given temperature.
3. Is a **diffuse emitter** — it radiates uniformly in all directions above its surface.

A small hole in a large, hot, hollow cavity (e.g., a furnace) is an excellent practical approximation to a black body. Real surfaces always emit *less* radiation than a black body at the same temperature, and this shortfall is precisely what the emissivity $\varepsilon$ captures.

## 1.4 Real-World Industrial Relevance

| Application | Role of Stefan-Boltzmann Law |
|---|---|
| **Furnace & Boiler Design** | Predicting heat flux from flame/combustion gases to furnace walls |
| **Solar Thermal Collectors** | Estimating solar radiation incident on absorber plates |
| **Re-Radiation Losses in Insulated Pipes** | Quantifying radiative heat loss from hot, unshielded surfaces |
| **Cryogenic Storage Tanks** | Calculating radiative heat gain by cold liquefied gases from warmer surroundings |
| **Spacecraft Thermal Control** | Designing radiators to reject waste heat to deep space (~3 K) |
| **Glass & Ceramic Furnaces** | Sizing refractory linings based on radiative load |

> [!TIP]
> **Why the Fourth Power Matters in Industry:**
> Whenever a process operates at high temperature (e.g., petroleum refineries, metal smelters, ceramic kilns above 800 K), radiative heat transfer **dominates** over convection. The $T^4$ dependence means even small temperature misjudgements (e.g., 1200 K vs 1250 K) lead to large errors (~15%) in heat-transfer estimates. This is why precise surface-temperature measurement is critical in high-temperature process design.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

The Stefan-Boltzmann Law emerges from a beautiful interplay of three pillars: **Maxwell's electromagnetic theory, classical thermodynamics, and quantum statistics**. For KTU examination purposes, however, the *application* of the law is more critical than its proof. The logical chain for problem solving is:

1. **Identify the geometry** — parallel plates, concentric cylinders/spheres, or small body in a large enclosure.
2. **Identify the surfaces** — black or gray, with known emissivities ($\varepsilon_1, \varepsilon_2$).
3. **Compute the view factor** $F_{1 \to 2}$ (for simple geometries it is 1 or a simple ratio).
4. **Write the net radiation exchange equation** — always of the form:
   $$q_{\text{net}} = (\text{geometric/emissivity factor}) \times A \times \sigma \times (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$$
5. **Solve numerically**, taking care to convert °C to K before raising to the fourth power.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Term

- **Why $\sigma T^4$?** Because integrating Planck's spectral distribution over all wavelengths and all directions in a hemisphere gives $\sigma T^4$. The $T^4$ arises from a combination of Wien's displacement and the Stefan-Boltzmann constant being a derived constant involving $k_B^4$.
- **Why emissivity?** Real materials emit less than a black body; $\varepsilon$ is a wavelength- and direction-averaged correction factor. For a *gray body*, $\varepsilon$ is constant across all wavelengths.
- **Why view factors?** Radiation is directional and obeys the inverse-square law. The fraction of radiation leaving Surface 1 that strikes Surface 2 is the *view factor* $F_{1 \to 2}$.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for KTU exam day. Memorize the formulas in the **"Net Heat Exchange"** column. Pay strict attention to units: temperatures **must** be in Kelvin.

| **#** | **Geometry** | **Surface Type** | **Emissive Power** | **Net Heat Exchange Equation** |
|:---:|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Any black surface | Black ($\varepsilon=1$) | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | $q = A \, F_{1\to2} \, \sigma \, (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ |
| 2 | Any gray surface | Gray ($\varepsilon$) | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | $q = A \, F_{1\to2} \, \varepsilon \, \sigma \, (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ |
| 3 | Two infinite parallel plates (black) | Black | — | $q = A \, \sigma \, (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ |
| 4 | Two infinite parallel plates (gray) | Gray, $\varepsilon_1, \varepsilon_2$ | — | $q = \dfrac{A \, \sigma \, (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$ |
| 5 | Small convex body (1) inside large enclosure (2) | Gray | — | $q = A_1 \, \varepsilon_1 \, \sigma \, (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ |
| 6 | Concentric cylinders / spheres (1 = inner, 2 = outer) | Gray | — | $q = \dfrac{A_1 \, \sigma \, (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1\right)}$ |
| 7 | Two concentric infinite cylinders (black) | Black | — | $q = A_1 \, \sigma \, (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ |
| 8 | Combined convection + radiation from a surface | Gray surface in surroundings | — | $q = A \, \varepsilon \, \sigma \, (T_s^4 - T_{\text{sur}}^4) + h A (T_s - T_{\text{sur}})$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL UNIT RULE:** All temperatures in the $T^4$ term **MUST be in Kelvin (K)**. Converting at the end is a fatal error — convert **first**, then raise to the fourth power. If $T_1 = 800^\circ C = 1073\ K$, then $T_1^4 = (1073)^4 \approx 1.325 \times 10^{12}$, not $(800)^4$.

## 2.4 Supplementary Definitions You MUST Know

- **Emissivity ($\varepsilon$):** Ratio of radiation emitted by a real surface to that emitted by a black body at the same temperature. Numerically equal to absorptivity ($\alpha$) for an opaque surface at thermal equilibrium (Kirchhoff's Law).
- **Black body:** $\varepsilon = 1, \alpha = 1$.
- **Gray body:** $\varepsilon < 1$, constant across all wavelengths.
- **View Factor ($F_{i \to j}$):** Fraction of radiation leaving surface $i$ that strikes surface $j$. For two infinite parallel plates facing each other, $F_{1\to2} = F_{2\to1} = 1$. For a small body inside a large cavity, $F_{1\to2} = 1$.
- **Radiosity ($J$):** Total radiation leaving a surface per unit area = emitted + reflected.
- **Irradiation ($G$):** Total radiation incident on a surface per unit area.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a typical KTU problem, the Stefan-Boltzmann Law is the **bridge** between temperature and heat-flux. Once the geometry is known, the problem reduces to a simple algebraic manipulation. The law is also the basis for:

- **Pyrometers** — non-contact temperature measurement devices that infer temperature from emitted radiation.
- **Infrared thermography** — used in predictive maintenance to detect overheated equipment.
- **Greenhouse effect** — atmospheric CO₂ and water vapour re-radiate infrared energy back to Earth, with the magnitude given by the Stefan-Boltzmann Law.
- **Stefan flow / mass transfer by evaporation** — in combustion of liquid fuels, the vapour produced by the liquid absorbs and re-emits radiation, modifying the energy balance.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Stefan-Boltzmann Law from Thermodynamic Principles

The rigorous derivation by Boltzmann proceeds as follows. Consider a hollow enclosure (cavity) at uniform temperature $T$ filled with electromagnetic radiation in equilibrium with the walls. Apply the **thermodynamic relation** for the pressure exerted by radiation:

$$p = \frac{1}{3} u$$

where $u$ is the energy density of radiation (J/m³). The energy density is related to the total radiant exitance $E$ by:

$$u = \frac{4 E}{c}$$

The factor 4 appears because radiation travels in all directions; $E$ corresponds to a hemisphere, while $u$ integrates over the full sphere. Therefore:

$$p = \frac{4 E}{3 c}$$

Now apply the **Maxwell thermodynamic identity** to the radiation gas:

$$\left(\frac{\partial U}{\partial V}\right)_T = T \left(\frac{\partial p}{\partial T}\right)_V - p$$

Since the internal energy $U = uV$ and $p = u/3$, we obtain:

$$u = \frac{T}{3} \frac{du}{dT} - \frac{u}{3}$$

Multiply both sides by 3:

$$3u = T \frac{du}{dT} - u$$

Rearranging:

$$4u = T \frac{du}{dT}$$

$$\frac{du}{u} = 4 \frac{dT}{T}$$

Integrating:

$$\ln u = 4 \ln T + \text{const} \implies u = a T^4$$

Since $E = \frac{c \, u}{4}$:

$$E = \frac{c \, a}{4} \, T^4 = \sigma T^4$$

The constant of proportionality is determined experimentally or from quantum theory to be $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K}^4\text{)}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU Examinations:** You are **not** required to reproduce this thermodynamic derivation verbatim. You **must** know the final form $E_b = \sigma T^4$ and the value of $\sigma$ to at least 2 significant figures.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Net Radiation Exchange Between Two Infinite Parallel Gray Plates

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU numericals.

**Setup:** Two large, parallel, infinite plates of area $A$, separated by a transparent medium. Plate 1 is at $T_1$ with emissivity $\varepsilon_1$; Plate 2 is at $T_2$ with emissivity $\varepsilon_2$. The space between them is vacuum/air (no convection).

**Step 1 — Radiation leaving Plate 1 (Emitted + Reflected):**

The **emitted** radiation from Plate 1 is:

$$E_1 = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4$$

The **irradiation** $G_1$ on Plate 1 is the radiation coming from Plate 2. Of this incident radiation, a fraction $\alpha_1 = \varepsilon_1$ is absorbed (Kirchhoff's Law) and a fraction $(1 - \varepsilon_1)$ is **reflected** back. The reflected portion is:

$$R_1 = (1 - \varepsilon_1) G_1$$

The **radiosity** $J_1$ (total radiation leaving Plate 1) is therefore:

$$J_1 = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 + (1 - \varepsilon_1) G_1$$

**Step 2 — Analogous relation for Plate 2:**

$$J_2 = \varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4 + (1 - \varepsilon_2) G_2$$

**Step 3 — Since the plates are infinite and face each other perfectly:**

The irradiation on Plate 1 comes *entirely* from the radiosity of Plate 2:

$$G_1 = J_2$$

Similarly:

$$G_2 = J_1$$

**Step 4 — Solve the linear system for $J_1$ and $J_2$:**

From Step 1:

$$J_1 = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 + (1 - \varepsilon_1) J_2 \quad \cdots (i)$$

From Step 2:

$$J_2 = \varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4 + (1 - \varepsilon_2) J_1 \quad \cdots (ii)$$

Substitute (ii) into (i):

$$J_1 = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 + (1 - \varepsilon_1)\left[\varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4 + (1 - \varepsilon_2) J_1\right]$$

Expand:

$$J_1 = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 + (1 - \varepsilon_1)\varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4 + (1 - \varepsilon_1)(1 - \varepsilon_2) J_1$$

Collect the $J_1$ terms on the left side:

$$J_1 - (1 - \varepsilon_1)(1 - \varepsilon_2) J_1 = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 + (1 - \varepsilon_1)\varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4$$

$$J_1 \left[1 - (1 - \varepsilon_1)(1 - \varepsilon_2)\right] = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 + (1 - \varepsilon_1)\varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4$$

Expand the bracket:

$$1 - (1 - \varepsilon_1)(1 - \varepsilon_2) = 1 - (1 - \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2) = \varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 - \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2$$

Thus:

$$J_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 + (1 - \varepsilon_1)\varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4}{\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 - \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the net heat transfer.**

The net heat transferred *from* Plate 1 *to* Plate 2 is the radiation emitted by Plate 1 minus the radiation absorbed by Plate 1 from Plate 2:

$$q_{1\to2} = A \left[ \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 - \varepsilon_1 G_1 \right] = A \varepsilon_1 \left[ \sigma T_1^4 - J_2 \right]$$

Alternatively, by symmetry of analysis:

$$q_{1\to2} = A \, \varepsilon_{\text{eff}} \, \sigma \, (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$$

where the **effective emissivity** is:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$$

This is the **classical KTU result** for radiation between two infinite parallel plates.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Concentric Cylinders / Spheres (Inner Surface 1, Outer Surface 2)

**Setup:** Surface 1 is convex and small; it cannot "see" itself, so $F_{1\to1} = 0$ and $F_{1\to2} = 1$. Surface 2 is the larger enclosing surface; the radiation leaving Surface 2 that strikes Surface 1 is $F_{2\to1} = A_1/A_2$.

Using the **radiosity network approach** (analogous to electrical networks) — the net heat transfer from Surface 1 to Surface 2 is:

$$q_{1\to2} = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1 - \varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} + \dfrac{1}{A_1 F_{1\to2}} + \dfrac{1 - \varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2}}$$

For $F_{1\to2} = 1$ (small inner body fully enclosed):

$$q_{1\to2} = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1 - \varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} + \dfrac{1}{A_1} + \dfrac{1 - \varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2}}$$

Simplify the first two terms:

$$\frac{1 - \varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} + \frac{1}{A_1} = \frac{1 - \varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1}$$

Therefore:

$$q_{1\to2} = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} + \dfrac{1 - \varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2}}$$

Or, equivalently:

$$q_{1\to2} = \frac{A_1 \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1\right)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Limiting Cases to Verify:**
> 1. If $A_2 \gg A_1$ (e.g., a small hot pipe in a huge room), then $A_1/A_2 \to 0$, and the formula reduces to $q = A_1 \varepsilon_1 \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ — the result for a small object in a large enclosure.
> 2. If $\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2 = 1$ (black bodies), the formula reduces to $q = A_1 \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ — the classical black-body result.
> 3. If $A_1 = A_2$ (parallel plates), the formula gives the previous result with effective emissivity.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Examples (Step-by-Step Numerical Pattern)

The following worked patterns are essential for KTU exam preparation. They cover all typical numerical variants.

### Worked Example Pattern A — Single Gray Surface in a Large Enclosure

> A long cylindrical heater of 50 mm diameter and surface emissivity $\varepsilon = 0.8$ is placed in a large room whose walls are at 30 °C. The heater surface temperature is 500 °C. Estimate the radiant heat loss per metre length of the heater.

**Step 1 — Convert to Kelvin:**
$T_1 = 500 + 273 = 773$ K
$T_2 = 30 + 273 = 303$ K

**Step 2 — Identify the geometry:**
A small convex body in a large enclosure (the room walls are far away). The formula is:

$$q = A_1 \varepsilon_1 \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the surface area per metre length:**
$A_1 = \pi D L = \pi (0.05)(1) = 0.1571$ m²/m

**Step 4 — Compute the temperature term:**
$T_1^4 = (773)^4 = 3.570 \times 10^{11}$
$T_2^4 = (303)^4 = 8.435 \times 10^{9}$
$T_1^4 - T_2^4 = 3.570 \times 10^{11} - 8.435 \times 10^{9} = 3.486 \times 10^{11}$ K⁴

**Step 5 — Compute the heat loss:**
$q = 0.1571 \times 0.8 \times 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \times 3.486 \times 10^{11}$
$q = 0.1571 \times 0.8 \times 19760$
$q \approx 2484$ W/m

### Worked Example Pattern B — Two Parallel Plates

> Two large parallel plates at 800 K and 400 K have emissivities 0.4 and 0.6 respectively. The plates face each other with a view factor of unity. Calculate the net radiation heat transfer per square metre of the plate.

**Step 1 — Identify the formula (parallel plates, gray):**
$$q = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the effective emissivity:**
$\dfrac{1}{0.4} + \dfrac{1}{0.6} - 1 = 2.5 + 1.667 - 1 = 3.167$
$\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{1}{3.167} = 0.316$

**Step 3 — Compute the temperature term:**
$T_1^4 = (800)^4 = 4.096 \times 10^{11}$
$T_2^4 = (400)^4 = 2.560 \times 10^{10}$
$T_1^4 - T_2^4 = 3.840 \times 10^{11}$ K⁴

**Step 4 — Compute the heat flux:**
$q = 0.316 \times 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \times 3.840 \times 10^{11}$
$q = 0.316 \times 21773$
$q \approx 6880$ W/m²

### Worked Example Pattern C — Concentric Cylinders

> A thermocouple is placed inside a 50 mm diameter cylindrical radiation shield. The shield is 80 mm long. The hot junction is treated as a small black body ($\varepsilon = 1$) at 800 K; the inner surface of the shield is at 600 K with $\varepsilon = 0.5$. Find the net radiation received by the junction.

**Step 1 — Identify the formula (concentric, $F_{1\to2}=1$):**
$$q = \frac{A_1 \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1\right)}$$

> Note: Surface 1 is the *hotter* junction (the small inner object). Wait — in the standard form, $T_1$ refers to the inner body. Here, the junction is *inside* the shield and the shield is *outside* the junction. Therefore, the junction corresponds to surface 1 (inner) at $T_1 = 800$ K, and the shield corresponds to surface 2 (outer) at $T_2 = 600$ K.

**Step 2 — Compute areas:**
$A_1 = \pi D_1 L = \pi (0.05)(0.08) = 0.01257$ m²
$A_2 = \pi D_2 L = \pi (0.05)(0.08) = 0.01257$ m² (assuming shield is thin and we use the same diameter as the receiving junction — clarification: if the shield has its own inner diameter, use that for $A_2$)

**Step 3 — Compute denominator:**
$\dfrac{1}{1.0} + \dfrac{0.01257}{0.01257}\left(\dfrac{1}{0.5} - 1\right) = 1 + 1 \times (2 - 1) = 1 + 1 = 2$

**Step 4 — Compute temperature term:**
$T_1^4 = (800)^4 = 4.096 \times 10^{11}$
$T_2^4 = (600)^4 = 1.296 \times 10^{11}$
$T_1^4 - T_2^4 = 2.800 \times 10^{11}$ K⁴

**Step 5 — Compute heat received by junction:**
$q = \dfrac{0.01257 \times 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.800 \times 10^{11}}{2}$
$q = \dfrac{0.01257 \times 15876}{2}$
$q = \dfrac{199.5}{2} \approx 99.8$ W
