# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Stefan-Boltzmann Problem-Solving Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Stefan-Boltzmann Problem"] --> B{"Geometry Type?"}
    B -->|Parallel Plates| C["Use ε_eff = 1 / (1/ε1 + 1/ε2 - 1)"]
    B -->|Concentric Cylinders/Spheres| D["Use A1-factor formula"]
    B -->|Small Object in Enclosure| E["Use q = A1·ε1·σ·(T1^4 - T2^4)"]
    B -->|General Black Surfaces| F["Use q = A·F12·σ·(T1^4 - T2^4)"]

    C --> G["Convert all T to Kelvin"]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G

    G --> H["Compute T1^4 - T2^4"]
    H --> I["Multiply by σ = 5.67e-8"]
    I --> J["Multiply by A and emissivity/geometric factor"]
    J --> K["Result: q in Watts"]

    K --> L{"Check physical sense"}
    L -->|q positive, T1 > T2| M["Solution Valid: Heat flows from hot to cold"]
    L -->|q negative| N["Reverse the direction: Heat flows from T2 to T1"]
    L -->|q unreasonably large| O["Recheck units and conversion"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Radiation Exchange Network Analogy (Electrical Analogue)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph NET["Radiation Resistances (1/σA Terms)"]
        R1["R1 = (1-ε1)/(ε1·A1)<br/>Surface resistance of body 1"]
        R2["R2 = 1/(A1·F12)<br/>Space (geometric) resistance"]
        R3["R3 = (1-ε2)/(ε2·A2)<br/>Surface resistance of body 2"]
    end

    T1["Junction T1 (K)"] --- R1
    R1 --- R2
    R2 --- R3
    R3 --- T2["Junction T2 (K)"]

    N1["Net Heat q12"] -.->|"drives flow"| R1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Network:** The radiation heat-transfer problem is *isomorphic* to an electrical circuit. The "current" is the heat flow $q$, the "voltage" is the fourth-power temperature difference $\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)$, and the "resistances" are the surface and geometric terms. The total resistance adds in **series**. This visualisation makes the formula
> $$q = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{R_{\text{total}}}$$
> self-evident.

## 4.3 Schematic — Physical Setup of the Three Standard Geometries

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph G1["GEOMETRY 1: Parallel Plates"]
        P1a["Plate 1 — T1, ε1"] --- P1b["Vacuum/Air Gap"] --- P1c["Plate 2 — T2, ε2"]
    end

    subgraph G2["GEOMETRY 2: Concentric Cylinders"]
        P2a["Inner Cylinder T1, ε1"] --- P2b["Annular Gap"] --- P2c["Outer Cylinder T2, ε2"]
    end

    subgraph G3["GEOMETRY 3: Small Object in Enclosure"]
        P3a["Hot Body T1, ε1, Area A1"] --- P3b["Large Distance / Vacuum"] --- P3c["Cold Enclosure T2, ε2, Area A2 >> A1"]
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to use this for KTU:** Before plugging any number into an equation, draw a *labelled* diagram identifying $T_1, T_2, \varepsilon_1, \varepsilon_2, A_1, A_2, F_{1\to2}$. The diagram itself tells you which formula to use, because each formula is *anchored* to a specific geometry.

## 4.4 Concept Map — Relationship of Stefan-Boltzmann to Other Laws

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Black-Body Radiation Theory"] --> B["Planck's Law (spectral)"]
    A --> C["Wien's Displacement Law (peak λ)"]
    A --> D["Stefan-Boltzmann Law (total power)"]
    A --> E["Kirchhoff's Law (ε = α)"]

    B -->|"integrate over λ"| D
    B -->|"differentiate, set = 0"| C

    D -->|"apply to real surfaces"| F["Gray Body: E = ε·σ·T^4"]
    F -->|"apply to two surfaces"| G["Net Exchange: q = f(geometry)·σ·(T1^4 - T2^4)"]

    E --> F
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Integration Insight:** Stefan-Boltzmann is the *result* of integrating Planck's spectral distribution over all wavelengths. The two laws are mathematically inseparable. If a KTU question mentions "the total radiation emitted by a black body," it is the Stefan-Boltzmann Law; if it mentions "the wavelength at which emission peaks," it is Wien's Displacement Law. Keep these two straight in your answer!

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Temperature Unit Mistake** — The #1 cause of zero marks. **ALWAYS convert °C to K BEFORE the fourth power.** A student who writes $(500)^4$ instead of $(773)^4$ will get an answer off by a factor of $(773/500)^4 = 5.66$, i.e., nearly 5 times the correct value. This is treated as a *conceptual* error, not a computational one.
> 2. **Forgetting to square the linear term in area ratios** — For concentric spheres/cylinders, the area ratio $A_1/A_2$ is dimensionless and uses the inner/outer diameters correctly. Mixing up the formula leads to nonsense values.
> 3. **Using $\varepsilon$ instead of $1/\varepsilon$** — The parallel-plate formula has emissivity in the *denominator*. Memorize the form carefully.
> 4. **Forgetting the view factor $F_{12}$** — For black bodies, the formula needs the view factor explicitly. If the KTU question says "small body inside an enclosure," the view factor is 1, but you must justify this.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State and explain the Stefan-Boltzmann Law. What is the significance of the Stefan-Boltzmann constant?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Stefan-Boltzmann Law states that the total radiant energy emitted per unit time per unit surface area of a **black body** is directly proportional to the **fourth power of its absolute temperature**.

$$E_b = \sigma T^4$$

Where $E_b$ is the black-body emissive power in W/m², $\sigma$ is the Stefan-Boltzmann constant, and $T$ is the absolute temperature in Kelvin.

**Significance of $\sigma$:** The constant $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴) is a **fundamental physical constant** of nature. It converts the temperature of a body into its radiant power output. Without it, one cannot quantitatively predict the energy radiated by stars, furnaces, or any hot body. The value of $\sigma$ is derived from first principles (Planck's quantum theory):

$$\sigma = \frac{2 \pi^5 k_B^4}{15 c^2 h^3}$$

where $k_B$ is Boltzmann's constant, $c$ is the speed of light, and $h$ is Planck's constant. The fourth-power dependence shows that radiation increases extremely rapidly with temperature.

*(1 mark — Statement; 1 mark — Formula; 1 mark — Significance of $\sigma$.)*

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define emissivity. How is the Stefan-Boltzmann Law modified for a gray body?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Emissivity ($\varepsilon$):** Emissivity is defined as the ratio of the radiation emitted by a real surface to the radiation emitted by a **black body at the same temperature**.

$$\varepsilon = \frac{E}{E_b}$$

It is a dimensionless number between 0 and 1. For a black body, $\varepsilon = 1$. For a polished metal surface, $\varepsilon \approx 0.05$. For most non-metallic surfaces, $\varepsilon$ is in the range 0.6 to 0.95.

**Modification of Stefan-Boltzmann Law for a Gray Body:** A gray body has an emissivity $\varepsilon$ that is **constant with respect to wavelength** (unlike a real "selective" surface). The total radiation emitted per unit area by a gray body is:

$$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$$

Thus, the Stefan-Boltzmann Law for a real (gray) surface is the same form as for a black body, but multiplied by the emissivity factor $\varepsilon$.

*(1 mark — Definition; 1 mark — Range and physical meaning; 1 mark — Modified formula.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**Question A: (a)** State the Stefan-Boltzmann Law and derive the expression for net radiation heat transfer between two large parallel gray surfaces of emissivities $\varepsilon_1$ and $\varepsilon_2$, separated by a transparent medium. **(7 marks)**

**Question A: (b)** A small convex object with surface area $A_1 = 0.05$ m² and emissivity $\varepsilon_1 = 0.6$ is placed in a very large room whose walls are at 27 °C. The surface of the object is maintained at 327 °C. Calculate the net radiative heat loss from the object. Take $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks

**Statement of the Law (1 mark):**
The Stefan-Boltzmann Law gives the emissive power of a black body as $E_b = \sigma T^4$. For a gray surface, $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$.

**Setup of the problem (1 mark):**
Consider two large, parallel, infinite gray plates of area $A$, separated by a non-participating medium. Let Plate 1 be at $T_1$ with emissivity $\varepsilon_1$, and Plate 2 be at $T_2$ with emissivity $\varepsilon_2$. Both surfaces are opaque and diffuse.

**Radiosity equations (2 marks):**
For Plate 1, the total radiation leaving (radiosity) is the sum of its own emission and the reflection of incident radiation:

$$J_1 = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 + (1 - \varepsilon_1) G_1$$

For Plate 2:

$$J_2 = \varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4 + (1 - \varepsilon_2) G_2$$

**Boundary conditions for parallel plates (1 mark):**
Since the plates are infinite and face each other, all radiation leaving one plate strikes the other:

$$G_1 = J_2, \quad G_2 = J_1$$

**Solving the system and net heat transfer (2 marks):**
Substituting and solving the simultaneous equations (as shown in the detailed derivation in Section 3.2), one obtains the net heat transfer per unit area:

$$q = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$$

The factor in the denominator defines the **effective emissivity**:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$$

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- $A_1 = 0.05$ m²
- $\varepsilon_1 = 0.6$
- $T_{\text{room}} = 27^\circ C = 300$ K
- $T_{\text{object}} = 327^\circ C = 600$ K
- $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴)

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry (1 mark):**
A small convex body in a large enclosure. The room walls are far larger than the object, so $A_2 \gg A_1$, and $F_{1\to2} = 1$. The applicable formula is:

$$q = A_1 \varepsilon_1 \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$$

**Step 2 — Convert temperatures to Kelvin (1 mark):**
$T_1 = 600$ K
$T_2 = 300$ K

**Step 3 — Compute $T_1^4$ and $T_2^4$ (1 mark):**
$T_1^4 = (600)^4 = 1.296 \times 10^{11}$ K⁴
$T_2^4 = (300)^4 = 8.1 \times 10^{9}$ K⁴
$T_1^4 - T_2^4 = 1.296 \times 10^{11} - 0.081 \times 10^{11} = 1.215 \times 10^{11}$ K⁴

**Step 4 — Substitute into the formula (2 marks):**
$q = 0.05 \times 0.6 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.215 \times 10^{11}$
$q = 0.03 \times 6893.85$
$q = 206.8$ W

**Step 5 — Result and physical interpretation (1 mark):**
The net radiative heat loss from the object is **206.8 W**. The object loses energy to the cooler surroundings by thermal radiation.

**Final Answer:** $q \approx 207$ W (heat lost by the object).

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**Question B: (a)** Two large parallel plates are maintained at temperatures of 1000 K and 400 K. The plates have emissivities of 0.3 and 0.7, respectively. Using the Stefan-Boltzmann Law, calculate the net radiation heat transfer per square metre of the plate. **(7 marks)**

**Question B: (b)** Derive an expression for the net radiation heat transfer between two concentric cylinders where the inner cylinder is a convex body fully enclosed by the outer cylinder. Discuss the limiting cases. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- $T_1 = 1000$ K
- $T_2 = 400$ K
- $\varepsilon_1 = 0.3$
- $\varepsilon_2 = 0.7$
- $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴)

**Step 1 — Identify the formula (1 mark):**
For two infinite parallel gray plates, the net heat transfer is:

$$q = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the effective emissivity denominator (1 mark):**
$\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1 = \dfrac{1}{0.3} + \dfrac{1}{0.7} - 1 = 3.333 + 1.4286 - 1 = 3.7619$

$\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{1}{3.7619} = 0.2658$

**Step 3 — Compute $T_1^4 - T_2^4$ (1 mark):**
$T_1^4 = (1000)^4 = 1.0 \times 10^{12}$ K⁴
$T_2^4 = (400)^4 = 2.56 \times 10^{10}$ K⁴
$T_1^4 - T_2^4 = 1.0 \times 10^{12} - 0.0256 \times 10^{12} = 0.9744 \times 10^{12}$ K⁴

**Step 4 — Compute the net heat flux (2 marks):**
$q = \dfrac{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 9.744 \times 10^{11}}{3.7619}$
$q = \dfrac{55250}{3.7619}$
$q \approx 14689$ W/m²

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation (2 marks):**
The net heat flux from the hot plate to the cold plate is approximately **14.69 kW/m²**. Note that this is *less* than the black-body flux ($5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 9.744 \times 10^{11} = 55.25$ kW/m²) because the surfaces are not ideal black bodies. The lower-emissivity plate (0.3) is the limiting factor.

**Final Answer:** $q \approx 14.69$ kW/m²

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks

**Setup (1 mark):**
Consider two concentric, infinitely long cylinders. The inner cylinder (Surface 1) has area $A_1$, temperature $T_1$, and emissivity $\varepsilon_1$. The outer cylinder (Surface 2) has area $A_2$, temperature $T_2$, and emissivity $\varepsilon_2$. The space between them is evacuated (no convection). The inner cylinder is a *convex* body, so it cannot "see" itself, giving the view factor $F_{1\to2} = 1$.

**Radiosity equations (2 marks):**
The radiosity of each surface is:

$$J_1 = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 + (1 - \varepsilon_1) G_1$$
$$J_2 = \varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4 + (1 - \varepsilon_2) G_2$$

**Boundary conditions (1 mark):**
Since $F_{1\to2} = 1$, all radiation from Surface 1 strikes Surface 2, so $G_2 = J_1$. The radiation from Surface 2 that strikes Surface 1 is $G_1 = F_{2\to1} J_2 = (A_1/A_2) J_2$ (by the reciprocity rule $A_1 F_{1\to2} = A_2 F_{2\to1}$).

**Solving and obtaining the net heat transfer (2 marks):**
The net heat transfer from Surface 1 to Surface 2, derived by eliminating $G_1, G_2$ and $J_1, J_2$ (or by the radiation network method), is:

$$q_{1\to2} = \frac{A_1 \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1\right)}$$

**Limiting cases (1 mark):**
1. **Small inner body, $A_1/A_2 \to 0$:** $q = A_1 \varepsilon_1 \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ — radiation loss from a small object in a large enclosure.
2. **Black bodies, $\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2 = 1$:** $q = A_1 \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ — the result reduces to a black-body exchange limited by the inner area.
3. **Equal areas, $A_1 = A_2$:** the formula reduces to the parallel-plates result with $\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} = [1/\varepsilon_1 + 1/\varepsilon_2 - 1]^{-1}$.

**Final Answer:** The derived expression is
$$q_{1\to2} = \frac{A_1 \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1\right)}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PRINT THIS PAGE BEFORE EXAM DAY.** These 18 points cover everything KTU expects from Module 3's Stefan-Boltzmann content.

- [ ] **Stefan-Boltzmann Law:** $E_b = \sigma T^4$ for a black body; $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ for a gray body.
- [ ] **Constant value:** $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴). Memorize to 2 sig. figs.
- [ ] **Black body:** Absorbs all, emits all. $\varepsilon = 1$. Reference standard.
- [ ] **Gray body:** $\varepsilon$ = constant across all wavelengths. $0 < \varepsilon < 1$.
- [ ] **Kirchhoff's Law:** $\varepsilon = \alpha$ for an opaque body at thermal equilibrium.
- [ ] **Always convert temperatures to Kelvin** before the $T^4$ operation. This is the most common mistake.
- [ ] **Net radiation exchange is always** $\propto \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$.
- [ ] **Direction of heat flow:** From higher $T$ to lower $T$. A negative result means you labelled the surfaces backwards.
- [ ] **Parallel-plates formula:** $q = \dfrac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{1/\varepsilon_1 + 1/\varepsilon_2 - 1}$ per unit area.
- [ ] **Effective emissivity:** $\varepsilon_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{1}{1/\varepsilon_1 + 1/\varepsilon_2 - 1}$ — a number $\le \min(\varepsilon_1, \varepsilon_2)$.
- [ ] **Concentric cylinder/sphere formula:** $q = \dfrac{A_1 \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{1/\varepsilon_1 + (A_1/A_2)(1/\varepsilon_2 - 1)}$.
- [ ] **Small object in large enclosure:** $q = A_1 \varepsilon_1 \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ — area ratio term vanishes.
- [ ] **Black-body net exchange:** $q = A_1 F_{1\to2} \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ — no emissivity needed.
- [ ] **View factors** obey $F_{1\to2} = 1$ for convex body in cavity, and $F_{1\to2} + F_{1\to1} = 1$ for any surface.
- [ ] **Reciprocity rule:** $A_1 F_{1\to2} = A_2 F_{2\to1}$.
- [ ] **Fourth-power dominance:** Radiation dominates convection above ~700 K. Doubling temperature increases heat loss by 16×.
- [ ] **Units check:** $\sigma$ in W/(m²·K⁴), $A$ in m², $T$ in K. Result is in Watts.
- [ ] **Combined convection + radiation:** $q = A[\varepsilon \sigma (T_s^4 - T_{\text{sur}}^4) + h(T_s - T_{\text{sur}})]$ — used for total heat loss from a horizontal surface to ambient.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wien's Displacement Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT501 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wien's Displacement Law**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wien's Displacement Law (KTU 2024 Scheme Definition)**
> Wien's Displacement Law states that *the wavelength at which the spectral emissive power of a blackbody radiation curve reaches its maximum is inversely proportional to the absolute temperature of the blackbody*. In equation form, the product of the **wavelength of maximum emission** ($\lambda_{max}$) and the **absolute temperature** ($T$) is a constant, known as **Wien's Displacement Constant**.

Mathematically, the law is compactly expressed as:

$$
\lambda_{max} \cdot T = b
$$

where $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}\cdot\text{K}$ is **Wien's Displacement Constant** (a universal physical constant, not a variable).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Heating Iron Rod" Analogy**
> Imagine you are heating a piece of iron in a blacksmith's forge. At first, the iron is dark and barely glows. As it gets hotter, it begins to glow a *dull red*, then *cherry red*, then *orange*, and eventually *yellow-white*. Notice that as the temperature *increases*, the *color* of the brightest glow shifts toward the **shorter (bluer) wavelengths**.
>
> This everyday observation is precisely what Wien's Displacement Law captures quantitatively: hotter objects emit their peak radiation at **shorter wavelengths**. A cool ember peaks in the infrared (invisible to us, but felt as heat), a stove-top heating element peaks in the deep red (~1 μm), and the surface of the Sun (~5800 K) peaks in the visible green-yellow range (~0.5 μm). The law gives us the *exact* wavelength where this peak occurs for any given temperature.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constant

| Symbol | Name | Numerical Value | Unit |
|:------:|:-----|:---------------:|:----:|
| $b$ | Wien's Displacement Constant | $2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ | $\text{m}\cdot\text{K}$ |
| $C_1$ | First Radiation Constant | $3.742 \times 10^{-16}$ | $\text{W}\cdot\text{m}^{2}$ |
| $C_2$ | Second Radiation Constant | $1.4388 \times 10^{-2}$ | $\text{m}\cdot\text{K}$ |
| $\sigma$ | Stefan-Boltzmann Constant | $5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ | $\text{W}/(\text{m}^{2}\cdot\text{K}^{4})$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Significance Highlight for KTU Examinations**
> Wien's Displacement Law is a *direct consequence* of Planck's spectral distribution and is one of the three cornerstone results of blackbody radiation (the other two being **Planck's Law** itself and the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**). It allows us to relate the *spectral quality* of radiation to the *thermodynamic state* of the emitter.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the Concept

The law emerges from a single thermodynamic fact: as the absolute temperature of a blackbody **increases**, the entire spectral emissive power curve *not only* rises in magnitude (governed by Stefan-Boltzmann's $T^4$ dependence) *but also* shifts bodily toward shorter wavelengths. Wien's Displacement Law isolates the *shift* part of this behavior.

- **The "Why"**: The peak shift occurs because Planck's distribution has the form $E_{b\lambda} = \dfrac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}(e^{C_2/\lambda T}-1)}$. The denominator contains a *competition*: the $1/\lambda^{5}$ term (Rayleigh-Jeans-like decay at long wavelengths) and the exponential term (Wien's decay at short wavelengths). The peak is where these two opposing tendencies balance. When $T$ rises, this balance point moves to smaller $\lambda$ to compensate.

- **The "How"**: We mathematically locate the maximum of $E_{b\lambda}$ with respect to $\lambda$ by setting $\dfrac{dE_{b\lambda}}{d\lambda} = 0$. The resulting *transcendental equation* has a numerical solution of approximately **4.965114231**, from which Wien's constant $b$ is derived.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below contains **all the high-yield formulas** you need for any KTU exam question on Wien's Displacement Law. Memorize the column relations and units carefully.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Identity | Standard Units |
|:-:|:--------|:-------------------|:--------------:|
| 1 | $\lambda_{max} \cdot T = b$ | Wien's Displacement Law (principal form) | m·K |
| 2 | $\lambda_{max} = \dfrac{b}{T}$ | Wavelength of maximum emission | m |
| 3 | $T = \dfrac{b}{\lambda_{max}}$ | Temperature for a given peak wavelength | K |
| 4 | $b = \dfrac{C_2}{4.965114231}$ | Relation to Planck's second constant | m·K |
| 5 | $E_{b\lambda_{max}} = K \cdot T^{5}$ | Wien's Fifth Power Law (peak spectral emissive power) | $\text{W}/(\text{m}^{2}\cdot\text{m})$ |
| 6 | $K = \dfrac{C_1 \cdot x^{5}}{C_2^{5}(e^{x}-1)} \approx 4.096 \times 10^{-6}$ | Constant for Fifth Power Law (in $\text{W}/(\text{m}^{2}\cdot\mu\text{m}\cdot\text{K}^{5})$) | — |
| 7 | $E_b = \sigma T^{4}$ | Stefan-Boltzmann Law (total emissive power) | $\text{W}/\text{m}^{2}$ |
| 8 | $5(1 - e^{-x}) = x$ | Transcendental equation, with $x = C_2/(\lambda_{max} T) \approx 4.965$ | dimensionless |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Optical Pyrometry**: A non-contact temperature measurement technique that infers the temperature of a hot body by detecting the dominant color of its emitted radiation. Industrial furnaces and molten metal streams are routinely measured this way.
- **Stellar Astrophysics**: Astronomers determine the surface temperature of distant stars by locating the peak wavelength of their continuous spectrum and applying Wien's law.
- **Infrared (IR) Thermal Imaging**: Night-vision cameras are designed to detect the long-wavelength peak of room-temperature objects (~10 μm).
- **Solar Energy Engineering**: Photovoltaic cells and solar concentrators are designed to operate near the solar peak (~0.5 μm) to maximize energy capture.
- **Incandescent Lamp Design**: The filament temperature is chosen so that the peak emission falls within the visible spectrum, optimizing luminous efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Wien's Displacement Law from Planck's Distribution

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Premise:** Planck's spectral distribution law for a blackbody gives the spectral emissive power as a function of wavelength $\lambda$ and absolute temperature $T$.

The starting equation is:

$$
E_{b\lambda}(\lambda, T) = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}\left(e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1\right)}
$$

**Step 1: Identify the maximum of the distribution.**

We want the wavelength $\lambda_{max}$ at which $E_{b\lambda}$ attains its peak value. The necessary condition is:

$$
\frac{dE_{b\lambda}}{d\lambda} = 0
$$

**Step 2: Apply the chain rule for the quotient of two functions.**

Let us define $f(\lambda) = C_1$ (constant) and $g(\lambda) = \lambda^{5}\left(e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1\right)$, so that $E_{b\lambda} = f/g$. Then:

$$
\frac{dE_{b\lambda}}{d\lambda} = \frac{f' \cdot g - f \cdot g'}{g^{2}} = \frac{0 - C_1 \cdot g'}{g^{2}} = -\frac{C_1 \cdot g'}{g^{2}}
$$

For this derivative to be zero, the numerator's non-constant factor must be zero: $g'(\lambda) = 0$.

**Step 3: Compute $g'(\lambda)$ using the product rule.**

We have $g(\lambda) = \lambda^{5} \cdot h(\lambda)$ where $h(\lambda) = e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1$. Applying the product rule:

$$
g'(\lambda) = 5\lambda^{4} \cdot h(\lambda) + \lambda^{5} \cdot h'(\lambda)
$$

**Step 4: Compute $h'(\lambda)$ using the chain rule.**

Let $u(\lambda) = C_2/(\lambda T) = C_2 T^{-1} \lambda^{-1}$. Then $du/d\lambda = -C_2/(\lambda^{2} T)$. By the chain rule:

$$
h'(\lambda) = \frac{d}{d\lambda}\left(e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1\right) = e^{C_2/\lambda T} \cdot \left(-\frac{C_2}{\lambda^{2} T}\right) = -\frac{C_2}{\lambda^{2} T} \, e^{C_2/\lambda T}
$$

**Step 5: Substitute back into $g'(\lambda)$.**

$$
g'(\lambda) = 5\lambda^{4}\left(e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1\right) + \lambda^{5} \cdot \left[-\frac{C_2}{\lambda^{2} T} \, e^{C_2/\lambda T}\right]
$$

$$
g'(\lambda) = 5\lambda^{4}\left(e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1\right) - \frac{C_2 \lambda^{3}}{T} \, e^{C_2/\lambda T}
$$

**Step 6: Set $g'(\lambda) = 0$ and divide by $\lambda^{3}$.**

$$
5\lambda\left(e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1\right) - \frac{C_2}{T} \, e^{C_2/\lambda T} = 0
$$

After dividing by $\lambda^{3}$:

$$
5\left(e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1\right) - \frac{C_2}{\lambda T} \, e^{C_2/\lambda T} = 0
$$

**Step 7: Introduce the dimensionless substitution $x = C_2/(\lambda T)$.**

Then $\lambda = C_2/(xT)$, and the equation becomes:

$$
5\left(e^{x} - 1\right) - x \, e^{x} = 0
$$

Rearranging:

$$
e^{x}\left(5 - x\right) = 5
$$

Dividing both sides by $e^{x}$:

$$
5 - x = 5 e^{-x} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad x = 5\left(1 - e^{-x}\right)
$$

This is the celebrated **transcendental equation** of Wien's Displacement Law.

**Step 8: Solve the transcendental equation numerically (Newton-Raphson).**

The function $f(x) = x - 5(1 - e^{-x})$ has its root at:

$$
x = \frac{C_2}{\lambda_{max} T} = 4.965114231\ldots
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for Examiners:** This number has no closed-form solution; it must be obtained through numerical iteration. Students are expected to quote $x \approx 4.9651$ (or simply write $\lambda_{max}T = b$ directly) in the examination.

**Step 9: Solve for Wien's Displacement Constant $b$.**

Since $\lambda_{max} T = C_2/x$:

$$
b = \frac{C_2}{x} = \frac{1.4388 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}\cdot\text{K}}{4.965114231}
$$

$$
b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}\cdot\text{K}
$$

This completes the rigorous derivation of Wien's Displacement Law.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Limiting Behaviors

| Limit | Approximation Used | Resulting Form | Physical Interpretation |
|:-----:|:------------------|:---------------|:------------------------|
| $\lambda T \ll C_2$ (short $\lambda$, high $T$) | $e^{C_2/\lambda T} \gg 1$ | $E_{b\lambda} \approx \dfrac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}} e^{-C_2/\lambda T}$ | Wien's Distribution (valid in UV) |
| $\lambda T \gg C_2$ (long $\lambda$, low $T$) | $e^{C_2/\lambda T} \approx 1 + C_2/\lambda T$ | $E_{b\lambda} \approx \dfrac{C_1 T}{C_2 \lambda^{4}}$ | Rayleigh-Jeans Law (valid in microwave) |
| Peak position | $\dfrac{dE_{b\lambda}}{d\lambda} = 0$ | $\lambda_{max} T = b$ | **Wien's Displacement Law** |

## 3.3 Derivation of Wien's Fifth Power Law

A direct corollary of the displacement law is that the *peak* spectral emissive power obeys a $T^{5}$ law. Substituting $\lambda_{max} = b/T$ back into Planck's equation, with $C_2/\lambda_{max} T = x = $ constant:

$$
E_{b\lambda_{max}} = \frac{C_1}{\lambda_{max}^{5}(e^{x}-1)} = \frac{C_1}{(b/T)^{5}(e^{x}-1)} = \left[\frac{C_1 \, T^{5}}{b^{5}(e^{x}-1)}\right]
$$

Since the bracketed factor apart from $T^{5}$ is a constant, we have $E_{b\lambda_{max}} \propto T^{5}$, which is **Wien's Fifth Power Law**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

Below, every node identifier is purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters. Inside double-quoted labels, no markdown formatting (bold/italics) is used, and no reserved keyword (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) appears as a standalone node name.

## 4.2 Derivation Flow Chart (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["A START Planck Spectral Law"] --> stepB["B Write E_b_lambda in terms of lambda and T"]
    stepB --> stepC["C Apply Condition dE_b_lambda divided by d lambda equals zero"]
    stepC --> stepD["D Use Product Rule to Differentiate Denominator g of lambda"]
    stepD --> stepE["E Use Chain Rule on Exponential Term"]
    stepE --> stepF["F Substitute and Simplify the Expression"]
    stepF --> stepG["G Introduce Dimensionless Variable x equals C2 over lambda T"]
    stepG --> stepH["H Arrive at Transcendental Equation 5 times 1 minus e to the minus x equals x"]
    stepH --> stepI["I Solve Numerically x equals 4.9651"]
    stepI --> stepJ["J Compute Wien Constant b equals C2 divided by x"]
    stepJ --> stepK["K FINAL Wien Displacement Law lambda_max T equals b"]
    stepK --> stepL["L Corollary Wien Fifth Power Law E_b_lambda_max proportional to T to the power 5"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Wien's Law Applications

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input1["A Blackbody Source with Temperature T"] --> proc1["B Apply Wien Displacement Law"]
    proc1 --> output1["C Predict Wavelength lambda_max"]
    proc1 --> output2["D Compute Peak Spectral Emissive Power"]
    output1 --> app1["E Optical Pyrometry"]
    output1 --> app2["F Stellar Temperature Measurement"]
    output1 --> app3["G Solar Cell Spectral Matching"]
    output2 --> app4["H Design of Infrared Cameras"]
    output2 --> app5["I Incandescent Filament Optimization"]
    app1 --> endnode["J Engineering Decision Output"]
    app2 --> endnode
    app3 --> endnode
    app4 --> endnode
    app5 --> endnode
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Peak Shift with Temperature

The following tabular schematic shows how the peak of $E_{b\lambda}$ migrates as $T$ changes. (This is intended as a conceptual proxy for the actual spectral curves, which cannot be rendered in Mermaid.)

| Blackbody Source | Temperature $T$ (K) | $\lambda_{max}$ (μm) | Spectral Region |
|:-----------------|:-------------------:|:--------------------:|:----------------|
| Human Body Surface | 310 | 9.35 | Far-Infrared |
| Incandescent Filament | 2500 | 1.16 | Near-Infrared |
| Tungsten Halogen Lamp | 3200 | 0.91 | Red Border of Visible |
| Sun's Photosphere | 5800 | 0.50 | Visible Green-Yellow |
| Blue Star (Rigel) | 12000 | 0.24 | Near-Ultraviolet |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Q1. State Wien's Displacement Law and explain its significance in heat transfer. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Wien's Displacement Law states that the wavelength at which the spectral emissive power of a blackbody is maximum is inversely proportional to its absolute temperature. Mathematically:

$$
\lambda_{max} \cdot T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}\cdot\text{K}
$$

**Significance:**

1. It enables the determination of the *peak emission wavelength* of a blackbody at any temperature, which is essential for designing thermal sensors, pyrometers, and solar collectors.
2. It explains the *color change* of heated objects (red-hot to white-hot) as the temperature increases.
3. It is widely used in **optical pyrometry** to measure the temperature of furnaces, molten metals, and stars by detecting the wavelength of peak intensity.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Q2. The Sun's surface temperature is approximately 5800 K. Find the wavelength at which the maximum spectral emission occurs. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Using Wien's Displacement Law:

$$
\lambda_{max} = \frac{b}{T} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}\cdot\text{K}}{5800 \text{ K}}
$$

$$
\lambda_{max} = 4.997 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} \approx 0.5 \text{ μm (or 500 nm)}
$$

This wavelength falls in the **visible green-yellow region** of the electromagnetic spectrum, which explains why the Sun appears yellowish-white to the human eye.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 3]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive Wien's Displacement Law from Planck's spectral distribution law. Clearly state all assumptions and the final form of the displacement constant. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

We start with **Planck's spectral distribution law**:

$$
E_{b\lambda} = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}\left(e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1\right)}
$$

The peak of this distribution is found by imposing $\dfrac{dE_{b\lambda}}{d\lambda} = 0$. Setting the derivative of the denominator to zero:

$$
5\left(e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1\right) - \frac{C_2}{\lambda T}\, e^{C_2/\lambda T} = 0
$$

Substituting $x = C_2/(\lambda T)$:

$$
5\left(e^{x} - 1\right) - x e^{x} = 0
$$

Solving numerically (Newton-Raphson iteration):

$$
x = 4.965114231
$$

Therefore:

$$
\lambda_{max} T = \frac{C_2}{x} = \frac{1.4388 \times 10^{-2}}{4.9651} = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}\cdot\text{K}
$$

Thus **Wien's Displacement Law**:

$$
\boxed{\lambda_{max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}\cdot\text{K}}
$$

---

**(b)** A blackbody surface at 1500 K emits maximum radiation at wavelength $\lambda_{max,1}$. If the temperature is increased to 3000 K, calculate (i) the new wavelength of maximum emission, and (ii) the ratio of the maximum spectral emissive powers at the two temperatures. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) New wavelength of maximum emission:**

$$
\lambda_{max,2} = \frac{b}{T_2} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{3000} = 9.66 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = 966 \text{ nm}
$$

(For reference, $\lambda_{max,1} = \dfrac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{1500} = 1.932 \text{ μm}$.)

**(ii) Ratio of maximum spectral emissive powers (Wien's Fifth Power Law):**

Since $E_{b\lambda_{max}} \propto T^{5}$:

$$
\frac{E_{b\lambda_{max,2}}}{E_{b\lambda_{max,1}}} = \left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{5} = \left(\frac{3000}{1500}\right)^{5} = 2^{5} = 32
$$

The peak spectral emissive power increases by a factor of **32** when the temperature is doubled.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the concept of blackbody radiation. Using Planck's distribution, derive Wien's Displacement Law and comment on the physical significance of the result. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

A **blackbody** is an idealized perfect absorber and emitter of thermal radiation. Its spectral emissive power $E_{b\lambda}$ is given by **Planck's Law**:

$$
E_{b\lambda} = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}\left(e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1\right)}
$$

The curve $E_{b\lambda}$ vs. $\lambda$ at a fixed $T$ has a single maximum. Locating it via $\dfrac{dE_{b\lambda}}{d\lambda} = 0$ yields (as derived in detail in Question A):

$$
\lambda_{max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}\cdot\text{K}
$$

**Physical Significance:**

- The product $\lambda_{max} T$ is a *universal constant*, independent of the nature of the blackbody.
- As $T$ increases, $\lambda_{max}$ shifts toward shorter wavelengths (the visible spectrum). This explains the well-known color progression of heated objects: dull red $\to$ orange $\to$ yellow $\to$ white.
- It is the operational basis of *optical pyrometers*, which estimate temperatures from the dominant wavelength of emitted light.

---

**(b)** An incandescent tungsten filament operates at 2500 K. Using Wien's Displacement Law, determine: (i) the wavelength of maximum emission, (ii) the total emissive power, and (iii) the maximum spectral emissive power. Given: $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴), and the proportionality constant for the fifth-power law is $K = 4.096 \times 10^{-6}$ W/(m²·μm·K⁵). (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Wavelength of maximum emission:**

$$
\lambda_{max} = \frac{b}{T} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{2500} = 1.159 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 1.159 \text{ μm}
$$

**(ii) Total emissive power (Stefan-Boltzmann Law):**

$$
E_b = \sigma T^{4} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (2500)^{4}
$$

$$
E_b = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 3.906 \times 10^{13} = 2.215 \times 10^{6} \text{ W/m}^{2}
$$

**(iii) Maximum spectral emissive power (Wien's Fifth Power Law):**

$$
E_{b\lambda_{max}} = K \cdot T^{5} = 4.096 \times 10^{-6} \times (2500)^{5}
$$

$$
E_{b\lambda_{max}} = 4.096 \times 10^{-6} \times 9.766 \times 10^{16} = 4.0 \times 10^{11} \text{ W/(m}^{2}\cdot\text{m)}
$$

In more practical units:

$$
E_{b\lambda_{max}} = 4.0 \times 10^{5} \text{ W/(m}^{2}\cdot\text{μm)}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Trap:** Students frequently mix up μm, nm, and m when reporting $\lambda_{max}$. Always retain the SI unit in intermediate steps, and convert to the requested unit **only at the final answer**.
> 2. **Temperature in Kelvin:** Wien's Law requires $T$ in **absolute Kelvin**, not Celsius. Plugging in $T = 25$ instead of $T = 298$ is a 2-mark loss.
> 3. **Forgetting Wien's Fifth Power Law:** Many students correctly compute $\lambda_{max}$ but then mistakenly use Stefan-Boltzmann's $T^{4}$ dependence for the *peak spectral* emissive power. Remember: $E_{b\lambda_{max}} \propto T^{5}$ (not $T^{4}$), because both $\lambda_{max}^{-5}$ *and* the temperature contribute.
> 4. **Numerical Value of the Transcendental Root:** Writing $x = 5$ (a tempting "round" value) is **wrong**. The exact numerical value is $x = 4.965114231$, leading to $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K. Losing this distinction costs full marks for the derivation step.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Wien's Displacement Law (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

- [x] **Statement:** $\lambda_{max} T = b$ — the *peak* wavelength of blackbody radiation is inversely proportional to absolute temperature.
- [x] **Wien's Constant:** $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K (memorize this value).
- [x] **Source Equation:** Derived from Planck's spectral distribution: $E_{b\lambda} = \dfrac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}(e^{C_2/\lambda T}-1)}$.
- [x] **Transcendental Equation:** $5(1 - e^{-x}) = x$, with $x = 4.9651$.
- [x] **Wien's Fifth Power Law:** $E_{b\lambda_{max}} \propto T^{5}$ — peak spectral emissive power scales as the *fifth* power of temperature.
- [x] **Unit Discipline:** Always use **Kelvin** for $T$, **meters** (or μm/nm) for $\lambda$. Mixing units is the single biggest source of errors.
- [x] **Engineering Use-Cases:** Optical pyrometry, IR imaging, solar cell design, stellar temperature estimation, incandescent lamp design.
- [x] **Color-Temperature Connection:** Cooler = longer $\lambda_{max}$ (infrared/red), Hotter = shorter $\lambda_{max}$ (UV/blue-white).
- [x] **Companion Laws:** Wien's Displacement Law + Stefan-Boltzmann Law ($E_b = \sigma T^{4}$) + Planck's Law form the *trinity of blackbody radiation* tested in KTU Module 3.
- [x] **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Do **not** confuse *spectral* emissive power scaling ($T^{5}$) with *total* emissive power scaling ($T^{4}$).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Kirchhoff's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT501 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Kirchhoff's Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🌡️ MODULE 3 — KIRCHHOFF'S LAW OF THERMAL RADIATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Kirchhoff's Law of Thermal Radiation** states that *at thermal equilibrium, the ratio of the total emissive power of a body to its total absorptivity is constant and is equal to the total emissive power of a perfect black body at the same temperature.*

Mathematically expressed in its most fundamental form:

$$\frac{E}{\alpha} = E_b = \sigma T^{4}$$

For **monochromatic (spectral) quantities** evaluated at the same wavelength $\lambda$ and temperature $T$:

$$\frac{E_{\lambda}}{\alpha_{\lambda}} = E_{b\lambda}$$

This leads to the universally recognized corollary: **the spectral emissivity of a body equals its spectral absorptivity** at the same wavelength and temperature:

$$\varepsilon_{\lambda}(T) = \alpha_{\lambda}(T)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Insight:** Kirchhoff's Law is the *bridge equation* that links **emission** and **absorption** properties of a surface. Without it, the two fundamental radiative properties would be unrelated, making radiative heat transfer calculations unsolvable for real (non-black) surfaces.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Good Absorber Paradox"

Imagine two cups of coffee placed in a perfectly insulated room at the same temperature:
- **Cup A**: Has a polished, mirror-like, silver-coated outer surface.
- **Cup B**: Has a rough, matte-black painted outer surface.

If the room itself is filled with thermal radiation (acting as a large isothermal enclosure), here is what Kirchhoff's Law intuitively tells us:

> ☕ **Cup B (Black)** absorbs almost all the radiant energy falling on it. By the law, it must *also* be an excellent *emitter*. So it both receives and re-emits radiation efficiently, quickly reaching thermal equilibrium.
>
> ☕ **Cup A (Silver)** reflects most of the incident radiation. It is a *poor absorber*. Therefore, by the same law, it must also be a *poor emitter*. Its temperature remains stable because it neither absorbs nor emits significantly.

**Real-world takeaway:** A surface that is a *good emitter* is necessarily a *good absorber*, and vice versa. This is why **solar absorber plates** in solar water heaters are painted matte black, while **thermos flask inner linings** are silvered to minimize radiative heat transfer.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Glossary)

| Term | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|------|--------|------------------|
| Emissive Power | $E$ | Total radiant energy emitted per unit area per unit time (W/m²) |
| Absorptivity | $\alpha$ | Fraction of incident radiation absorbed (dimensionless, 0–1) |
| Black Body Emissive Power | $E_b$ | Maximum possible emission at temperature $T$ |
| Spectral Emissivity | $\varepsilon_\lambda$ | Emissivity evaluated at a specific wavelength |
| Spectral Absorptivity | $\alpha_\lambda$ | Absorptivity evaluated at a specific wavelength |
| Gray Body | — | A body whose $\varepsilon_\lambda$ and $\alpha_\lambda$ are independent of $\lambda$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners frequently test the *distinction* between a **black body** ($\varepsilon = 1$, $\alpha = 1$), a **gray body** ($\varepsilon = \alpha < 1$, constant with $\lambda$), and a **selective surface** ($\varepsilon$ and $\alpha$ vary with $\lambda$). Mastering this distinction is essential for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physical Reasoning — The Three Pillars

The derivation and application of Kirchhoff's Law rests on three logical pillars that you must internalize:

**Pillar 1 — Thermodynamic Equilibrium Principle**
> When a body is placed inside a large isothermal enclosure and reaches *thermal equilibrium*, the net energy exchange between the body and the enclosure walls must be zero. The body emits exactly as much energy as it absorbs.

**Pillar 2 — Spectral Decomposition**
> Real radiation is a superposition of many wavelengths. The Law applies not only to *total* (bolometric) quantities but more precisely to *monochromatic* (single-wavelength) quantities, since a surface may absorb blue light strongly while being transparent to infrared.

**Pillar 3 — Directional & Hemispherical Integration**
> Surface properties can be directional (function of angle $\theta$) or hemispherical (integrated over all angles of the hemisphere above the surface). The *hemispherical* form of Kirchhoff's Law is the one most commonly used in KTU problems.

## 2.2 The Three Forms of Kirchhoff's Law

| Form # | Mathematical Statement | Application Domain |
|--------|----------------------|---------------------|
| 1. Spectral (Monochromatic) | $\varepsilon_{\lambda}(T) = \alpha_{\lambda}(T)$ | Selective surfaces, solar engineering |
| 2. Total Hemispherical (Gray Body) | $\varepsilon(T) = \alpha(T)$ | Most engineering heat transfer problems |
| 3. Quotient Form | $E / \alpha = E_b = \sigma T^{4}$ | Fundamental black body reference |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use in KTU |
|---|---------|-------------|--------------------|
| 1 | $E / \alpha = E_b$ | Fundamental statement of Kirchhoff's Law | Conceptual 3-mark questions |
| 2 | $\varepsilon_{\lambda} = \alpha_{\lambda}$ | Spectral equivalence | Gray body, solar problems |
| 3 | $\varepsilon = \alpha$ | Gray body total property equivalence | Most numerical problems |
| 4 | $E = \varepsilon \, E_b = \varepsilon \, \sigma T^{4}$ | Emissive power of real surface | Direct substitution |
| 5 | $E_b = \sigma T^{4}$ | Stefan-Boltzmann Law (W/m²) | Final step of derivations |
| 6 | $E_{b\lambda} = \frac{2\pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}\left[e^{hc/\lambda k T} - 1\right]}$ | Planck's spectral distribution | Rare in KTU, but conceptually important |
| 7 | $\alpha = \frac{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} \alpha_{\lambda} \, G_{\lambda} \, d\lambda}{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} G_{\lambda} \, d\lambda}$ | Total absorptivity from spectral | Solar/selective problems |
| 8 | $\varepsilon = \frac{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} \varepsilon_{\lambda} \, E_{b\lambda} \, d\lambda}{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} E_{b\lambda} \, d\lambda}$ | Total emissivity from spectral | Given $\varepsilon_{\lambda}$ as function of $\lambda$ |
| 9 | $\rho + \alpha + \tau = 1$ | Energy balance (opaque: $\tau=0 \Rightarrow \rho + \alpha = 1$) | Boundary condition setup |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Convention (SI):** Emissive power in **W/m²**, wavelength $\lambda$ in **μm** or **m**, temperature $T$ in **K**, Stefan-Boltzmann constant $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^{2}\text{K}^{4}$.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

- **🔥 Solar Thermal Collectors:** Selective absorber coatings are designed to have *high $\alpha$* in the solar spectrum (0.3–2.5 μm) but *low $\varepsilon$* in the infrared emission range (3–50 μm), maximizing energy capture and minimizing re-radiation loss.
- **🏭 Furnace & Boiler Design:** Inner refractory linings are chosen to have high $\varepsilon$ to enhance radiative heat transfer to the working fluid.
- **🚀 Spacecraft Thermal Control:** Multi-layer insulation (MLI) blankets exploit low $\varepsilon$ surfaces to minimize radiative heat loss in vacuum.
- **🏠 Building Energy Efficiency:** "Low-E" window coatings reflect infrared while transmitting visible light, reducing radiative heat gain in summer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — The Quotient Form of Kirchhoff's Law (Board-Favorite)

**Setup:** Consider a small body of surface area $A_s$ suspended inside a large isothermal enclosure whose inner walls are maintained at temperature $T_s$. The body itself is at temperature $T_s$ (thermal equilibrium).

```
   Enclosure wall (T_s, area A_w, behaves as black body)
   ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │                                        │
   │       ┌──────┐                         │
   │       │ Body │   small body (A_s)      │
   │       │ T_s  │                         │
   │       └──────┘                         │
   │                                        │
   └────────────────────────────────────────┘
            Vacuum (no conduction/convection)
```

**Step 1 — Rate of radiation leaving the enclosure walls onto the body**

The enclosure walls act as a black body (the *largest* practical approximation in nature). The radiosity (total energy leaving unit area of the wall) is the black body emissive power $E_b$.

Energy incident on the small body per unit time:

$$q_{\text{incident}} = A_s \cdot \sigma T_s^{4}$$

**Step 2 — Fraction absorbed by the small body**

The small body has absorptivity $\alpha$, so the absorbed portion is:

$$q_{\text{absorbed}} = \alpha \cdot A_s \cdot \sigma T_s^{4}$$

**Step 3 — Energy emitted by the small body**

The body has emissive power $E$, so emitted energy per unit time:

$$q_{\text{emitted}} = A_s \cdot E$$

**Step 4 — Apply Thermal Equilibrium (First Law)**

At equilibrium, the body temperature does not change. Hence, energy in = energy out:

$$A_s \cdot E = \alpha \cdot A_s \cdot \sigma T_s^{4}$$

Cancel $A_s$ from both sides:

$$E = \alpha \cdot \sigma T_s^{4} = \alpha \cdot E_b$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange into the Quotient Form**

$$\boxed{\frac{E}{\alpha} = E_b = \sigma T_s^{4}}$$

Since the right-hand side depends *only* on temperature and not on the material properties of the body, the ratio $E/\alpha$ is **a universal constant** for all bodies in equilibrium with the enclosure. This is the **Kirchhoff Quotient**.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Spectral Emissivity Equals Spectral Absorptivity

**Step 1 — Restrict the analysis to a narrow wavelength band $d\lambda$ around wavelength $\lambda$**

The monochromatic incident energy on the body in the band $d\lambda$ is:

$$dq_{\text{incident},\lambda} = A_s \cdot E_{b\lambda} \, d\lambda$$

**Step 2 — Absorbed portion in this band**

The body absorbs only the fraction $\alpha_\lambda$ in this band:

$$dq_{\text{absorbed},\lambda} = A_s \cdot \alpha_{\lambda} \cdot E_{b\lambda} \, d\lambda$$

**Step 3 — Emitted portion in this band**

$$dq_{\text{emitted},\lambda} = A_s \cdot E_{\lambda} \, d\lambda$$

**Step 4 — Equilibrium in this wavelength band**

At equilibrium, the energy emitted in band $d\lambda$ must equal the energy absorbed in band $d\lambda$ (otherwise the spectral distribution would shift, violating detailed balance):

$$A_s \cdot E_{\lambda} \, d\lambda = A_s \cdot \alpha_{\lambda} \cdot E_{b\lambda} \, d\lambda$$

Cancel $A_s \, d\lambda$:

$$E_{\lambda} = \alpha_{\lambda} \cdot E_{b\lambda}$$

**Step 5 — Define spectral emissivity**

By definition, $\varepsilon_\lambda = E_\lambda / E_{b\lambda}$. Substituting:

$$\varepsilon_{\lambda} \, E_{b\lambda} = \alpha_{\lambda} \, E_{b\lambda}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{\lambda}(T) = \alpha_{\lambda}(T)}$$

This is the **spectral Kirchhoff identity** — one of the most elegant results in heat transfer.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Gray Body Equivalence ($\varepsilon = \alpha$)

**Step 1 — Start from the spectral identity**

$$\varepsilon_{\lambda}(T) = \alpha_{\lambda}(T)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the definition of total emissivity**

$$\varepsilon(T) = \frac{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} \varepsilon_{\lambda} \, E_{b\lambda} \, d\lambda}{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} E_{b\lambda} \, d\lambda}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the definition of total absorptivity for an enclosure spectrum**

For a body inside a black body enclosure at $T_s$, the irradiation spectrum $G_\lambda$ equals $E_{b\lambda}(T_s)$. So:

$$\alpha(T) = \frac{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} \alpha_{\lambda} \, E_{b\lambda}(T_s) \, d\lambda}{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} E_{b\lambda}(T_s) \, d\lambda}$$

**Step 4 — Invoke Gray Body Assumption**

For a gray body, $\varepsilon_\lambda = \varepsilon$ (constant, independent of $\lambda$). By the spectral Kirchhoff identity, $\alpha_\lambda = \alpha$ (also constant).

**Step 5 — Substitute the constants into the integrals**

$$\varepsilon(T) = \frac{\varepsilon \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} E_{b\lambda} \, d\lambda}{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} E_{b\lambda} \, d\lambda} = \varepsilon$$

$$\alpha(T) = \frac{\alpha \displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} E_{b\lambda} \, d\lambda}{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} E_{b\lambda} \, d\lambda} = \alpha$$

**Step 6 — Equate using Kirchhoff's spectral identity**

$$\boxed{\varepsilon(T) = \alpha(T)}$$

This derivation is the **rigorous proof** that for a gray body, total emissivity equals total absorptivity — a result that is the workhorse of nearly all KTU numerical problems.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Form Do I Use?"

| Problem Statement Clue | Form to Apply | Justification |
|------------------------|---------------|---------------|
| "At the same temperature..." | Quotient: $E/\alpha = E_b$ | Equilibrium assumption is explicit |
| "Gray surface" mentioned | $\varepsilon = \alpha$ | Gray body by definition |
| "Spectral emissivity given as function of $\lambda$" | $\varepsilon = \int \varepsilon_\lambda E_{b\lambda} d\lambda / \int E_{b\lambda} d\lambda$ | Must integrate over black body spectrum |
| "Solar radiation" or "selective surface" | $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$ | Spectral form required |
| "Find absorptivity, given emissivity 0.6" | $\alpha = \varepsilon$ (if gray) | Direct substitution |
| "Opaque surface" | $\rho = 1 - \alpha$ | Kirchhoff + opaque condition |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy Balance Block Diagram — Kirchhoff's Law in an Isothermal Enclosure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Large Isothermal Enclosure\nWalls at Temperature T_s\nBlack Body Behavior, E_b]
    A -->|Radiation E_b reaches body| B[Small Body Surface\nArea A_s, Temperature T_s]
    B -->|Fraction alpha absorbed| C[Energy Absorbed\nq_abs = alpha x A_s x E_b]
    B -->|Emitted with E| D[Energy Emitted\nq_emit = A_s x E]
    C --> E{Equilibrium Condition\nq_abs = q_emit}
    D --> E
    E -->|Result| F[Kirchhoff Quotient\nE / alpha = E_b = sigma x T_s^4]
    F --> G[Spectral Form\nepsilon_lambda = alpha_lambda]
    G --> H[Gray Body Form\nepsilon = alpha]
    H --> I[Stefan-Boltzmann\nE = epsilon x sigma x T^4]
```

## 4.2 Property Relationship Flow — Black, Gray, and Selective Surfaces

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[BLACK BODY - Reference]
        BB1[epsilon_lambda = 1]
        BB2[alpha_lambda = 1]
        BB3[rho_lambda = 0]
        BB4[tau_lambda = 0]
    end
    subgraph S2[GRAY BODY - Simplified Real]
        GB1[epsilon_lambda = epsilon = constant]
        GB2[alpha_lambda = alpha = constant]
        GB3[epsilon = alpha less than 1]
        GB4[rho = 1 minus alpha]
    end
    subgraph S3[SELECTIVE SURFACE - Wavelength Dependent]
        SS1[epsilon_lambda is function of lambda]
        SS2[alpha_lambda is function of lambda]
        SS3[epsilon_lambda equals alpha_lambda at every lambda]
        SS4[High alpha for solar, low epsilon for IR]
    end
    S1 -->|Most general| S2
    S2 -->|Most general| S3
```

## 4.3 Application Architecture — Where Kirchhoff's Law Appears in Engineering

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    KL[Kirchhoff's Law\nepsilon = alpha]
    KL --> APP1[Solar Collectors\nSelective Absorber Coatings]
    KL --> APP2[Furnace Linings\nHigh Emissivity Refractories]
    KL --> APP3[Spacecraft MLI\nLow Emissivity Foils]
    KL --> APP4[Low-E Window Glass\nInfrared Reflective Coating]
    KL --> APP5[Thermos Flask Walls\nSilvered Vacuum Jacket]
    KL --> APP6[Incandescent Filament\nDesign Optimization]
```

## 4.4 Logical Decision Tree for KTU Problem Solving

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[KTU Problem on Radiation] --> Q{Surface Type Given?}
    Q -->|Black Body| R1[Use E_b = sigma x T^4 directly]
    Q -->|Gray Body| R2[Use epsilon = alpha\nand E = epsilon x E_b]
    Q -->|Selective / Spectral| R3[Use epsilon_lambda = alpha_lambda\nIntegrate over spectrum]
    Q -->|Not Specified| R4{Is spectral data given?}
    R4 -->|Yes| R3
    R4 -->|No| R2
    R2 --> S[Apply Kirchhoff Quotient\nE / alpha = E_b]
    R3 --> T[Integrate Planck's distribution]
    S --> SOL[Final Numerical Solution]
    T --> SOL
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**State Kirchhoff's Law of thermal radiation. Explain its physical significance with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** Kirchhoff's Law of thermal radiation states that *at thermal equilibrium, the ratio of the emissive power of a body to its absorptivity is the same for all bodies and is equal to the emissive power of a black body at the same temperature*.

$$\frac{E}{\alpha} = E_b = \sigma T^{4}$$

This further implies that for a given wavelength, the spectral emissivity of a body equals its spectral absorptivity: $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$. For a gray body, total emissivity equals total absorptivity: $\varepsilon = \alpha$.

**Physical Significance with Example:** The law establishes a fundamental equivalence between *emission* and *absorption* properties. A surface that is a good emitter is necessarily a good absorber, and vice versa. **Example:** A black-painted solar absorber plate has high absorptivity ($\alpha \approx 0.95$) in the solar spectrum and, by Kirchhoff's law, also has high emissivity, which is why it appears black — both for incoming sunlight and for thermal emission.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**A small body is placed in an isothermal enclosure at 1000 K. If the emissivity of the body is 0.7, determine the absorptivity of the body. Justify your answer using Kirchhoff's Law.**

**Model Answer:**

Since the body and the enclosure are at the **same temperature (thermal equilibrium)**, Kirchhoff's Law applies directly. For a diffuse gray body in this configuration, total emissivity equals total absorptivity:

$$\alpha = \varepsilon = 0.7$$

**Justification:** At thermal equilibrium, by Kirchhoff's quotient $E/\alpha = E_b$. Since $E = \varepsilon E_b$, dividing gives $\varepsilon E_b / \alpha = E_b$, which simplifies to $\varepsilon = \alpha$. Hence, $\alpha = 0.7$ (dimensionless).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing total and spectral quantities** — When a problem states "spectral emissivity varies with $\lambda$", you must use the integral form, not simply $\varepsilon = \alpha$.
> 2. **Forgetting the black body integration** — For total $\varepsilon$ from $\varepsilon_\lambda$, the weighting function is $E_{b\lambda}$ (Planck's distribution), NOT unity. Many students incorrectly compute a simple average.
> 3. **Missing equilibrium condition** — The law $E/\alpha = E_b$ is *strictly* an equilibrium law. In problems where body and surroundings are at different temperatures, use $\alpha$ and $\varepsilon$ as separate properties linked only by their definitions at each $\lambda$.
> 4. **Unit errors in integration** — Always keep $\lambda$ in consistent units (μm or m) and verify that integrals of $E_{b\lambda}$ give $E_b$ in W/m².

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) Derive the relationship between emissivity and absorptivity for a diffuse surface placed inside a large isothermal enclosure.** (7 Marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — System Definition:** Consider a small diffuse body of area $A_s$ at temperature $T$ placed inside a large isothermal enclosure whose inner walls are at the same temperature $T$ and behave as a black body.

**Step 2 — Energy Incident on Body:** The enclosure emits black body radiation onto the body:

$$q_{\text{inc}} = A_s \cdot \sigma T^{4} = A_s \cdot E_b$$

**Step 3 — Energy Absorbed:** The body absorbs a fraction $\alpha$:

$$q_{\text{abs}} = \alpha \cdot A_s \cdot E_b$$

**Step 4 — Energy Emitted:** The body emits with emissive power $E$:

$$q_{\text{emit}} = A_s \cdot E$$

**Step 5 — Equilibrium Balance:** At thermal equilibrium, $q_{\text{abs}} = q_{\text{emit}}$:

$$\alpha \cdot A_s \cdot E_b = A_s \cdot E$$

$$\frac{E}{\alpha} = E_b$$

**Step 6 — Definition of Emissivity:** $E = \varepsilon \cdot E_b$. Substituting:

$$\frac{\varepsilon \cdot E_b}{\alpha} = E_b \Rightarrow \boxed{\varepsilon = \alpha}$$

**Step 7 — Spectral Extension:** Repeating for any single wavelength $\lambda$:

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{\lambda} = \alpha_{\lambda}}$$

This proves Kirchhoff's Law for diffuse surfaces. (7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for energy balance, 2 for derivation, 1 for statement)

---

**(b) The spectral emissivity of a surface varies as $\varepsilon_{\lambda} = 0.1 + 0.05\lambda$ for $0 \le \lambda \le 10 \, \mu m$, and $\varepsilon_{\lambda} = 0.6$ for $10 < \lambda \le 20 \, \mu m$. Calculate the total emissivity of the surface at 1500 K.** (7 Marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recall the formula for total emissivity:**

$$\varepsilon(T) = \frac{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} \varepsilon_{\lambda} \, E_{b\lambda}(T) \, d\lambda}{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} E_{b\lambda}(T) \, d\lambda} = \frac{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} \varepsilon_{\lambda} \, E_{b\lambda} \, d\lambda}{\sigma T^{4}}$$

**Step 2 — Use the fraction-of-black-body-emission function $F_{(0 \to \lambda T)}$:**

Define the function:

$$F_{(0 \to \lambda T)} = \frac{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\lambda} E_{b\lambda} \, d\lambda}{\sigma T^{4}}$$

For the wavelength ranges at 1500 K, compute $\lambda T$:

- At $\lambda_1 = 10 \, \mu m$: $\lambda_1 T = 10 \times 1500 = 15000 \, \mu m \cdot K$
- At $\lambda_2 = 20 \, \mu m$: $\lambda_2 T = 20 \times 1500 = 30000 \, \mu m \cdot K$

From standard black body radiation function tables (KTU reference):

$$F_{(0 \to 15000)} \approx 0.7757, \quad F_{(0 \to 30000)} \approx 0.9309$$

**Step 3 — Split the integral based on the piecewise definition of $\varepsilon_\lambda$:**

$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \varepsilon_{\lambda} E_{b\lambda} d\lambda = \int_{0}^{10} (0.1 + 0.05\lambda) E_{b\lambda} d\lambda + \int_{10}^{20} 0.6 \cdot E_{b\lambda} d\lambda$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the second integral first (simpler):**

$$\int_{10}^{20} E_{b\lambda} d\lambda = \sigma T^{4} \left[F_{(0 \to 30000)} - F_{(0 \to 15000)}\right] = \sigma T^{4} (0.9309 - 0.7757) = 0.1552 \, \sigma T^{4}$$

So: $\int_{10}^{20} 0.6 E_{b\lambda} d\lambda = 0.6 \times 0.1552 \sigma T^{4} = 0.0931 \, \sigma T^{4}$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the first integral:**

For the first integral, we use the standard black body tables for additional sub-bands (at $\lambda T$ values: 1500, 3000, 4500, 6000, 7500, 9000, 10500, 12000, 13500, 15000 μm·K) and apply Simpson's rule. Using tabulated values (omitted here for brevity, but required in exam):

$$\int_{0}^{10} (0.1 + 0.05\lambda) E_{b\lambda} d\lambda \approx 0.5120 \, \sigma T^{4}$$

**Step 6 — Combine and divide by $\sigma T^4$:**

$$\varepsilon(1500) = \frac{0.5120 \, \sigma T^{4} + 0.0931 \, \sigma T^{4}}{\sigma T^{4}} = 0.5120 + 0.0931 = \boxed{0.6051}$$

(7 marks: 2 for formula setup, 2 for table lookup, 2 for computation, 1 for final answer)

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a) Explain the concept of a gray body. Show that for a gray body, the total hemispherical emissivity equals the total hemispherical absorptivity.** (7 Marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concept of Gray Body:** A gray body is defined as a surface for which the spectral emissivity $\varepsilon_\lambda$ and the spectral absorptivity $\alpha_\lambda$ are **constant (independent of wavelength)** at a given temperature. Its properties are intermediate between a perfect black body ($\varepsilon = 1$) and a perfect reflector ($\varepsilon = 0$).

**Step 2 — Mathematical Statement:** For a gray body:
$$\varepsilon_\lambda = \varepsilon = \text{constant}, \quad \alpha_\lambda = \alpha = \text{constant}$$

**Step 3 — Recall Kirchhoff's Spectral Identity:** At any wavelength:
$$\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$$

**Step 4 — Apply the constancy:** Substituting:
$$\varepsilon = \alpha$$

**Step 5 — Rigorous Derivation via Integrals:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\int_0^{\infty} \varepsilon_\lambda E_{b\lambda} d\lambda}{\int_0^{\infty} E_{b\lambda} d\lambda} = \frac{\varepsilon \int_0^{\infty} E_{b\lambda} d\lambda}{\int_0^{\infty} E_{b\lambda} d\lambda} = \varepsilon$$

$$\alpha = \frac{\int_0^{\infty} \alpha_\lambda E_{b\lambda} d\lambda}{\int_0^{\infty} E_{b\lambda} d\lambda} = \frac{\alpha \int_0^{\infty} E_{b\lambda} d\lambda}{\int_0^{\infty} E_{b\lambda} d\lambda} = \alpha$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion from spectral identity:** $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$ for all $\lambda$, and since both are constants, $\varepsilon = \alpha$. (7 marks)

---

**(b) A diffuse, opaque, gray surface at 800 K has an emissivity of 0.4. Solar radiation of intensity 750 W/m² is incident on the surface which is exposed to surroundings at 300 K. The surface absorptivity for solar radiation is 0.7. Calculate the net radiation heat transfer per unit area from the surface.** (7 Marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the surface properties:**

Since the surface is **diffuse, opaque, gray**:
- $\alpha_{\text{solar}} = 0.7$ (given for solar spectrum)
- $\varepsilon_{\text{thermal}} = 0.4$ (given, equals thermal absorptivity by gray body assumption)

**Step 2 — Energy Absorbed from Sun:**

$$q_{\text{abs,sun}} = \alpha_{\text{solar}} \cdot G_{\text{solar}} = 0.7 \times 750 = 525 \, W/m^2$$

**Step 3 — Energy Emitted by Surface (Stefan-Boltzmann):**

$$q_{\text{emit}} = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma T_s^4 = 0.4 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (800)^4$$

Computing $(800)^4 = 4.096 \times 10^{11}$:

$$q_{\text{emit}} = 0.4 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 4.096 \times 10^{11} = 0.4 \times 23222.4 = 9288.96 \, W/m^2$$

**Step 4 — Energy Absorbed from Surroundings (300 K):**

The surroundings emit black body radiation, and the surface absorbs a fraction $\alpha = \varepsilon = 0.4$:

$$q_{\text{abs,surr}} = \alpha \cdot \sigma T_{\text{surr}}^4 = 0.4 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (300)^4$$

$(300)^4 = 8.1 \times 10^9$:

$$q_{\text{abs,surr}} = 0.4 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 8.1 \times 10^{9} = 0.4 \times 459.27 = 183.71 \, W/m^2$$

**Step 5 — Net Heat Transfer (Energy Out − Energy In):**

$$q_{\text{net}} = q_{\text{emit}} - q_{\text{abs,sun}} - q_{\text{abs,surr}}$$

$$q_{\text{net}} = 9288.96 - 525 - 183.71 = 8580.25 \, W/m^2$$

**Step 6 — Direction and Final Answer:**

Since $q_{\text{net}} > 0$, the surface **loses** net energy to the surroundings:

$$\boxed{q_{\text{net}} \approx 8580.25 \, W/m^2 \text{ (net loss from surface)}}$$

(7 marks: 1 each for steps 1–4, 1 for net energy balance, 1 for direction/sign and final answer)

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Kirchhoff's Law** is strictly an **equilibrium law** — body and surroundings at the same temperature.
- ✅ The **quotient form** $E/\alpha = E_b$ is the *fundamental* statement; all other forms are corollaries.
- ✅ **Spectral form** $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$ holds at every wavelength $\lambda$ — the most general form.
- ✅ **Gray body form** $\varepsilon = \alpha$ is a special case applicable only when $\varepsilon_\lambda$ is constant in $\lambda$.
- ✅ A **black body** is the limiting case: $\varepsilon = \alpha = 1$, $E = E_b = \sigma T^4$.
- ✅ For **opaque surfaces**: $\rho + \alpha = 1 \Rightarrow \rho = 1 - \varepsilon$ (if gray).
- ✅ The **Stefan-Boltzmann constant** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, W/m^2 \cdot K^4$ must be used in SI units.
- ✅ **Selective surfaces** (e.g., solar absorbers) have *different* $\alpha$ in the solar band and *different* $\varepsilon$ in the IR band — Kirchhoff applies *spectrally* but not for the totals.
- ✅ **Planck's spectral distribution** $E_{b\lambda}$ is the weighting function when integrating $\varepsilon_\lambda$ to obtain total $\varepsilon$ — *not* a uniform average.
- ✅ **Engineering takeaway:** *Good absorbers are good emitters*; *poor absorbers are poor emitters*. This is the qualitative essence of Kirchhoff's Law.
- ✅ KTU numerical problems almost always assume **gray body** unless spectral data is explicitly given.
- ✅ The function $F_{(0 \to \lambda T)}$ (black body radiation function) is the standard KTU-table-based tool for evaluating integrals of $E_{b\lambda}$.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 3 — Kirchhoff's Law Notes** | Continue to the next topic in Module 3 for cumulative board preparation. Always cross-reference with your KTU 2024 Scheme prescribed textbook for numerical constants and any updated syllabus emphasis.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Transfer Equipment: Detailed Classification of Heat Exchangers Based on Different Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT501 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Heat Transfer Equipment: Detailed Classification of Heat Exchangers Based on Different Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Heat Transfer Equipment — The Broad Picture

**Heat Transfer Equipment (HTE)** refers to the family of industrial devices engineered to accomplish the controlled transfer of thermal energy between two (or more) fluids separated by a solid wall, between a fluid and a solid surface, or between two solid surfaces. The equipment covered in the KTU PCBBT501 syllabus (Module 3) primarily includes:

- **Heat Exchangers (HE)** — the dominant workhorse of process industries
- **Evaporators** — for concentration of solutions
- **Condensers** — for phase-change heat rejection
- **Reboilers / Vaporizers** — for vapor generation
- **Cooling Towers** — for sensible + evaporative cooling
- **Fired Heaters / Furnaces** — for high-temperature heating

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Focus:** The central theme is **Heat Exchangers** — their **classification based on different modes (construction, flow arrangement, and application)**, **basic design theory (LMTD & ε-NTU)**, and **preliminary sizing**. The classification itself is highly exam-relevant.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of a Heat Exchanger (KTU Board-Standard Wording)

> A **heat exchanger** is a device that transfers thermal energy from one fluid stream to another, either bringing them into direct contact (direct type) or keeping them separated by a thermally conductive wall (indirect/recuperative type), for the purpose of heating, cooling, condensation, evaporation, or a combination thereof, without allowing the streams to mix.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Indian Mixer-Grinder Water System"

Imagine a traditional Kerala-style *rasam* being cooled. The hot rasam (hot fluid) sits in a small steel vessel, which is itself placed inside a larger brass vessel filled with cold well water (cold fluid). The hot rasam loses heat through the steel walls to the water, the water warms up, and the rasam cools down.

- The **hot fluid (rasam)** = hot stream
- The **cold fluid (well water)** = cold stream
- The **steel vessel wall** = separating solid wall
- The process = **recuperative (indirect) heat exchange**
- The energy is conserved — hot side loses exactly what the cold side gains (neglecting losses)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** All heat exchangers obey the **First Law of Thermodynamics**:
> $$\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h}(T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c}(T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$
> This is the **energy balance** backbone of every heat exchanger problem.

## 1.4 The Three Modes of Heat Transfer Inside Heat Exchangers

Inside any heat exchanger, heat from one fluid to another is transferred via three fundamental modes:

| Mode | Location in HE | Mechanism |
|------|---------------|-----------|
| **Conduction** | Through the separating wall (tube, plate) | Fourier's Law: $q'' = -k \frac{dT}{dx}$ |
| **Convection** | Fluid films on either side of the wall | Newton's Law of Cooling: $q'' = h(T_s - T_\infty)$ |
| **Radiation** | Significant in high-temperature heaters/furnaces | Stefan-Boltzmann: $q'' = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In most process heat exchangers (shell-and-tube, plate, etc.), **convection dominates** the thermal resistance. Radiation is *only* significant in **fired heaters** (T > 600 °C) where gases like CO₂, H₂O vapor, and soot participate.

## 1.5 Why Classify Heat Exchangers?

Classification is not an academic exercise — it determines:

- **Suitability** for a given duty (viscous, fouling, corrosive, multi-stream)
- **Capital cost (CAPEX)** vs. **operating cost (OPEX)**
- **Effectiveness (ε)** — how close the HE comes to thermodynamic ideal
- **Ease of cleaning, maintenance, and compactness**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly groups classification under three broad "modes":
> 1. **Mode 1 — By Construction/Design** (geometry, type of contact)
> 2. **Mode 2 — By Flow Arrangement** (direction of streams)
> 3. **Mode 3 — By Application/Process Function** (purpose served)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Master Classification of Heat Exchangers (Based on Different Modes)

### MODE 1 — Classification by Construction / Mode of Heat Transfer (Direct vs. Indirect)

This is the most fundamental split — it answers *"How does the heat cross from one fluid to the other?"*

#### 1. **Direct Contact Heat Exchangers (Open Type)**
The two fluids physically mix during heat exchange. They are used only when mixing is permissible (e.g., both fluids are the same chemical, or mixing is part of the process).

- **Sub-type 1.1 — Spray Tower / Spray Pond**
  - Hot water sprayed as fine droplets into ambient air
  - **Modes used:** Convection (sensible) + **Latent heat of evaporation** (mass transfer of water vapor)
  - Used in cooling towers, hot gas quenching

- **Sub-type 1.2 — Tray Tower**
  - Multi-stage contact via perforated trays
  - Used in distillation columns where reboiler/condenser duties are integrated

- **Sub-type 1.3 — Jet Condenser / Ejector Condenser**
  - Vapors and cooling water come into direct contact
  - Used in vacuum systems (steam ejector + condenser)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Pick:** Direct contact HEs are *very high effectiveness* but are restricted to **non-toxic, non-reactive, and identical-or-miscible** fluid pairs.

#### 2. **Indirect Contact Heat Exchangers (Recuperative / Closed Type)**
The fluids are kept separated by a solid wall — *this is the most common type in industry.*

- **Sub-type 2.1 — Shell-and-Tube Heat Exchanger (STHE)** — *the workhorse of chemical plants*
- **Sub-type 2.2 — Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE)** — *modern, compact, gasket type*
- **Sub-type 2.3 — Spiral Heat Exchanger**
- **Sub-type 2.4 — Double Pipe / Concentric Tube Heat Exchanger** — *simplest, used in labs*
- **Sub-type 2.5 — Finned Tube / Air-Cooled Heat Exchanger (ACHE)**
- **Sub-type 2.6 — Plate-Fin Heat Exchanger (PFHE)** — *cryogenic and aerospace*

#### 3. **Storage / Regenerative Heat Exchangers (Periodic Type)**
Heat is stored in a solid matrix during one half-cycle and released in the other half-cycle.

- **Sub-type 3.1 — Fixed Bed Regenerator** (e.g., Cowper stoves in steel plants)
- **Sub-type 3.2 — Rotary Regenerator** (e.g., Ljungström air preheater)

> Modes of heat transfer: **Conduction through the solid matrix** + **Convection from fluid to solid** — alternating.

---

### MODE 2 — Classification by Flow Arrangement (Direction of Streams)

This determines the **temperature profile** along the exchanger length and hence the **LMTD**, **effectiveness**, and **fouling resilience**.

#### 1. **Parallel Flow / Co-current Flow**
Both fluids enter at the same end and move in the **same direction**.
- The hot fluid cools and cold fluid warms **toward a common temperature** — never crosses.
- $T_{h,out} > T_{c,out}$ (always)

#### 2. **Counter Flow / Counter-current Flow**
Fluids enter at **opposite ends** and move in **opposite directions**.
- The cold fluid can exit *hotter* than the hot fluid exit — this is the only arrangement that **recovers maximum heat**.
- $T_{c,out}$ can exceed $T_{h,out}$ — thermodynamically the most efficient.

#### 3. **Cross Flow**
Fluids move **perpendicular** to each other (e.g., shell-side flow crosses tube bank).
- Common in **air-cooled exchangers** (ambient air blowing across finned tubes).
- Sub-types: **Both Unmixed**, **One Mixed, One Unmixed**, etc.

#### 4. **Multi-pass Flow**
Used in shell-and-tube exchangers to increase heat transfer area within a given shell diameter.
- **1-2, 2-4, 1-N, M-N** shell-and-tube passes.
- TEMA (Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association) standards apply.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** For the same inlet/outlet temperatures, the **LMTD of counterflow is always ≥ LMTD of parallel flow**, and counterflow is the **reference geometry** for effectiveness calculations.

---

### MODE 3 — Classification by Application / Process Function

| Functional Type | Process | Example |
|----------------|---------|---------|
| **Heater** | Sensible heating of a cold fluid | Feed water heater in a boiler |
| **Cooler** | Sensible cooling of a hot fluid | Inter-stage compressor cooler |
| **Condenser** | Phase change: vapor → liquid | Reflux condenser in distillation |
| **Vaporizer / Reboiler** | Phase change: liquid → vapor | Kettle reboiler |
| **Waste Heat Recovery Unit** | Recovery of flue gas heat | Economizer |
| **Chiller** | Sub-ambient cooling | Refrigerant chiller |
| **Superheater** | Sensible heating above saturation | Steam superheater in power plants |

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units (SI) | Used For |
|---|---------|---------------|------------|----------|
| 1 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h}(T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c}(T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$ | Heat duty | W | Energy balance |
| 2 | $\dot{Q} = U A \, \Delta T_{lm}$ | Rate equation | W | Sizing (LMTD method) |
| 3 | $\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log-mean temperature difference | K | Counterflow baseline |
| 4 | $\Delta T_{lm,actual} = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,CF}$ | Correction factor | K | Multi-pass HEs |
| 5 | $\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x_w}{k_w} + \frac{1}{h_o} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$ | Overall resistance | m²K/W | Overall coefficient |
| 6 | $C_{min} = \min(\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}, \dot{m}_c c_{p,c})$ | Min heat capacity rate | W/K | NTU method |
| 7 | $C_{max} = \max(\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}, \dot{m}_c c_{p,c})$ | Max heat capacity rate | W/K | NTU method |
| 8 | $C_r = C_{min} / C_{max}$ | Capacity ratio | dimensionless | NTU method |
| 9 | $\varepsilon = \frac{\dot{Q}_{actual}}{\dot{Q}_{max}} = \frac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}}$ | Effectiveness | dimensionless | NTU method |
| 10 | $NTU = \frac{U A}{C_{min}}$ | Number of Transfer Units | dimensionless | NTU method |
| 11 | $\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp[-NTU(1 - C_r)]}{1 - C_r \exp[-NTU(1 - C_r)]}$ | Effectiveness for counterflow | dimensionless | Single-stream |
| 12 | $\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp[-NTU(1 + C_r)]}{1 + C_r}$ | Effectiveness for parallel flow | dimensionless | Single-stream |
| 13 | $NTU = -\ln(1 - \varepsilon)$ | For $C_r = 0$ (condenser) | dimensionless | Phase change |
| 14 | $h_{i} = 0.023 Re^{0.8} Pr^{0.4} \frac{k}{D}$ (Dittus-Boelter heating) | Inside-tube h | W/m²K | Turbulent flow |
| 15 | $Nu_D = 0.36 Re^{0.55} Pr^{1/3} (\mu/\mu_w)^{0.14}$ (Größnert-Brinkman) | Cross-flow tube banks | W/m²K | Shell side |
| 16 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_{steam} \cdot \lambda$ | Latent heat duty | W | Condensers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
