> **Vertical pipe `|` rule:** All mathematical bars and absolute-value operators above are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve Markdown table integrity. KTU answer scripts must use absolute-value or descriptive phrasing instead of `|` inside tables.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of This Classification

- **Petrochemical refineries** rely on **shell-and-tube, multi-pass, counterflow** exchangers for crude preheating, overhead condensation, and product cooling.
- **Dairy and food industries** use **plate heat exchangers** for pasteurization due to high heat transfer coefficients and CIP (clean-in-place) capability.
- **Cryogenic LNG plants** use **plate-fin exchangers** for compact, multi-stream duties (up to 10+ streams in one block).
- **Power plants** use **regenerative air preheaters** (Ljungström) for thermodynamic efficiency.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Log-Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)

The LMTD is the **engine of HE design** under the KTU syllabus. We derive it for a counterflow exchanger.

### Step 1 — Local Heat Transfer
For a differential element $dA$ at distance $x$ along the exchanger:

$$d\dot{Q} = U \, (T_h - T_c) \, dA$$

### Step 2 — Energy Balance on Each Fluid

For the hot fluid losing heat:

$$d\dot{Q} = -\dot{m}_h c_{p,h} \, dT_h$$

For the cold fluid gaining heat:

$$d\dot{Q} = +\dot{m}_c c_{p,c} \, dT_c$$

### Step 3 — Form the Temperature Difference Derivative

Let $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$. Differentiate:

$$d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c$$

From the energy balances:

$$dT_h = -\frac{d\dot{Q}}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}}, \quad dT_c = +\frac{d\dot{Q}}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}$$

Therefore:

$$d(\Delta T) = -\frac{d\dot{Q}}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} - \frac{d\dot{Q}}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}$$

Combining the two capacity rates:

$$\frac{1}{C_h} = \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}}, \quad \frac{1}{C_c} = \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}$$

We get:

$$d(\Delta T) = -d\dot{Q}\left(\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right) = -d\dot{Q}\left(\frac{C_h + C_c}{C_h C_c}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Substitute $d\dot{Q} = U \, \Delta T \, dA$

$$d(\Delta T) = -U \Delta T \, dA \left(\frac{C_h + C_c}{C_h C_c}\right)$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \left(\frac{C_h + C_c}{C_h C_c}\right) dA$$

### Step 5 — Integrate from Inlet (1) to Outlet (2)

$$\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \left(\frac{C_h + C_c}{C_h C_c}\right) \int_0^A dA$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -U A \left(\frac{C_h + C_c}{C_h C_c}\right)$$

Exponentiating and solving for $A$:

$$A = \frac{1}{U} \cdot \frac{C_h C_c}{C_h + C_c} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)$$

But the **total heat duty** is:

$$\dot{Q} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\dfrac{1}{C_c} - \dfrac{1}{C_h}} = \frac{C_h C_c}{C_h - C_c}(\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) \quad \text{(for counterflow)}$$

Substituting and simplifying:

$$A = \frac{\dot{Q}}{U} \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1 \cdot \Delta T_2 \cdot \ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)} \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 \cdot \Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}$$

After cancellation:

$$\boxed{A = \frac{\dot{Q}}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}}}$$

where the **Log-Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)** is:

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}}$$

with $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$ for counterflow.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Effectiveness for Counterflow (NTU Method)

### Step 1 — Define Effectiveness and Capacity Ratio

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\dot{Q}}{\dot{Q}_{max}} = \frac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}}$$

For a counterflow exchanger where $C_{min}$ is the minimum capacity rate:

$$\dot{Q}_{max} = C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$$

### Step 2 — Energy Balances on Each Side

Assume $C_{min} = C_c$ (cold side is minimum). Then:

$$T_{c,out} = T_{c,in} + \frac{\dot{Q}}{C_{min}}$$
$$T_{h,out} = T_{h,in} - \frac{\dot{Q}}{C_{max}}$$

For counterflow, define the temperature difference at each end:

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in} - \frac{\dot{Q}}{C_{min}}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = T_{h,in} - \frac{\dot{Q}}{C_{max}} - T_{c,in}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute $\dot{Q} = \varepsilon C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$

Let $\theta = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$ (max possible temperature span).

$$\Delta T_1 = \theta - \varepsilon \theta = \theta(1 - \varepsilon)$$

$$\Delta T_2 = \theta - \frac{\varepsilon C_{min}\theta}{C_{max}} = \theta\left(1 - \varepsilon C_r\right)$$

where $C_r = C_{min}/C_{max}$.

### Step 4 — Apply LMTD Relation with $\dot{Q} = U A \Delta T_{lm} = \varepsilon C_{min} \theta$

$$\varepsilon C_{min} \theta = U A \cdot \frac{\theta(1-\varepsilon) - \theta(1-\varepsilon C_r)}{\ln\left[\dfrac{1-\varepsilon}{1-\varepsilon C_r}\right]}$$

$$\varepsilon C_{min} = \frac{UA}{C_{min}} \cdot \frac{C_{min} \cdot \varepsilon(1 - C_r)}{\ln\left[\dfrac{1-\varepsilon}{1-\varepsilon C_r}\right]}$$

Recognizing $NTU = UA / C_{min}$:

$$1 = \frac{NTU \cdot (1 - C_r)}{\ln\left[\dfrac{1-\varepsilon}{1-\varepsilon C_r}\right]}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for ε

$$\ln\left[\frac{1-\varepsilon}{1-\varepsilon C_r}\right] = -NTU(1 - C_r)$$

$$\frac{1-\varepsilon}{1-\varepsilon C_r} = \exp[-NTU(1 - C_r)]$$

$$1 - \varepsilon = (1 - \varepsilon C_r) \exp[-NTU(1 - C_r)]$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp[-NTU(1 - C_r)]}{1 - C_r \exp[-NTU(1 - C_r)]}}$$

This is the **canonical counterflow effectiveness equation** (for $C_r < 1$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Case — $C_r = 0$ (e.g., condenser with phase change on one side):** The exponential limit yields $\varepsilon = 1 - \exp(-NTU)$, which simplifies to $NTU = -\ln(1 - \varepsilon)$.

---

## 3.3 LMTD Correction Factor F (for Multi-pass / Cross-flow)

For non-counterflow geometries, the LMTD derived above is *not* directly usable. We apply a **correction factor F**:

$$\Delta T_{lm,actual} = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,CF}$$

where $F$ is read from standard charts (KTU textbooks provide these) as a function of two dimensionless groups:

$$P = \frac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}}, \quad R = \frac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Rule:** If $F < 0.75$, the design must be revised (more shell passes, etc.). A value $F \geq 0.75$ is industrially accepted.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Counterflow HE Sizing (LMTD Method)

**Problem:** A counterflow shell-and-tube exchanger cools 2.5 kg/s of oil ($c_p = 2.1$ kJ/kg·K) from 150 °C to 80 °C using water entering at 25 °C and exiting at 60 °C. The overall $U = 500$ W/m²·K. Find the area $A$ and LMTD.

### Step 1 — Heat Duty

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_o c_{p,o} (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})$$

$$\dot{Q} = 2.5 \times 2.1 \times 10^3 \times (150 - 80) = 3.675 \times 10^5 \text{ W}$$

### Step 2 — Verify Cold-Side Energy Balance

$$\dot{m}_w = \frac{\dot{Q}}{c_{p,w} (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})} = \frac{3.675 \times 10^5}{4180 \times 35} = 2.51 \text{ kg/s}$$

### Step 3 — Compute LMTD (Counterflow)

For counterflow:

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 150 - 60 = 90 \text{ °C}$$
$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 80 - 25 = 55 \text{ °C}$$

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{90 - 55}{\ln(90/55)} = \frac{35}{\ln(1.6364)} = \frac{35}{0.4925} = 71.07 \text{ °C}$$

### Step 4 — Calculate Area

$$A = \frac{\dot{Q}}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \frac{3.675 \times 10^5}{500 \times 71.07} = 10.34 \text{ m}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** $A \approx 10.3 \text{ m}^2$ — a small, compact exchanger sufficient for this duty.

---

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["HEAT TRANSFER EQUIPMENT"] --> B["Heat Exchangers"]
    A --> C["Evaporators"]
    A --> D["Condensers"]

    B --> G["MODE 1 By Construction"]
    B --> H["MODE 2 By Flow Arrangement"]

    G --> G1["Direct Contact"]
    G --> G2["Indirect Contact"]
    G --> G3["Storage Regenerative"]

    G1 --> G1a["Spray Tower"]
    G1 --> G1b["Tray Tower"]
    G1 --> G1c["Jet Condenser"]

    G2 --> G2a["Shell and Tube"]
    G2 --> G2b["Plate Type"]
    G2 --> G2c["Double Pipe"]
    G2 --> G2d["Air Cooled Finned"]
    G2 --> G2e["Plate Fin"]

    G3 --> G3a["Fixed Bed Cowper"]
    G3 --> G3b["Rotary Ljungstrom"]

    H --> H1["Parallel Co Current"]
    H --> H2["Counter Flow"]

    H --> H4["Multi Pass"]

    I --> I1["Heater"]
    I --> I2["Cooler"]
    I --> I3["Condenser"]

```

## 4.2 Counterflow vs. Parallel-Flow Temperature Profile (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph CF["COUNTERFLOW EXCHANGER"]
        direction LR
        A1["Hot In 150C"] -->|Hot Side| B1["Hot Out 80C"]
        D1["Cold In 25C"] -->|Cold Side| C1["Cold Out 60C"]
        A1 -.parallel path.-> B1
        D1 -.parallel path.-> C1
    end

    subgraph PF["PARALLEL CO CURRENT EXCHANGER"]
        direction LR

        D2 -.same direction.-> C2

```

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture of a Shell-and-Tube Heat Exchanger

```mermaid
graph TB
    INLET_H["Hot Fluid Inlet Nozzle"] --> SHELL_IN["Shell Side Entry"]
    SHELL_H1["Baffles Directing Shell Flow"] --> SHELL_MID["Cross Flow Over Tubes"]
    SHELL_MID --> SHELL_OUT["Shell Outlet Nozzle"]
    SHELL_OUT --> OUTLET_H["Hot Fluid Outlet"]

    INLET_C["Cold Fluid Inlet"] --> TUBE_INLET["Channel Head Inlet"]
    TUBE_INLET --> TUBE_PASS1["Tube Pass 1 Forward"]

    TUBE_RETURN --> TUBE_PASS2["Tube Pass 2 Forward"]

    TUBE_PASS2 -.heat transfer through wall.-> SHELL_H1
```

```mermaid
graph LR
    T_HOT["T bulk HOT"] --> R1["R conv inside h_i"]
    R1 --> R2["R fouling inside R_fi"]
    R2 --> R3["R wall xw kw"]
    R3 --> R4["R fouling outside R_fo"]
    R4 --> R5["R conv outside h_o"]
    R5 --> T_COLD["T bulk COLD"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The thermal resistance network visually conveys the **series resistance analogy** central to the $1/U$ equation. KTU board diagrams typically reproduce this as a chain of blocks with arrows.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Classify heat exchangers based on the mode of heat transfer. Give one industrial example for each class.*

**Model Answer:**

Heat exchangers are classified by the mode of heat transfer into:

1. **Direct Contact Type (Open Type):** Both fluids come into direct physical contact. Heat transfer occurs through convection and latent heat exchange (e.g., spray-type cooling tower, jet condenser).

2. **Indirect Contact / Recuperative Type:** Fluids are separated by a solid wall. Heat flows by **conduction through the wall** combined with **convection on both fluid sides** (e.g., shell-and-tube heat exchanger, plate heat exchanger).

3. **Storage / Regenerative Type:** Heat is stored in a solid matrix during one half-cycle and released in the next. Heat transfer alternates between **conduction in the solid** and **convection to/from the fluid** (e.g., Cowper stove, Ljungström air preheater).

*(3 marks: 1 for classification, 2 for examples with correct mode description)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between parallel flow and counterflow heat exchangers. State which arrangement gives a higher LMTD for the same terminal temperatures.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Parallel Flow | Counter Flow |
|---------|---------------|--------------|
| Fluid direction | Same end, same direction | Opposite ends, opposite direction |
| Outlet temperature | $T_{h,out} > T_{c,out}$ (always) | $T_{c,out}$ can exceed $T_{h,out}$ |
| Temperature difference | Decreases monotonically | Stays nearly constant or is symmetric |
| LMTD | Smaller | **Larger** |
| Effectiveness | Lower | **Higher** |
| Heat recovery | Limited | Maximum possible |
| Industrial use | Rare, viscous fluids | Almost universal |

For the **same inlet and outlet temperatures**, the counterflow arrangement yields a **higher LMTD** and hence a **smaller area** for the same duty — making it the **preferred industrial geometry**.

*(3 marks: 2 for table, 1 for conclusion)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule (ESE):** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and provides internal choice. The standard split is **7 + 7** marks across sub-parts (a) and (b).

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, Module 3]** *A 1-2 shell-and-tube heat exchanger is used to condense 0.8 kg/s of saturated steam at 100 kPa (latent heat = 2257 kJ/kg) using cooling water entering at 25 °C and exiting at 55 °C. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 1500$ W/m²·K. The exchanger has counterflow-equivalent correction factor $F = 0.9$. Determine:*

**(a)** *(7 marks)* The heat duty, water mass flow rate, and LMTD for an equivalent counterflow exchanger.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* The required heat transfer area and the number of tubes if each tube is 2 m long with 20 mm outer diameter.

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Heat Duty:**

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_{steam} \cdot \lambda = 0.8 \times 2257 \times 10^3 = 1.806 \times 10^6 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Water Mass Flow Rate (energy balance):**

$$\dot{m}_w = \frac{\dot{Q}}{c_{p,w}(T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})} = \frac{1.806 \times 10^6}{4180 \times 30} = 14.40 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent Counterflow LMTD:**

Since steam condenses, $T_{h,in} = T_{h,out} = T_{sat}(100 \text{ kPa}) \approx 99.6$ °C.

For counterflow: $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 99.6 - 55 = 44.6$ °C
$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 99.6 - 25 = 74.6$ °C

$$\Delta T_{lm,CF} = \frac{74.6 - 44.6}{\ln(74.6/44.6)} = \frac{30}{\ln(1.6726)} = \frac{30}{0.5139} = 58.37 \text{ °C}$$

**Part (b):**

**Step 4 — Actual LMTD with Correction Factor:**

$$\Delta T_{lm,actual} = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,CF} = 0.9 \times 58.37 = 52.53 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 5 — Required Area:**

$$A = \frac{\dot{Q}}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm,actual}} = \frac{1.806 \times 10^6}{1500 \times 52.53} = 22.92 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 6 — Number of Tubes:**

Area per tube (using outer diameter) = $\pi D_o L = \pi \times 0.020 \times 2 = 0.1257$ m²

$$N = \frac{A}{A_{tube}} = \frac{22.92}{0.1257} \approx 183 \text{ tubes}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Forgetting the correction factor F** when the exchanger is NOT pure counterflow — most students lose **2–3 marks** here.
> - **Using saturated temperature at 100 kPa = 100 °C** (rounded) instead of 99.6 °C is accepted by most valuators; do not get confused.
> - **Always use outer diameter** for area when $U$ is based on outside area — the KTU 2024 syllabus does NOT standardize this, so read the problem statement carefully.
> - **Unit mismatch (kJ vs. J):** Multiply by 1000 to convert latent heat to J/kg before substituting.

---

### Question B (Alternative, 14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** *A counterflow double-pipe heat exchanger cools 1.0 kg/s of a process fluid ($c_p = 3.5$ kJ/kg·K) from 120 °C to 50 °C using cooling water ($c_p = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K) entering at 20 °C. The water mass flow rate is 2.0 kg/s. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 800$ W/m²·K. Determine:*

**(a)** *(7 marks)* The water outlet temperature, LMTD, and heat transfer area.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* The effectiveness of the exchanger, the capacity ratio, and the NTU. Verify using the counterflow ε-NTU relation.

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Heat Duty from Hot Side:**

$$\dot{Q} = 1.0 \times 3.5 \times 10^3 \times (120 - 50) = 2.45 \times 10^5 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Water Outlet Temperature:**

$$T_{c,out} = T_{c,in} + \frac{\dot{Q}}{\dot{m}_w c_{p,w}} = 20 + \frac{2.45 \times 10^5}{2.0 \times 4180} = 20 + 29.31 = 49.31 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 3 — LMTD (Counterflow):**

$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 120 - 49.31 = 70.69$ °C
$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 50 - 20 = 30$ °C

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{70.69 - 30}{\ln(70.69/30)} = \frac{40.69}{\ln(2.3563)} = \frac{40.69}{0.8570} = 47.48 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 4 — Heat Transfer Area:**

$$A = \frac{\dot{Q}}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \frac{2.45 \times 10^5}{800 \times 47.48} = 6.45 \text{ m}^2$$

**Part (b):**

**Step 5 — Capacity Rates:**

$C_h = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h} = 1.0 \times 3500 = 3500$ W/K
$C_c = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} = 2.0 \times 4180 = 8360$ W/K

$$C_{min} = 3500 \text{ W/K}, \quad C_{max} = 8360 \text{ W/K}, \quad C_r = 3500/8360 = 0.4187$$

**Step 6 — Effectiveness:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}} = \frac{120 - 50}{120 - 20} = \frac{70}{100} = 0.70$$

**Step 7 — NTU:**

$$NTU = \frac{U A}{C_{min}} = \frac{800 \times 6.45}{3500} = 1.474$$

**Step 8 — Verification Using Counterflow ε-NTU Relation:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp[-NTU(1 - C_r)]}{1 - C_r \exp[-NTU(1 - C_r)]}$$

$1 - C_r = 1 - 0.4187 = 0.5813$
$NTU(1 - C_r) = 1.474 \times 0.5813 = 0.857$
$\exp(-0.857) = 0.4243$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1 - 0.4243}{1 - 0.4187 \times 0.4243} = \frac{0.5757}{1 - 0.1776} = \frac{0.5757}{0.8224} = 0.700 \checkmark$$

Effectiveness verified at 0.70.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> - **Mixing up $C_{min}$ and $C_{max}$:** Always identify the *minimum* heat capacity rate correctly — KTU valuators deduct 1 mark for this.
> - **Confusing NTU with Nusselt Number (Nu):** These are *not* the same. NTU is the **Number of Transfer Units** in HE design.
> - **The verification step is essential** — KTU 2024 rubrics reward the final consistency check with full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Page Quick Recall (Pin This Before Your Exam!)**

- ✅ Heat exchangers are classified by **construction (direct / indirect / regenerative)**, **flow arrangement (parallel / counter / cross / multi-pass)**, and **application (heater / cooler / condenser / vaporizer)**.
- ✅ **Direct contact HEs** have the highest effectiveness but require fluids to be miscible and non-reactive.
- ✅ **Indirect contact (recuperative) HEs** are the most common industrial type — shell-and-tube, plate, double-pipe, plate-fin, and air-cooled.
- ✅ **Regenerative HEs** use a solid matrix; Cowper stoves and Ljungström preheaters are classic examples.
- ✅ **Counterflow** gives the **maximum LMTD and effectiveness**; it is the *reference geometry* for all other arrangements.
- ✅ **LMTD formula:** $\Delta T_{lm} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)/\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$ — applicable directly to **counterflow only**.
- ✅ For non-counterflow geometries, use **$F$-correction factor**: $\Delta T_{lm,actual} = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,CF}$, with $F \geq 0.75$ as the industrial acceptance criterion.
- ✅ **NTU method** is preferred when outlet temperatures are unknown or in phase-change service (condensers, vaporizers).
- ✅ **Counterflow ε-NTU:** $\varepsilon = [1 - \exp(-NTU(1 - C_r))] / [1 - C_r \exp(-NTU(1 - C_r))]$.
- ✅ **Parallel flow ε-NTU:** $\varepsilon = [1 - \exp(-NTU(1 + C_r))] / (1 + C_r)$.
- ✅ **Condenser / Vaporizer (C_r = 0):** $\varepsilon = 1 - \exp(-NTU)$ and $NTU = -\ln(1 - \varepsilon)$.
- ✅ **Overall resistance:** $1/U = 1/h_i + x_w/k_w + 1/h_o + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$ — never forget fouling resistances in industrial design.
- ✅ **TEMA** (Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association) governs shell-and-tube HE nomenclature (e.g., AES, BEM, CFU).
- ✅ **Plate heat exchangers** give $U$ values 3–5× higher than shell-and-tube, but are limited by gasket pressure and temperature.
- ✅ **Effectiveness ceiling for parallel flow** is always $< 0.5$ at high $C_r$, while counterflow can approach 1.0.
- ✅ **Exchanger types by application:** Heater, Cooler, Condenser, Reboiler, Waste Heat Recovery Unit, Chiller, Superheater.

> **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** *"Classify by three modes — construction, flow, and function. Always default to counterflow for LMTD. Use F-factor for multi-pass. Reach for ε-NTU when streams phase-change."* 🎯

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — SHELL AND TUBE HEAT EXCHANGERS (STHE)

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition
A **Shell and Tube Heat Exchanger (STHE)** is the most common and versatile type of **indirect-contact heat exchanger** used in the chemical, petrochemical, power, refrigeration, and process industries. It consists of a bundle of parallel tubes enclosed within a cylindrical pressure vessel (the **shell**). One fluid flows through the inside of the tubes (the **tube-side fluid**), while the second fluid flows over the outside of the tubes, but inside the shell (the **shell-side fluid**). Heat is transferred between the two streams through the tube walls, without the fluids physically mixing.

According to the **Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association (TEMA)** standards, STHEs are formally classified by their **front-end head type, shell type, and rear-end head type** (e.g., TEMA designation **AES**, **BEM**, **CFU**, etc.), and are designed per the **ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code, Section VIII**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> In Module 3, students must learn (i) classification of STHEs, (ii) the **function of every internal component** (tube sheets, baffles, tie rods, impingement plates, pass partitions, gaskets), and (iii) the constructional drawings/identifications. Numerical design aspects (LMTD, NTU, individual/foul U) follow in subsequent modules.

### 1.1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Building Pipe Network"
Imagine a tall apartment building in a cold hill station. The building has two completely separate plumbing systems that never mix:
- **System A (Tube Side):** Hot water from the geyser runs through a network of small, thin copper pipes running up the walls.
- **System B (Shell Side):** Cold lake water, used for cooling, is pumped into a large outer tank (the "shell") that completely engulfs those copper pipes.

Heat from the hot copper pipes passes *through* the pipe wall into the cold water surrounding them. The hot water cools down, the cold water warms up — but the two waters **never touch each other**. The copper pipes are the "**tubes**," the large tank is the "**shell**," the thin copper wall is the "**tube wall**," and the temperature of the pipes is what determines the rate of heat exchange. By adding zig-zag **baffles** inside the tank, the cold water is forced to swirl and crash repeatedly against the pipes, ensuring maximum contact — just like baffles in a real STHE increase heat transfer.

### 1.1.3 Why STHEs Dominate Industry
- High **Heat Transfer Area per Unit Volume** (compact, ~50–500 m²/m³).
- Can withstand **high pressures (up to ~100 bar)** and **high temperatures (~800 °C)**.
- Easy to **clean, dismantle, and repair**.
- Available in a wide variety of materials of construction (carbon steel, SS-304/316, copper, titanium, Inconel, Monel, glass-lined, graphite).
- The large **operating flexibility** allows multi-pass, multi-stream, and phase-change (boiling/condensing) duties.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Bodies**
> - **TEMA** (Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association) — Governs naming, mechanical design, and tolerances.
> - **ASME** Section VIII — Governs pressure-vessel code.
> - **API 660** — For refinery and petrochemical shell-and-tube exchangers.
> - **HEI** (Heat Exchange Institute) — For condensers.

### 1.1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Standard Units)
- **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U):** typically **200 – 6000 W/m²·K** depending on duty.
- **Fouling Resistance (R_f):** 0.0001 – 0.001 m²·K/W.
- **LMTD:** typically **20 – 100 °C** for liquids, up to **300 °C** for condensers.
- **Tube Diameter (OD):** standardized at **19.05 mm (3/4 in), 25.4 mm (1 in)**, common wall gauges are **BWG 14, 16, 18, 20** (Birmingham Wire Gauge).
- **Tube Length:** 1.5 m, 3 m, 4.88 m (16 ft), 6 m, standard.

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Master Classification of Shell and Tube Heat Exchangers

Shell and tube exchangers are classified based on the **mechanical features that allow for thermal expansion, cleaning access, and bundle removal**. The TEMA designation is a **three-letter code**:

| Position | Symbol | Meaning |
|----------|--------|---------|
| **Front End (Stationary Head)** | **A / B / C / D / N** | Channel & removable cover / Bonnet / Integral cover / Special / Pass partition |
| **Shell Type** | **E / F / G / H / J / K / X** | One-pass shell / Two-pass with longitudinal baffle / Split-flow / Double split-flow / Division wall / Kettle / Cross-flow |
| **Rear End (Head)** | **L / M / N / P / S / T / U / W** | Fixed tube sheet like stationary head / Fixed tube sheet / Fixed tube sheet (no bonnet) / Outside-packed floating head / Split backing ring / Pull-through floating head / U-tube bundle / Divided channel |

### 2.1.1 Classification by Head/Sheet Arrangement

### **A. Fixed Tube Sheet Heat Exchanger (TEMA: AEL, AEM, BEN, NEN)**

**Construction:**
- **Tubes** are mechanically rolled (expanded) into two **stationary tube sheets**.
- The tube sheets are welded (or bolted) to the shell.
- A **channel** with a removable cover is bolted to one or both ends.
- **NO** allowance for thermal expansion between shell and tubes.

**Advantages:**
- Simplest, cheapest, and most compact design.
- Can be cleaned on the tube side without disturbing the bundle.
- Can withstand the **highest pressures and temperatures**.

**Disadvantages:**
- The bundle **cannot be removed** for cleaning.
- Shell-side cleaning is very difficult (requires chemical cleaning only).
- The temperature difference between shell and tube materials is limited to a maximum of about **80–100 °C** for a single fixed-sheet design (otherwise the differential thermal expansion will shear the tubes from the tube sheet). For larger ΔT, an **expansion bellow** must be added to the shell.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Expansion Bellow (or Expansion Joint)** is a corrugated metallic sleeve fitted to the shell. It absorbs differential thermal expansion between the shell and the tube bundle, preventing tube-sheet leakage. Each bellow typically absorbs ~10 mm of axial movement.

### **B. U-Tube Heat Exchanger (TEMA: BEU, AEU, CEU)**

**Construction:**
- The tube bundle is shaped like the letter **"U"** — a single tube sheet holds the **U-bend return bends**.
- The shell is a simple cylinder with an integral or bonnetted channel at one end.
- The U-bend allows each tube to expand **independently** because one end is free-floating.

**Advantages:**
- The **U-bend** allows free thermal expansion → no bellow required.
- The **bundle can be easily removed** by pulling it out from the shell.
- Cheap and efficient for clean services.

**Disadvantages:**
- **U-bends are difficult to clean mechanically** — only chemical cleaning is possible on the inside of the U-bend.
- The minimum bend radius limits the number of tubes that can fit in a given shell diameter.
- The outermost tubes (at the bend) are longer → some **bypass maldistribution** occurs.
- Cannot handle services with frequent **fouling, coking, or polymerisation** on the inside of the U-bend.

### **C. Floating Head Heat Exchanger (TEMA: AES, AEP, BEP, AEW, CFU pull-through)**

**Construction:**
- One tube sheet is **fixed** to the shell; the other is attached to a "**floating head**" that can slide axially inside the shell.
- A **split backing ring** or **outside-packed lantern ring** allows the bundle to be removed while isolating the shell-side fluid.

**Sub-Types:**
- **TEMA "S" — Outside-Packed Floating Head:** A floating head cover is bolted to the tube sheet; packing is outside the shell. Easy to clean and inspect.
- **TEMA "P" — Outside-Packed Floating Head with Backing Ring:** Provides better sealing for high pressures.
- **TEMA "T" — Pull-Through Floating Head:** The floating head is large enough to be pulled back through the shell — no bonnet needed, maximum bundle removal clearance.

**Advantages:**
- **Complete thermal expansion tolerance** — the floating head slides with the tube bundle.
- **Both tube and shell sides can be mechanically cleaned** easily.
- The bundle is fully removable.

**Disadvantages:**
- Most **expensive** and complex design.
- The floating head joint is a potential leak point and limits maximum pressure (typically ≤ 70 bar).
- More downtime for maintenance.

### **D. Kettle Reboiler (TEMA: AKT, BKU)**

**Construction:**
- A large-diameter shell, often a **fired kettle** or reboiler.
- The upper section is enlarged ("kettle") to provide vapour disengagement space; the lower section contains the tube bundle immersed in boiling liquid.
- Vapour rises and exits from the top, while the liquid re-circulates naturally (thermosyphon) or by pumping.

**Used For:** Boiling of liquids, e.g., in distillation column reboilers.

### **E. Hairpin / Double-Pipe Exchanger**
- Consists of a large outer pipe (shell) and a concentric smaller pipe (tube) bent into a hairpin shape.
- Used for **very small heat duty** or **high-fouling streams** that need frequent mechanical cleaning of the inside of the larger pipe.
- One tube-side fluid and one shell-side fluid per hairpin; multiple hairpins can be stacked in series/parallel.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are essential for the KTU board examination. Memorize the units and the boundary conditions.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|--------------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | **Basic Rate Equation** | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_m$ | Q in W, U in W/m²·K, A in m², ΔTₘ in K |
| 2 | **Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)** | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ | For counter-current flow |
| 3 | **Corrected LMTD** | $\Delta T_{actual} = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$ | F is the correction factor (0 ≤ F ≤ 1) |
| 4 | **Overall Resistance (clean)** | $\dfrac{1}{U_c} = \dfrac{1}{h_i}\left(\dfrac{D_o}{D_i}\right) + \dfrac{x \ln(D_o/D_i)}{k_w} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$ | All based on outside area |
| 5 | **Overall Resistance (fouled)** | $\dfrac{1}{U_d} = \dfrac{1}{U_c} + R_{f,i}\left(\dfrac{D_o}{D_i}\right) + R_{f,o}$ | R_f are fouling resistances |
| 6 | **Capacity Ratio** | $R = \dfrac{T_{hot,in} - T_{hot,out}}{T_{cold,out} - T_{cold,in}}$ | Used in LMTD correction chart |
| 7 | **Effectiveness Ratio** | $S = \dfrac{T_{cold,out} - T_{cold,in}}{T_{hot,in} - T_{cold,in}}$ | Used in LMTD correction chart |
| 8 | **Tube-Side Velocity** | $u_t = \dfrac{4 m_t}{\pi \rho D_i^2 N_t}$ | N_t is the number of tubes per pass |
| 9 | **Shell-Side Velocity (Bell-Delaware)** | $u_s = \dfrac{m_s}{\rho_s \cdot D_s \cdot L_{btc} \cdot (1 - \phi_B)}$ | $L_{btc}$ = baffle spacing centre-to-centre; $\phi_B$ = fractional bypass area |
| 10 | **NTU-Effectiveness** | $\varepsilon = 1 - e^{-NTU(1+C^*)}$ | For C = C_min/C_max |
| 11 | **Reynolds Number (Tube)** | $Re_t = \dfrac{\rho u_t D_i}{\mu}$ | Transition at Re_t ≈ 10 000 for tubes |
| 12 | **Heat Capacity Rate** | $C = \dot{m} \cdot C_p$ | W/K |

> [!NOTE]
> **Fouling Resistance Values (R_f, m²·K/W)**
> - Distilled water: 0.0001
> - Sea water: 0.00035
> - Cooling tower water: 0.0005
> - River water: 0.0005 – 0.001
> - Light hydrocarbons: 0.0002
> - Heavy hydrocarbons: 0.0005 – 0.001
> - Steam (clean): 0.0001

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Constructional Details & Function of Each Component

### 2.3.1 **The Shell**
- A cylindrical pressure vessel, normally of **carbon steel** (clad or lined for corrosion resistance).
- Equipped with **inlet and outlet nozzles** for the shell-side fluid.
- Fitted with **vents** (top) and **drains** (bottom).
- May have **inspection ports / hand holes** for cleaning.
- Designed per ASME Section VIII with appropriate **corrosion allowance (typically 1.5–3 mm)**.

### 2.3.2 **Tubes**
- Standard OD: **19.05 mm (3/4") or 25.4 mm (1")**.
- Wall thickness expressed in **BWG** (Birmingham Wire Gauge): 14, 16, 18, 20, 22.
- Materials: Carbon steel, admiralty brass, copper, SS-304/316, Inconel, titanium, Monel, cupro-nickel (90/10, 70/30).
- **Tube pitch** (distance between tube centres): commonly **1.25 × OD** (triangular pitch) or **1.25 × OD** (square pitch).
- **Tube layout patterns:**
  - **Triangular (30°, 60°, 90°):** Highest heat transfer, hardest to clean.
  - **Square (90°):** Best for mechanical cleaning of outside.
  - **Rotated Square (45°):** Compromise between cleaning and heat transfer.

### 2.3.3 **Tube Sheets**
- Thick **circular plates** drilled with a hole pattern matching the tube layout.
- Tubes are **expanded** (mechanically rolled) into the tube sheet holes for a leak-proof joint.
- Tube sheets are the **primary pressure-retaining components**.
- They support the bundle and act as the boundary between the tube-side and shell-side fluids.

### 2.3.4 **Baffles (Most Critical Internal Component)**
Baffles are **perforated plates** installed perpendicular to the tubes inside the shell. Their **functions** are:
1. To **support the tubes** against vibration, sagging, and sagging-induced failure.
2. To **direct the shell-side fluid** in a zig-zag flow path across the tube bundle.
3. To **increase the shell-side velocity**, thereby increasing the shell-side heat transfer coefficient.
4. To **create turbulence** at lower bulk velocities.

**Types of Baffles:**
- **Segmental Baffles (Single-Segmental):** The most common. They are circular plates with a chord cut out, occupying typically **20–45 %** of the shell cross-section. Baffle cut is the **height of the chord segment removed / shell diameter**.
- **Double-Segmental Baffles:** Used for low-pressure-drop services.
- **Disc-and-Donut Baffles:** Alternate solid disc and donut-shaped baffles; used in fouling services.
- **Orifice Baffles:** For low ΔP services (low-pressure drop).
- **Longitudinal (Shell-Side) Baffles:** Used in TEMA "F" (two-pass shell) and "G" (split flow) shells.
- **Baffle Spacing:** The centre-to-centre distance between adjacent baffles. Minimum spacing = 1/5 of shell diameter (or 50.8 mm); Maximum = shell diameter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Baffle Cut:**
> - **25% cut** — Most common; good heat transfer, moderate pressure drop.
> - **45% cut** — Low pressure drop, low heat transfer.
> - **20% cut** — High heat transfer, high pressure drop (risk of dead zones).

### 2.3.5 **Tie Rods and Spacers**
- **Tie Rods:** Long threaded rods that run the full length of the shell, parallel to the tubes, just outside the tube bundle.
- They **hold all the baffles in place** at the correct spacing.
- **Spacers:** Short pieces of tubing cut to the precise baffle spacing; they slip over the tie rods to fix the baffles.
- **Number of tie rods:** usually 4–8, depending on shell diameter.

### 2.3.6 **Impingement Plate (Entrance Baffle)**
- A solid plate located where the shell-side fluid **enters** the shell.
- **Function:** Protects the tubes from **erosion** caused by high-velocity fluid impinging on them (especially with two-phase or slurry streams).
- It is usually an **unsupported flat plate** or **supported by rods**.

### 2.3.7 **Channel, Channel Cover, and Pass Partition**
- **Channel:** The end chamber that distributes the tube-side fluid into the tube inlets.
- **Channel Cover (Bonnet):** A removable flange cover that allows access to the tubes for cleaning.
- **Pass Partition:** A flat plate inside the channel that divides it into compartments, creating a **multi-pass** arrangement (e.g., 2-pass, 4-pass, 6-pass on the tube side).

### 2.3.8 **Gaskets and Seals**
- Provide **leak-tight joints** between the channel-cover-channel, tube-sheet-shell, and floating-head joints.
- Materials: Compressed asbestos fibre (CAF), graphite, PTFE, spiral-wound metal + graphite, rubber (NBR, EPDM, Viton) for low temperatures.

### 2.3.9 **Support Brackets / Saddles**
- The shell rests on **saddle supports** (similar to horizontal pressure vessels) on the floor.
- Lifting lugs on top allow the bundle to be hoisted out.

### 2.3.10 **Expansion Bellow**
- Corrugated metallic sleeve welded to the shell.
- **Function:** Absorbs **differential thermal expansion** between the shell (at shell-side temp) and the tubes (at tube-side temp).

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)

This is the **most important derivation** in the heat-exchanger module. KTU examiners test this regularly for full marks.

### 3.1.1 Assumptions
1. **Steady-state** operation.
2. **Constant** overall heat transfer coefficient $U$ along the length of the exchanger.
3. **Constant** mass flow rates $\dot{m}_h$ and $\dot{m}_c$ (no phase change).
4. **Constant** specific heats $C_{ph}$ and $C_{pc}$.
5. **No heat loss** to surroundings.
6. **Pure counter-current** flow (this derivation is for counter-current; a correction factor $F$ is later applied for multi-pass).

### 3.1.2 Setup
Consider a counter-current shell-and-tube heat exchanger. Let:
- $T_h(x)$ = hot fluid temperature at position $x$ (along length).
- $T_c(x)$ = cold fluid temperature at position $x$.
- $\dot{m}_h, \dot{m}_c$ = mass flow rates of hot and cold fluids.
- $C_{ph}, C_{pc}$ = specific heats.

At any point $x$, let $\Delta T(x) = T_h(x) - T_c(x)$.

**At Inlet (x = 0):** $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$
**At Outlet (x = L):** $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$

### 3.1.3 Energy Balance on a Differential Element

For a differential element of area $dA$ at position $x$:

Heat transferred from hot fluid to cold fluid:

$$dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T(x)$$

But the same heat is also given by the change in enthalpy of each fluid:

$$dQ = -\dot{m}_h \cdot C_{ph} \cdot dT_h = \dot{m}_c \cdot C_{pc} \cdot dT_c$$

(The negative sign for the hot fluid indicates its temperature decreases as $x$ increases.)

### 3.1.4 Substituting

Differentiate $\Delta T(x) = T_h(x) - T_c(x)$ with respect to $x$:

$$d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c$$

Using the energy balances:

$$dT_h = -\frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_h C_{ph}}, \quad dT_c = \frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_c C_{pc}}$$

Therefore:

$$d(\Delta T) = -dQ \left( \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{ph}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{pc}} \right)$$

### 3.1.5 Isolating Variables

Since $dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T$:

$$d(\Delta T) = -U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T \left( \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{ph}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{pc}} \right)$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \left( \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{ph}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{pc}} \right) dA$$

### 3.1.6 Integration Across the Exchanger

Integrate both sides from $x=0$ to $x=L$ (i.e., from $\Delta T_1$ to $\Delta T_2$, and from $A=0$ to $A=A$):

$$\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \left( \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{ph}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{pc}} \right) \int_0^A dA$$

The left side integrates to $\ln(\Delta T_2 / \Delta T_1)$:

$$\ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -U A \left( \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{ph}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{pc}} \right)$$

### 3.1.7 Expressing Heat Duty

The total heat duty is:

$$Q = \dot{m}_h C_{ph}(T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = \dot{m}_c C_{pc}(T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$

Note that $\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2 = (T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}) - (T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}) = (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) - (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$.

Substituting $\dot{m}_h C_{ph} = Q / (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})$ and similarly for the cold side:

$$\frac{1}{\dot{m}_h C_{ph}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c C_{pc}} = \frac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{Q} + \frac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{Q} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{Q}$$

So:

$$\ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -U A \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{Q}$$

### 3.1.8 Final Form of LMTD

Solve for $Q$:

$$Q = U A \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$

Defining the **Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)**:

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}}$$

The final heat-transfer rate equation is:

$$\boxed{Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of STHE Types

| Feature | Fixed Tube Sheet | U-Tube | Floating Head (S/P) | Kettle Reboiler |
|---------|------------------|--------|---------------------|-----------------|
| **TEMA Code** | BEM / NEN | BEU / AEU | AES / AEP | BKU / AKT |
| **Thermal Expansion Allowance** | Limited (needs bellow) | Excellent (U-bend) | Excellent (sliding head) | Excellent |
| **Bundle Removal** | NOT possible | Possible | Possible | Possible |
| **Tube-Side Cleaning** | Easy | Difficult (U-bend) | Easy | Easy |
| **Shell-Side Cleaning** | Very Difficult | Difficult | Easy | Easy |
| **Max. Pressure** | Highest (~100 bar) | Medium | Medium (~70 bar) | Low |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Medium | Highest | Medium |
| **Fouling Tolerance** | Poor (shell side) | Poor (U-bend) | Best | Best |
| **Phase Change Use** | Yes (boiling/cond.) | Yes (clean) | Yes (best) | Designed for boiling |
| **Mechanical Reliability** | Best | Good | Good (joints) | Good |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:**
A 1-2 shell-and-tube heat exchanger heats **30 000 kg/h** of water from **30 °C to 80 °C** using an oil stream cooling from **150 °C to 100 °C**. The water flows through the tubes (2 passes, 200 tubes per pass, OD 25.4 mm, wall 2.6 mm). The overall heat transfer coefficient $U_o$ (based on outside area) is **1500 W/m²·K**. Find the area required using LMTD.

**Step 1 — Heat Duty**
Take $C_{p,water} = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K.

$$Q = \dot{m}_w C_{pw} \Delta T_w = \frac{30000}{3600} \times 4.18 \times (80-30) = 1741.7 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Find ΔT₁ and ΔT₂ (Counter-current Reference)**

In counter-current:
- $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 150 - 80 = 70$ °C
- $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 100 - 30 = 70$ °C

Since $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$, the LMTD = 70 °C (a special case).

**Step 3 — Calculate Area**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \frac{1741700}{1500 \times 70} = 16.59 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Correction Factor**
The exchanger is **1 shell pass, 2 tube passes (1-2)**, with $R = 1$ and $S = 1$ (from temperature values), so $F \approx 0.88$ from the correction chart. Applying:

$$A_{actual} = \frac{16.59}{0.88} = 18.85 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Tube Length**

Outside area per tube per metre length = $\pi \times D_o \times 1 = \pi \times 0.0254 \approx 0.0798$ m²/m.

Total tube length = $A_{actual} / (\pi D_o \times 200) = 18.85 / (0.0798 \times 200) = 1.18$ m per pass, total ≈ 2.36 m → choose 8 ft (2.44 m) standard.
