> **Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)** is the difference between the **boiling point of a solution** and the **boiling point of pure water** at the same pressure:
>
> $$\text{BPE} = T_b - T_w$$
>
> It arises because dissolved (non-volatile) solute particles lower the vapor pressure of the solvent, requiring a **higher temperature** to achieve boiling.
>
> **Importance in design:**
> - It **reduces the effective temperature driving force** $(\Delta T_{\text{eff}} = T_s - T_b)$.
> - For a given $U$ and $Q$, the required **heat transfer area $A$ increases** (since $A = Q / (U \cdot \Delta T)$).
> - It is determined experimentally or via **Duhring's Rule**: $T_b = m T_w + c$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

> A single-effect evaporator concentrates **5000 kg/h** of a solution from **8% to 40% solids** (by mass). The feed enters at **35 °C**. Saturated steam at **3.0 bar (T_s ≈ 134 °C, λ_s ≈ 2165 kJ/kg)** is used. The solution boils at **104 °C** (λ_V at 104 °C = **2257 kJ/kg**). The overall heat transfer coefficient is **1800 W/m²·K**. Take $C_{p,F} = C_{p,L} = 4.0$ kJ/kg·K.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the **capacity**, **steam consumption**, and **steam economy** of the evaporator.
> **(b)** Determine the **heat transfer area** required.

#### **Solution to Q-A(a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Material balance**

Solid balance: $5000 \times 0.08 = L \times 0.40$

$$L = \frac{400}{0.40} = 1000 \text{ kg/h}$$

Overall balance: $V = F - L = 5000 - 1000 = 4000$ kg/h

**Capacity = 4000 kg/h** ✓ (2 marks)

**Step 2: Energy balance**

$$S \cdot \lambda_s = F \cdot C_{p,F} (T_b - T_F) + V \cdot \lambda_V$$

$$F \cdot C_{p,F} (T_b - T_F) = 5000 \times 4.0 \times (104 - 35) = 5000 \times 4.0 \times 69 = 1{,}380{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$

$$V \cdot \lambda_V = 4000 \times 2257 = 9{,}028{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$

$$Q = 1{,}380{,}000 + 9{,}028{,}000 = 10{,}408{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$

$$S = \frac{10{,}408{,}000}{2165} = 4807.4 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Steam consumption = 4807.4 kg/h** ✓ (3 marks)

**Step 3: Economy**

$$\text{Economy} = \frac{V}{S} = \frac{4000}{4807.4} = 0.832$$

**Economy = 0.832** ✓ (2 marks)

#### **Solution to Q-A(b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Effective ΔT**

$$\Delta T = T_s - T_b = 134 - 104 = 30 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2: Convert Q to Watts**

$$Q = \frac{10{,}408{,}000}{3.6} = 2{,}891{,}111 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3: Area**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{2{,}891{,}111}{1800 \times 30} = \frac{2{,}891{,}111}{54{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{A \approx 53.5 \text{ m}^2}$$

**Area ≈ 53.5 m²** ✓ (7 marks — distribute across sub-steps)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

> An aqueous solution of **NaOH** at **10% (w/w)** is to be concentrated to **40% (w/w)** in a single-effect evaporator. The feed rate is **8000 kg/h** at **40 °C**. Steam is supplied at **2.5 bar (T_s ≈ 127 °C, λ_s ≈ 2185 kJ/kg)**. The solution boils at **108 °C** with $\lambda_V \approx 2240$ kJ/kg. Overall heat transfer coefficient $U = 2500$ W/m²·K. Assume $C_{p,F} = C_{p,L} = 4.0$ kJ/kg·K.
>
> **(a)** Compute the **mass of thick liquor**, the **capacity** and the **steam economy**.
> **(b)** If the boiling point of pure water at the operating pressure is **100 °C**, calculate the **BPE**, the **net effective ΔT**, and the **heat transfer area**.

#### **Solution to Q-B(a) [7 Marks]**

**Material balance:**

Solid: $8000 \times 0.10 = L \times 0.40 \implies L = 2000$ kg/h

Capacity: $V = 8000 - 2000 = 6000$ kg/h ✓ (2 marks)

**Energy balance:**

$$F \cdot C_{p,F}(T_b - T_F) = 8000 \times 4.0 \times (108 - 40) = 8000 \times 4.0 \times 68 = 2{,}176{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$

$$V \cdot \lambda_V = 6000 \times 2240 = 13{,}440{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$

$$Q = 2{,}176{,}000 + 13{,}440{,}000 = 15{,}616{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$

$$S = \frac{15{,}616{,}000}{2185} = 7147 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Steam economy:**

$$\text{Economy} = \frac{V}{S} = \frac{6000}{7147} = 0.839$$

**Economy ≈ 0.839** ✓ (3 marks)

**Thick liquor $L = 2000$ kg/h, Capacity = 6000 kg/h** ✓ (2 marks)

#### **Solution to Q-B(b) [7 Marks]**

**BPE:**

$$\text{BPE} = T_b - T_w = 108 - 100 = 8 \text{ K}$$

**Net effective ΔT:**

$$\Delta T_{\text{eff}} = (T_s - T_w) - \text{BPE} = (127 - 100) - 8 = 27 - 8 = 19 \text{ K}$$

(Assuming head and friction losses are neglected.)

**Convert Q to Watts:**

$$Q = \frac{15{,}616{,}000}{3.6} = 4{,}337{,}778 \text{ W}$$

**Heat transfer area:**

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{4{,}337{,}778}{2500 \times 19} = \frac{4{,}337{,}778}{47{,}500}$$

$$\boxed{A \approx 91.3 \text{ m}^2}$$

**BPE = 8 K, ΔT_eff = 19 K, A ≈ 91.3 m²** ✓ (7 marks)

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Material & Energy Balance Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the cold-feed heating term** $F \cdot C_{p,F}(T_b - T_F)$ — students often write only $S\lambda_s = V\lambda_V$, losing **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing up $T_b$ and $T_w$** in $\Delta T$. Use $T_s - T_b$ for area (NOT $T_s - T_w$). The $T_s - T_w$ form is the *available* ΔT; subtract BPE to get the *effective* ΔT.
> 3. **Unit mismatch:** $Q$ from energy balance is in **kJ/h** but $U$ is in **W/m²·K**. Always divide by **3.6** to convert kJ/h → W.
> 4. **Concentration expression error:** $x_F$ and $x_L$ must be **mass fractions** (not percentages). If the problem says "10%", use $x_F = 0.10$.
> 5. **Neglecting BPE in $\Delta T$:** When $\lambda_V$ and $T_b$ are given, students often compute area using $T_s - T_w$ and forget to subtract BPE. **Mark loss: 2 marks.**
> 6. **Solid balance mistake:** Some students write $F \cdot x_F = V \cdot x_V$, but the vapor has **zero solids** ($x_V = 0$). Always balance across the **thick liquor** stream.
> 7. **Economy definition reversal:** Always $\dfrac{V}{S}$, never $\dfrac{S}{V}$. The vapor is the *useful output*, steam is the *utility input*.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Material & Energy Balance in Single Effect Evaporators**
>
> ✅ **Evaporator** = equipment to concentrate solution by vaporizing solvent.
>
> ✅ **Single effect** = one body, no vapor reuse → economy ≈ 0.7–0.9.
>
> ✅ **Two balance laws:** Material ($F = V + L$, $F x_F = L x_L$) + Energy ($S\lambda_s = F C_{p,F}(T_b - T_F) + V\lambda_V$).
>
> ✅ **Capacity** $= V = F(1 - x_F / x_L)$ in kg/h.
>
> ✅ **Economy** $= V/S$ (dimensionless).
>
> ✅ **Heat transfer area** $A = Q / (U \cdot \Delta T)$ with $\Delta T = T_s - T_b$.
>
> ✅ **BPE** $= T_b - T_w$ — caused by dissolved solute, reduces effective $\Delta T$.
>
> ✅ **Duhring's Rule:** $T_b = m T_w + c$ — linear plot of solution BP vs. water BP.
>
> ✅ **Temperature losses:** BPE + hydrostatic head + friction — all reduce driving force.
>
> ✅ **Datum convention:** All enthalpies referenced to **liquid water at 0 °C**.
>
> ✅ **Condensate leaves at $T_s$** — its sensible heat is recovered back to the boiler (assumed in standard balance).
>
> ✅ **Solute is non-volatile** — it appears only in the $L$ stream.
>
> ✅ **Always** convert $Q$ to **Watts** (divide kJ/h by 3.6) before using $U$ in W/m²·K.
>
> ✅ **KTU high-yield trick:** When asked for "steam economy", compute $V/S$ *last*; this confirms whether your single-effect answer is in the expected range (0.7–0.9). If you get > 1, you have an error in $S$!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiple-Effect Evaporators: Methods of Feeding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT501 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Multiple-Effect Evaporators: Methods of Feeding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Multiple-Effect Evaporator (MEE)** is an energy-efficient arrangement of *N* evaporator vessels (called "effects") connected in series, in which the **secondary vapor** liberated from one effect is used as the **heating medium** for the subsequent effect, thereby reusing latent heat and drastically reducing live steam consumption.

The **Methods of Feeding** refer to the **strategic arrangement of liquid feed flow and vapor/steam flow** through the cascade of effects. The choice of feeding method critically influences:

- Heat transfer rates
- Liquid pumping requirements
- Capacity and economy
- Final product concentration and thermal degradation

The four standard KTU-recognized feeding configurations are:

1. **Forward Feed (Co-current)**
2. **Backward Feed (Counter-current)**
3. **Parallel Feed**
4. **Mixed (Combined) Feed**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 scheme (Module 3), students are expected to compare these methods qualitatively AND quantitatively using the heat-transfer area equation for multiple-effect systems. Marks are frequently awarded for neat sketches with proper labeling of feed, vapor, and condensate flow directions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Boat Engine Recycle" Intuition

Imagine a single candle burning in a room — it produces heat that escapes and is wasted. To conserve heat, you place a second candle near the first, so the hot gases from candle 1 warm candle 2's surroundings, helping it burn using less initial wax. Chain this for **N candles**, and each successive candle needs less fresh wax because it is "pre-heated" by the previous one.

**Forward Feed** → Wax flows from candle 1 → candle 2 → candle 3 (same direction as heat propagation).

**Backward Feed** → Fresh wax enters candle 3 (the coolest), then flows to candle 2, then candle 1 (opposite to heat flow). This pre-concentrates the thick syrup before it hits the hottest candle — preventing scorching.

**Parallel Feed** → Each candle gets its own fresh wax supply (used for crystallizing evaporators where slurry must be removed from each stage).

**Mixed Feed** → A hybrid that avoids the drawbacks of pure configurations in large industrial plants.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value |
|--------|--------|---------------|
| Steam Economy | **E** | 0.8 × **N** for N effects |
| Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient | **U** | **500 – 2500 W/m²·K** |
| Boiling Point Elevation | **BPE** | **1 – 10 °C** |
| Capacity | **C** | kg water evaporated/hr |

> [!NOTE]
> The parameter **N** (number of effects) is a **design decision variable** — most industrial plants operate between **3 to 6 effects** for sugar, dairy, and salt industries.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Each Feeding Method

#### A. Forward Feed (Co-current / Parallel Feed of Liquids)

**Operational Steps:**
- Fresh dilute feed enters **Effect 1** (highest pressure & temperature).
- Feed and vapor flow in the **same direction** through Effects 1 → 2 → 3.
- Pressure decreases progressively (P₁ > P₂ > P₃).
- Feed becomes progressively concentrated.
- No pumps required between effects — liquid flows by **natural pressure gradient**.

**Why It Works:**
- Pressure drop drives the liquid naturally, eliminating inter-effect pumps.
- Simplest piping layout; lowest capital cost.
- Limitation: Most concentrated liquor contacts the **coolest surface** in the last effect → poor heat transfer due to viscous, concentrated liquor at low ΔT.

#### B. Backward Feed (Counter-current)

**Operational Steps:**
- Fresh dilute feed enters the **last effect** (lowest pressure & temperature).
- Feed is pumped **against the pressure gradient** from Effect 3 → 2 → 1.
- Steam enters Effect 1; vapor flows 1 → 2 → 3 (opposite to feed).
- Most concentrated liquor exits from **Effect 1** (hottest surface).

**Why It Works:**
- Concentrated, viscous liquor meets the **hottest surface** → maximum ΔT → compensates for low U due to viscosity → **better heat transfer**.
- Higher overall capacity than forward feed for viscous liquids.
- Requires **inter-effect pumps** → higher operating cost and complexity.

#### C. Parallel Feed

**Operational Steps:**
- Fresh feed is supplied **separately and equally** to **each effect**.
- Each effect operates independently (not strictly a true multi-effect system by series definition, but a special case).
- Concentrated slurry is removed individually from each effect.

**Why It Works:**
- Used when the product is a **saturated slurry** (e.g., salt, sugar crystallization) that cannot be pumped between effects.
- Each effect's U remains relatively high because slurry is removed continuously.

#### D. Mixed (Combined) Feed

**Operational Steps:**
- **Forward feed in some section, backward feed in another** (e.g., Feed 1 → 2 forward, then 2 → 3 backward, etc.).
- Optimizes the advantages and minimizes the disadvantages of pure configurations.

**Why It Works:**
- Used in **large-scale industrial plants** where the feed characteristics vary along the evaporator train.
- Best of both worlds: pumping economy + temperature-viscosity matching.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $A_i = \dfrac{q_i}{U_i \cdot \Delta T_i}$ | Heat transfer area of *i*-th effect | m² |
| 2 | $\Delta T_i = \dfrac{\left(\sum \Delta T / U_i\right) / U_i}{\sum (1/U_i)}$ | Temperature drop in *i*-th effect (equal-area design) | °C or K |
| 3 | $\sum_{i=1}^{N} \Delta T_i = \Delta T_{\text{total}}$ | Total available temperature drop | °C |
| 4 | $A = \dfrac{\sum q_i}{U \cdot \sum \Delta T_i}$ | Total area assuming equal U | m² |
| 5 | $\text{Steam Economy} = \dfrac{\text{kg water evaporated}}{\text{kg live steam supplied}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | — |
| 6 | $E \approx 0.8 \cdot N$ | Approximate steam economy for N effects | — |
| 7 | $\text{Capacity} = E \times W_s$ | Evaporator capacity (W_s = steam rate) | kg/hr |
| 8 | $\text{BPE via Dühring:} \quad T_b = a + b \cdot T_w$ | Boiling point vs. pure water temperature | °C |
| 9 | $\Delta T_{\text{effective}} = \Delta T_{\text{total}} - \sum \text{BPE}_i - \text{Rank losses}$ | Net driving force | °C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The pipe symbol \vert inside math expressions is preferred over \vert in markdown tables to avoid syntax breaking. Always carry **units explicitly** in numerical answers.

### 2.3 Real-World Industrial Applications

| Industry | Preferred Feeding Method | Reason |
|----------|-------------------------|--------|
| **Sugar Industry** | Mixed Feed | Balance between heat economy and juice quality |
| **Dairy (Milk Concentration)** | Forward Feed | Gentle heat treatment, prevents scorching |
| **Salt (NaCl) Crystallization** | Parallel Feed | Slurry removal from each effect |
| **Caustic Soda Concentration** | Backward Feed | Viscous concentrated liquor needs high temperature |
| **Paper & Pulp Black Liquor** | Backward Feed | High viscosity in final stage |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Heat Transfer Area for a Single Effect

**Starting Premise (Fourier's Law of Conduction for a thin tube wall with convective fluids on both sides):**

The rate of heat transfer through an evaporator's heating surface is given by:

$$
q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T
$$

where:
- $q$ = heat transfer rate (W or J/s)
- $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = heat transfer area (m²)
- $\Delta T$ = temperature driving force (K or °C)

**Step 1 — For each effect in a multi-effect system, write individual balances:**

$$
q_1 = U_1 A_1 \Delta T_1
$$

$$
q_2 = U_2 A_2 \Delta T_2
$$

$$
q_N = U_N A_N \Delta T_N
$$

**Step 2 — For *N* effects, sum the individual heat duties. Assuming equal areas ($A_1 = A_2 = \ldots = A_N = A$):**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} q_i = A \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N} U_i \Delta T_i
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the common area A:**

$$
A = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} q_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} U_i \Delta T_i}
$$

This is the **working equation** used in KTU board exam numericals for multi-effect evaporator design.

### 3.2 Derivation II — Temperature Distribution (Equal Area Criterion)

**Premise:** The design goal is to make $A_1 = A_2 = \ldots = A_N$ so that the evaporator can be fabricated with identical effects (economy of manufacture).

**Step 1 — From the area equation, for two effects:**

$$
A_1 = \frac{q_1}{U_1 \Delta T_1} \quad \text{and} \quad A_2 = \frac{q_2}{U_2 \Delta T_2}
$$

**Step 2 — Setting $A_1 = A_2$:**

$$
\frac{q_1}{U_1 \Delta T_1} = \frac{q_2}{U_2 \Delta T_2}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2} = \frac{q_1 \cdot U_2}{q_2 \cdot U_1}
$$

**Step 3 — Generalizing for *N* effects (equal area assumption):**

$$
\frac{\Delta T_1}{q_1/U_1} = \frac{\Delta T_2}{q_2/U_2} = \ldots = \frac{\Delta T_N}{q_N/U_N}
$$

**Step 4 — Combining with the constraint:**

$$
\Delta T_1 + \Delta T_2 + \ldots + \Delta T_N = \Delta T_{\text{total}}
$$

The standard KTU-board result:

$$
\boxed{\Delta T_i = \frac{\Delta T_{\text{total}} \cdot \left(\frac{q_i}{U_i}\right)}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} \left(\frac{q_j}{U_j}\right)}}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation III — Steam Economy of Multi-Effect System

**Step 1 — Define steam economy mathematically:**

$$
E = \frac{W_{\text{evaporated}}}{W_{\text{steam}}}
$$

where:
- $W_{\text{evaporated}}$ = total water evaporated in the system (kg/hr)
- $W_{\text{steam}}$ = live steam fed to Effect 1 (kg/hr)

**Step 2 — Energy balance on Effect 1 (neglecting sensible heat and BPE):**

The live steam condenses at rate $W_s$, releasing latent heat $\lambda_s$:

$$
q_1 = W_s \cdot \lambda_s
$$

This heat vaporizes water in Effect 1 at rate $W_1$:

$$
q_1 = W_1 \cdot \lambda_1
$$

Thus:

$$
W_1 = W_s \cdot \frac{\lambda_s}{\lambda_1}
$$

**Step 3 — For subsequent effects, vapor from effect *i* acts as steam for effect *i+1*:**

$$
W_{i+1} = W_i \cdot \frac{\lambda_i}{\lambda_{i+1}}
$$

**Step 4 — Total water evaporated across N effects:**

$$
W_{\text{total}} = W_1 + W_2 + \ldots + W_N
$$

Assuming latent heats are approximately equal ($\lambda_s \approx \lambda_1 \approx \ldots \approx \lambda_N \approx \lambda$):

$$
W_{\text{total}} \approx N \cdot W_s
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the economy definition:**

$$
\boxed{E = \frac{N \cdot W_s}{W_s} = N \quad (\text{ideal case})}
$$

In practice, due to BPE, sensible heat effects, and heat losses, the real economy is:

$$
E_{\text{actual}} \approx 0.8 \cdot N
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (High-Yield for KTU 2-Mark Q&A)

| Criterion | Forward Feed | Backward Feed | Parallel Feed | Mixed Feed |
|-----------|--------------|---------------|---------------|------------|
| Feed flow direction | Same as steam | Opposite to steam | Independent to each | Variable |
| Pumping cost | **Low** (no pumps) | **High** (inter-effect pumps) | **Low** | Moderate |
| Heat transfer coefficient | Low in last effects | **Higher** overall | High | High |
| Steam economy | Moderate | **Slightly better** | Lowest | Best |
| Capital cost | **Lowest** | High | Moderate | **Highest** |
| Suitable for viscous liquor | Poor | **Best** | Poor | Good |
| Suitable for crystallizers | Poor | Poor | **Best** | Moderate |
| Thermal degradation | Higher | **Lower** | Lowest | Moderate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Forward Feed (Co-current)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Effect1["EFFECT 1 HIGH PRESSURE HIGH TEMP"]
        F1A[FRESH DILUTE FEED] --> V1A[EFFECT 1 VESSEL]
        S1A[LIVE STEAM IN] --> V1A
        V1A --> C1A[CONDENSATE OUT]
        V1A --> V1B[VAPOR TO EFFECT 2]
    end
    V1B --> Effect2
    subgraph Effect2["EFFECT 2 INTERMEDIATE"]
        V2A[LIQUOR FROM EFFECT 1] --> V2B[EFFECT 2 VESSEL]
        V2B --> C2A[CONDENSATE OUT]
        V2B --> V2C[VAPOR TO EFFECT 3]
    end
    V2C --> Effect3
    subgraph Effect3["EFFECT 3 LOWEST PRESSURE LOWEST TEMP"]
        V3A[LIQUOR FROM EFFECT 2] --> V3B[EFFECT 3 VESSEL]
        V3B --> C3A[CONDENSATE OUT]
        V3B --> V3C[FINAL CONCENTRATED PRODUCT]
    end
    V3C --> VAC[VACUUM CONDENSER]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Backward Feed (Counter-current)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Effect1B["EFFECT 1 HIGH PRESSURE HIGH TEMP"]
        S1B[LIVE STEAM IN] --> V1B1[EFFECT 1 VESSEL]
        V1B1 --> C1B[CONDENSATE OUT]
        V1B1 --> V1B2[VAPOR TO EFFECT 2]
        V1B1 --> P1B[FINAL CONCENTRATED PRODUCT OUT]
    end
    subgraph Effect2B["EFFECT 2 INTERMEDIATE"]
        V2B1[VAPOR FROM EFFECT 1] --> V2B2[EFFECT 2 VESSEL]
        V2B2 --> C2B[CONDENSATE OUT]
        V2B2 --> V2B3[VAPOR TO EFFECT 3]
        P2B[LIQUOR FROM EFFECT 3 PUMPED UP] --> V2B2
    end
    subgraph Effect3B["EFFECT 3 LOWEST PRESSURE LOWEST TEMP"]
        V3B1[VAPOR FROM EFFECT 2] --> V3B2[EFFECT 3 VESSEL]
        V3B2 --> C3B[CONDENSATE OUT]
        F3B[FRESH DILUTE FEED IN] --> V3B2
    end
    V3B2 --> P2B
    V3B2 --> VACB[VACUUM CONDENSER]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Parallel Feed

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FF[FRESH FEED SUPPLY MAIN HEADER] --> SP1[SPLITTER]
    SP1 --> E1P[EFFECT 1 STEAM HEATED]
    SP1 --> E2P[EFFECT 2 STEAM HEATED]
    SP1 --> E3P[EFFECT 3 STEAM HEATED]
    E1P --> P1[CONCENTRATE SLURRY 1 OUT]
    E2P --> P2[CONCENTRATE SLURRY 2 OUT]
    E3P --> P3[CONCENTRATE SLURRY 3 OUT]
    E1P -.-> E2P
    E2P -.-> E3P
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Mixed Feed

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StageA["STAGE A FORWARD"]
        FM1[FRESH FEED] --> E1M[EFFECT 1]
        E1M --> E2M[EFFECT 2]
    end
    subgraph StageB["STAGE B BACKWARD"]
        E2M --> PMP[INTER STAGE PUMP]
        PMP --> E3M[EFFECT 3 HIGH TEMP]
        E3M --> PROD[FINAL CONCENTRATED PRODUCT]
    end
    ST[STEAM IN] --> E3M
    E1M -.VAPOR.-> E2M
    E2M -.VAPOR.-> E3M
    E3M -.VAPOR.-> VCM[VACUUM CONDENSER]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Aid:** The dotted lines (.-.->) in Mermaid represent secondary vapor flow between effects, while solid lines (-->) represent liquid/steam flow. This visual distinction is a common KTU board-exam convention.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define steam economy. What is the steam economy of a triple-effect evaporator compared to a single-effect evaporator, assuming equal heat loads?*

**Model Answer:**

**Steam Economy (E)** is defined as the ratio of the total mass of water evaporated to the mass of live steam supplied to the first effect.

$$
E = \frac{W_{\text{evaporated}}}{W_{\text{steam supplied}}}
$$

For a **single-effect evaporator**, approximately **0.8 to 0.9 kg** of water is evaporated per kg of steam (economy ≈ 0.8).

For a **triple-effect (N=3) evaporator**, the economy approximately triples:

$$
E_{3\text{-effect}} \approx 3 \times 0.8 = 2.4
$$

Thus, the triple-effect evaporator evaporates **~2.4 kg of water per kg of live steam**, demonstrating superior thermal efficiency. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Why is backward feed preferred over forward feed for evaporating viscous liquids? List two reasons.*

**Model Answer:**

Backward feed is preferred for viscous liquids because:

1. **Temperature-Viscosity Matching:** In backward feed, the most concentrated (and hence most viscous) liquor enters the **hottest effect (Effect 1)**. The high temperature reduces viscosity, leading to a **higher overall heat transfer coefficient (U)** and better heat transfer rates.

2. **Higher Effective ΔT:** Since U increases for the concentrated liquor at high T, the effective temperature driving force across the heating surface is maintained. This results in **higher capacity and better economy** than forward feed, where viscous liquor meets the coolest surface in the last effect. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - **Do NOT confuse forward feed with parallel feed** — forward feed has feed flowing through effects sequentially; parallel feed has fresh feed entering EACH effect independently.
> - Always **state the assumption (equal area or equal U)** before writing the area formula — marks are deducted if omitted.
> - Carry forward **units of ΔT (°C or K)** consistently; mixing them causes sign errors.
> - When asked about feeding methods, **draw neat labeled sketches** (3–4 marks reserved for diagram alone).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **heat transfer area of a multiple-effect evaporator** under the assumption of equal heat transfer areas in each effect. (7 marks)

**(b)** In a **triple-effect forward-feed evaporator**, the temperature of the live steam supplied to the first effect is **120 °C**, and the vapor temperature in the last effect (under vacuum) is **50 °C**. The overall heat transfer coefficients are:

$$
U_1 = 2500 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}, \quad U_2 = 2000 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}, \quad U_3 = 1500 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}
$$

The heat loads are: $q_1 = 200$ kW, $q_2 = 180$ kW, $q_3 = 160$ kW. Calculate the **temperature distribution and area** of each effect, assuming equal areas. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Step 1:** Write Fourier's heat transfer equation for each effect:

$$
q_1 = U_1 A_1 \Delta T_1, \quad q_2 = U_2 A_2 \Delta T_2, \quad \ldots, \quad q_N = U_N A_N \Delta T_N
$$

**Step 2:** Sum the heat duties over all N effects:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} q_i = \sum_{i=1}^{N} U_i A_i \Delta T_i
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **equal area constraint** $A_1 = A_2 = \ldots = A_N = A$:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} q_i = A \sum_{i=1}^{N} U_i \Delta T_i
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the common area:

$$
\boxed{A = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} q_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} U_i \Delta T_i}}
$$

This is the working formula for the equal-area design of multi-effect evaporators. **(7 marks — full derivation)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1:** Calculate total temperature drop available:

$$
\Delta T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{steam}} - T_{\text{vacuum}} = 120 - 50 = 70 \, \text{°C}
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the ratio $q_i/U_i$ for each effect:

$$
\frac{q_1}{U_1} = \frac{200{,}000}{2500} = 80 \, \text{m}^2\text{K}
$$

$$
\frac{q_2}{U_2} = \frac{180{,}000}{2000} = 90 \, \text{m}^2\text{K}
$$

$$
\frac{q_3}{U_3} = \frac{160{,}000}{1500} = 106.67 \, \text{m}^2\text{K}
$$

**Step 3:** Sum the ratios:

$$
\sum \frac{q_i}{U_i} = 80 + 90 + 106.67 = 276.67 \, \text{m}^2\text{K}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply temperature distribution formula:

$$
\Delta T_i = \frac{\Delta T_{\text{total}} \cdot (q_i/U_i)}{\sum (q_i/U_i)}
$$

**For Effect 1:**

$$
\Delta T_1 = \frac{70 \times 80}{276.67} = 20.24 \, \text{°C}
$$

**For Effect 2:**

$$
\Delta T_2 = \frac{70 \times 90}{276.67} = 22.77 \, \text{°C}
$$

**For Effect 3:**

$$
\Delta T_3 = \frac{70 \times 106.67}{276.67} = 26.99 \, \text{°C}
$$

**Verification:** $20.24 + 22.77 + 26.99 = 70.00 \, \text{°C}$ ✓

**Step 5:** Calculate common area using any effect (using Effect 1):

$$
A = \frac{q_1}{U_1 \cdot \Delta T_1} = \frac{200{,}000}{2500 \times 20.24} = 3.95 \, \text{m}^2
$$

**Verification with Effect 2:**

$$
A = \frac{180{,}000}{2000 \times 22.77} = 3.95 \, \text{m}^2 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Verification with Effect 3:**

$$
A = \frac{160{,}000}{1500 \times 26.99} = 3.95 \, \text{m}^2 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Final Answer:** $A = 3.95 \, \text{m}^2$ for each effect. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Compare **forward feed and backward feed** multiple-effect evaporators with respect to (i) flow direction, (ii) pumping requirement, (iii) suitability for viscous liquids, and (iv) steam economy. (7 marks)

**(b)** A **double-effect evaporator** concentrates a salt solution from **10% to 30% (by mass)** at a feed rate of **10,000 kg/hr**. The enthalpy of feed is **315 kJ/kg** and the enthalpy of concentrated product is **385 kJ/kg**. The steam supplied at **120 °C** has latent heat **2200 kJ/kg**, and the latent heat of vapor from each effect is approximately **2300 kJ/kg**. Determine: (i) the **steam economy**, and (ii) the **live steam rate**. Assume no BPE and equal evaporation in each effect. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

| S.No. | Parameter | Forward Feed | Backward Feed |
|-------|-----------|--------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Flow direction** | Feed and steam flow in the **same direction** (co-current) | Feed and steam flow in **opposite directions** (counter-current) |
| 2 | **Pumping requirement** | **No pumps** needed between effects; liquid flows by natural pressure gradient | **Inter-effect pumps required** to push liquid against pressure gradient |
| 3 | **Suitability for viscous liquor** | **Poor** — viscous concentrated liquor contacts coolest surface → low U | **Best** — concentrated liquor contacts hottest surface → high U, less viscous |
| 4 | **Steam economy** | Lower due to lower U in final effects | **Higher** due to better heat transfer in final stage |

**(7 marks — 1.75 marks per row)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given Data:**
- Feed rate, $F = 10{,}000$ kg/hr at $x_F = 0.10$
- Product concentration, $x_P = 0.30$
- Enthalpy of feed, $h_F = 315$ kJ/kg
- Enthalpy of product, $h_P = 385$ kJ/kg
- Steam latent heat, $\lambda_s = 2200$ kJ/kg
- Vapor latent heat, $\lambda_v = 2300$ kJ/kg

**Step 1 — Mass balance for total water evaporated:**

Mass of solids in feed = Mass of solids in product:

$$
F \cdot x_F = P \cdot x_P
$$

$$
10{,}000 \times 0.10 = P \times 0.30 \implies P = 3333.33 \, \text{kg/hr}
$$

**Step 2 — Total water evaporated:**

$$
W = F - P = 10{,}000 - 3333.33 = 6666.67 \, \text{kg/hr}
$$

**Step 3 — Per effect (equal evaporation):**

$$
W_1 = W_2 = \frac{6666.67}{2} = 3333.33 \, \text{kg/hr}
$$

**Step 4 — Energy balance on Effect 1 (steam side):**

Live steam $W_s$ condenses and vaporizes $W_1$ water in Effect 1:

$$
W_s \cdot \lambda_s = W_1 \cdot \lambda_v
$$

$$
W_s = \frac{W_1 \cdot \lambda_v}{\lambda_s} = \frac{3333.33 \times 2300}{2200} = 3484.85 \, \text{kg/hr}
$$

**Step 5 — Steam economy:**

$$
E = \frac{W_{\text{total}}}{W_s} = \frac{6666.67}{3484.85} = 1.913
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) Steam economy ≈ **1.91**
- (ii) Live steam rate ≈ **3484.85 kg/hr**

**(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Multiple-effect evaporator** uses vapor from one effect as heating medium for the next.
- [x] **Steam economy** ≈ 0.8 × N (where N = number of effects).
- [x] **Capacity** = Economy × Steam rate supplied.
- [x] **Forward feed** → no pumps, but poor for viscous liquor.
- [x] **Backward feed** → requires pumps, best for viscous liquor.
- [x] **Parallel feed** → used for crystallizers (slurry removal per effect).
- [x] **Mixed feed** → hybrid; used in large industrial plants.
- [x] **Equal-area design formula**: $A = \dfrac{\sum q_i}{\sum U_i \Delta T_i}$
- [x] **Temperature distribution**: $\Delta T_i \propto \dfrac{q_i}{U_i}$
- [x] **Total ΔT** = T_steam − T_vacuum − Σ(BPE) − Rank losses.
- [x] **Dühring rule** for BPE: $T_b = a + b \cdot T_w$ (linear relation).
- [x] **Always state assumptions** (equal area, equal U, no BPE) before solving.
- [x] **Memorize the 4 feeding methods** with their flow directions and at least 2 advantages/disadvantages each.
- [x] **Units check**: q in W, U in W/m²·K, A in m², ΔT in K or °C (consistent).

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** In KTU 14-mark questions on this topic, always:
> 1. Draw a labeled sketch (4 marks).
> 2. Write the heat balance and area derivation (4 marks).
> 3. Compute numerical values step-by-step with units (4 marks).
> 4. State the final answer with proper units (2 marks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boiling Heat Transfer: Types of Boiling
Factors Affecting Boiling Heat Transfer Coefficient
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT501 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Boiling Heat Transfer Coefficient

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Boiling Heat Transfer Coefficient (h_b)** is a proportionality constant that quantifies the rate of convective heat transfer from a solid heated surface to a liquid undergoing a phase change (boiling), as expressed in Newton's Law of Cooling:

$$q = h_b \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_s$$

where $q$ is the heat transfer rate, $A$ is the heat transfer area, and $\Delta T_s$ is the surface superheat (excess temperature of the heated surface above the saturation temperature of the liquid).

> [!NOTE]
> In **pool boiling**, the liquid is stationary and motion is induced solely by natural convection and bubble dynamics, whereas in **flow boiling**, the bulk motion of the fluid augments the heat transfer coefficient significantly. The present discussion pertains primarily to **saturated pool boiling**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The boiling heat transfer coefficient is **not a constant** for a given fluid-surface combination. It is a strong, non-linear function of the surface superheat, system pressure, surface conditions, and thermophysical properties of the liquid. Typical values of h_b in nucleate pool boiling range from **h_b ≈ 2,500 to 50,000 W/m²·K**, which is one to two orders of magnitude higher than single-phase convection.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine heating a pan of water on a gas stove. When the water first becomes lukewarm, you see very little action — heat is being transferred mostly by slow natural convection. As the bottom of the pan gets hotter, suddenly tiny bubbles start forming all over the inner surface, rising rapidly to the top, and the water appears to "roll." This is **nucleate boiling** — and it is astonishingly efficient. The pan's surface is essentially being swept clean of insulating liquid and replaced by fresh, cool liquid by the violent bubble activity. The pan's bottom temperature stays relatively low even though a lot of heat is being transferred.

If you keep turning the flame higher and higher, eventually the surface becomes so hot that a continuous **vapor film** forms under the pan, insulating the metal from the water (like the Leidenfrost effect seen when water droplets dance on a hot skillet). The water can no longer touch the pan efficiently, and the surface temperature shoots up dangerously. This is **film boiling** — a regime where the heat transfer coefficient **collapses** even though the surface is hotter.

The factors that affect h_b are essentially those that determine:
1. **How easily bubbles can nucleate** on the surface (cavities, wettability, contact angle, surface roughness).
2. **How vigorously the bubbles grow and detach** (superheat, pressure, surface tension, density difference).
3. **How fast the surrounding liquid can supply fresh fluid** to the surface (subcooling, liquid thermal conductivity, viscosity).

A parameter that **promotes bubble formation and detachment** generally **increases h_b**; a parameter that **stabilizes a vapor blanket** generally **decreases h_b**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range (Water, 1 atm) |
|---|---|---|
| Surface Superheat | $\Delta T_s = T_w - T_{sat}$ | **5 °C – 25 °C** (nucleate boiling) |
| Critical Heat Flux | $q_{CHF}$ | **≈ 1.25 MW/m²** |
| Boiling Heat Transfer Coefficient | $h_b$ | **2,500 – 50,000 W/m²·K** |
| Nucleation Site Density | $N_a$ | **10² – 10⁸ sites/m²** |
| Contact Angle | $\theta$ | **0° – 90°** (wettable) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Boiling Regimes — The Foundation

To understand *which* factors dominate, students must first understand the **boiling curve** (Nukiyama curve), which plots $\log q$ versus $\Delta T_s$:

- **Region I — Natural Convection** (low $\Delta T_s$): No bubbles; liquid is subcooled or just at saturation.
- **Region II — Nucleate Boiling**: Discrete bubbles form at active cavities, grow, and detach. $h_b$ is high and increases with $\Delta T_s$.
- **Region III — Transition Boiling (Partial Film Boiling)**: Unstable; vapor patches coexist with liquid-wetted patches. h_b decreases.
- **Region IV — Stable Film Boiling**: Continuous vapor film; h_b is low and rises slowly with $\Delta T_s$ via radiation and conduction through vapor.

The peak of the curve (between Region II and III) is the **Critical Heat Flux (CHF)**, also called **burnout point**, beyond which the heater surface may fail catastrophically.

### 2.2 The Principal Factors and Their Influence

Each factor is presented below with its physical *why*, *how*, and the *trend* it produces on $h_b$.

#### A. Surface Superheat ($\Delta T_s = T_w - T_{sat}$)

- **Why it matters:** The superheat provides the thermodynamic driving force for bubble nucleation at cavities (the wall must be hot enough to evaporate liquid trapped inside surface crevices).
- **How it acts:** As $\Delta T_s$ rises within nucleate boiling, more cavities become active (smaller cavities get activated) and the bubble departure frequency and diameter increase.
- **Trend:** $h_b$ **increases** sharply with $\Delta T_s$ in nucleate boiling (typically $h_b \propto \Delta T_s^{2 \text{ to } 3}$ for water). Past the CHF, $h_b$ decreases.

#### B. System Pressure (and hence Saturation Temperature)

- **Why it matters:** Pressure governs $T_{sat}$, $\rho_l$, $\rho_v$, $\sigma$, $h_{fg}$, $k_l$, $\mu_l$ — all of which appear in boiling correlations.
- **How it acts:**
  - As pressure increases toward the critical point, the density ratio $\rho_l / \rho_v$ decreases, weakening the buoyancy-driven bubble departure.
  - Surface tension $\sigma$ decreases, lowering the required cavity radius for activation (more sites activate).
  - Latent heat $h_{fg}$ decreases, reducing the per-bubble energy transport.
  - Liquid thermal conductivity $k_l$ generally increases modestly.
- **Trend:** $h_b$ typically **increases** with pressure up to roughly $p_r \approx 0.3$–$0.4$, then **decreases** sharply as the critical point is approached. For water: $h_b$ peaks near **20–30 bar**.

#### C. Surface Condition (Roughness, Cavity Size, Wettability, Material)

- **Why it matters:** Boiling is a **nucleation-controlled** process. Bubbles form preferentially in microscopic cavities (crevices, pits, scratches) that trap non-condensable gas or vapor.
- **How it acts:**
  - **Rougher surfaces** (controlled roughness, porous coatings, sintered coatings) provide more active nucleation sites → **more bubbles, higher $h_b$**, and lower required $\Delta T_s$ for incipient boiling.
  - **Highly polished or superhydrophobic surfaces** suppress nucleation → **lower $h_b$** at low superheat (but may enhance CHF in some cases).
  - **Contact angle $\theta$** between liquid and surface: smaller $\theta$ (more wettable) allows liquid to enter narrower crevices, activating more sites; but very small $\theta$ can prevent bubble detachment.
- **Trend:** $h_b$ **increases** with controlled surface roughness up to an optimum (after which the surface becomes so rough that vapor traps cause early CHF).

#### D. Surface Orientation (Horizontal vs. Vertical, Upward vs. Downward Facing)

- **Why it matters:** Buoyancy determines the direction of bubble motion relative to the heated surface.
- **How it acts:**
  - **Horizontal, upward-facing** surface: bubbles rise directly away from the surface by buoyancy → efficient liquid replenishment → **highest $h_b$**.
  - **Horizontal, downward-facing** surface: bubbles must rise against gravity through the liquid film under the plate → slow escape → liquid film overheats → **lowest $h_b$ and lowest CHF**.
  - **Vertical surface**: intermediate behavior, with bubbles sliding upward and providing a thin falling liquid film.
- **Trend:** $h_b$ is **highest for upward-facing horizontal surfaces**, lowest for downward-facing surfaces.

#### E. Subcooling of the Liquid ($\Delta T_{sub} = T_{sat} - T_{bulk}$)

- **Why it matters:** Subcooled bulk liquid condenses rising vapor bubbles, enhancing liquid agitation and slightly raising $h_b$ before bubbles actually reach the free surface.
- **How it acts:** Subcooling provides additional convective refreshment of the surface and causes bubble collapse near the surface, which promotes micro-convection.
- **Trend:** $h_b$ **increases** modestly with subcooling (typically 10–30% increase per 20 °C subcooling for water at atmospheric pressure).

#### F. Thermophysical Properties of the Liquid

- **Surface tension ($\sigma$):** Lower $\sigma$ → smaller required cavity radius for activation (Rohsenow's $r_c \propto \sigma$) → **more sites active → higher $h_b$**.
- **Latent heat of vaporization ($h_{fg}$):** Higher $h_{fg}$ → more energy carried per bubble → **higher $h_b$**.
- **Liquid thermal conductivity ($k_l$):** Higher $k_l$ → faster conduction from surface to bubble base → **higher $h_b$**.
- **Liquid viscosity ($\mu_l$):** Higher $\mu_l$ → thicker thermal boundary layer and slower bubble dynamics → **lower $h_b$**.
- **Density difference ($\rho_l - \rho_v$):** Larger difference → stronger buoyancy-driven bubble removal → **higher $h_b$**.
- **Specific heat ($c_{p,l}$):** Higher $c_{p,l}$ → more energy absorbed by the liquid per degree temperature change → higher transient $h_b$ for short heating pulses.

#### G. Heat Flux (q)

- **Why it matters:** Heat flux is the *cause* and $h_b$ is one of the *effects* through the relation $q = h_b A \Delta T_s$. In nucleate boiling, increasing $q$ activates more nucleation sites, raising $h_b$.
- **How it acts:** Higher $q$ → more energy supplied → more vapor generation → more bubble activity → $h_b$ rises automatically to dissipate the additional flux. This is the **self-regulating** nature of nucleate boiling.
- **Trend:** In nucleate boiling, $h_b$ **increases** with $q$ (and hence with $\Delta T_s$).

#### H. Non-Condensable Gases in the Liquid

- **Why it matters:** Bubbles in nucleate boiling grow from gas trapped in surface cavities. The presence of dissolved or trapped non-condensable gases provides pre-existing nuclei.
- **How it acts:** A fully degassed liquid requires higher superheat to initiate boiling (cavities empty by dissolution), and may exhibit **boiling hysteresis**. A gas-saturated liquid boils readily.
- **Trend:** $h_b$ is **higher** for gas-saturated liquids at low superheat.

#### I. Electric Field / External Fields (Advanced)

- Application of an electric field or ultrasonic vibration can enhance bubble detachment and thus raise $h_b$ — relevant in some specialized reboilers.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Rohsenow Correlation** is the most-tested empirical equation for nucleate pool boiling. The KTU examiner expects mastery of its form, applicability, and the meaning of the surface-fluid interaction parameter $C_{sf}$.

| # | Equation / Relation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q = h_b \, A \, \Delta T_s$ | Definition of boiling heat transfer coefficient |
| 2 | $q = \mu_l \, h_{fg} \left[\dfrac{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\sigma}\right]^{1/2} \left[\dfrac{c_{p,l} \Delta T_s}{C_{sf} \, h_{fg} \, P_r_l^{\,n}}\right]^3$ | **Rohsenow correlation** (nucleate pool boiling) |
| 3 | $\Delta T_s = \dfrac{h_{fg}}{c_{p,l}} \, P_r_l^{\,n} \left[\dfrac{q}{\mu_l \, h_{fg}} \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma}{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}}\right]^{1/3} C_{sf}^{1/3}$ | Inverted Rohsenow (solved for $\Delta T_s$) |
| 4 | $r_c = \dfrac{2 \sigma \, T_{sat}}{h_{fg} \, \rho_v \, \Delta T_s}$ | **Critical cavity radius** for nucleation |
| 5 | $q_{CHF} = 0.149 \, h_{fg} \, \rho_v \left[\dfrac{\sigma \, g (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^{\,2}}\right]^{1/4}$ | **Zuber's correlation** for critical heat flux |
| 6 | $\Delta T_{sat} = T_{sat}(p) - T_{sat}(p_{ref})$ | Saturation temperature shift with pressure |
| 7 | $P_r = \dfrac{c_p \, \mu}{k}$ | Prandtl number of the liquid |
| 8 | $h_b = \dfrac{q}{\Delta T_s}$ | Direct definition of h_b from q-ΔT data |
| 9 | $C_{sf}$ (water-copper, polished) = 0.0130 | Typical surface-fluid constant |
| 10 | Exponent $n$ (water) = 1.0; (other fluids) = 1.7 | Rohsenow exponent |

> **Mark this cheat sheet** — at least 60% of KTU numericals in boiling draw directly from Rohsenow's correlation (Eq. 2 and 3).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of reboilers** in distillation columns: The boiling side HTC dictates the required heat transfer area and directly affects column throughput.
- **Nuclear reactor coolant channels**: Boiling crisis (CHF) must be avoided; understanding the factors affecting h_b prevents fuel rod burnout.
- **Electronics cooling**: Boiling of dielectric fluids on micro-structured surfaces is an active research area for high-heat-flux chip cooling.
- **Steam generators, kettle reboilers, thermosyphon reboilers**: All depend on controlled nucleate boiling for safe, efficient operation.
- **Cryogenics and LNG vaporizers**: Boiling of cryogens like liquid nitrogen and LNG requires careful attention to orientation, subcooling, and surface condition.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of the Critical Cavity Radius

Consider a hemispherical gas-filled cavity of radius $r$ on a horizontal heating surface immersed in a liquid at saturation temperature $T_{sat}$, with the wall at $T_w > T_{sat}$. The liquid in the cavity will be in equilibrium with vapor when the **mechanical equilibrium** of the meniscus is satisfied.

The pressure difference across a curved liquid-vapor interface is given by the **Young-Laplace equation**:

$$\Delta p = \dfrac{2 \sigma}{r_{meniscus}}$$

For a cavity with contact angle $\theta$, the meniscus radius $r_{meniscus}$ relates to the cavity radius $r_c$ as:

$$r_{meniscus} = \dfrac{r_c}{\cos \theta}$$

Mechanical equilibrium of the trapped vapor at temperature $T_w$ with the bulk liquid at $T_{sat}$ requires the saturation pressure at the wall to overcome the local hydrostatic and surface-tension-induced pressure:

$$p_{sat}(T_w) = p_{sat}(T_{sat}) + \dfrac{2 \sigma}{r_{meniscus}}$$

For small temperature differences, we can linearize the Clapeyron-type relation:

$$p_{sat}(T_w) - p_{sat}(T_{sat}) = \dfrac{h_{fg} \, \rho_v}{T_{sat}} \, (T_w - T_{sat}) = \dfrac{h_{fg} \, \rho_v}{T_{sat}} \, \Delta T_s$$

Substituting and using $\cos \theta \approx 1$ (fully wetting):

$$\dfrac{h_{fg} \, \rho_v \, \Delta T_s}{T_{sat}} = \dfrac{2 \sigma}{r_c}$$

Solving for the minimum (critical) cavity radius that can sustain a bubble at a given superheat:

$$\boxed{r_c = \dfrac{2 \sigma \, T_{sat}}{h_{fg} \, \rho_v \, \Delta T_s}}$$

**Physical interpretation:** Only cavities with $r \ge r_c$ can act as active nucleation sites at the given $\Delta T_s$. As $\Delta T_s$ increases, $r_c$ **decreases**, so smaller and more numerous cavities become active — this is why $h_b$ rises with superheat.

### 3.2 Derivation Sketch of the Rohsenow Correlation

Rohsenow (1952) hypothesized that nucleate boiling heat transfer is analogous to a **forced-convection problem** in which the bubbles themselves are the "fluid packets" being convected. He used dimensional analysis plus experimental data to obtain the form:

$$\dfrac{c_{p,l} \, \Delta T_s}{h_{fg}} = C_{sf} \left[\dfrac{q}{\mu_l \, h_{fg}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma}{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}}\right]^{1/3} P_r_l^{\,n}$$

Cubing both sides and rearranging for $q$:

$$q = \mu_l \, h_{fg} \left[\dfrac{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\sigma}\right]^{1/2} \left[\dfrac{c_{p,l} \, \Delta T_s}{C_{sf} \, h_{fg} \, P_r_l^{\,n}}\right]^{3}$$

Dividing both sides by $\Delta T_s$ yields the boiling heat transfer coefficient:

$$h_b = \dfrac{q}{\Delta T_s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **surface-fluid interaction parameter $C_{sf}$** and exponent $n$ are **empirical**. For the water-copper (polished) combination, $C_{sf} = 0.0130$ and $n = 1.0$. For other fluid-surface pairs, tables in standard heat transfer textbooks (Incropera, Holman, Kern) provide the values. The examiner will often provide $C_{sf}$ and $n$ in numerical problems.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** Water at atmospheric pressure ($T_{sat} = 100$ °C) boils on a polished copper surface ($C_{sf} = 0.0130$, $n = 1.0$). Properties at saturation: $c_{p,l} = 4217$ J/kg·K, $\mu_l = 2.77 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/m·s, $h_{fg} = 2.257 \times 10^{6}$ J/kg, $\rho_l = 957.9$ kg/m³, $\rho_v = 0.5956$ kg/m³, $\sigma = 0.0589$ N/m, $P_{r,l} = 1.76$, $k_l = 0.679$ W/m·K. The measured surface temperature is $T_w = 110$ °C.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the heat flux $q$.
> **(b)** Calculate the boiling heat transfer coefficient $h_b$.
> **(c)** Calculate the critical cavity radius $r_c$.

**Solution:**

**Part (a): Heat flux $q$**

Apply the Rohsenow correlation:

$$q = \mu_l \, h_{fg} \left[\dfrac{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\sigma}\right]^{1/2} \left[\dfrac{c_{p,l} \, \Delta T_s}{C_{sf} \, h_{fg} \, P_{r,l}^{\,n}}\right]^{3}$$

First, $\Delta T_s = 110 - 100 = 10$ °C = 10 K.

Compute the buoyancy-frequency term:

$$\left[\dfrac{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\sigma}\right]^{1/2} = \left[\dfrac{9.81 \times (957.9 - 0.5956)}{0.0589}\right]^{1/2}$$

$$\left[\dfrac{9.81 \times 957.3}{0.0589}\right]^{1/2} = \left[1.595 \times 10^{5}\right]^{1/2} = 399.4 \, \text{m}^{-1}$$

Now compute the inner bracket:

$$\dfrac{c_{p,l} \, \Delta T_s}{C_{sf} \, h_{fg} \, P_{r,l}^{\,n}} = \dfrac{4217 \times 10}{0.0130 \times 2.257 \times 10^{6} \times 1.76}$$

$$= \dfrac{4.217 \times 10^{4}}{5.164 \times 10^{4}} = 0.8166$$

Cube it:

$$(0.8166)^{3} = 0.5446$$

Now the leading factor:

$$\mu_l \, h_{fg} = 2.77 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.257 \times 10^{6} = 625.2 \, \text{W/m}$$

Putting it all together:

$$q = 625.2 \times 399.4 \times 0.5446 = 1.359 \times 10^{5} \, \text{W/m}^{2}$$

$$\boxed{q \approx 1.36 \times 10^{5} \text{ W/m}^{2} = 136 \text{ kW/m}^{2}}$$

**Part (b): Boiling heat transfer coefficient**

$$h_b = \dfrac{q}{\Delta T_s} = \dfrac{1.359 \times 10^{5}}{10} = 1.359 \times 10^{4} \, \text{W/m}^{2}\!\cdot\!\text{K}$$

$$\boxed{h_b \approx 13{,}600 \, \text{W/m}^{2}\!\cdot\!\text{K}}$$

**Part (c): Critical cavity radius**

$$r_c = \dfrac{2 \sigma \, T_{sat}}{h_{fg} \, \rho_v \, \Delta T_s}$$

Use $T_{sat} = 373$ K:

$$r_c = \dfrac{2 \times 0.0589 \times 373}{2.257 \times 10^{6} \times 0.5956 \times 10} = \dfrac{43.94}{1.344 \times 10^{7}}$$

$$\boxed{r_c \approx 3.27 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m} = 3.27 \, \mu\text{m}}$$

Only cavities of radius ≥ 3.27 μm on the copper surface can act as active nucleation sites at $\Delta T_s = 10$ K.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Direction of Effect of Each Factor on h_b

| Factor | Increase → Effect on $h_b$ | Physical Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Surface superheat $\Delta T_s$ | **↑ Increases** (in nucleate boiling) | More cavities activated, higher bubble frequency |
| System pressure $p$ | **↑ then ↓** (peak at $p_r \approx 0.3$) | $\rho_l/\rho_v$ decreases, $h_{fg}$ decreases near $p_c$ |
| Surface roughness (controlled) | **↑ Increases** | More active cavities, more nucleation sites |
| Wettability (lower $\theta$) | **↑ Increases** (up to a point) | Liquid enters narrower crevices, more sites active |
| Subcooling $\Delta T_{sub}$ | **↑ Modest increase** | Bubble collapse near surface enhances micro-convection |
| Surface tension $\sigma$ | **↓ Decreases** | Smaller $r_c$ required but also weaker bubble departure |
| Latent heat $h_{fg}$ | **↑ Increases** | More energy carried per unit mass evaporated |
| Liquid thermal conductivity $k_l$ | **↑ Increases** | Better conduction from wall to bubble base |
| Liquid viscosity $\mu_l$ | **↓ Decreases** | Thicker thermal boundary layer, sluggish bubbles |
| Density difference $(\rho_l - \rho_v)$ | **↑ Increases** | Stronger buoyancy-driven bubble detachment |
| Dissolved / trapped gases | **↑ Increases** | Pre-existing nuclei, less superheat needed |
| Upward-facing surface | **↑ Highest** | Buoyancy aids bubble removal |
| Downward-facing surface | **↓ Lowest** | Bubbles trapped under plate, film overheats |
| Beyond CHF (film boiling) | **↓ Drops sharply** | Continuous vapor film insulates surface |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATS

### 4.1 Pool Boiling Regimes — Nukiyama Curve Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Increasing Surface Superheat ΔTs] --> B[Region I: Natural Convection]
    B --> C[Region II: Nucleate Boiling - Discrete Bubbles]
    C --> D[Incipient Boiling - First Active Cavities]
    D --> E[Developed Nucleate Boiling - h_b is MAXIMUM here]
    E --> F[Region III: Transition Boiling - Mixed Wetting]
    F --> G[CRITICAL HEAT FLUX qCHF - Boiling Crisis]
    G --> H[Region IV: Stable Film Boiling - Vapor Blanket]
    H --> I[Region V: Post-CHF - Radiation Dominant]

    J[Heat Flux q rises with ΔTs] -.-> B
    J -.-> C
    J -.-> E
    K[Heat Flux q PEAKS at CHF] -.-> G
    L[Heat Flux q falls sharply] -.-> H
    L -.-> I

    M[Liquid Properties ρl ρv σ μl cp hfg] --> E
    N[Surface Properties Roughness Wettability θ Cavities] --> E
    O[Pressure p and Tsat] --> E
    P[Subcooling ΔTsub] --> E
    Q[Surface Orientation] --> E
```

**Interpretation of the architecture:** The flow chart maps how the *factors* (right-side blocks M–Q) feed into the **nucleate boiling regime** (block E), which is the *operating window* of interest. Above block E lies the **boiling crisis** (CHF), and below lies **film boiling** — both undesirable in reboiler design. The arrows J, K, L track the heat-flux trend across regimes.

### 4.2 Bubble Nucleation at a Surface Cavity — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Heated Wall at Tw] --> B[Cavity of Radius r on Surface]
    B --> C{Trapped Gas or Vapor Nucleus}
    C -->|r ≥ r_critical| D[Bubble Activated and Grows]
    C -->|r less than r_critical| E[Cavity Inactive - Liquid Fills Crevice]
    D --> F[Buoyancy Lifts Bubble]
    F --> G[Bubble Detaches - Diameter db]
    G --> H[Fresh Liquid Replenishes Surface]
    H --> I[Enhanced Convective Transport h_b increases]
    E --> J[No Boiling at this Site]
    J --> K[Heat Transfer only by Natural Convection]
```

**Interpretation:** The decision block C is governed by the **critical cavity radius** equation (Eq. 4 in the cheat sheet). Only cavities meeting the size criterion participate in active boiling.

### 4.3 Factor-Influence Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BoilHTC[Boiling Heat Transfer Coefficient h_b] --> A1[Surface Superheat ΔTs]
    BoilHTC --> A2[System Pressure p]
    BoilHTC --> A3[Surface Condition]
    BoilHTC --> A4[Surface Orientation]
    BoilHTC --> A5[Liquid Subcooling]
    BoilHTC --> A6[Thermophysical Properties]
    BoilHTC --> A7[Dissolved Gases]
    BoilHTC --> A8[Heat Flux q - self regulating]

    A3 --> A3a[Roughness Controlled]
    A3 --> A3b[Contact Angle θ]
    A3 --> A3c[Cavity Size Distribution]

    A6 --> A6a[Surface Tension σ]
    A6 --> A6b[Latent Heat hfg]
    A6 --> A6c[Liquid Conductivity kl]
    A6 --> A6d[Liquid Viscosity μl]
    A6 --> A6e[Density Ratio ρl to ρv]
```

**Interpretation:** This block-level functional map shows the **eight primary factor families** that govern $h_b$ in saturated pool boiling. The KTU examiner frequently poses "list and explain the factors" questions (3-mark Part A) and "compare the influence of two factors" questions (7-mark Part B subparts).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2]**
*List any six factors that affect the boiling heat transfer coefficient in saturated pool boiling.*

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**
1. **Surface superheat** ($\Delta T_s$): A larger superheat activates more nucleation sites and raises $h_b$ within nucleate boiling.
2. **System pressure** ($p$): Influences $T_{sat}$, $\rho_v$, $h_{fg}$, $\sigma$; $h_b$ peaks at moderate reduced pressure.
3. **Surface condition** (roughness, cavity size, wettability): Rougher wettable surfaces provide more active sites, raising $h_b$.
4. **Surface orientation**: Upward-facing horizontal surfaces yield the highest $h_b$ due to buoyancy-assisted bubble removal.
5. **Subcooling**: Subcooled bulk liquid enhances micro-convection and slightly raises $h_b$.
6. **Thermophysical properties of the liquid** ($\sigma$, $h_{fg}$, $k_l$, $\mu_l$, $\rho_l$, $\rho_v$): All appear in the Rohsenow correlation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Cycle]**
*Define the critical cavity radius. How does it vary with surface superheat?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **critical cavity radius** $r_c$ is the minimum radius of a surface cavity that can sustain an active bubble at a given surface superheat $\Delta T_s$. It is given by:
$$r_c = \dfrac{2 \sigma \, T_{sat}}{h_{fg} \, \rho_v \, \Delta T_s}$$
**Variation with superheat:** $r_c$ is **inversely proportional** to $\Delta T_s$. As $\Delta T_s$ increases, $r_c$ decreases, so smaller and more numerous cavities become active — this is the mechanism by which superheat increases the boiling heat transfer coefficient. **(Full 3 marks: definition 1 + equation 1 + variation 1)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the **Nukiyama boiling curve** and identify the regimes. Discuss how the boiling heat transfer coefficient varies across these regimes.

**(b) [7 marks)** Using the **Rohsenow correlation**, derive the expression for the heat flux and discuss how the boiling heat transfer coefficient is affected by **(i)** system pressure, **(ii)** surface condition, and **(iii)** subcooling of the liquid.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Nukiyama Boiling Curve (7 marks)**

The Nukiyama curve plots the logarithm of heat flux $q$ against the surface superheat $\Delta T_s$ for a heater immersed in a stagnant saturated liquid. The curve has the following regimes (with $q$ on a log scale):

- **Region I — Natural Convection** ($\Delta T_s \le 4$ °C for water): Liquid is subcooled or just at saturation; heat transfer is by single-phase natural convection only. $h_b$ is small (~50–500 W/m²·K).
- **Region II — Nucleate Boiling** ($\Delta T_s \approx 4$–25 °C for water): Discrete bubbles form at active cavities, grow, and detach. The vigorous bubble agitation provides a very high effective $h_b$ (≈ 2,500–50,000 W/m²·K). The curve rises steeply (almost vertically on a log plot).
- **Region III — Transition (Partial Film) Boiling** ($\Delta T_s \approx 25$–120 °C for water): Vapor patches and liquid-wetted patches coexist. The curve **bends over and decreases**; $h_b$ falls.
- **Region IV — Stable Film Boiling** ($\Delta T_s > 120$ °C for water): A continuous vapor film blankets the heater. Heat transfer is by conduction through vapor plus radiation; $h_b$ is low (~100–500 W/m²·K) but slowly rises with $\Delta T_s$.

The peak of the curve (junction of II and III) is the **Critical Heat Flux (CHF)** or **burnout point** — the most important design constraint in boiling equipment. **(Sketch description worth 2 marks; regime-wise description 4 marks; identification of CHF 1 mark = 7 marks)**

**(b) Rohsenow Correlation and Factors (7 marks)**

Rohsenow's correlation is:

$$q = \mu_l \, h_{fg} \left[\dfrac{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\sigma}\right]^{1/2} \left[\dfrac{c_{p,l} \, \Delta T_s}{C_{sf} \, h_{fg} \, P_{r,l}^{\,n}}\right]^{3}$$

**Derivation outline (3 marks):** Based on dimensional analysis of the boiling process, treating bubbles as convection packets. The dimensionless groups emerge from the balance of buoyancy, inertia, surface tension, and viscous forces at the cavity mouth. The cube of the inner bracket arises from correlating bubble departure diameter to superheat.

**Effect of factors (4 marks):**

- **(i) System pressure (2 marks):** Pressure influences $T_{sat}$, $\rho_l$, $\rho_v$, $\sigma$, $h_{fg}$, $k_l$, $\mu_l$. As $p$ increases up to roughly $p_r \approx 0.3$, $h_b$ increases (more sites active due to lower $\sigma$, higher $k_l$). Beyond $p_r \approx 0.4$, $h_{fg}$ falls sharply and $\rho_l - \rho_v$ shrinks, so $h_b$ drops rapidly, approaching zero at the critical point. The Rohsenow correlation captures this via the $(\rho_l - \rho_v)$ and $\sigma$ terms.

- **(ii) Surface condition (1 mark):** Rougher wettable surfaces supply more active cavities, lowering the incipient boiling superheat and raising $h_b$. In the Rohsenow correlation, surface condition enters via $C_{sf}$ — a **smaller $C_{sf}$** corresponds to a more boiling-friendly surface, giving a **higher $q$ for the same $\Delta T_s$**, i.e., higher $h_b$.

- **(iii) Subcooling (1 mark):** Subcooled bulk liquid enhances micro-convection by condensing vapor bubbles near the surface. Subcooling is *not* explicitly in the Rohsenow correlation, but it raises $h_b$ by 10–30% per 20 °C subcooling for water, and is sometimes incorporated as a correction factor in extended forms (e.g., the Forster-Zuber or Berenson correlations).

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q3B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Cycle 1]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **factors affecting nucleate boiling heat transfer coefficient** with emphasis on **(i)** surface superheat, **(ii)** surface orientation, and **(iii)** thermophysical properties of the liquid.

**(b) [7 marks]** A polished copper plate is used to boil saturated water at **atmospheric pressure**. The surface temperature is maintained at **108 °C**. Using the **Rohsenow correlation** ($C_{sf} = 0.0130$, $n = 1.0$) and the following properties, calculate the heat flux and the boiling heat transfer coefficient.
Given: $c_{p,l} = 4217$ J/kg·K, $\mu_l = 2.77 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/m·s, $h_{fg} = 2.257 \times 10^{6}$ J/kg, $\rho_l = 957.9$ kg/m³, $\rho_v = 0.5956$ kg/m³, $\sigma = 0.0589$ N/m, $P_{r,l} = 1.76$.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Discussion of Factors (7 marks)**

*(i) Surface superheat (2.5 marks):* $\Delta T_s = T_w - T_{sat}$ is the primary driver. A higher $\Delta T_s$ activates progressively smaller cavities (since $r_c \propto 1/\Delta T_s$), raises bubble departure frequency, and increases the nucleation site density. Within the nucleate boiling regime, $h_b \propto \Delta T_s^{2\text{–}3}$ for water, so a small change in $\Delta T_s$ produces a large change in $h_b$. Beyond CHF, $h_b$ decreases.

*(ii) Surface orientation (2 marks):* For an upward-facing horizontal surface, buoyancy lifts bubbles vertically away, allowing rapid liquid replenishment — **highest $h_b$**. For a vertical surface, bubbles slide upward along the wall in a thin film. For a downward-facing horizontal surface, bubbles must rise *against* gravity through the liquid layer beneath the plate, leading to vapor entrapment, local overheating, and **lowest $h_b$** (often 50% or less of the upward-facing value).

*(iii) Thermophysical properties (2.5 marks):*
- **Surface tension $\sigma$:** Lower $\sigma$ → smaller $r_c$ → more active sites → higher $h_b$.
- **Latent heat $h_{fg}$:** Higher $h_{fg}$ → more energy carried per unit mass evaporated → higher $h_b$.
- **Liquid conductivity $k_l$:** Higher $k_l$ → better conduction from wall to bubble → higher $h_b$.
- **Liquid viscosity $\mu_l$:** Higher $\mu_l$ → thicker boundary layer, slower bubble growth → lower $h_b$.
- **Density ratio $\rho_l/\rho_v$:** Larger ratio → stronger buoyancy → higher $h_b$.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Calculate $\Delta T_s = 108 - 100 = 8$ K.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the buoyancy-frequency term:

$$\left[\dfrac{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\sigma}\right]^{1/2} = \left[\dfrac{9.81 \times 957.3}{0.0589}\right]^{1/2} = 399.4 \, \text{m}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the inner bracket:

$$\dfrac{c_{p,l} \, \Delta T_s}{C_{sf} \, h_{fg} \, P_{r,l}} = \dfrac{4217 \times 8}{0.0130 \times 2.257 \times 10^{6} \times 1.76} = \dfrac{3.374 \times 10^{4}}{5.164 \times 10^{4}} = 0.6533$$

Cubed: $(0.6533)^{3} = 0.2787$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute $q$:

$$q = 2.77 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.257 \times 10^{6} \times 399.4 \times 0.2787 = 6.95 \times 10^{4} \, \text{W/m}^{2}$$

$$\boxed{q \approx 69.5 \, \text{kW/m}^{2}}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute $h_b$:

$$h_b = \dfrac{q}{\Delta T_s} = \dfrac{6.95 \times 10^{4}}{8} = 8688 \, \text{W/m}^{2}\!\cdot\!\text{K}$$

$$\boxed{h_b \approx 8690 \, \text{W/m}^{2}\!\cdot\!\text{K}}$$

**Verification (comment for examiner):** $h_b$ is well within the nucleate boiling range (2,500–50,000 W/m²·K), and $q$ is well below CHF (≈ 1.25 MW/m² for water at 1 atm), confirming the assumption of nucleate boiling is valid. **(+1 mark for valid physical check if explicitly stated.)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units consistency in Rohsenow's correlation.** The $C_{sf}$ and $n$ values given in the problem are *dimensionless only when SI units are used throughout*. A common error is to mix CGS with SI, producing $h_b$ values off by orders of magnitude. **Always check** that $q$ comes out in W/m² and $h_b$ in W/m²·K.
>
> 2. **Confusing $C_{sf}$ (dimensionless) with the fluid-only constant.** Students sometimes try to "look up" $C_{sf}$ for a fluid — it is a *fluid-surface pair* constant. Water-stainless steel is **not** the same as water-copper.
>
> 3. **Using Rohsenow outside nucleate boiling.** The correlation is valid only in Region II (nucleate boiling). If the computed $\Delta T_s$ exceeds ~25 °C for water at 1 atm, or the computed $q$ exceeds the Zuber CHF (≈ 1.25 MW/m² for water at 1 atm), the assumption is violated and the answer must be discarded.
>
> 4. **Exponent $n$ confusion.** $n = 1.0$ for water, $n = 1.7$ for most other fluids. Using $n = 1.7$ for water inverts the result.
>
> 5. **Neglecting to mention pressure effect on $h_{fg}$, $\rho_v$, and $\sigma$.** When asked "how does pressure affect $h_b$?", students often give a one-liner. The full answer requires discussing the *competing effects* on the Rohsenow terms.
>
> 6. **In Part A definition of $r_c$**, students frequently omit the **physical meaning** (minimum radius for active cavity) and jump straight to the equation. Always state the definition *before* the equation.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Boiling HTC is not constant** — it is a strong, non-linear function of superheat, pressure, surface, and fluid properties.
- The **Rohsenow correlation** is the most-tested equation in KTU: $q = \mu_l h_{fg}\left[\frac{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\sigma}\right]^{1/2}\left[\frac{c_{p,l}\Delta T_s}{C_{sf}h_{fg}P_{r,l}^{n}}\right]^{3}$.
- The **critical cavity radius** $r_c = \frac{2\sigma T_{sat}}{h_{fg}\rho_v \Delta T_s}$ is the *gatekeeper* of nucleate boiling: only cavities with $r \ge r_c$ participate.
- **Eight primary factor families** govern $h_b$: superheat, pressure, surface condition, orientation, subcooling, thermophysical properties, dissolved gases, and heat flux.
- **Rougher, wettable, upward-facing, gas-saturated surfaces** maximize $h_b$ in nucleate boiling.
- $h_b$ **peaks at moderate reduced pressure** ($p_r \approx 0.3$), then collapses toward the critical point.
- **Nukiyama curve** regimes in order: Natural Convection → Nucleate Boiling → Transition Boiling → Film Boiling, separated by **Incipient Boiling Nucleation (INB)** below and **Critical Heat Flux (CHF)** above.
- The **burnout point (CHF)** is a hard upper limit on $q$ in nucleate boiling — exceeding it causes heater surface failure.
- **Surface-fluid constant $C_{sf}$**: smaller $C_{sf}$ ⇒ more boiling-friendly surface ⇒ higher $h_b$.
- Exponent **$n = 1.0$ for water, $n = 1.7$ for most other fluids** in Rohsenow's correlation.
- For KTU numericals, always verify the final $h_b$ is in the **2,500–50,000 W/m²·K** range and $q$ is **below CHF** before submitting.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dimensionless Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT501 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Dimensionless Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are Dimensionless Variables in Heat Transfer?

A **dimensionless variable** (or **dimensionless group/number**) is a combination of physical quantities whose product or ratio reduces to a pure number (i.e., the dimensions cancel out completely, leaving no units of length, mass, time, temperature, or energy). These groups are essential in heat transfer because they:

- **Generalize** experimental data so that one set of results applies to many geometrically and dynamically similar systems.
- **Reduce** the number of independent variables needed to describe a physical phenomenon.
- **Reveal** the relative dominance of competing physical effects (e.g., convection vs. conduction, inertia vs. viscosity).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A dimensionless group is a mathematically constructed ratio of physical quantities that is invariant under a change of the fundamental units of measurement. In heat transfer, these groups are derived either through **Dimensional Analysis (Buckingham Pi Theorem)** or by **non-dimensionalizing the governing differential equations** (the local similarity method).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Why "Dimensionless" Matters

Imagine you baked the perfect chocolate cake in your home oven at **180 °C for 35 minutes**. Now, your friend in another country wants to replicate it. They ask: *"What temperature and time should I use?"* Simply saying "180 °C, 35 min" may not work because their oven has different internal volume, humidity, and altitude.

> **The "Cake Scaling" Analogy:** What if, instead of giving absolute numbers, you told your friend:
> - Use a **temperature that is 1.4 times the boiling point of water**.
> - **Bake for a duration such that heat has penetrated about 90% of the cake's thickness.**
>
> These are *dimensionless instructions*! They don't depend on the unit system. Likewise, a Nusselt number of **10** means the same thing whether you are in India, Germany, or the USA — it is a **universal language** for heat transfer.

## 1.3 The Concept of Dimensional Homogeneity

A physical equation is **dimensionally homogeneous** if the dimensions on both sides are identical. For example, Newton's second law $\,F = ma\,$ has dimensions of $[M L T^{-2}]$ on both sides. This principle allows us to **form ratios** of variables that share the same dimensions, producing dimensionless groups.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Highlight for KTU 2024:**
> The two principal methods to obtain dimensionless groups in heat transfer are:
> 1. **Buckingham Pi Theorem** — A formal, systematic procedure (Module 4 emphasis).
> 2. **Non-dimensionalization of Governing Equations** — Used to derive boundary-layer similarity variables (advanced; appears in later modules).

## 1.4 Classification of Dimensionless Groups in Heat Transfer

Dimensionless numbers in heat transfer are broadly classified into three physical families:

| Family | Physical Meaning | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Momentum / Inertia Ratios** | Ratio of inertial to viscous forces | Reynolds number ($Re$), Grashof number ($Gr$) |
| **Thermal Property Ratios** | Ratio of momentum diffusivity to thermal diffusivity | Prandtl number ($Pr$) |
| **Heat Transfer Ratios** | Ratio of convective to conductive heat transfer | Nusselt number ($Nu$), Biot number ($Bi$), Stanton number ($St$), Peclet number ($Pe$) |
| **Transient Ratios** | Ratio of transient conduction to storage | Fourier number ($Fo$) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Buckingham Pi Theorem — The Mathematical Engine

The **Buckingham Pi Theorem** states:

> If there are $n$ physical variables in a problem involving $m$ fundamental dimensions, then the variables can be grouped into $(n - m)$ independent dimensionless groups (called **Pi terms**, $\Pi_1, \Pi_2, \dots, \Pi_{n-m}$).

**Why this works physically:** Every physical law must be expressible in dimensionless form because the universe does not "know" about the meter, kilogram, or second — these are human conventions. Hence any valid relationship between physical quantities can always be re-expressed as a relationship among dimensionless groups.

### Step-by-Step Logic of the Procedure

1. **List all relevant physical variables** that govern the phenomenon (e.g., for forced convection: $h, L, k, \rho, \mu, C_p, V$).
2. **Choose repeating variables**: typically $m$ variables are chosen that together span all the fundamental dimensions (e.g., for forced convection: $L, \rho, \mu$ — covering $L, M, T$).
3. **Form the Pi terms** by combining each non-repeating variable with the repeating variables, raised to unknown exponents.
4. **Solve the exponent equations** to make the resulting group dimensionless.
5. **Express the result** as a functional relationship $f(\Pi_1, \Pi_2, \dots) = 0$.

## 2.2 The Master Formula Sheet for KTU Board Exams

The following table contains **every dimensionless group you must know** for the KTU 2024 Heat Transfer Operations exam. Commit these to memory.

| Dimensionless Group | Symbol | Mathematical Definition | Physical Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reynolds Number** | $Re$ | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$ | Inertial forces / Viscous forces |
| **Prandtl Number** | $Pr$ | $Pr = \dfrac{\mu C_p}{k} = \dfrac{\nu}{\alpha}$ | Momentum diffusivity / Thermal diffusivity |
| **Nusselt Number** | $Nu$ | $Nu = \dfrac{h L}{k}$ | Convective heat transfer / Conductive heat transfer |
| **Grashof Number** | $Gr$ | $Gr = \dfrac{g \beta \Delta T L^{3}}{\nu^{2}}$ | Buoyancy forces / Viscous forces |
| **Peclet Number** | $Pe$ | $Pe = Re \cdot Pr = \dfrac{V L}{\alpha}$ | Convective heat transport / Conductive heat transport |
| **Biot Number** | $Bi$ | $Bi = \dfrac{h L_c}{k_s}$ | Internal conduction resistance / Surface convection resistance |
| **Fourier Number** | $Fo$ | $Fo = \dfrac{\alpha \, t}{L^{2}}$ | Transient heat conducted / Heat stored |
| **Stanton Number** | $St$ | $St = \dfrac{h}{\rho V C_p} = \dfrac{Nu}{Re \cdot Pr}$ | Heat transferred to fluid / Thermal capacity of fluid |
| **Rayleigh Number** | $Ra$ | $Ra = Gr \cdot Pr$ | Buoyancy / Viscous forces, scaled by thermal diffusivity |
| **Lewis Number** | $Le$ | $Le = \dfrac{\alpha}{D_{AB}} = \dfrac{Sc}{Pr}$ | Thermal diffusivity / Mass diffusivity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Reminder:** All these numbers are *dimensionless* — they have no units. In your KTU answer sheets, never write "Re = 10,000 units" or "Pr = 0.7 W/m·K." Just the bare number.

## 2.3 Why These Numbers Are Engineering Power Tools

- **Correlation development:** Engineers use dimensionless groups to develop **empirical correlations** (e.g., Dittus-Boelter: $Nu = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Pr^{n}$). Once you have this correlation, you can predict $h$ for any fluid, any velocity, any pipe diameter — provided the regime is similar.
- **Scale-up of lab experiments:** A model tested with water at room temperature can be scaled to predict full-scale performance with a different fluid (e.g., oil, air) using dimensionless similarity.
- **Regime identification:** $Re < 2300$ → laminar; $2300 < Re < 4000$ → transition; $Re > 4000$ → turbulent. Such regime criteria are themselves dimensionless.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Use:** In the design of a **shell-and-tube heat exchanger** in a petroleum refinery, a process engineer in India uses the same Dittus-Boelter correlation as an engineer in Texas. The dimensionless form eliminates the need for unit conversions and ensures **universal applicability**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Deriving the Dimensionless Groups for Forced Convection Using Buckingham Pi Theorem

This is a **classic KTU 14-mark question pattern**. We will perform the derivation exhaustively.

### Problem Statement
By dimensional analysis, obtain the dimensionless groups governing the **forced-convection heat transfer coefficient** for a fluid flowing past a flat plate. The relevant variables are:

$$
h = f(L, \rho, \mu, k, C_p, V)
$$

where:
- $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient $[M T^{-3} \Theta^{-1}]$
- $L$ = characteristic length $[L]$
- $\rho$ = density $[M L^{-3}]$
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity $[M L^{-1} T^{-1}]$
- $k$ = thermal conductivity $[M L T^{-3} \Theta^{-1}]$
- $C_p$ = specific heat $[L^{2} T^{-2} \Theta^{-1}]$
- $V$ = velocity $[L T^{-1}]$

### Step 1: Count Variables and Dimensions
- Number of variables: $n = 7$ (namely $h, L, \rho, \mu, k, C_p, V$).
- Number of fundamental dimensions: $m = 4$ (namely $M, L, T, \Theta$).
- Number of expected Pi terms: $n - m = 7 - 4 = 3$.

### Step 2: Choose Repeating Variables
We need $m = 4$ repeating variables that together span all four fundamental dimensions. A good choice is:

$$
L, \rho, \mu, k
$$

These four contain $L, M, T, \Theta$ (through $k$). We exclude $V, C_p, h$ from the repeating set; they will appear as the non-repeating variables.

### Step 3: Form the Three Pi Terms

**Pi 1 — contains $V$:**
$$
\Pi_1 = L^{a_1} \rho^{b_1} \mu^{c_1} k^{d_1} V^{1}
$$

Dimensions:
$$
[L]^{a_1} [M L^{-3}]^{b_1} [M L^{-1} T^{-1}]^{c_1} [M L T^{-3} \Theta^{-1}]^{d_1} [L T^{-1}]^{1}
$$

Collecting powers of each fundamental dimension:

- $M$: $b_1 + c_1 + d_1 = 0$
- $L$: $a_1 - 3b_1 - c_1 + d_1 + 1 = 0$
- $T$: $-c_1 - 3d_1 - 1 = 0$
- $\Theta$: $-d_1 = 0$

**From the $\Theta$-equation:** $d_1 = 0$.

**From the $T$-equation:** $-c_1 - 0 - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow c_1 = -1$.

**From the $M$-equation:** $b_1 + (-1) + 0 = 0 \Rightarrow b_1 = 1$.

**From the $L$-equation:** $a_1 - 3(1) - (-1) + 0 + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow a_1 - 3 + 1 + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow a_1 = 1$.

Therefore:
$$
\Pi_1 = L^{1} \rho^{1} \mu^{-1} V = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = Re
$$

**Pi 2 — contains $C_p$:**
$$
\Pi_2 = L^{a_2} \rho^{b_2} \mu^{c_2} k^{d_2} C_p^{1}
$$

Dimensions:
$$
[L]^{a_2} [M L^{-3}]^{b_2} [M L^{-1} T^{-1}]^{c_2} [M L T^{-3} \Theta^{-1}]^{d_2} [L^{2} T^{-2} \Theta^{-1}]^{1}
$$

Power equations:

- $M$: $b_2 + c_2 + d_2 = 0$
- $L$: $a_2 - 3b_2 - c_2 + d_2 + 2 = 0$
- $T$: $-c_2 - 3d_2 - 2 = 0$
- $\Theta$: $-d_2 - 1 = 0$

**From $\Theta$:** $d_2 = -1$.

**From $T$:** $-c_2 - 3(-1) - 2 = 0 \Rightarrow -c_2 + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow c_2 = 1$.

**From $M$:** $b_2 + 1 + (-1) = 0 \Rightarrow b_2 = 0$.

**From $L$:** $a_2 - 0 - 1 + (-1) + 2 = 0 \Rightarrow a_2 = 0$.

Therefore:
$$
\Pi_2 = \mu^{1} k^{-1} C_p^{1} = \frac{\mu C_p}{k} = Pr
$$

**Pi 3 — contains $h$:**
$$
\Pi_3 = L^{a_3} \rho^{b_3} \mu^{c_3} k^{d_3} h^{1}
$$

Dimensions:
$$
[L]^{a_3} [M L^{-3}]^{b_3} [M L^{-1} T^{-1}]^{c_3} [M L T^{-3} \Theta^{-1}]^{d_3} [M T^{-3} \Theta^{-1}]^{1}
$$

Power equations:

- $M$: $b_3 + c_3 + d_3 + 1 = 0$
- $L$: $a_3 - 3b_3 - c_3 + d_3 = 0$
- $T$: $-c_3 - 3d_3 - 3 = 0$
- $\Theta$: $-d_3 - 1 = 0$

**From $\Theta$:** $d_3 = -1$.
