**From $T$:** $-c_3 - 3(-1) - 3 = 0 \Rightarrow -c_3 = 0 \Rightarrow c_3 = 0$.

**From $M$:** $b_3 + 0 + (-1) + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow b_3 = 0$.

**From $L$:** $a_3 - 0 - 0 + (-1) = 0 \Rightarrow a_3 = 1$.

Therefore:
$$
\Pi_3 = L^{1} k^{-1} h = \frac{h L}{k} = Nu
$$

### Step 4: Final Functional Relationship

$$
\Pi_3 = f(\Pi_1, \Pi_2) \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; Nu = f(Re, Pr)
$$

This is the foundation of every forced-convection correlation. The exact form of the function $f$ is determined experimentally (e.g., Dittus-Boelter, Sieder-Tate, etc.).

## 3.2 Quick Derivation: The Biot Number

For transient conduction with surface convection, the heat transfer problem depends on:
- Thermal conductivity of solid $k_s$
- Specific heat $C_p$
- Density $\rho$
- Characteristic length $L_c$
- Time $t$
- Surface heat transfer coefficient $h$
- Temperature difference $\Delta T$

Using dimensional analysis, the governing dimensionless groups are:
- **Biot number:** $Bi = \dfrac{h L_c}{k_s}$
- **Fourier number:** $Fo = \dfrac{\alpha t}{L_c^{2}} = \dfrac{k_s \, t}{\rho C_p L_c^{2}}$

The transient temperature response is then universally expressed as:

$$
\frac{T - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = f(Bi, Fo)
$$

This means: **one dimensionless curve fits every geometry** (within a shape class). A KTU examiner loves this — it shows whether students understand *why* we normalize temperatures, lengths, and times.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of $Bi$:** When $Bi \ll 1$, the internal conduction resistance is small compared to the surface convection resistance, so the temperature inside the solid is essentially uniform. When $Bi \gg 1$, the surface heats up much faster than the interior, leading to steep internal temperature gradients.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 3-Mark)

**Problem:** Air at $20 \,^\circ\text{C}$ flows at $5 \, \text{m/s}$ across a flat plate of length $L = 0.5 \, \text{m}$. The plate is maintained at $80 \,^\circ\text{C}$ and the convection coefficient is $h = 25 \, \text{W/m}^{2}\text{·K}$. Calculate the Reynolds number and check whether the flow is laminar. Given: $\rho = 1.2 \, \text{kg/m}^{3}$, $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{kg/m·s}$, $k = 0.026 \, \text{W/m·K}$, $C_p = 1005 \, \text{J/kg·K}$.

**Solution:**

$$
Re_L = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{(1.2)(5)(0.5)}{1.81 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.66 \times 10^{5}
$$

Since $Re_L = 1.66 \times 10^{5} < 5 \times 10^{5}$, the flow over the plate is **laminar** (the transition for a flat plate typically occurs at $Re_L \approx 5 \times 10^{5}$).

$$
Pr = \frac{\mu C_p}{k} = \frac{(1.81 \times 10^{-5})(1005)}{0.026} = 0.70
$$

$$
Nu = 0.664 \, Re_L^{1/2} \, Pr^{1/3} = (0.664)(1.66 \times 10^{5})^{0.5} (0.70)^{1/3} \approx 304
$$

This $Nu$ value then yields $h_{calc} = \dfrac{Nu \cdot k}{L} \approx 15.8 \, \text{W/m}^{2}\text{·K}$ — close to the given $h = 25 \, \text{W/m}^{2}\text{·K}$ (the discrepancy suggests mixed conditions or property variation; acceptable for KTU).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: Dimensional Analysis Workflow

The following block diagram maps the **complete procedure** for applying the Buckingham Pi Theorem — a process flow that KTU examiners expect students to be able to reproduce in a board exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify the Physical Problem] --> B[Step 1: List all relevant physical variables n]
    B --> C[Step 2: Identify fundamental dimensions m]
    C --> D[Step 3: Calculate expected Pi terms n minus m]
    D --> E[Step 4: Choose m repeating variables spanning all dimensions]
    E --> F[Step 5: Form Pi terms with unknown exponents]
    F --> G[Step 6: Solve linear system for exponents]
    G --> H[Step 7: Identify each Pi as a known dimensionless number]
    H --> I[Step 8: Write final functional relationship f Pi1 Pi2 equals 0]
    I --> J[Step 9: Validate via known physical limits or experiment]
    J --> K[Step 10: Develop empirical correlation for engineering use]
```

## 4.2 Hierarchy of the Eight Key Dimensionless Groups

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Dimensionless Groups in Heat Transfer] --> A[Momentum Family]
    ROOT --> B[Thermal Property Family]
    ROOT --> C[Heat Transfer Ratio Family]
    ROOT --> D[Transient Family]
    A --> A1[Reynolds Re]
    A --> A2[Grashof Gr]
    A --> A3[Rayleigh Ra]
    B --> B1[Prandtl Pr]
    B --> B2[Lewis Le]
    C --> C1[Nusselt Nu]
    C --> C2[Stanton St]
    C --> C3[Peclet Pe]
    D --> D1[Biot Bi]
    D --> D2[Fourier Fo]
```

## 4.3 Physical-Interpretation Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RE[Reynolds Re] -->|Inertial over Viscous| FLOW[Flow Regime]
    PR[Prandtl Pr] -->|Momentum over Thermal Diffusivity| FLUID[Fluid Type]
    NU[Nusselt Nu] -->|Convection over Conduction| HTC[Heat Transfer Intensity]
    BI[Biot Bi] -->|Internal over Surface Resistance| LUMP[Lumped vs Non Lumped]
    FO[Fourier Fo] -->|Transient over Storage| TIME[Time Scale]
    GR[Grashof Gr] -->|Buoyancy over Viscous| NAT[Natural Convection Trigger]
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Mapping of Pi Terms to Physical Forces (Convection Triangle)

The **convection triangle** is a classical visualization used in heat transfer pedagogy. It captures the three-way relationship between fluid motion, thermal transport, and material properties.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V[Velocity V rho mu] -->|defines| RE[Reynolds Re]
    K[Thermal Conductivity k alpha] -->|defines| NU[Nusselt Nu]
    CP[Heat Capacity mu Cp k] -->|defines| PR[Prandtl Pr]
    RE --> MASTER[Convection Master Equation Nu equals f Re Pr]
    NU --> MASTER
    PR --> MASTER
    MASTER --> PRED[Predict h for any geometry and fluid]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip (KTU Valuation):** When asked to "explain the significance of the dimensionless approach," examiners give full credit when students draw a clear block diagram of the procedure AND state the functional relationship $Nu = f(Re, Pr)$. Diagrams + equations = full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Nusselt number and Prandtl number. State their physical significance.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Nusselt number** is defined as the ratio of convective to conductive heat transfer at a fluid boundary layer:

$$
Nu = \frac{h L}{k}
$$

It represents the **enhancement** of heat transfer due to convection over pure conduction. A value of $Nu = 1$ indicates pure conduction, while $Nu \gg 1$ indicates strong convection. It is dimensionless.

- **Prandtl number** is defined as the ratio of momentum diffusivity to thermal diffusivity:

$$
Pr = \frac{\mu C_p}{k} = \frac{\nu}{\alpha}
$$

It characterizes the relative thickness of the velocity and thermal boundary layers. $Pr \ll 1$ (e.g., liquid metals) means the thermal boundary layer is much thicker than the velocity boundary layer. $Pr \gg 1$ (e.g., oils) means the opposite. For gases, $Pr \approx 0.7$, and both layers have similar thickness.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**What is the Buckingham Pi Theorem? Why is it useful in heat transfer?**

**Model Answer:**

The Buckingham Pi Theorem states that if a physical phenomenon involves $n$ variables in $m$ fundamental dimensions, the relationship among them can be expressed in terms of $(n - m)$ independent dimensionless groups (Pi terms).

$$
f(\Pi_1, \Pi_2, \dots, \Pi_{n-m}) = 0
$$

**Why it is useful in heat transfer:**
- It reduces a complex multi-variable problem to a smaller number of dimensionless groups.
- It allows experimental data from a small lab model to be applied to large industrial systems (**dynamic similarity**).
- It enables the development of universally valid correlations (e.g., $Nu = C \, Re^m \, Pr^n$) that are independent of the unit system.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Format:** Each question has Part (a) [7 marks] and Part (b) [7 marks].

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

#### Q. (a) **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Using Buckingham Pi Theorem, derive the dimensionless groups governing natural convection from a vertical plate heated in a quiescent fluid. The variables involved are:** $h, L, \rho, \mu, k, C_p, g \beta \Delta T$.

**Model Solution:**

Number of variables: $n = 7$ ($h, L, \rho, \mu, k, C_p, g\beta\Delta T$).
Number of fundamental dimensions: $m = 4$ ($M, L, T, \Theta$).
Expected Pi terms: $n - m = 3$.

**Choosing repeating variables:** $L, \rho, \mu, k$ (spanning $L, M, T, \Theta$).
**Non-repeating variables:** $C_p$, $g\beta\Delta T$, $h$.

**Pi 1** — with $C_p$ (exponent 1):
Dimensions $[L^{2} T^{-2} \Theta^{-1}]$.
Equations: $b + c + d = 0$, $a - 3b - c + d + 2 = 0$, $-c - 3d - 2 = 0$, $-d - 1 = 0$.
Solving: $d = -1$, $c = 1$, $b = 0$, $a = 0$.
Therefore: $\Pi_1 = \dfrac{\mu C_p}{k} = Pr$.

**Pi 2** — with $g\beta\Delta T$ (exponent 1):
Dimensions $[L T^{-2}]$.
Equations: $b + c + d = 0$, $a - 3b - c + d + 1 = 0$, $-c - 3d - 2 = 0$, $-d = 0$.
Solving: $d = 0$, $c = -2$, $b = 2$, $a = 3$.
Therefore: $\Pi_2 = \dfrac{L^{3} \rho^{2} (g\beta\Delta T)}{\mu^{2}} = Gr$ (Grashof number).

**Pi 3** — with $h$ (exponent 1):
Same derivation as Section 3.1, Step 3.
$\Pi_3 = \dfrac{h L}{k} = Nu$ (Nusselt number).

**Final result:**

$$
Nu = f(Gr, Pr)
$$

This is the **fundamental natural convection correlation** (e.g., Churchill-Chu: $Nu = \left\{0.825 + \dfrac{0.387 \, Ra^{1/6}}{[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}]^{8/27}}\right\}^{2}$).

---

#### Q. (b) **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**A steel ball of diameter 8 cm is quenched in an oil bath at 30 °C. The ball is initially at 500 °C. The thermal conductivity and thermal diffusivity of steel are $k = 45 \,\text{W/m·K}$ and $\alpha = 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$. The surface heat transfer coefficient is $h = 1100 \, \text{W/m}^{2}\text{·K}$. Determine (i) the Biot number and (ii) whether the lumped capacitance method is valid. Characteristic length $L_c = r = D/2$ for a sphere.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $D = 0.08$ m, $L_c = r = 0.04$ m, $k = 45$ W/m·K, $h = 1100$ W/m²·K.

**(i) Biot number:**

$$
Bi = \frac{h L_c}{k} = \frac{(1100)(0.04)}{45} = 0.978
$$

**(ii) Validity of Lumped Capacitance:**

The lumped capacitance method is valid only when $Bi \le 0.1$.
Since $Bi = 0.978 \gg 0.1$, **the lumped capacitance method is NOT valid** for this situation. There is a significant temperature gradient inside the ball, and one must use the **one-term approximation of the transient conduction series solution** (involving the Fourier number and roots of the transcendental equation $\lambda \cot \lambda + Bi - 1 = 0$).

For a sphere with $Bi = 0.978$, the eigenvalue from the standard table: $\lambda_1 \approx 1.608$ rad.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Q. (a) **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2021]**
**Define the following dimensionless numbers and give one physical interpretation for each: (i) Reynolds number, (ii) Peclet number, (iii) Stanton number, (iv) Fourier number.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Reynolds number:** $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$
**Interpretation:** Ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces. Determines whether flow is **laminar** ($Re < 2300$ in pipes), **transitional**, or **turbulent** ($Re > 4000$).

**(ii) Peclet number:** $Pe = Re \cdot Pr = \dfrac{V L}{\alpha}$
**Interpretation:** Ratio of convective heat transport to conductive heat transport in the fluid. High $Pe$ means convection dominates; the thermal boundary layer is very thin compared to the velocity boundary layer.

**(iii) Stanton number:** $St = \dfrac{h}{\rho V C_p} = \dfrac{Nu}{Re \cdot Pr}$
**Interpretation:** Ratio of the actual heat transferred to the fluid to the maximum possible heat transfer if the fluid were brought to the wall temperature over the entire flow cross-section. It is also the inverse of the convective heat transfer capacity of the fluid.

**(iv) Fourier number:** $Fo = \dfrac{\alpha t}{L^{2}}$
**Interpretation:** Dimensionless time. Represents the ratio of the rate of heat conduction through a body to the rate of thermal energy storage. Governs the time-dependence of all transient heat conduction problems.

---

#### Q. (b) **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam — May 2022]**
**Water flows at 1.2 m/s in a 25-mm-diameter tube at 40 °C. The tube wall is maintained at 90 °C. Using the Dittus-Boelter correlation, estimate the heat transfer coefficient. Properties of water at 40 °C: $\rho = 992 \, \text{kg/m}^{3}$, $\mu = 6.5 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{kg/m·s}$, $k = 0.628 \, \text{W/m·K}$, $C_p = 4175 \, \text{J/kg·K}$, $Pr = 4.34$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Reynolds number:**

$$
Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{(992)(1.2)(0.025)}{6.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 45,784
$$

Since $Re > 10{,}000$, the flow is **turbulent** and Dittus-Boelter is applicable.

**Step 2 — Dittus-Boelter correlation** (heating: $n = 0.4$):

$$
Nu = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Pr^{0.4}
$$

$$
Nu = 0.023 \times (45{,}784)^{0.8} \times (4.34)^{0.4}
$$

Compute term by term:

$$
(45{,}784)^{0.8} = e^{0.8 \ln(45{,}784)} = e^{0.8 \times 10.732} = e^{8.586} \approx 5327
$$

$$
(4.34)^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln(4.34)} = e^{0.4 \times 1.4676} = e^{0.587} \approx 1.799
$$

$$
Nu = 0.023 \times 5327 \times 1.799 \approx 220.4
$$

**Step 3 — Heat transfer coefficient:**

$$
h = \frac{Nu \cdot k}{D} = \frac{(220.4)(0.628)}{0.025} = 5537 \, \text{W/m}^{2}\text{·K}
$$

**Answer:** $h \approx 5537 \, \text{W/m}^{2}\text{·K}$ (a typical value for water heating in turbulent pipe flow).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting dimensional verification:** Many students write the final Pi term without verifying the dimensions actually cancel. KTU examiners **do** check this and deduct up to 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $L_c$ for the Biot number:** For a cylinder, use $L_c = r$ (not $D$); for a sphere, $L_c = r$; for a slab, $L_c = $ half-thickness. Wrong $L_c$ = wrong $Bi$ = full 7-mark loss in Part (b).
> 3. **Wrong exponent on Pr in Dittus-Boelter:** Use $n = 0.4$ for **heating** and $n = 0.3$ for **cooling**. Mixing them up is a frequent error.
> 4. **Units in dimensionless numbers:** Never write "Re = 10,000 units" or "Pr = 0.7 (W/m·K)." They are pure numbers.
> 5. **Repeating variable choice:** The repeating variables together must span **all** $m$ fundamental dimensions. A common error is choosing $V$ and $C_p$ as repeating variables when they don't span $\Theta$ — leading to no solution.
> 6. **Final functional form:** Always end with the **functional relationship** $f(\Pi_1, \Pi_2, \dots) = 0$ or $Nu = f(Re, Pr)$. The function form is essential.

---

## 5.4 📝 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU 2024 Board Exam:**

- ✅ A **dimensionless group** is a pure number formed by combining physical variables such that all fundamental dimensions cancel out.
- ✅ **Buckingham Pi Theorem:** Expected number of Pi terms = (number of variables) − (number of fundamental dimensions).
- ✅ The **Reynolds number** identifies flow regime; **Prandtl number** characterizes the fluid; **Nusselt number** measures convective intensity; **Biot number** checks lumped-capacitance validity; **Fourier number** is the dimensionless time.
- ✅ Standard forced-convection result (memorize): $Nu = f(Re, Pr)$.
- ✅ Standard natural-convection result (memorize): $Nu = f(Gr, Pr) = f(Ra)$.
- ✅ **Dittus-Boelter** correlation: $Nu = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Pr^{0.4}$ (heating) or $Pr^{0.3}$ (cooling); valid for $Re > 10{,}000$, $0.7 < Pr < 160$, $L/D > 60$.
- ✅ **Biot number validity criterion for lumped capacitance:** $Bi \le 0.1$.
- ✅ **Characteristic lengths** for $Bi$: $L_c = r$ (sphere, long cylinder), $L_c = $ half-thickness (slab).
- ✅ **Rayleigh number** $Ra = Gr \cdot Pr$ is often used in natural convection correlations instead of $Gr$ and $Pr$ separately.
- ✅ **Peclet number** $Pe = Re \cdot Pr$ = ratio of bulk flow heat transport to molecular conduction.
- ✅ **Stanton number** $St = h / (\rho V C_p)$ links convective heat transfer to the convective heat capacity of the fluid stream.
- ✅ Always show **dimensional verification** of each Pi term in the board exam — it earns you extra credit.
- ✅ Always conclude with the **functional form** $f(\Pi_1, \Pi_2, \dots) = 0$ — it is mandatory in KTU's model answer scheme.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pool Boiling Curve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT501 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pool Boiling Curve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Pool Boiling** is a mode of boiling heat transfer in which a stationary bulk mass of liquid, contained in a vessel, is heated from a submerged solid surface (typically a horizontal plate or tube) maintained at a temperature $T_s$ higher than the liquid's saturation temperature $T_{sat}$. The liquid is unforced — there is no external pump or bulk flow driving it across the heater surface.

The **Pool Boiling Curve** is the experimentally obtained graphical relationship between the **heat flux** $q$ (in W/m²) supplied by the heater surface and the **excess temperature** $\Delta T_{ex}$ (in °C or K), defined rigorously as:

$$\Delta T_{ex} = T_s - T_{sat}$$

This curve was first obtained experimentally by **Shiro Nukiyama (1934)** using a nichrome wire immersed in water. The curve is the cornerstone of convective boiling theory and is the foundation for the design of steam generators, reboilers, nuclear reactors, and electronic cooling systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Definition (Board Standard):**
> *"Pool boiling is the boiling process in which the liquid is essentially stationary, and the motion of the fluid is induced solely by natural convection and the buoyancy effects of the generated vapor bubbles, the heat flux being a strong function of the excess temperature."*

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**Analogy — Boiling Water in an Open Vessel:**
Imagine a stainless steel pot resting on a gas stove filled with water at **atmospheric pressure (1.013 bar, $T_{sat}$ = 100 °C)**. The stove knob controls the heat input.

- **Low flame setting:** The water at the bottom warms up; hot water rises naturally (no bubbles). This is **free convection**.
- **Medium flame setting:** You hear a soft "hissing" — small vapor bubbles nucleate at the bottom and rise. This is **nucleate boiling** (efficient, high heat transfer).
- **High flame setting (red-hot pot base):** A continuous vapor film forms beneath the pot; the surface "screams" (Leidenfrost phenomenon), and the pot bottom is now *too hot for water to touch*. The heat transfer *drops* dramatically — this is **film boiling**.

The "sweet spot" right before the film forms is the **Critical Heat Flux (CHF)** — the maximum heat flux the system can deliver. Push beyond CHF, and you get **burnout**, which can melt a nuclear fuel rod or a kettle's heating element.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (Water, 1 atm) |
|---|---|---|
| Latent heat of vaporization | $h_{fg}$ | **2257 kJ/kg** |
| Surface tension | $\sigma$ | **0.0589 N/m** |
| Density of saturated liquid | $\rho_l$ | **957.9 kg/m³** |
| Density of saturated vapor | $\rho_v$ | **0.5956 kg/m³** |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Critical heat flux (water) | $q_{max}$ | **≈ 1.25 MW/m²** |
| Excess temperature at CHF (water) | $\Delta T_{ex,CHF}$ | **≈ 30 °C** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The dimensionless **Bond number**, **Jakob number**, and the physical property groupings $\sqrt{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)/\sigma}$ and $[g\sigma(\rho_l - \rho_v)/\rho_v^2]^{1/4}$ must be memorized for the CHF derivation sub-questions.

### 1.4 Why is the Boiling Curve Important for KTU?

Every industrial equipment dealing with phase change (kettle reboilers in distillation columns, nuclear reactor coolant channels, cryogenic LNG vaporizers, electronics chip cooling) operates *somewhere* on this curve. Selecting the operating regime is the difference between an efficient, safe plant and a catastrophic burnout.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Regimes of the Pool Boiling Curve

The complete Nukiyama curve is divided into **six distinct heat transfer regimes** as the excess temperature $\Delta T_{ex}$ is increased gradually. We must analyze each one with respect to its dominant physical mechanism.

**Regime 1 — Free (Natural) Convection (Region A–B):**
- The surface temperature is only slightly above $T_{sat}$ (typically $\Delta T_{ex} < 4$ °C for water at 1 atm).
- No bubbles form because the superheat is insufficient to overcome the surface tension at the available nucleation sites.
- Heat transfer is governed by single-phase natural convection (analyzed via Nusselt-type correlations).
- Heat flux is low and grows linearly with $\Delta T_{ex}$.

**Regime 2 — Onset of Nucleate Boiling, ONB (Point B):**
- The minimum $\Delta T_{ex}$ at which the first stable vapor bubble nucleates.
- This point is highly sensitive to surface conditions (roughness, wettability, cavity size).
- For polished, smooth surfaces, ONB can be delayed significantly.

**Regime 3 — Partial / Isolated Bubble Nucleate Boiling (Region B–C):**
- Discrete, individual vapor bubbles form at discrete nucleation sites and detach.
- Each bubble agitates the surrounding liquid, enhancing heat transfer.
- Heat flux grows rapidly with $\Delta T_{ex}$.

**Regime 4 — Slug / Columnar Nucleate Boiling (Region C–D):**
- Bubble generation rate is so high that bubbles coalesce into slugs and columns of vapor before detaching.
- The thermal boundary layer is highly disturbed; heat transfer is at its most efficient.
- The curve reaches the **Critical Heat Flux (CHF)** at point D.

**Regime 5 — Transition Boiling / Unstable Film (Region D–E):**
- A vapor blanket begins to intermittently form and collapse over the heater.
- Heat flux *decreases* with increasing $\Delta T_{ex}$ (negative slope, $dq/d(\Delta T_{ex}) < 0$).
- This regime is **inherently unstable** — any small disturbance pushes the system either back to nucleate boiling (forward path) or deeper into film boiling.

**Regime 6 — Stable Film Boiling (Region E–F):**
- A continuous, stable vapor film blankets the heater surface.
- Heat transfer occurs by **conduction through the vapor film** plus radiation at high $\Delta T_{ex}$.
- Heat flux again rises with $\Delta T_{ex}$ due to increased radiation contribution.
- Point **E** is the **Leidenfrost Point** (minimum heat flux, $q_{min}$), where the film first becomes thermally stable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hysteresis Phenomenon:**
> If we *decrease* $\Delta T_{ex}$ from the film boiling regime, the system does NOT retrace the forward path. The heater must cool well below the original CHF temperature before nucleate boiling restarts. This is **boiling hysteresis**, caused by the energy required to re-establish liquid contact with the superheated surface.

### 2.2 The Three Critical Points on the Boiling Curve

| Point | Name | Heat Flux | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **B** | Onset of Nucleate Boiling (ONB) | Low (rising) | Minimum $\Delta T_{ex}$ for boiling to begin |
| **D** | Critical Heat Flux / Burnout Point | **$q_{max}$** (peak) | **Design limit for safe operation** |
| **E** | Leidenfrost Point / Minimum Flux | **$q_{min}$** | Lowest heat flux; below this, nucleate boiling re-establishes |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Correlation Name | Governing Equation | Applicable Regime |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Rohsenow Correlation** (Nucleate Boiling) | $\displaystyle q = \mu_l \, h_{fg} \left[ \frac{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\sigma} \right]^{1/2} \left[ \frac{c_{p,l} \, \Delta T_{ex}}{C_{sf} \, h_{fg} \, Pr_l^n} \right]^3$ | Nucleate Boiling (B–D) |
| 2 | **Zuber Correlation** (Critical Heat Flux) | $\displaystyle q_{max} = 0.149 \, h_{fg} \, \rho_v \left[ \frac{\sigma \, g \, (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^2} \right]^{1/4}$ | CHF Prediction |
| 3 | **Zuber-Stability Theory** (Hydrodynamic Instability) | $\displaystyle q_{max} = \frac{\pi}{24} \, h_{fg} \, \rho_v \left[ \frac{\sigma \, g \, (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^2} \right]^{1/4}$ | Theoretical CHF (advanced) |
| 4 | **Berenson Correlation** (Minimum Heat Flux) | $\displaystyle q_{min} = 0.09 \, \rho_v \, h_{fg} \left[ \frac{g \, \sigma \, (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{(\rho_l + \rho_v)^2} \right]^{1/4}$ | Leidenfrost Point |
| 5 | **Bromley Correlation** (Film Boiling) | $\displaystyle h = 0.943 \left[ \frac{k_v^3 \, \rho_v \, (\rho_l - \rho_v) \, g \, h_{fg}^{\prime}}{\mu_v \, \Delta T_{ex} \, D} \right]^{1/4}$ | Stable Film Boiling (E–F) |
| 6 | **Modified Latent Heat** (for Bromley) | $\displaystyle h_{fg}^{\prime} = h_{fg} + 0.4 \, c_{p,v} \, \Delta T_{ex}$ | Sub-cool correction |
| 7 | **Film Boiling Heat Flux** | $\displaystyle q = h \, \Delta T_{ex}$ | Stable Film |
| 8 | **Bond Number** | $\displaystyle Bo = \frac{g(\rho_l - \rho_v) D^2}{\sigma}$ | Dimensionless group |
| 9 | **Jakob Number** | $\displaystyle Ja = \frac{c_{p,l} \Delta T_{ex}}{h_{fg}}$ | Superheat parameter |
| 10 | **Superheat criterion** for ONB | $\displaystyle \Delta T_{ex,ONB} \approx \frac{2 \sigma \, T_{sat}}{\rho_v \, h_{fg} \, r_{cavity}}$ | Onset of boiling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board-Specific Tip:**
> - In **Rohsenow**, $n = 1.0$ for **water** and $n = 1.7$ for most other fluids. $C_{sf}$ is a surface-fluid combination constant (water-copper polished: 0.0130; water-stainless steel mechanically polished: 0.0132; water-stainless steel Teflon-pitted: 0.0058).
> - Always use $\Delta T_{ex}$ in **K (or °C, both numerically equal for temperature differences)** — *not* in °F or R.
> - The Zuber constant is **0.149** (not 0.131 or 0.18 — KTU boards deduct marks for this!).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Nuclear Reactor Design:** Fuel rods are designed to operate *below* CHF to avoid cladding meltdown (this is what happened in the Chernobyl and Fukushima accidents).
- **Chemical Reboilers (Kettle type):** Operate in nucleate boiling regime to maximize $U$ (overall heat transfer coefficient).
- **Electronic Chip Cooling:** Use dielectric fluorinerts boiling in the nucleate regime; CHF determines maximum allowable power dissipation.
- **Cryogenics:** LNG spills exhibit film boiling; understanding the Leidenfrost point is critical for fire safety modeling.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Zuber Correlation for Critical Heat Flux (CHF)

The CHF is governed by **Helmholtz instability** of the liquid-vapor interface (Taylor-Helmholtz wave theory, modified by Zuber, 1959).

**Step 1 — Physical Setup:**
At CHF, jets of vapor are ejected normal to the heater surface. These jets accelerate through the liquid under buoyancy. The instability wavelength $\lambda_d$ that maximizes the disturbance growth rate is given by the Taylor-Helmholtz theory:

$$\lambda_d = 2\pi \sqrt{3} \left[ \frac{\sigma}{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)} \right]^{1/2}$$

**Step 2 — Vapor Velocity at Instability:**
The vapor velocity $u_v$ at the wave interface corresponds to the most-unstable wave:

$$u_v = C \left[ \frac{\sigma \, g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_l^2} \right]^{1/4}$$

where $C$ is a constant (Zuber showed $C \approx 1$ for a planar heater, refined to $0.149 \times 24/\pi$ numerically).

**Step 3 — Conservation of Mass:**
The mass flow rate of vapor per unit area, $\dot{m}''$, leaving the surface is related to the velocity and density:

$$\dot{m}'' = \rho_v \, u_v$$

**Step 4 — Energy Balance for CHF:**
The heat flux required to vaporize this mass flow rate is:

$$q_{max} = \dot{m}'' \, h_{fg} = \rho_v \, u_v \, h_{fg}$$

**Step 5 — Substituting the Instability Velocity:**

$$q_{max} = \rho_v \, h_{fg} \left[ \frac{\sigma \, g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_l^2} \right]^{1/4}$$

Since $\rho_l \gg \rho_v$, we approximate $\rho_l \approx \rho_l + \rho_v \approx \rho_l$. The empirical constant is fitted to experimental data, yielding:

$$\boxed{q_{max} = 0.149 \, h_{fg} \, \rho_v \left[ \frac{\sigma \, g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^2} \right]^{1/4}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** CHF is **independent of heater surface material and roughness** (a key observation in Zuber's theory). It depends only on **thermo-physical properties** of the fluid and the gravitational body force. This is why the same $q_{max}$ is reported for copper, steel, and brass heaters.

### 3.2 Derivation of Berenson Correlation for Minimum Heat Flux (Leidenfrost)

Berenson (1961) extended the Taylor-Helmholtz analysis to find the point at which the vapor film becomes **stable** (i.e., the vapor column does not collapse back into nucleate boiling).

**Step 1 — Critical Wavelength for Film Stability:**
The film is stable when the buoyancy-driven vapor flow overcomes the disturbance waves. The minimum heat flux corresponds to the most-unstable wavelength for a *thick* vapor blanket.

**Step 2 — Vapor Mass Flux in Stable Film:**

$$\dot{m}''_{min} = C' \left[ \frac{\sigma \, g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{(\rho_l + \rho_v)^2} \right]^{1/4} \rho_v$$

The denominator uses $\rho_l + \rho_v$ (not $\rho_v^2$) because the wave kinematics now involve both phases equivalently (inertia of liquid + vapor is important).

**Step 3 — Energy Balance:**

$$q_{min} = \dot{m}''_{min} \, h_{fg}$$

**Step 4 — Empirical Constant from Leidenfrost Data:**

$$\boxed{q_{min} = 0.09 \, \rho_v \, h_{fg} \left[ \frac{g \, \sigma (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{(\rho_l + \rho_v)^2} \right]^{1/4}}$$

### 3.3 Full Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A horizontal copper plate in saturated water at **1 atm** ($T_{sat}$ = 100 °C) has its surface maintained at **125 °C**. Estimate:
- (a) The heat flux using the **Rohsenow correlation**.
- (b) The **Critical Heat Flux** using Zuber's correlation.
- Compare the operating heat flux with $q_{max}$ to determine the operating regime.

**Given Data (Water, 100 °C):**
- $\mu_l = 279 \times 10^{-6}$ N·s/m²
- $c_{p,l} = 4216$ J/kg·K
- $Pr_l = 1.76$
- $\rho_l = 957.9$ kg/m³
- $\rho_v = 0.5956$ kg/m³
- $h_{fg} = 2257 \times 10^3$ J/kg
- $\sigma = 0.0589$ N/m
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $C_{sf} = 0.0130$ (water–copper, polished), $n = 1.0$

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate Excess Temperature:**

$$\Delta T_{ex} = T_s - T_{sat} = 125 - 100 = 25 \; \text{K}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Rohsenow Correlation:**

$$q = \mu_l h_{fg} \left[ \frac{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\sigma} \right]^{1/2} \left[ \frac{c_{p,l} \Delta T_{ex}}{C_{sf} h_{fg} Pr_l^n} \right]^3$$

First, compute the property group $A$:

$$A = \frac{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\sigma} = \frac{9.81 \times (957.9 - 0.5956)}{0.0589} = \frac{9389.51}{0.0589} = 1.5941 \times 10^5 \; \text{m}^{-1}$$

$$\sqrt{A} = 399.26 \; \text{m}^{-1/2}$$

Next, the dimensionless superheat group $B$:

$$B = \frac{c_{p,l} \Delta T_{ex}}{C_{sf} h_{fg} Pr_l^n} = \frac{4216 \times 25}{0.0130 \times 2257 \times 10^3 \times 1.76}$$

$$B = \frac{105400}{51{,}635.36} = 2.0411$$

$$B^3 = (2.0411)^3 = 8.5037$$

Finally, the full $q$:

$$q = (279 \times 10^{-6}) \times (2257 \times 10^3) \times 399.26 \times 8.5037$$

$$q = (0.6296) \times 399.26 \times 8.5037 = 2137.36 \; \text{kW/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{q \approx 2.14 \; \text{MW/m}^2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Zuber Correlation for CHF:**

$$q_{max} = 0.149 \, h_{fg} \, \rho_v \left[ \frac{\sigma g (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^2} \right]^{1/4}$$

Compute the bracketed group:

$$\frac{\sigma g (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^2} = \frac{0.0589 \times 9.81 \times 957.30}{(0.5956)^2} = \frac{553.0}{0.3547} = 1559.1$$

$$\left[1559.1\right]^{1/4} = (1559.1)^{0.25} \approx 6.282$$

Then:

$$q_{max} = 0.149 \times 2257 \times 10^3 \times 0.5956 \times 6.282$$

$$q_{max} = 0.149 \times 2257000 \times 0.5956 \times 6.282$$

$$q_{max} = 149 \times 10^{-3} \times 2257 \times 10^3 \times 3.7417$$

$$q_{max} = 149 \times 2257 \times 3.7417 \times 10^{0} \;\text{(W/m}^2\text{)}$$

$$q_{max} = 149 \times 8443.6 \approx 1.258 \times 10^6 \; \text{W/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{q_{max} \approx 1.26 \; \text{MW/m}^2}$$

**Step 4 — Compare and Conclude:**

$$\frac{q}{q_{max}} = \frac{2.14}{1.26} = 1.70 > 1$$

**Conclusion:** The computed heat flux (2.14 MW/m²) **exceeds** the Critical Heat Flux (1.26 MW/m²). The plate cannot actually sustain this condition — at $\Delta T_{ex} = 25$ K, the system would be in the **transition boiling regime** or **stable film boiling** (since $\Delta T_{ex} = 25$ K is well past the Leidenfrost point). The Rohsenow correlation's validity breaks down above CHF.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Lesson:** The actual operating excess temperature must be reduced to roughly $\Delta T_{ex} \approx 18$–$20$ K to keep the system in the nucleate boiling regime (i.e., $q < q_{max}$). This is precisely why reboiler design engineers work with a *safety margin* of typically 25% below CHF.

### 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation of the Rohsenow Correlation Form

Rohsenow (1952) proposed that the convective heat transfer coefficient in nucleate boiling is analogous to single-phase natural convection, with the bubble departure diameter as the characteristic length.

**Step 1 — Convective Heat Transfer Form:**

$$h = \frac{q}{\Delta T_{ex}} = C \, Re^n \, Pr^{0.4} \cdot \frac{k_l}{D_b}$$

where $D_b$ is the bubble departure diameter, given by **Fritz's correlation**:

$$D_b = 0.0208 \, \beta \sqrt{\frac{2 \sigma}{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}}$$

with $\beta$ the contact angle. For water, $D_b \approx 1$–$2$ mm.

**Step 2 — Reynolds Number for Bubble:**

$$Re = \frac{D_b \, u_b \, \rho_l}{\mu_l}$$

The bubble velocity at departure (force balance: buoyancy = drag) yields $u_b \sim \sqrt{g D_b}$.

**Step 3 — Expressing in Excess Temperature:**
The Clausius-Clapeyron relation gives the saturation pressure increase due to $\Delta T_{ex}$. The liquid superheat adjacent to the bubble is governed by the thermal boundary layer around the bubble, which sets $Re \sim Ja^{1/3}$. Combining constants and re-arranging:

$$q = \mu_l h_{fg} \left[\frac{g(\rho_l-\rho_v)}{\sigma}\right]^{1/2} \left[\frac{c_{p,l}\Delta T_{ex}}{C_{sf} h_{fg} Pr_l^n}\right]^3$$

The cube of the dimensionless superheat group is the key Rohsenow form, as it must be calibrated using $C_{sf}$ against experimental data.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Correlation

| Operating Condition | Use This Correlation | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| $\Delta T_{ex}$ small, no bubbles visible | Natural Convection (Nusselt) | Single-phase regime |
| $4 \text{ K} < \Delta T_{ex} < 30 \text{ K}$, bubbles visible | **Rohsenow** | Valid in fully developed nucleate boiling |
| Predicting maximum safe heat flux | **Zuber** | CHF design limit |
| Predicting Leidenfrost point / film stability | **Berenson** | Minimum heat flux |
| Film boiling regime, $T_s$ very high | **Bromley** | Conduction through vapor film |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define the term "Pool Boiling Curve" and explain the physical significance of the Critical Heat Flux (CHF).**

**Model Answer:**

The **Pool Boiling Curve** is a plot of heat flux $q$ (W/m²) transferred from a heater surface to a stagnant (pool) liquid versus the excess temperature $\Delta T_{ex} = T_s - T_{sat}$ (K). It was experimentally obtained by Nukiyama in 1934 and exhibits **six distinct regimes**: free convection, onset of nucleate boiling, isolated bubble nucleate boiling, slug nucleate boiling, transition boiling, and stable film boiling.

The **Critical Heat Flux (CHF)**, denoted $q_{max}$, is the **peak heat flux** on the curve (point D). Physically, it represents the **maximum heat flux the boiling system can sustain in a stable manner**. Beyond CHF, the vapor blanket cannot be replenished by the liquid; the surface temperature rises rapidly, and the system transitions to film boiling.

**Engineering significance:**
- In nuclear reactors, exceeding CHF leads to **fuel rod cladding meltdown**.
- In chemical reboilers, CHF sets the **design heat flux limit**.
- The corresponding Zuber correlation: $q_{max} = 0.149 \, h_{fg} \, \rho_v \left[ \frac{\sigma \, g (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^2} \right]^{1/4}$.

*(Marks distribution: Definition — 1; Six regimes — 1; CHF significance with formula — 1)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the Leidenfrost Point? State and explain the Berenson correlation for the minimum heat flux.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Leidenfrost Point** is the location (Point E) on the pool boiling curve where the heat flux attains its **minimum value** ($q_{min}$) in the film boiling regime. At this point, the vapor film becomes **thermally stable** — it no longer collapses back into nucleate boiling upon further reduction in $\Delta T_{ex}$.

**Berenson Correlation (1961):**

$$q_{min} = 0.09 \, \rho_v \, h_{fg} \left[ \frac{g \, \sigma \, (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{(\rho_l + \rho_v)^2} \right]^{1/4}$$

**Explanation:**
- The constant 0.09 is fitted from experimental data.
- The denominator uses $\rho_l + \rho_v$ (not $\rho_v^2$ as in CHF) because **both liquid and vapor inertia** are significant during the hydrodynamic instability at the film stability threshold.
- $q_{min}$ is important in **cryogenic safety** (LNG spills), **post-dryout heat transfer** in nuclear reactors, and in fire suppression modeling.

*(Marks distribution: Leidenfrost definition — 1; Berenson formula — 1; Physical interpretation — 1)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]

**(a)** With the help of a neat labeled sketch, describe the **Nukiyama Pool Boiling Curve** and identify the **six distinct regimes** with their physical mechanisms.

**(b)** A horizontal stainless steel plate (mechanically polished) is immersed in saturated water at **1 atm** ($T_{sat} = 100$ °C). The plate surface is maintained at **$T_s$ = 122 °C**. Using the **Rohsenow correlation**, determine the **heat flux** and the **Reynolds number of the bubble** at departure. Given: $C_{sf} = 0.0132$, $n = 1.7$, $\mu_l = 279 \times 10^{-6}$ N·s/m², $c_{p,l} = 4216$ J/kg·K, $Pr_l = 1.76$, $\rho_l = 957.9$ kg/m³, $\rho_v = 0.5956$ kg/m³, $h_{fg} = 2257 \times 10^3$ J/kg, $\sigma = 0.0589$ N/m, contact angle $\beta = 50°$.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Sketch and Regimes (7 Marks):**

The Pool Boiling Curve is a plot of $q$ (Y-axis, W/m²) versus $\Delta T_{ex} = T_s - T_{sat}$ (X-axis, K). The six regimes are:

| Region | Name | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| A–B | Free Convection | Single-phase natural convection; no bubbles |
| B | ONB (Onset of Nucleate Boiling) | First stable vapor bubble forms |
| B–C | Isolated Bubble Nucleate Boiling | Discrete bubbles nucleate at cavities and detach |
| C–D | Slug / Columnar Nucleate Boiling | Bubbles coalesce into vapor slugs |
| D | Critical Heat Flux (CHF) | Peak heat flux; hydrodynamic instability |
| D–E | Transition Boiling | Intermittent vapor film; unstable; $dq/d\Delta T_{ex} < 0$ |
| E | Leidenfrost Point / $q_{min}$ | Vapor film becomes thermally stable |
| E–F | Stable Film Boiling | Conduction through vapor film + radiation |

**Key points to mention:**
- Hysteresis: backward path differs from forward path
- CHF is independent of surface material (Zuber's key result)
- B is ONB, D is CHF, E is Leidenfrost

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Excess temperature:

$$\Delta T_{ex} = 122 - 100 = 22 \; \text{K}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the property group:

$$\frac{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\sigma} = \frac{9.81 \times 957.30}{0.0589} = 1.5941 \times 10^5 \; \text{m}^{-1}$$

**Step 3:** Superheat group:

$$\frac{c_{p,l} \Delta T_{ex}}{C_{sf} h_{fg} Pr_l^n} = \frac{4216 \times 22}{0.0132 \times 2257 \times 10^3 \times (1.76)^{1.7}}$$

Compute $Pr_l^{1.7} = (1.76)^{1.7}$. Using $1.76^{1.7} = e^{1.7 \ln 1.76} = e^{1.7 \times 0.5653} = e^{0.9610} = 2.614$.

Denominator: $0.0132 \times 2257000 \times 2.614 = 77{,}887.6$

Numerator: $4216 \times 22 = 92{,}752$

Superheat group $= 92752 / 77887.6 = 1.1908$

Cube: $(1.1908)^3 = 1.6890$

**Step 4:** Heat flux:

$$q = (279 \times 10^{-6}) \times (2257 \times 10^3) \times \sqrt{1.5941 \times 10^5} \times 1.6890$$

$$\sqrt{1.5941 \times 10^5} = 399.26$$

$$q = 0.6296 \times 10^{3} \times 399.26 \times 1.6890 = 424.5 \; \text{kW/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{q \approx 424.5 \; \text{kW/m}^2}$$

**Step 5:** Bubble departure diameter (Fritz correlation):

$$D_b = 0.0208 \, \beta \sqrt{\frac{2 \sigma}{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}}$$

Convert $\beta = 50°$ to radians: $0.0208 \times \beta$ uses the angle in **radians** in some texts and in **degrees** in others. The most common KTU convention uses $\beta$ in **radians**:

$$\beta = 50° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.8727 \; \text{rad}$$

Compute $\frac{2 \sigma}{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)} = \frac{2 \times 0.0589}{9389.5} = 1.2544 \times 10^{-5}$

$$\sqrt{1.2544 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.542 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}$$

$$D_b = 0.0208 \times 0.8727 \times 3.542 \times 10^{-3} = 6.43 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m} = 64.3 \; \mu \text{m}$$

**Step 6:** Bubble velocity at departure (buoyancy-balance):

$$u_b = \sqrt{g D_b} = \sqrt{9.81 \times 6.43 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.0251 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 7:** Bubble Reynolds number:

$$Re_b = \frac{\rho_l D_b u_b}{\mu_l} = \frac{957.9 \times 6.43 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.0251}{279 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$Re_b = \frac{1.546 \times 10^{-3}}{2.79 \times 10^{-4}} = 5.54$$

$$\boxed{Re_b \approx 5.5}$$

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question] (Alternative to Question A)

**(a)** Derive the **Zuber correlation** for the Critical Heat Flux in pool boiling. State the assumptions and discuss why $q_{max}$ is independent of the heater surface material.

**(b)** Estimate the **Critical Heat Flux** and the **Minimum Heat Flux (Leidenfrost)** for a horizontal plate heater in **saturated liquid nitrogen** at 1 atm. Given: $T_{sat} = 77$ K, $\rho_l = 804$ kg/m³, $\rho_v = 4.44$ kg/m³, $h_{fg} = 199 \times 10^3$ J/kg, $\sigma = 8.94 \times 10^{-3}$ N/m, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Zuber Correlation (7 Marks):**

*Assumptions:*
1. The vapor-liquid interface at CHF is unstable due to **Helmholtz instability** (a shear-driven wave instability between two parallel streams of different densities and velocities).
2. The wavelength of the most-unstable Taylor-Helmholtz wave is:

$$\lambda_d = 2\pi \sqrt{3} \left[ \frac{\sigma}{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)} \right]^{1/2}$$

3. The corresponding interfacial velocity is:

$$u_v = C \left[ \frac{\sigma \, g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_l^2} \right]^{1/4}$$

4. The vapor mass flux equals $\dot{m}'' = \rho_v u_v$.

*Derivation:*
The CHF is reached when the vapor velocity equals the Helmholtz instability velocity. Equating the energy supplied (heat flux) to the latent heat carried away:

$$q_{max} = \dot{m}'' \cdot h_{fg} = \rho_v u_v h_{fg}$$

Substituting $u_v$:

$$q_{max} = \rho_v h_{fg} \left[ \frac{\sigma \, g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_l^2} \right]^{1/4}$$

Zuber fitted the constant to experimental data, obtaining $C = 0.149$:

$$q_{max} = 0.149 \, h_{fg} \, \rho_v \left[ \frac{\sigma \, g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^2} \right]^{1/4}$$

*Why surface-independent:* The expression contains **no surface properties** (no contact angle, no roughness, no thermal conductivity of the heater). It depends only on **fluid properties** ($\rho_l, \rho_v, h_{fg}, \sigma$) and **gravity** $g$. This is consistent with experiments on copper, brass, and steel plates of different roughness that all report the same $q_{max}$ for the same fluid.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Property group for CHF:

$$\frac{\sigma g (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^2} = \frac{8.94 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.81 \times (804 - 4.44)}{(4.44)^2}$$

$$= \frac{0.0877 \times 799.56}{19.7136} = \frac{70.11}{19.7136} = 3.557$$

$$(3.557)^{1/4} = (3.557)^{0.25} \approx 1.374$$

**Step 2:** $q_{max}$:

$$q_{max} = 0.149 \times 199 \times 10^3 \times 4.44 \times 1.374$$

$$= 0.149 \times 199000 \times 6.101 = 0.149 \times 1{,}214{,}099 = 180{,}900 \; \text{W/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{q_{max} \approx 181 \; \text{kW/m}^2}$$

**Step 3:** Property group for $q_{min}$:

$$\frac{g \sigma (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{(\rho_l + \rho_v)^2} = \frac{9.81 \times 8.94 \times 10^{-3} \times 799.56}{(804 + 4.44)^2} = \frac{70.11}{654{,}491} = 1.0713 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$(1.0713 \times 10^{-4})^{1/4} = (1.0713)^{0.25} \times 10^{-1} = 1.0175 \times 10^{-1} = 0.1018$$

**Step 4:** $q_{min}$:

$$q_{min} = 0.09 \times 4.44 \times 199 \times 10^3 \times 0.1018$$

$$= 0.09 \times 4.44 \times 199000 \times 0.1018 = 8{,}094 \; \text{W/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{q_{min} \approx 8.09 \; \text{kW/m}^2}$$

**Step 5 — Verification of design margin:** The ratio $q_{max}/q_{min} = 181/8.09 \approx 22.4$. This means there is a factor of 22 difference between peak and valley heat fluxes in liquid nitrogen — a much narrower margin than for water (≈ 1.26/0.13 ≈ 9.7). **Liquid nitrogen systems therefore require more careful heater design.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Wrong exponent on Prandtl number in Rohsenow:** Students often write $Pr_l^{0.4}$ instead of $Pr_l^n$ where $n = 1.0$ for water and $n = 1.7$ for others. **[-1 mark]**
> 2. **Confusing CHF and MHF constants:** Berenson's constant is **0.09**, Zuber's is **0.149**. Swapping these gives a wildly wrong $q_{min}$. **[-1 mark]**
> 3. **Using $\rho_v^2$ in Berenson instead of $(\rho_l + \rho_v)^2$:** This is a *very* common slip. **[-1 mark]**
> 4. **Forgetting to convert $\Delta T_{ex}$ to Kelvin:** The temperature differences are numerically equal in K and °C, but examiners expect you to write "K" or justify the conversion. **[-0.5 mark]**
> 5. **Drawing the boiling curve upside-down (heat flux on X-axis):** KTU boards are strict — $q$ on Y, $\Delta T_{ex}$ on X. **[-1 mark]**
> 6. **Not identifying all six regimes in sketch question:** Just naming "nucleate" and "film" without subdivisions loses 3 marks.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Board Exam Priority Topics:**

- **Nukiyama Curve Plot:** Heat flux $q$ (Y-axis) vs. $\Delta T_{ex} = T_s - T_{sat}$ (X-axis). Always draw $q$ vertical.
- **Six Regimes (in order):** Free Convection → ONB → Isolated Bubble Nucleate → Slug Nucleate → Transition (unstable film) → Stable Film. Memorize as **F-O-I-S-T-S**.
- **Three Critical Points:** **B = ONB** (start of boiling), **D = CHF / Burnout** (peak), **E = Leidenfrost / $q_{min}$** (valley after the peak).
- **CHF Independence:** Zuber's correlation is **independent of surface material and roughness** — depends only on fluid properties and $g$. This is a favorite KTU one-mark question.
- **Key Constants:** Zuber = **0.149**, Berenson = **0.09**, Rohsenow $n$ = **1.0 (water)** or **1.7 (other)**, Bromley = **0.943**.
- **Property Group in Zuber:** $\left[ \sigma g (\rho_l - \rho_v) / \rho_v^2 \right]^{1/4}$ — the **$\rho_v$ in the denominator is squared**.
- **Property Group in Berenson:** $\left[ g \sigma (\rho_l - \rho_v) / (\rho_l + \rho_v)^2 \right]^{1/4}$ — the **denominator is $(\rho_l + \rho_v)^2$**, NOT $\rho_v^2$.
- **Hysteresis:** The cooling path lies *below* the heating path; re-establishment of nucleate boiling requires $\Delta T_{ex}$ to fall below the Leidenfrost point.
- **Rohsenow Cube Law:** $q \propto (\Delta T_{ex})^3$ — a small change in $\Delta T_{ex}$ causes a large change in $q$. This is why nucleate boiling is highly temperature-sensitive.
- **Industrial Application Hierarchy:** Kettle reboilers → nucleate boiling; Nuclear fuel rods → strictly below CHF; Cryogenic spills → film boiling (Leidenfrost).
- **Bromley's Modified Latent Heat:** $h_{fg}' = h_{fg} + 0.4 c_{p,v} \Delta T_{ex}$ — corrects for vapor superheat in film boiling.
- **Fritz Bubble Diameter:** $D_b = 0.0208 \beta \sqrt{2\sigma/[g(\rho_l - \rho_v)]}$ — required for the $Re_b$ sub-part in numericals.
- **Safety Rule of Thumb (Water, 1 atm):** $q_{max} \approx 1.25$ MW/m²; design reboilers at 70–80% of $q_{max}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

Critical Heat Flux: Zuber Correlation and Minimum Heat Flux
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT501 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Critical Heat Flux: Zuber Correlation and Minimum Heat Flux

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Critical Heat Flux: Zuber Correlation and Minimum Heat Flux

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Heat Flux (CHF), denoted $q_{max}$ or $q_{CHF}$**, is defined as the *maximum attainable heat flux* during **nucleate pool boiling** on a heated surface. It represents the upper limit of the nucleate boiling regime on the classical **Nukiyama pool boiling curve**, beyond which the boiling mechanism transitions abruptly into **film boiling** (transition boiling).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minimum Heat Flux (MHF), denoted $q_{min}$**, is the *lowest sustainable heat flux* of stable **film boiling**. Below $q_{min}$, the vapor blanket becomes unstable, collapses, and the surface gets rewetted, causing the system to revert to **nucleate boiling** (or **transition boiling**).

The region between $q_{min}$ and $q_{max}$ is called the **Transition Boiling Region** — an unstable, hysteresis-prone regime that is practically impossible to maintain in steady-state operation.

---

## 1.2 The Nukiyama Pool Boiling Curve (Recap)

| Regime | Heat Flux Range | Heat Transfer Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| Natural Convection | Low | Buoyancy-driven fluid motion |
| Nucleate Boiling | $0 \to q_{max}$ | Bubble nucleation at active sites |
| **Transition Boiling** | $q_{min} \to q_{max}$ | Unstable — partial vapor coverage |
| Stable Film Boiling | $> q_{min}$ | Continuous vapor film insulates surface |

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Over-Boiling Saucepan** 🍲
> Imagine heating a saucepan of water on a stove. At first, small bubbles gently form at the bottom (nucleate boiling). As you turn up the stove, more bubbles form — this is the safe operating range. Suddenly, you reach a point where the bottom *appears* to be covered by a thin, transparent vapor cushion; the surface temperature jumps dramatically, and the bubbling noise is replaced by a hissing sound. **That instant is the Critical Heat Flux.** If you try to push the stove even higher, the vapor film stabilizes and the heater runs dangerously hot. If you reduce the heat below a certain point (the Minimum Heat Flux), the vapor film collapses and nucleate boiling resumes.

> **Industrial Significance:** CHF dictates the **thermal safety limit** of:
> - Nuclear reactor fuel rods (Departure from Nucleate Boiling — **DNB**)
> - Rocket engine coolant channels (burnout)
> - High-power electronic chip cooling
> - Metallurgical quenching baths

---

## 1.4 Why These Two Fluxes Matter in Engineering Design

> [!WARNING]
> Operating a heat exchanger above $q_{max}$ leads to **thermal runaway** — the surface temperature rises rapidly (often by hundreds of degrees within milliseconds), causing material failure, burnout, or even explosion. The **Zuber correlation** provides the *first-principles* theoretical framework to predict this limit *without* running expensive burnout experiments.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hydrodynamic Instability Theory (Zuber, 1959)

Zuber, building on the **Taylor–Helmholtz instability** of planar liquid–vapor interfaces, proposed that CHF occurs when a hydrodynamic instability develops at the **vapor–liquid interface** above the heater. At this limit, the heater is no longer able to remove vapor in the form of discrete bubbles fast enough — instead, large **vapor columns / jets** coalesce and are ejected from the surface.

### Key Physical Assumptions of the Zuber Model

1. The vapor–liquid interface above the heater is treated as a **planar inviscid interface**.
2. A small perturbation of wavelength $\lambda$ grows if it is unstable (Helmholtz criterion).
3. The most dangerous (critical) wavelength $\lambda_c$ is determined by a balance between **gravity, surface tension, and inertia**.
4. Beyond CHF, vapor escapes as **coherent jets/columns** rather than discrete bubbles.
5. The fluid is at saturation; subcooling is neglected in the classical form.

---

## 2.2 The Taylor Wavelength

The wavelength of the most-unstable perturbation at the interface:

$$\lambda_c = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{\sigma}{g \, (\rho_l - \rho_v)}}$$

**Interpretation:** Larger $\sigma$ (surface tension) stabilizes smaller wavelengths; larger $\Delta\rho$ destabilizes them.

---

## 2.3 The Helmholtz Instability Velocity

The characteristic velocity at which the vapor–liquid interface becomes unstable:

$$V_c = \left[ \frac{2 \, g \, \sigma \, (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_l^{2}} \right]^{1/4}$$

This represents the **critical ejection velocity** of vapor columns from the heater surface at CHF.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **authoritative KTU-ready reference** for this topic. All formulas, units, and boundary conditions are compiled for direct use in the examination hall.

| # | Quantity | Correlation / Formula | Units (SI) | Validity / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Taylor wavelength | $\lambda_c = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma}{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}}$ | m | Most-unstable disturbance wavelength |
| 2 | Helmholtz critical velocity | $V_c = \left[ \dfrac{2 g \sigma (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_l^{2}} \right]^{1/4}$ | m/s | Vapor jet ejection velocity at CHF |
| 3 | **Zuber CHF — large flat heater** | $q_{max} = 0.131 \, h_{fg} \, \rho_v^{1/2} \left[ \sigma g (\rho_l - \rho_v) \right]^{1/4}$ | W/m² | Infinite plate, $\pi/24$ constant |
| 4 | **Zuber CHF — finite cylinder** | $q_{max} = 0.131 \, h_{fg} \, \rho_v^{1/2} \left[ \sigma g (\rho_l - \rho_v) \right]^{1/4} \left( \dfrac{D \lambda_c}{2} \right)^{-1/6}$ | W/m² | Berenson correction for diameter $D$ |
| 5 | **Berenson MHF correlation** | $q_{min} = 0.09 \, h_{fg} \, \rho_v \left[ \dfrac{\sigma g (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{(\rho_l + \rho_v)^{2}} \right]^{1/4}$ | W/m² | Lower limit of stable film boiling |
| 6 | Lienhard–Eichhorn correction | $q_{max} = 0.131 \, h_{fg} \, \rho_v^{1/2} \left[ \sigma g (\rho_l - \rho_v) \right]^{1/4} \, f(D/\lambda_c)$ | W/m² | More accurate for $D/\lambda_c < 4$ |
| 7 | Constant (Zuber) | $C = \pi/24 \approx 0.131$ | dimensionless | Universal constant for large plates |

**Universal physical constants used:**
- $g = 9.81$ **m/s²** (acceleration due to gravity)
- $\pi \approx 3.14159$

---

## 2.5 Physical Interpretation of the Correlations

- **$q_{max} \propto h_{fg}$** — Higher latent heat ⇒ more energy carried per unit mass of vapor ⇒ higher CHF.
- **$q_{max} \propto \rho_v^{1/2}$** — Denser vapor (e.g., high-pressure systems) ⇒ higher CHF. This is why high-pressure water-cooled reactors have inherently safer CHF margins.
- **$q_{max} \propto \sigma^{1/4}$** — Stronger surface tension stabilizes the interface, slightly *increasing* CHF.
- **$q_{max} \propto (\Delta\rho)^{1/4}$** — Larger density difference (low pressure) ⇒ more buoyancy-driven instability ⇒ lower CHF.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Zuber correlation is used in the **design safety analysis** of pressurized water reactors (PWRs), boiling water reactors (BWRs), and cryogenic liquid storage systems to determine **burnout safety margins**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations

## 3.1 Derivation of the Zuber CHF Correlation

The derivation proceeds from the **hydrodynamic instability** of the vapor–liquid interface above the heater.

### Step 1: Set Up the Interface Perturbation

Consider a planar vapor–liquid interface just above the heater surface. A small sinusoidal perturbation of wavelength $\lambda$ and amplitude $\eta$ is imposed. The interface position is:

$$z(x,t) = \eta(x,t) = \eta_0 \, \cos(kx - \omega t)$$

where the wave number is $k = 2\pi/\lambda$.

### Step 2: Apply the Linearized Helmholtz Instability Condition

For an inviscid interface between two fluids of densities $\rho_l$ and $\rho_v$, with surface tension $\sigma$ and gravity $g$ acting normally, the **dispersion relation** (linearized, inviscid) is:

$$\omega^{2} = \frac{(\rho_l - \rho_v) g \, k + \sigma k^{3}}{\rho_l + \rho_v}$$

### Step 3: Find the Condition for Instability

The interface is **unstable** when $\omega^{2} < 0$ (exponential growth of perturbation). This requires the numerator to be negative when $\rho_l > \rho_v$, which is automatically satisfied since both terms are positive. The condition reduces to: a real positive $\omega$ exists (oscillatory) only when $\omega^{2} > 0$. Therefore, the *most-unstable* wavelength is found by minimizing $\omega^{2}/k$ — equivalent to minimizing the right-hand side with respect to $k$:

$$\frac{d}{dk}\left[ \frac{(\rho_l - \rho_v) g}{k} + \sigma k \right] = 0$$

Solving:

$$-\frac{(\rho_l - \rho_v) g}{k^{2}} + \sigma = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k_c^{2} = \frac{(\rho_l - \rho_v) g}{\sigma}$$

Thus the **critical (Taylor) wavelength** is:

$$\lambda_c = \frac{2\pi}{k_c} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{\sigma}{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}}$$

### Step 4: Determine the Critical Velocity

The maximum growth rate corresponds to the minimum of $\omega^{2}/k$ which, by dimensional analysis and the Helmholtz–Taylor analysis, yields the **critical vapor-column velocity**:

$$V_c = \left[ \frac{2 \, g \, \sigma (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_l^{2}} \right]^{1/4}$$

This is the velocity at which vapor jets are ejected from the heater at CHF.

### Step 5: Compute the Mass Flux of Vapor

The mass of vapor leaving the heater per unit area per unit time is:

$$\dot{m}'' = \rho_v \cdot V_c = \rho_v \left[ \frac{2 \, g \, \sigma (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_l^{2}} \right]^{1/4}$$

### Step 6: Compute the Critical Heat Flux

Each kilogram of vapor carries latent heat $h_{fg}$. Therefore:

$$q_{max} = \dot{m}'' \cdot h_{fg} = \rho_v \, h_{fg} \left[ \frac{2 \, g \, \sigma (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_l^{2}} \right]^{1/4}$$

### Step 7: Re-arrange into the Standard Zuber Form

Recognize that $2^{1/4} \approx 1.189$, and Zuber collected the numerical prefactor with an additional factor (from the actual Helmholtz dispersion analysis) to obtain:

$$q_{max} = \underbrace{\frac{\pi}{24}}_{= \, 0.131} \, h_{fg} \, \rho_v^{1/2} \left[ \sigma \, g (\rho_l - \rho_v) \right]^{1/4}$$

The factor $\pi/24$ arises from a more refined stability analysis of the two-dimensional jets at the interface (Zuber, 1959).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result:**
> $$\boxed{q_{max} = 0.131 \, h_{fg} \, \rho_v^{1/2} \left[ \sigma \, g (\rho_l - \rho_v) \right]^{1/4}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Berenson Minimum Heat Flux Correlation

### Physical Concept

At MHF, the vapor film is on the verge of instability. The vapor film thickness becomes comparable to the Taylor wavelength, and the film collapses, allowing nucleate boiling to resume.

### Step 1: Vapor Film Thickness at MHF

Berenson (1961) proposed that at $q_{min}$, the film thickness $\delta_{min}$ is such that the **hydrodynamic wavelength of the most-dangerous disturbance equals the film thickness**:

$$\delta_{min} = \lambda_c = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{\sigma}{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}}$$

This is the assumption that defines the MHF point.

### Step 2: Mass Flux Through the Film

The vapor generated must pass through the film of thickness $\delta_{min}$ at a velocity determined by the same Helmholtz instability:

$$\dot{m}'' = \rho_v \cdot V_c = \rho_v \left[ \frac{2 g \sigma (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_l^{2}} \right]^{1/4}$$

### Step 3: Construct the Energy Balance

However, the difference from CHF is that now the liquid above the film is **also in motion** (in counter-flow). This adds the liquid inertia to the denominator. The effective mean density becomes $(\rho_l + \rho_v)/2$ in the denominator under the square root, but a more careful Taylor–Helmholtz analysis gives the combined density factor as $(\rho_l + \rho_v)$.

Therefore the ejection velocity is reduced to:

$$V_{min} = \left[ \frac{2 g \sigma (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{(\rho_l + \rho_v)^{2}} \right]^{1/4}$$

### Step 4: Combine with Latent Heat

The minimum heat flux is then:

$$q_{min} = \rho_v \cdot h_{fg} \cdot V_{min}$$

Experimentally, Berenson introduced an empirical coefficient of **0.09** (≈ 0.09) to match data:

$$q_{min} = 0.09 \, h_{fg} \, \rho_v \left[ \frac{\sigma g (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{(\rho_l + \rho_v)^{2}} \right]^{1/4}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Result:**
> $$\boxed{q_{min} = 0.09 \, h_{fg} \, \rho_v \left[ \frac{\sigma \, g (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{(\rho_l + \rho_v)^{2}} \right]^{1/4}}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** Saturated water at 1 atm boils on a large flat plate. Given:

- $h_{fg} = 2257 \times 10^{3}$ J/kg
- $\rho_l = 957.9$ kg/m³
- $\rho_v = 0.5955$ kg/m³
- $\sigma = 0.0589$ N/m
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

Estimate $q_{max}$ and $q_{min}$ using the Zuber and Berenson correlations.

### Step 1: Compute the Driving Term

$$g(\rho_l - \rho_v) = 9.81 \times (957.9 - 0.5955) = 9.81 \times 957.3 = 9391.1 \text{ N/m}^3$$

### Step 2: Compute the CHF (Zuber)

$$q_{max} = 0.131 \times 2257 \times 10^{3} \times (0.5955)^{1/2} \times (0.0589 \times 9391.1)^{1/4}$$

$$= 0.131 \times 2.257 \times 10^{6} \times 0.7717 \times (553.14)^{1/4}$$

$$(553.14)^{1/4} = (553.14)^{0.25} \approx 4.852$$

$$q_{max} = 0.131 \times 2.257 \times 10^{6} \times 0.7717 \times 4.852$$

$$= 1.106 \times 10^{6} \text{ W/m}^{2} \approx 1.11 \text{ MW/m}^{2}$$

### Step 3: Compute the MHF (Berenson)

$$(\rho_l + \rho_v)^{2} = (958.5)^{2} = 9.187 \times 10^{5} \text{ (kg/m}^3)^{2}$$

$$\frac{\sigma g (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{(\rho_l + \rho_v)^{2}} = \frac{553.14}{9.187 \times 10^{5}} = 6.021 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$(6.021 \times 10^{-4})^{1/4} = 0.1570$$

$$q_{min} = 0.09 \times 2.257 \times 10^{6} \times 0.5955 \times 0.1570$$

$$q_{min} = 1.899 \times 10^{4} \text{ W/m}^{2} \approx 19 \text{ kW/m}^{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ratio $q_{max}/q_{min} \approx 58$, meaning the **transition boiling region is enormous** for water at 1 atm — this is why industrial boilers are operated well within the nucleate boiling regime.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pool Boiling Regimes and Heat Flux Limits

