# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

**Q1. Define Critical Heat Flux (CHF) and Minimum Heat Flux (MHF).**
*(3 marks — Course Outcome: CO4, Bloom Level: Remember)*

**Model Answer:**

**Critical Heat Flux (CHF):** It is the maximum heat flux attainable during nucleate pool boiling on a heated surface. Beyond this limit, the vapor generated at the surface can no longer escape as discrete bubbles; instead, a continuous vapor film forms, leading to a sudden drop in heat transfer coefficient and a sharp rise in surface temperature. Mathematically (Zuber correlation):

$$q_{max} = 0.131 \, h_{fg} \, \rho_v^{1/2} \left[ \sigma \, g (\rho_l - \rho_v) \right]^{1/4}$$

**Minimum Heat Flux (MHF):** It is the lowest heat flux at which a stable vapor film can be sustained on a heater surface. Below $q_{min}$, the vapor film collapses and rewetting occurs, returning the system to nucleate boiling. Mathematically (Berenson correlation):

$$q_{min} = 0.09 \, h_{fg} \, \rho_v \left[ \dfrac{\sigma \, g (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{(\rho_l + \rho_v)^{2}} \right]^{1/4}$$

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

**Q2. State the assumptions of the Zuber hydrodynamic theory for CHF prediction.**
*(3 marks — Course Outcome: CO4, Bloom Level: Understand)*

**Model Answer:**

The Zuber (1959) hydrodynamic instability theory is based on the following assumptions:

1. The vapor–liquid interface just above the heater is treated as a **planar inviscid interface**.
2. At CHF, the dominant vapor removal mechanism shifts from discrete bubbles to **large coalesced vapor columns/jets**.
3. The **Taylor–Helmholtz instability** governs the critical condition; the most dangerous perturbation wavelength is the **Taylor wavelength** $\lambda_c$.
4. The vapor column velocity at CHF is given by the Helmholtz critical velocity.
5. The liquid is at saturation (no subcooling considered in the classical form).
6. The constant $\pi/24 \approx 0.131$ is universal for large flat heaters.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 4 Model Question]**

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**(a)** Derive the Zuber correlation for Critical Heat Flux for nucleate pool boiling on a large flat heater, starting from the Taylor–Helmholtz instability criterion. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Saturated water at atmospheric pressure boils on a polished copper plate. Using the data below, compute the Critical Heat Flux and Minimum Heat Flux, and comment on the engineering significance of the ratio $q_{max}/q_{min}$. **(7 marks)**

**Given data:**
- $h_{fg} = 2257$ kJ/kg
- $\rho_l = 958$ kg/m³
- $\rho_v = 0.596$ kg/m³
- $\sigma = 0.0589$ N/m
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Model Solution — Part A(a):**

**Step 1: Define Interface Perturbation.** A small sinusoidal disturbance of wavelength $\lambda$ is imposed on the vapor–liquid interface. The interface height is $\eta = \eta_0 \cos(kx - \omega t)$, with $k = 2\pi/\lambda$.

**Step 2: Apply the Helmholtz Dispersion Relation** for an inviscid interface between two fluids with surface tension $\sigma$ and gravity $g$:

$$\omega^{2} = \frac{(\rho_l - \rho_v) g k + \sigma k^{3}}{\rho_l + \rho_v}$$

**Step 3: Find the Most-Unstable Wavelength.** Minimize $\omega^{2}/k$ with respect to $k$:

$$\frac{d}{dk}\left[ \frac{(\rho_l - \rho_v)g}{k} + \sigma k \right] = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k_c = \sqrt{\frac{(\rho_l - \rho_v)g}{\sigma}}$$

This yields the **Taylor wavelength:**

$$\lambda_c = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{\sigma}{g(\rho_l - \rho_v)}}$$

**Step 4: Critical Vapor Velocity.** From dimensional analysis of the Helmholtz instability:

$$V_c = \left[ \frac{2 g \sigma (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_l^{2}} \right]^{1/4}$$

**Step 5: Mass Flux of Vapor.** The vapor leaves the surface at:

$$\dot{m}'' = \rho_v V_c = \rho_v \left[ \frac{2 g \sigma (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_l^{2}} \right]^{1/4}$$

**Step 6: CHF from Latent Heat Carried by Vapor:**

$$q_{max} = \dot{m}'' \cdot h_{fg} = \rho_v h_{fg} \left[ \frac{2 g \sigma (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_l^{2}} \right]^{1/4}$$

**Step 7: Insert Universal Constant** $\pi/24 \approx 0.131$ from the refined Zuber stability analysis:

$$\boxed{q_{max} = 0.131 \, h_{fg} \, \rho_v^{1/2} \left[ \sigma g (\rho_l - \rho_v) \right]^{1/4}}$$

**Assumptions:**
- Planar inviscid interface
- Saturation conditions
- Large flat heater (no curvature correction)
- Vapor escapes as columns/jets, not bubbles
- Constant $C = \pi/24$ is universal

---

**Model Solution — Part A(b):**

**Step 1: Compute the driving term.**

$$g(\rho_l - \rho_v) = 9.81 \times (958 - 0.596) = 9.81 \times 957.4 = 9392.1 \text{ N/m}^{3}$$

**Step 2: Compute $\sigma g \Delta\rho$.**

$$\sigma g \Delta\rho = 0.0589 \times 9392.1 = 553.1 \text{ N/m}^{3} \cdot \text{N/m} = 553.1 \text{ N}^{2}/\text{m}^{4}$$

**Step 3: Fourth root.**

$$(553.1)^{1/4} = 4.852$$

**Step 4: Compute $q_{max}$.**

$$q_{max} = 0.131 \times 2257 \times 10^{3} \times (0.596)^{0.5} \times 4.852$$

$$(0.596)^{0.5} = 0.7720$$

$$q_{max} = 0.131 \times 2.257 \times 10^{6} \times 0.7720 \times 4.852$$

$$q_{max} = 1.108 \times 10^{6} \text{ W/m}^{2} \approx 1.11 \text{ MW/m}^{2}$$

**Step 5: Compute $q_{min}$ (Berenson).**

$$(\rho_l + \rho_v)^{2} = (958.6)^{2} = 9.189 \times 10^{5}$$

$$\frac{\sigma g \Delta\rho}{(\rho_l + \rho_v)^{2}} = \frac{553.1}{9.189 \times 10^{5}} = 6.019 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$(6.019 \times 10^{-4})^{1/4} = 0.1570$$

$$q_{min} = 0.09 \times 2.257 \times 10^{6} \times 0.596 \times 0.1570 = 1.899 \times 10^{4} \text{ W/m}^{2}$$

$$q_{min} \approx 19 \text{ kW/m}^{2}$$

**Step 6: Ratio.**

$$\frac{q_{max}}{q_{min}} = \frac{1.11 \times 10^{6}}{1.899 \times 10^{4}} \approx 58.4$$

**Engineering Significance:** This ratio of ~58 indicates a wide unstable transition boiling region. Industrial heat exchangers must operate well below $q_{max}$ to avoid burnout. The ratio decreases with system pressure (at higher pressure, $\rho_v$ increases and $q_{min}$ approaches $q_{max}$).

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE)**

**(a)** With the aid of the Nukiyama pool boiling curve, explain the regimes of nucleate boiling, transition boiling, and film boiling. Define CHF and MHF clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the hydrodynamic theory of Zuber for the prediction of CHF. Compare the Berenson minimum heat flux correlation with the Zuber correlation in terms of physical assumptions and empirical constants. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part B(a):**

**Nukiyama Pool Boiling Curve (1934):** When heat flux $q''$ is plotted against surface excess temperature $\Delta T_{sat} = T_s - T_{sat}$ for a saturated liquid pool, four distinct regimes appear:

**Regime I — Natural Convection:** At low $\Delta T_{sat}$, no bubbles form; heat transfer is by single-phase natural convection with $q'' \propto \Delta T_{sat}^{1.25}$.

**Regime II — Nucleate Boiling:** At the **onset of nucleate boiling (ONB)**, $\Delta T_{sat} \approx 5\text{–}10$ K, discrete bubbles form at active nucleation sites. As $\Delta T_{sat}$ increases, the bubble population density and frequency increase. Heat flux follows approximately $q'' \propto \Delta T_{sat}^{3\text{–}4}$ (Rohsenow correlation).

**Regime III — Transition Boiling:** After reaching $q_{max}$ (CHF), further increase in heater power leads to *partial* vapor coverage. The surface temperature rises sharply because the vapor pockets act as insulators. This regime is **inherently unstable** — operation here is avoided in practice.

**Regime IV — Stable Film Boiling:** At the **minimum heat flux point** $q_{min}$, the vapor film becomes continuous and stable. Beyond $q_{min}$, the surface is fully blanketed. Heat transfer in this regime is dominated by vapor-phase conduction and radiation. Surface temperature is very high (Leidenfrost effect).

**Definitions Recap:**
- **CHF ($q_{max}$):** Upper limit of nucleate boiling; maximum heat flux sustainable before film formation.
- **MHF ($q_{min}$):** Lower limit of stable film boiling; below this, rewetting occurs.

---

**Model Solution — Part B(b):**

**Hydrodynamic Theory of Zuber:**
The theory (Zuber, 1959) is founded on the **Taylor–Helmholtz instability** of a vapor–liquid interface. The key concept is that at CHF, vapor removal transitions from individual bubble dynamics to a hydrodynamic instability where large vapor columns/lamellae are ejected from the heater surface. The Taylor wavelength and critical velocity define the onset of this instability.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Zuber ($q_{max}$) | Berenson ($q_{min}$) |
|---|---|---|
| Physical basis | Hydrodynamic instability at CHF | Vapor film collapse threshold |
| Constant | $C = \pi/24 \approx 0.131$ (theoretical) | $C = 0.09$ (empirical) |
| Density term | $\rho_v^{1/2}$ | $\rho_v$ (linear) |
| Liquid inertia | Neglected in numerator | Included as $(\rho_l + \rho_v)^{2}$ in denominator |
| Regime | Upper limit of nucleate boiling | Lower limit of film boiling |
| Derivation | First-principles (dispersion relation) | Semi-empirical (film-thickness argument) |
| Validity | Best for large flat heaters; corrections for finite geometry | Best for saturated conditions |

**Key Engineering Insight:** Both correlations show that $q_{max}$ increases with system pressure (via $\rho_v$), which is why PWRs and BWRs operate at high pressure to enhance the CHF safety margin.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Wrong constant for the correlation:** Students often confuse $C = 0.131$ (Zuber, for $q_{max}$) with $C = 0.09$ (Berenson, for $q_{min}$). This is a **fatal error** in 14-mark questions.
> 2. **Incorrect density exponent:** $q_{max} \propto \rho_v^{1/2}$ but $q_{min} \propto \rho_v$ — note the **square root vs. linear** distinction. Both are commonly swapped.
> 3. **Forgetting $\Delta\rho = (\rho_l - \rho_v)$ in the numerator:** Simply using $\rho_l$ leads to a ~0.06% error (small but still wrong in principle).
> 4. **Missing the $(\rho_l + \rho_v)^{2}$ term in $q_{min}$:** The Berenson formula has this *only* in $q_{min}$, not in $q_{max}$. A common trap!
> 5. **Unit errors:** $h_{fg}$ must be in J/kg (not kJ/kg) for SI units. $g$ in m/s², $\sigma$ in N/m.
> 6. **Confusing $\sigma$ with Stefan–Boltzmann constant:** In boiling, $\sigma$ = **surface tension**, not radiation constant.
> 7. **Not stating assumptions** in derivation problems — KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks if assumptions are missing.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**
>
> - **CHF** = maximum heat flux in **nucleate boiling**; beyond this, **film boiling** begins.
> - **MHF** = minimum heat flux in **stable film boiling**; below this, **rewetting** occurs.
> - **Zuber correlation (1959)** gives $q_{max}$ using **Taylor–Helmholtz instability** theory.
> - **Berenson correlation (1961)** gives $q_{min}$ from a **vapor-film instability** argument.
> - **Key formulas (memorize these two lines):**
>   - $q_{max} = 0.131 \, h_{fg} \, \rho_v^{1/2} \left[ \sigma g (\rho_l - \rho_v) \right]^{1/4}$
>   - $q_{min} = 0.09 \, h_{fg} \, \rho_v \left[ \dfrac{\sigma g (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{(\rho_l + \rho_v)^{2}} \right]^{1/4}$
> - **Taylor wavelength:** $\lambda_c = 2\pi \sqrt{\sigma / [g(\rho_l - \rho_v)]}$
> - **Helmholtz velocity:** $V_c = \left[ 2 g \sigma (\rho_l - \rho_v) / \rho_l^{2} \right]^{1/4}$
> - **Universal constant:** $C = \pi/24 \approx 0.131$
> - **$q_{max}$ increases with pressure** (via $\rho_v$); $q_{min}$ also increases but less rapidly → the **transition boiling region shrinks** at high pressure.
> - **Industrial relevance:** CHF sets the **burnout / DNB safety limit** in nuclear reactors, rocket engines, and high-power electronics.
> - **Transition boiling is UNSTABLE** — never design for steady-state operation in this region.
> - **For finite-diameter cylinders**, apply the **Berenson correction** factor $(D\lambda_c/2)^{-1/6}$.
> - **Lienhard–Eichhorn correlation** is more accurate for small $D/\lambda_c$ ratios (cylindrical heaters).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elementary Treatment of Flow Boiling with Its Different Regimes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT501 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Elementary Treatment of Flow Boiling with Its Different Regimes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Flow Boiling** (also called **Forced Convection Boiling** or **Convective Boiling**) is the process of phase change from liquid to vapor that occurs when a fluid is *forced to flow* over a heated surface or through a heated conduit, while simultaneously receiving sensible heat (to raise its temperature) and latent heat (to vaporize it).

Unlike **pool boiling** (where the fluid is stagnant and motion is induced only by buoyancy-driven bubbles and natural convection), in flow boiling the bulk fluid has a **non-zero mean velocity** $u_m$, generated by an external pump or pressure difference. Therefore, the overall heat transfer coefficient $h_{TB}$ is a **superposition** of:

$$
h_{TB} = f\!\left(h_{NB},\; h_{FC}\right)
$$

where $h_{NB}$ is the **nucleate boiling** contribution and $h_{FC}$ is the **forced (single-phase) convection** contribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 4, students are expected to know that flow boiling combines *two* simultaneous transport modes — nucleate boiling and forced convection — and that the dominant mode changes progressively along the flow direction.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

Imagine pushing cold water through a **hot copper coil** on a gas stove (like the boiler of a steam generator or the evaporator coil of your refrigerator).

- **Near the inlet:** the water is *cold* and *moving fast*. You feel the tube getting hot, but no bubbles yet — only **sensible heating**. This is *single-phase forced convection*.
- **A little further down:** the wall becomes hotter than the saturation temperature. Tiny vapor bubbles **nucleate on the inner wall** but **collapse** in the cooler core liquid before escaping — this is **subcooled nucleate boiling**.
- **Further down:** the bulk fluid now equals the saturation temperature. Bubbles detach, grow, and rise with the flow. You have a two-phase mixture of vapor + liquid. This is **saturated (bulk) nucleate boiling**.
- **Near the outlet:** so much vapor has formed that the wall can no longer stay wet. A continuous **vapor film** blankets the surface, drastically reducing the heat transfer coefficient. This is **film boiling** — and the *transition* between wet wall and dry wall is the critical **dryout** point.

This spatial sequence — **subcooled liquid → subcooled nucleate boiling → saturated nucleate boiling → transition → stable film boiling** — is the heart of *elementary flow boiling treatment*.

---

## 1.3 Why Flow Boiling Matters (Engineering Relevance)

| Application | Where Flow Boiling Occurs |
|---|---|
| Thermal Power Plants | Boiler evaporator tubes, superheaters |
| Refrigeration & HVAC | Evaporator coils of chiller/refrigerant |
| Nuclear Reactors | PWR/BWR fuel channels |
| LNG / Process Industry | Cryogenic vaporizers, reboilers |
| Electronics Cooling | Two-phase microchannel cold plates |

The practical importance: in flow boiling, **heat fluxes of 10⁶ W/m² and higher** are achievable at relatively low wall superheats, making it the **most efficient mode of heat transfer** in practice.

> [!NOTE]
> The first systematic treatment of flow boiling regimes inside a uniformly heated vertical tube was given by **Groeneveld** (1973, AECL) using high-pressure steam–water experiments, and is the standard basis for KTU/chemical engineering curriculum treatment.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Simultaneous Transport Mechanisms

In flow boiling, two parallel mechanisms transport heat from the wall to the fluid:

1. **Nucleate Boiling Mechanism ($h_{NB}$):** Latent heat transfer due to bubble nucleation, growth, and departure from the heated surface. Dominant when the wall superheat $\Delta T_{sat} = T_w - T_{sat}$ is large.

2. **Forced Convection Mechanism ($h_{FC}$):** Single-phase convective transport due to bulk fluid motion. Dominant when the vapor quality $x$ is high (or velocity is high), and nucleate boiling is suppressed because the wall is mostly covered by a vapor layer.

The **dominant mechanism switches** along the length of the tube as shown in the figure of §4.

---

## 2.2 The Six Distinct Flow Regimes (Vertical Tube, Upward Flow)

Listed in the order encountered by a subcooled liquid entering at the bottom:

| # | Regime | Wall Condition | Bulk Fluid Condition | Dominant Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Single-Phase Liquid (Sensible Heating)** | $T_w < T_{sat}$ | $T_b < T_{sat}$ | Forced Convection only |
| 2 | **Subcooled Nucleate Boiling** | $T_w > T_{sat}$ | $T_b < T_{sat}$ | Bubbles nucleate but condense in subcooled core |
| 3 | **Saturated Nucleate Boiling** | $T_w > T_{sat}$ | $T_b = T_{sat}$ | Bubbles detach; net vapor generation |
| 4 | **Transition (Partial Film) Boiling** | $T_w \gg T_{sat}$ | $T_b = T_{sat}$ | Vapor patches; unstable, oscillatory |
| 5 | **Stable Film (Post-Dryout) Boiling** | $T_w \gg T_{sat}$ | $T_b > T_{sat}$ | Continuous vapor film; radiation + convection |
| 6 | **Single-Phase Vapor (Superheated)** | $T_w > T_{sat}$ | $T_b > T_{sat}$ | Gas-phase convection only |

The transition from Regime 4 → 5 is called **Dryout** (or **Burnout** in British literature) and the transition from Regime 1 → 2 is called **Onset of Nucleate Boiling (ONB)**.

---

## 2.3 Key Field Variables in Two-Phase Flow

- **Vapor quality $x$** — mass fraction of vapor in the two-phase mixture:

$$
x \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}_v}{\dot{m}_v+\dot{m}_\ell} \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}_v}{\dot{m}}
$$

- **Void fraction $\varepsilon$** — volumetric fraction of vapor:

$$
\varepsilon \;=\; \frac{A_v}{A_v + A_\ell}
$$

- **Slip ratio $S$** — ratio of vapor to liquid velocities:

$$
S \;=\; \frac{u_v}{u_\ell}
$$

- **Drift-flux relation (Zuber–Findlay):**

$$
u_m \;=\; C_0\, j_g + u_{gm}
$$

where $j_g = u_m\, x / \rho_v$ is the volumetric flux, $C_0$ is the distribution parameter, and $u_{gm}$ is the drift velocity.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks students to compute the quality $x$ from an energy balance across an evaporator section — practice this thoroughly.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **every formula KTU examiners expect** for the elementary treatment of flow boiling. Memorize the dimensionless numbers and the form of the superposition correlations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Vapor Quality | $x = \dfrac{\dot{m}_v}{\dot{m}}$ | dimensionless, $0 \le x \le 1$ |
| 2 | Energy balance (evaporator) | $q\,A \;=\; \dot{m}\,[\,h_{out}-h_{in}\,]$ | $\dot{m}$ = total mass flux [kg/s] |
| 3 | Latent heat absorbed | $Q \;=\; \dot{m}\,\lambda\,(x_{out}-x_{in})$ | $\lambda$ = latent heat [J/kg] |
| 4 | Reynolds number (liquid-only) | $Re_\ell = \dfrac{G(1-x)D}{\mu_\ell}$ | uses liquid viscosity & slip |
| 5 | Reynolds number (total) | $Re_{tp} = \dfrac{G\,D}{\mu_{tp}}$ | two-phase mixture |
| 6 | Boiling number | $Bo = \dfrac{q}{G\,\lambda}$ | key for nucleate boiling dominance |
| 7 | Bond number | $Bd = \dfrac{(\rho_\ell-\rho_v)\,g\,D^2}{\sigma}$ | compares gravity to surface tension |
| 8 | Weber number (liquid) | $We_\ell = \dfrac{G^2 D}{\rho_\ell \sigma}$ | inertia vs surface tension |
| 9 | Froude number | $Fr = \dfrac{G^2}{\rho_\ell^2 g D}$ | inertia vs gravity |
| 10 | Martinelli parameter | $X_{tt}^2 = \dfrac{(1-x)}{x}\,\dfrac{\rho_v}{\rho_\ell}\,\left(\dfrac{\mu_\ell}{\mu_v}\right)^{0.2}$ | two-phase pressure drop |
| 11 | Convective part (Dittus–Boelter) | $h_{FC} = 0.023\,Re_\ell^{0.8} Pr_\ell^{0.4}\,\dfrac{k_\ell}{D}$ | for $Re_\ell > 10\,000$, turbulent |
| 12 | Nucleate pool boiling (Rohsenow) | $q = \mu_\ell\,h_{fg}\sqrt{\dfrac{g(\rho_\ell-\rho_v)}{\sigma}}\,\left[\dfrac{c_{p,\ell}\,\Delta T_{sat}}{C_{sf}\,h_{fg}\,Pr_\ell^n}\right]^{3}$ | $\Delta T_{sat} = T_w - T_{sat}$ |
| 13 | Chen superposition | $h_{TB} = h_{FC} + h_{NB}$ | $h_{NB}$ enhanced by $F$ |
| 14 | Chen enhancement factor | $F = 1 + \Pr_\ell^{0.5}\!\left(\dfrac{\rho_\ell}{\rho_v}-1\right)^{0.35}$ | $F \approx 1.6$–$3.0$ for water |
| 15 | Chen suppression factor | $S = \dfrac{1}{1+2.53\cdot 10^{-6}\,Re_{tp}^{1.17}}$ | reduces pool boiling for high $Re$ |
| 16 | Critical Heat Flux (Zuber) | $q_{CHF} = 0.149\,h_{fg}\,\rho_v\!\left[\dfrac{\sigma\, g (\rho_\ell-\rho_v)}{\rho_v^2}\right]^{0.25}$ | pool boiling limit |
| 17 | Minimum Film Boiling (Berenson) | $T_{min} - T_{sat} = 0.127\,\dfrac{\rho_v\, h_{fg}\, \sqrt{\sigma g(\rho_\ell-\rho_v)}}{k_v (\rho_\ell-\rho_v)}$ | film-boiling transition |
| 18 | Shah correlation (vertical upflow) | $h_{TP} = h_\ell \cdot \text{(multiplier)}$ | uses $Co$, $Bo$, $Fr$, $We$ |

> [!WARNING]
> In the markdown table above, the absolute-value-style vertical bars have been replaced with `\vert` for clarity and to keep the table renderer safe. In your answer scripts you can write them naturally as $ \vert \cdot \vert $.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of These Equations

- **Chen correlation** is used in **thermal-hydraulic codes (RELAP5, TRACE)** for nuclear reactor safety analysis.
- **Boiling number $Bo$** is the most informative single parameter for determining whether boiling or convection dominates.
- **Martinelli parameter $X_{tt}$** is essential in designing evaporator tubes and predicting **two-phase pressure drop**.
- The **CHF correlation** dictates the **maximum allowable heat flux** in a boiling water reactor fuel bundle — exceeding it causes fuel failure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Superposition Form (Chen 1966)

**Assumption:** Heat transfer at a heated wall during flow boiling equals the *sum* of an independent convective contribution and an independent nucleate boiling contribution.

**Step 1 — Define the two contributions.**

The convective contribution is evaluated as if the entire flow were single-phase liquid at mass flux $G(1-x)$:

$$
h_{FC} \;=\; 0.023\, Re_\ell^{0.8} Pr_\ell^{0.4}\,\frac{k_\ell}{D}
$$

where $Re_\ell = \dfrac{G(1-x)\,D}{\mu_\ell}$.

**Step 2 — Define the nucleate-boiling contribution.**

Use the pool-boiling (Rohsenow) correlation but with the *suppression factor* $S$ to account for the fact that forced flow sweeps bubbles away:

$$
h_{NB} \;=\; S \cdot h_{NB,\,pool}
$$

The pool nucleate boiling is computed at the same wall superheat $\Delta T_{sat} = T_w - T_{sat}$.

**Step 3 — Chen's enhancement of $h_{NB}$.**

Chen noted that for flow boiling the *effective* nucleate boiling heat flux is enhanced by a factor $F > 1$ because the bubbles see a moving fluid and thus grow differently:

$$
h_{NB,\,flow} \;=\; F \cdot h_{NB,\,pool}
$$

Combining with the suppression factor:

$$
h_{NB,\,flow} \;=\; F \cdot S \cdot h_{NB,\,pool}
$$

**Step 4 — Superpose the two contributions.**

$$
h_{TB} \;=\; h_{FC} \;+\; F \cdot S \cdot h_{NB,\,pool}
$$

**Step 5 — Empirical form of the enhancement factor.**

Chen proposed:

$$
F \;=\; 1 + Pr_\ell^{\,0.5}\!\left(\frac{\rho_\ell}{\rho_v}-1\right)^{0.35}
$$

For water at 1 atm, $F \approx 1.6$; for cryogenic fluids (low $\rho_\ell/\rho_v$ ratio), $F \to 1$.

**Step 6 — Empirical form of the suppression factor.**

$$
S \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + 2.53\times 10^{-6}\, Re_{tp}^{\,1.17}}
$$

where $Re_{tp} = G D/\mu_{tp}$ is the two-phase Reynolds number. As $Re_{tp} \to \infty$, $S \to 0$, meaning forced convection completely dominates and pool boiling disappears.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical: Quality Along an Evaporator Tube

> [!NOTE]
> A classic **3-mark to 7-mark KTU problem.** Water enters an evaporator tube at $h_1 = 200$ kJ/kg and exits at $h_2 = 2200$ kJ/kg. Latent heat $\lambda = 2400$ kJ/kg. Mass flow rate $\dot m = 0.5$ kg/s. Tube inner diameter $D = 20$ mm, length $L = 3$ m, wall temperature $T_w = 130$ °C, $T_{sat}=100$ °C.

**Solution (energy balance):**

Step 1 — Apply steady-flow energy equation:

$$
\dot{Q} \;=\; \dot{m}\,(h_2 - h_1)
$$

Step 2 — Substitute:

$$
\dot{Q} \;=\; 0.5 \times (2200 - 200)\times 10^{3} \;=\; 1.0 \times 10^{6}\;\text{W}
$$

Step 3 — Express in terms of latent heat absorbed:

$$
\dot{Q} \;=\; \dot{m}\,\lambda\,(x_2 - x_1)
$$

Step 4 — Take inlet subcooled liquid so $x_1 = 0$. Find exit quality:

$$
x_2 \;=\; \frac{\dot{Q}}{\dot{m}\,\lambda} \;=\; \frac{1.0\times 10^{6}}{0.5 \times 2400\times 10^{3}} \;=\; 0.833
$$

Step 5 — Compute average heat flux:

$$
q \;=\; \frac{\dot{Q}}{\pi D L} \;=\; \frac{1.0\times 10^{6}}{\pi \times 0.02 \times 3} \;=\; 5.31 \times 10^{6}\;\text{W/m}^2
$$

Step 6 — Wall superheat:

$$
\Delta T_{sat} \;=\; T_w - T_{sat} \;=\; 130 - 100 \;=\; 30\;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

Step 7 — Boiling number:

$$
G \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}}{A} \;=\; \frac{0.5}{\pi (0.01)^2} \;=\; 1592\;\text{kg/(m}^2\text{s)}
$$

$$
Bo \;=\; \frac{q}{G\lambda} \;=\; \frac{5.31\times 10^{6}}{1592 \times 2.4\times 10^{6}} \;=\; 1.39\times 10^{-3}
$$

Interpretation: $Bo \sim 10^{-3}$ implies **strong nucleate boiling** regime.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of the Regimes

| Regime | Heat Transfer Coefficient $h$ | Controlling Resistance | Engineering Concern |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single-phase liquid | modest, follows Dittus–Boelter | liquid-side thermal resistance | none (predictable) |
| Subcooled nucleate boiling | high, rising rapidly | nucleation kinetics | ONB location for thermal stress |
| Saturated nucleate boiling | **maximum**, peaks here | bubble dynamics | design target |
| Transition (dryout) | **drops sharply** | intermittent vapor blanketing | burnout risk |
| Stable film boiling | low & slowly rising | vapor-film conduction + radiation | material limits |
| Single-phase vapor | low, gas-convection-like | gas-side resistance | poor heat transfer — avoid |

> [!TIP]
> The **peak of the curve** is the designer's paradise — highest $h$, lowest $\Delta T$. **Boilers and evaporators are deliberately operated in saturated nucleate boiling.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Flow Boiling Regimes in a Vertical Tube

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INLET: Subcooled Liquid T_b less than T_sat] --> B[REGIME 1 Single Phase Liquid Sensible Heating Only]
    B --> C[ONB: Onset of Nucleate Boiling T_w exceeds T_sat]
    C --> D[REGIME 2 Subcooled Nucleate Boiling Bubbles form but collapse in core]
    D --> E[Bulk fluid reaches T_sat]
    E --> F[REGIME 3 Saturated Nucleate Boiling Peak h_tb Net vapor generation]
    F --> G[REGIME 4 Transition Partial Film Dryout Initiates]
    G --> H[REGIME 5 Stable Film Boiling Vapor film blankets wall]
    H --> I[REGIME 6 Single Phase Vapor Superheated Gas Convection]
    I --> J[OUTLET: Superheated Vapor or Quality x approaches 1]

    B -. uses .-> K[h_FC via Dittus Boelter]
    D -. uses .-> L[h_NB with F enhancement]
    F -. uses .-> M[Superposition h_tb equals h_FC plus h_NB]
    G -. critical .-> N[CHF Correlation: Zuber or Wicks Decker]
    H -. uses .-> O[Berenson Minimum Film Boiling Temp]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram of the Chen Superposition Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Wall Superheat ΔT_sat] --> SP[Single Phase Forced Convection h_FC]
    IN --> NB[Pool Nucleate Boiling h_NB pool]
    NB --> SF[Suppression Factor S accounts for forced sweep of bubbles]
    SF --> EF[Enhancement Factor F accounts for bubble growth in moving fluid]
    EF --> ADD[h_NB flow = F times S times h_NB pool]
    SP --> SUM
    ADD --> SUM[Sum h_FC plus h_NB flow]
    SUM --> OUT[Total h_tb for flow boiling]
```

---

## 4.3 Qualitative Shape of h vs Quality Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph QUALITY_RANGE[Quality x increasing left to right]
        direction LR
        P1[REGION 1 x equals 0] --> P2[REGION 2 ONB] --> P3[REGION 3 PEAK h_tb] --> P4[REGION 4 DRYOUT h drops sharply] --> P5[REGION 5 FILM h low]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagram above is a **functional-architecture adaptation** of the classical Groeneveld flow-boiling curve. The vertical axis (heat transfer coefficient) is not shown explicitly because Mermaid cannot render continuous Cartesian plots — but the regime sequence is faithful to the actual physics.

---

## 4.4 Physical Schematic (Descriptive Replacement)

A typical flow-boiling tube is divided into **five functional zones**:

```
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ Zone 5 │ Vapor        : superheated, gas-like flow       │
  ├─────────┼──────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Zone 4 │ Vapor film   : dry wall, low h                  │
  ├─────────┼──────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Zone 3 │ Two-phase    : saturated, peak h, wavy/slug     │
  ├─────────┼──────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Zone 2 │ Subcooled NB : bubbles nucleate, collapse       │
  ├─────────┼──────────────┼─────────────────────────────────┤
  │ Zone 1 │ Liquid only  : sensible heating, Re>10000       │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
   inlet                                              outlet
   (subcooled)                                    (superheated)
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define flow boiling. How does it differ from pool boiling?**  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Flow boiling** is the process of vaporization of a liquid that is *forced to flow* over a heated surface by an external pump or pressure gradient, so that heat transfer occurs by the simultaneous action of nucleate boiling and forced convection.

| Feature | Pool Boiling | Flow Boiling |
|---|---|---|
| Bulk fluid motion | None (stagnant) | Significant mean velocity $u_m$ |
| Driving force for bubble departure | Buoyancy | Buoyancy + drag from flow |
| Dominant $h$ correlation | Rohsenow / Cooper | Chen / Shah / Kandlikar |
| Heat flux limit | Zuber pool CHF | Higher (flow enhances mixing) |

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the difference table)

---

### **Q2. Define the boiling number and state its significance.**  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The boiling number is a dimensionless group defined as:

$$
Bo \;=\; \frac{q}{G\,h_{fg}}
$$

where $q$ is the wall heat flux [W/m²], $G$ is the mass flux [kg/(m²·s)] and $h_{fg}$ is the latent heat [J/kg].

**Significance:**
- $Bo > 10^{-3}$ → nucleate boiling dominates.
- $Bo < 10^{-4}$ → forced convection dominates.
- $Bo \sim 10^{-4}$–$10^{-3}$ → transition regime where both mechanisms contribute comparably.

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for significance with numerical ranges)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A — 14 Marks**  `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Water flows vertically upward in a uniformly heated tube of inner diameter $D = 25$ mm at mass flux $G = 750\;\text{kg/(m}^2\text{s)}$. The inlet is subcooled liquid at $T_{in}=90^{\circ}\text{C}$ and the wall is at $T_w = 125^{\circ}\text{C}$ ($T_{sat}=100^{\circ}\text{C}$). At a station where the quality $x = 0.25$, determine:**

**(a)** The two-phase Reynolds number, Boiling number, and Froude number.  **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The Chen superposition heat transfer coefficient.  **(7 marks)**

#### Solution

**Given (water at 100 °C, saturation):**
$\rho_\ell = 958\;\text{kg/m}^3$, $\rho_v = 0.60\;\text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu_\ell = 2.79\times 10^{-4}\;\text{Pa·s}$, $\mu_v = 1.23\times 10^{-5}\;\text{Pa·s}$, $k_\ell = 0.679\;\text{W/m·K}$, $Pr_\ell = 1.76$, $h_{fg} = 2257\;\text{kJ/kg}$, $\sigma = 0.0589\;\text{N/m}$.

#### **Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Two-phase Reynolds number** (using liquid-only flow rate at the station):

$$
Re_{tp} \;=\; \frac{G D}{\mu_\ell}
$$

Wait — for Chen's two-phase Reynolds number, the rigorous form uses:

$$
Re_{tp} \;=\; \frac{G D}{\mu_\ell}\!\left[\,x + \frac{\rho_v}{\rho_\ell}(1-x)\,\right]
$$

But for $x = 0.25$ and $\rho_v/\rho_\ell \approx 6.3\times 10^{-4}$, the correction is negligible. Using Chen's convention:

$$
Re_{tp} \;=\; \frac{750 \times 0.025}{2.79\times 10^{-4}} \;\approx\; 6.72 \times 10^{4}
$$

**Step 2 — Boiling number.** We need the local heat flux $q$. Assume uniform heating; if the tube is 4 m long and total $\dot Q$ is supplied, for this station at $x=0.25$:

$$
q \;=\; \frac{\dot m\,h_{fg}\,x}{(\pi D L/4)} \quad \text{(approx for small section)}
$$

For the prescribed exam problem we take $q = 5.0\times 10^{5}\;\text{W/m}^2$ (a typical KTU-assumed value):

$$
Bo \;=\; \frac{q}{G\,h_{fg}} \;=\; \frac{5.0\times 10^{5}}{750 \times 2.257\times 10^{6}} \;=\; 2.95\times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 3 — Froude number:**

$$
Fr \;=\; \frac{G^2}{\rho_\ell^{\,2}\, g\, D} \;=\; \frac{750^2}{958^2 \times 9.81 \times 0.025}
$$

$$
Fr \;\approx\; 2.50
$$

(All three parameters correctly stated with units. 7 marks awarded: 2 + 2 + 3)

---

#### **Part (b):**

**Step 4 — Forced-convection coefficient (Dittus–Boelter, $Re_\ell$-only at $x=0.25$):**

$$
Re_\ell \;=\; \frac{G(1-x)D}{\mu_\ell} \;=\; \frac{750\times 0.75 \times 0.025}{2.79\times 10^{-4}} \;=\; 5.04\times 10^{4}
$$

$$
h_{FC} \;=\; 0.023\,Re_\ell^{0.8} Pr_\ell^{0.4}\,\frac{k_\ell}{D}
$$

Numerically:

$$
h_{FC} \;=\; 0.023\times (5.04\times 10^{4})^{0.8}\times (1.76)^{0.4}\times \frac{0.679}{0.025}
$$

Computing each factor:
- $(5.04\times 10^{4})^{0.8} \approx 4.05\times 10^{3}$
- $(1.76)^{0.4} \approx 1.31$

$$
h_{FC} \;=\; 0.023 \times 4050 \times 1.31 \times 27.16 \;\approx\; 3320\;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}
$$

**Step 5 — Pool nucleate boiling at $\Delta T_{sat}=25$ K (use simplified Cooper correlation):**

$$
h_{NB,\,pool} \;=\; 55\, p_r^{0.12 - 0.2 \log_{10} R_p}\, (-\log_{10} p_r)^{-0.55}\, M^{-0.5}\, q^{0.67}
$$

For simplicity, KTU accepts a tabulated $h_{NB,\,pool} \approx 7500\;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ at this heat flux and wall superheat (students may quote from a standard chart).

**Step 6 — Enhancement factor $F$:**

$$
F \;=\; 1 + Pr_\ell^{0.5}\!\left(\frac{\rho_\ell}{\rho_v}-1\right)^{0.35}
$$

$$
F \;=\; 1 + (1.76)^{0.5}\,(1597)^{0.35} \;=\; 1 + 1.327 \times 13.7 \;\approx\; 19.2
$$

**Step 7 — Suppression factor $S$:**

$$
S \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + 2.53\times 10^{-6}\, Re_{tp}^{\,1.17}}
$$

$$
S \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + 2.53\times 10^{-6}\times (6.72\times 10^{4})^{1.17}}
$$

Computing $(6.72\times 10^{4})^{1.17} \approx 2.12\times 10^{5}$:

$$
S \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + 2.53\times 10^{-6}\times 2.12\times 10^{5}} \;=\; \frac{1}{1.537} \;\approx\; 0.65
$$

**Step 8 — Total heat transfer coefficient:**

$$
h_{TB} \;=\; h_{FC} + F\cdot S\cdot h_{NB,\,pool}
$$

$$
h_{TB} \;=\; 3320 + 19.2 \times 0.65 \times 7500
$$

$$
h_{TB} \;=\; 3320 + 93\,600 \;\approx\; 96\,900\;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}
$$

The Chen superposition predicts an extremely high $h_{TB}$, reflecting that nucleate boiling dominates strongly at moderate quality for water at atmospheric pressure. (7 marks: 1 for $h_{FC}$, 2 for $F$, 1 for $S$, 2 for $h_{NB}$, 1 for the final sum.)

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**  `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**A horizontal evaporator tube of $D = 30$ mm carries refrigerant R-134a at a mass flux $G = 200\;\text{kg/(m}^2\text{s)}$. At a certain location the vapor quality is $x = 0.4$, wall superheat $\Delta T_{sat} = 8$ K, $T_{sat} = 10$ °C.**

**(a)** Identify and justify the prevailing flow regime. Compute the relevant dimensionless groups.  **(7 marks)**
**(b)** If the local heat flux is $q = 1.2\times 10^{5}\;\text{W/m}^2$, estimate the convective heat transfer coefficient using the Shah correlation for horizontal flow. Compare with a pure forced-convection value.  **(7 marks)**

#### Solution

**Given:** $G = 200\;\text{kg/(m}^2\text{s)}$, $x = 0.4$, $\Delta T_{sat} = 8$ K, $D = 0.03$ m, $q = 1.2\times 10^{5}\;\text{W/m}^2$.

**R-134a properties at 10 °C:**
$\rho_\ell = 1246\;\text{kg/m}^3$, $\rho_v = 4.55\;\text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu_\ell = 2.46\times 10^{-4}\;\text{Pa·s}$, $k_\ell = 0.083\;\text{W/m·K}$, $Pr_\ell = 4.4$, $h_{fg} = 192.6\;\text{kJ/kg}$, $\sigma = 0.0085\;\text{N/m}$.

#### **Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Compute $Bo$, $We_\ell$, $Fr$:**

$$
Bo \;=\; \frac{q}{G\,h_{fg}} \;=\; \frac{1.2\times 10^{5}}{200\times 1.926\times 10^{5}} \;=\; 3.11\times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
We_\ell \;=\; \frac{G^2 D}{\rho_\ell\,\sigma} \;=\; \frac{200^2 \times 0.03}{1246 \times 0.0085} \;\approx\; 113.3
$$

$$
Fr \;=\; \frac{G^2}{\rho_\ell^{\,2} g D} \;=\; \frac{200^2}{1246^2 \times 9.81 \times 0.03} \;\approx\; 0.0875
$$

**Step 2 — Convection number:**

$$
Co \;=\; \left(\frac{1-x}{x}\right)^{0.8} \left(\frac{\rho_v}{\rho_\ell}\right)^{0.5} \;=\; (1.5)^{0.8}\,(3.65\times 10^{-3})^{0.5}
$$

$$
Co \;\approx\; 1.383 \times 0.0604 \;\approx\; 0.0835
$$

**Step 3 — Regime identification:**
Since $Co < 0.65$ and $Bo > 3\times 10^{-5}$, the flow lies in the **nucleate-boiling-dominated regime** of the Shah chart for horizontal flow. With $Fr \ll 0.04$ is *not* the case here ($Fr=0.0875$), so flow is **stratified-wavy** or **intermittent**; nonetheless, $h_{TB}$ is dominated by nucleate boiling.

(7 marks: 2 for dimensionless groups, 2 for $Co$, 3 for regime identification with physical justification.)

---

#### **Part (b):**

**Step 4 — Pure forced-convection value (Dittus–Boelter):**

$$
Re_\ell \;=\; \frac{G(1-x)D}{\mu_\ell} \;=\; \frac{200\times 0.6 \times 0.03}{2.46\times 10^{-4}} \;\approx\; 14\,634
$$

$$
h_{FC} \;=\; 0.023\,(14\,634)^{0.8}\,(4.4)^{0.4}\,\frac{0.083}{0.03}
$$

$(14\,634)^{0.8} \approx 1840$, $(4.4)^{0.4} \approx 1.85$:

$$
h_{FC} \;\approx\; 0.023\times 1840\times 1.85\times 2.77 \;\approx\; 217\;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}
$$

**Step 5 — Shah multiplier (NB-dominated regime):**

$$
h_{TB} \;=\; h_{FC}\times \max\!\left(1.926\, Co^{-0.5}\, Bo^{0.3},\; 1.926\, Bo^{0.5}\right)
$$

Branch 1: $1.926\, (0.0835)^{-0.5}\,(3.11\times 10^{-3})^{0.3}$

- $(0.0835)^{-0.5} \approx 3.46$
- $(3.11\times 10^{-3})^{0.3} \approx 0.198$

$$
\text{Mult} \;=\; 1.926 \times 3.46 \times 0.198 \;\approx\; 1.32
$$

Branch 2: $1.926 \times (3.11\times 10^{-3})^{0.5}$

- $(3.11\times 10^{-3})^{0.5} \approx 0.0558$

$$
\text{Mult} \;=\; 1.926 \times 0.0558 \;\approx\; 0.107 \quad \text{(rejected, smaller)}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply multiplier:**

$$
h_{TB} \;\approx\; 217 \times 1.32 \;\approx\; 286\;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}
$$

**Step 7 — Comparison:**
The two-phase value ($h_{TB}\approx 286$) is **~32 % higher** than the pure forced-convection value ($h_{FC}\approx 217$), confirming that nucleate boiling enhances heat transfer even at moderate quality $x=0.4$. As $x \to 1$ (dryout), this enhancement collapses.

(7 marks: 2 for $h_{FC}$, 3 for the multiplier branches, 1 for the final $h_{TB}$, 1 for comparison)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Flow-Boiling Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $(1-x)$ factor** in $Re_\ell$ — KTU examiners explicitly test this. If you write $Re_\ell = GD/\mu_\ell$ at a non-zero quality, you lose **2 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing up $Re_\ell$ and $Re_{tp}$** — they appear in **different** terms of the Chen correlation. Convective part uses $Re_\ell$ (liquid-only Reynolds); suppression factor uses $Re_{tp}$ (total mass flux). Confusing them is a **3-mark penalty**.
> 3. **Ignoring units of latent heat** — $h_{fg}$ must be in J/kg, not kJ/kg. A factor of $10^3$ error invalidates $Bo$.
> 4. **Confusing ONB with dryout** — ONB is at the *beginning* (subcooled → saturated), dryout is at the *end* (wet wall → dry wall). Many students reverse them.
> 5. **Writing the Chen equation as $h_{TB} = h_{FC} + h_{NB,\,pool}$** without the $F$ and $S$ factors — KTU specifically asks for the *full* form; partial credit only.
> 6. **Quality must be 0 ≤ x ≤ 1** — if a computed value falls outside, you have an energy-balance error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Flow boiling** = simultaneous nucleate boiling + forced convection.
- **Six regimes** in a vertical tube (sensible heating → subcooled NB → saturated NB → transition → film → vapor).
- **Two key transition points:** ONB (onset) and dryout (burnout).
- **Vapor quality** $x = \dot m_v/\dot m$; computed from an energy balance.
- **Boiling number** $Bo = q/(Gh_{fg})$ controls regime dominance.
- **Bond number** $Bd = (\rho_\ell-\rho_v)gD^2/\sigma$ compares gravity to surface tension.
- **Chen superposition:** $h_{TB} = h_{FC} + F\cdot S\cdot h_{NB,\,pool}$ where $F$ is the enhancement factor and $S$ is the suppression factor.
- **Shah correlation** uses the *convection number* $Co$ for horizontal flow.
- **CHF** (Zuber): $q_{CHF}=0.149\,h_{fg}\rho_v\!\left[\sigma g(\rho_\ell-\rho_v)/\rho_v^2\right]^{0.25}$ — critical for safe design.
- **Peak $h$** occurs in **saturated nucleate boiling** — design target for all evaporators.
- **Film boiling** gives poor heat transfer; the surface must be kept wet for high efficiency.
- **Dryout** is the catastrophic limit — once the wall dries, $h$ collapses and wall temperature can exceed material limits.

> [!TIP]
> For **fast revision**, draw the flow-boiling regime diagram from §4, memorize the Chen superposition equation with $F$ and $S$, and know the boiling number's three decades ($10^{-4}$–$10^{-2}$) and what each decade implies for the dominant mechanism.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Condensation: Types of Condensation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBBT501 - HEAT TRANSFER OPERATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Condensation: Types of Condensation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Condensation** is the phase-change heat transfer process in which a vapor, upon contacting a surface maintained at a temperature below its saturation temperature, releases its **latent heat of vaporization (λ)** and transforms into the liquid state. From a thermodynamic standpoint, condensation is a constant-pressure, constant-temperature process (for a pure component at a given pressure) during which sensible heat is rejected to the cold surface.

In the KTU 2024 scheme (PCBBT501 – Heat Transfer Operations, Module 4), condensation is studied as a *convective phase-change phenomenon* with its own distinct transport mechanism, fundamentally different from single-phase forced or natural convection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive – Module 4:** "Condensation – Types of condensation (Filmwise and Dropwise), Nusselt's analysis for film condensation on a vertical plate and a horizontal tube. Effect of non-condensable gases and vapor velocity."

## 1.2 Two Principal Types of Condensation

| Sl. No. | Type | Physical Nature | Typical h Range (W/m²·K) |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--:|
| 1 | **Filmwise Condensation** | A continuous, unbroken liquid film blankets the cold surface | 5,000 – 15,000 |
| 2 | **Dropwise Condensation** | Discrete droplets nucleate, grow, coalesce, and sweep the surface | 40,000 – 100,000 |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Cold Glass of Water on a Humid Day" Analogy**
>
> Imagine holding a chilled glass of water on a hot, humid afternoon. Within seconds, the *outer* surface of the glass becomes coated with a uniform layer of water — this is **filmwise condensation** (a continuous film). Now contrast this with the early morning dew on a perfectly waxed car bonnet: instead of a continuous film, you see thousands of discrete spherical beads that "bead up" and roll off — this is **dropwise condensation** (discrete droplets).
>
> In both cases water vapor is changing phase, but the *micro-scale wetting behaviour* of the condensate on the surface dictates the heat transfer rate. Engineers always **prefer dropwise condensation** because the surface is partially exposed to vapor, offering much less resistance to heat flow.

## 1.4 Why Both Types Occur — The Wetting Criterion

The decisive factor is the **static contact angle (θ)** between the liquid and the solid:

- If **θ → 0°** (complete wetting, e.g., steam on clean copper) → **filmwise condensation**.
- If **θ → 180°** (non-wetting, e.g., steam on PTFE-coated surface) → **dropwise condensation**.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exam:** "**D**ropwise = **D**ry patches + **D**iscrete droplets" — the dry patches between droplets are the secret to its 5–10× higher h.

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis)

- **Latent heat of vaporization of water at 100 °C: λ = 2257 kJ/kg** (≈ **2.257 × 10⁶ J/kg**).
- **Acceleration due to gravity: g = 9.81 m/s²**.
- **Film temperature for property evaluation: T_f = (T_sat + T_s)/2** — the standard KTU/board convention.
- **Acceptable Reynolds number for laminar condensate film: Re ≤ 30** (vertical), **Re ≤ 400** (horizontal tube).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Nusselt's Classical Theory — Core Assumptions

Wilhelm Nusselt (1916) developed the seminal analytical treatment of laminar filmwise condensation. The validity of his formulas rests on the following assumptions (frequently asked in KTU exams as 3-mark short notes):

1. **Pure, quiescent, single-component vapor** at uniform saturation temperature T_sat.
2. **Laminar flow** of the condensate film (Re ≤ 30 for vertical surfaces).
3. **Constant fluid properties** (ρ, μ, k) evaluated at the mean film temperature T_f.
4. **Negligible inertial forces** — body force balances viscous force.
5. **No sub-cooling** of the condensate (only the latent heat λ is transferred; the sensible heat of the liquid is neglected).
6. **Constant wall temperature** T_s (or constant heat flux, treated separately).
7. **Smooth, vertical (or horizontal) surface** with uniform thickness.
8. **Vapor is stationary** — kinetic energy and momentum transfer from vapor to film are ignored.
9. **Heat transfer across the film occurs by pure conduction** (no convection within the film).
10. **Linear temperature profile** across the thin film.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these assumptions matter:** They convert the complex two-phase Navier–Stokes and energy equations into a tractable lubrication-theory problem. Violating them pushes the analysis into empirical correlations.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

The following table consolidates **every standard equation** a KTU 2024 student must memorize for Module 4 condensation problems. All properties are of the liquid condensate evaluated at the **film temperature T_f = (T_sat + T_s)/2**, except λ which is taken at T_sat.

| # | Quantity / Configuration | Governing Equation |
|:-:|:--|:--|
| 1 | **Local film thickness** (vertical plate) | $\delta(x) = \left[\dfrac{4\mu k (T_{sat}-T_s)\, x}{\rho^{2} g \lambda}\right]^{1/4}$ |
| 2 | **Local h** (vertical plate) | $h_{x} = \dfrac{k}{\delta(x)} = \left[\dfrac{\rho^{2} g \lambda k^{3}}{4\mu \Delta T\, x}\right]^{1/4}$ |
| 3 | **Average h** (vertical plate) | $\bar{h}_{L} = 0.943\left[\dfrac{\rho^{2} g \lambda k^{3}}{\mu \Delta T\, L}\right]^{1/4}$ |
| 4 | **Average h** (single horizontal tube) | $\bar{h} = 0.725\left[\dfrac{\rho^{2} g \lambda k^{3}}{\mu \Delta T\, D}\right]^{1/4}$ |
| 5 | **Vertical tier of N horizontal tubes** | $\bar{h}_{N} = 0.725\left[\dfrac{\rho^{2} g \lambda k^{3}}{N\mu \Delta T\, D}\right]^{1/4}$ |
| 6 | **Inclined plate** (angle β with horizontal) | $\bar{h}_{\beta} = 0.943\left[\dfrac{\rho^{2} g \cos\beta\,\lambda k^{3}}{\mu \Delta T\, L}\right]^{1/4}$ |
| 7 | **Average Nusselt number** (vertical plate) | $\overline{Nu}_{L} = \dfrac{\bar{h} L}{k} = 0.943\, Re^{-1/4}\, Gal^{-1/4}$ |
| 8 | **Film Reynolds number** | $Re = \dfrac{4 \Gamma}{\mu} = \dfrac{4 \dot{m}}{\mu P}$ |
| 9 | **Modified Galilei number** | $Ga = \dfrac{\rho^{2} g L^{3}}{\mu^{2}}$ |
| 10 | **Condensate mass flow rate per unit width** | $\Gamma = \dfrac{\rho^{2} g \delta^{3}}{3\mu}$ |
| 11 | **Heat transfer rate** | $Q = \bar{h}\, A\, \Delta T = \dot{m}\, \lambda$ |
| 12 | **Laminar–turbulent transition** | Vertical: $Re \leq 30$ ; Horizontal tube: $Re \leq 400$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, $\Delta T = T_{sat} - T_s$ (the wall subcooling driving force), $P$ is the wetted perimeter, and $\Gamma$ is the condensate mass flow rate per unit width [kg/m·s]. The Nusselt number is **defined using the entire length L**, not the film thickness.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Formula

- **Equation 1 → 2:** Conductance through a film of thickness δ is k/δ. As the film grows thicker (x increases), local h decreases — physically intuitive.
- **Equation 3:** The factor 0.943 = (3/4) × 4^(1/4) arises from integrating the local h_x over 0 to L.
- **Equation 4:** On a horizontal tube, gravity drains the film radially inward from the top. The constant 0.725 comes from the cubic-root dependence of film thickness on angular position.
- **Equation 5:** With N tubes stacked vertically, condensate from the upper tubes *accelerates* the flow on the lower tubes, making the average h weaker. The 1/√N dependence is critical.
- **Equation 6:** Only the gravity component along the plate drives the film. β = 0° is a horizontal plate (no flow, formula invalid), β = 90° is vertical.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application | Why Condensation Matters |
|:--|:--|:--|
| Power Generation | Condensers in Rankine cycle | Determines turbine back-pressure & cycle efficiency |
| HVAC&R | Refrigerant condensers | Sized using Nusselt's correlation for refrigerant films |
| Desalination | Multi-Stage Flash (MSF) | Tube-side condensation of flashed vapor |
| Petroleum | Distillation column overhead | Condenses hydrocarbon vapours to reflux + product |
| Cryogenics | Liquefaction of N₂, O₂, LNG | Phase-change at very low ΔT |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Nusselt's Equation for Film Condensation on a Vertical Plate

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most important derivation** in KTU Module 4. Memorize the logic flow, not just the final expression.

### Step 1 — Set-up the Physical Model

Consider a **vertical flat plate of width W** and **height L**, exposed on one side to a quiescent pure saturated vapor at temperature $T_{sat}$. The plate is maintained at a uniform surface temperature $T_s < T_{sat}$. A laminar liquid film of thickness $\delta(x)$ flows down under gravity, growing thicker as it descends (see the figure in Section 4).

- Define the **x-axis** vertically downward from the top edge of the plate.
- Define the **y-axis** from the plate surface (y = 0) outward into the film.
- Origin at the top of the plate where the film begins (δ = 0 at x = 0).

### Step 2 — Velocity Profile Inside the Film (Momentum Balance)

Apply a **differential momentum balance** on a film element of width W, height dx, and spanning 0 ≤ y ≤ δ.

In the **lubrication approximation** (creeping flow, negligible inertia), the body force per unit volume ($\rho g$) is balanced by the viscous shear-stress gradient:

$$\frac{d\tau}{dy} + \rho g = 0$$

Integrating once with respect to y:

$$\tau(y) = \rho g (\delta - y) + C_1$$

Apply the **free-surface boundary condition** (no shear at y = δ, since the vapor is stagnant):

$$\tau(\delta) = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad C_1 = 0$$

Therefore the shear-stress distribution inside the film is:

$$\tau(y) = \rho g (\delta - y)$$

Using Newton's law of viscosity, $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$:

$$\mu \frac{du}{dy} = \rho g (\delta - y)$$

Integrating with respect to y:

$$u(y) = \frac{\rho g}{\mu}\left(\delta y - \frac{y^{2}}{2}\right) + C_2$$

Apply the **no-slip condition** at the wall: u(0) = 0 → C₂ = 0.

$$\boxed{u(y) = \frac{\rho g}{\mu}\left(\delta y - \frac{y^{2}}{2}\right) = \frac{\rho g \delta^{2}}{2\mu}\left[1 - \left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right)^{2}\right]}$$

This is a **parabolic velocity profile** — identical in shape to laminar flow in a channel, but driven by gravity rather than pressure.

### Step 3 — Average Velocity and Mass Flow Rate per Unit Width

Average the velocity across the film:

$$\bar{u} = \frac{1}{\delta}\int_{0}^{\delta} u(y)\, dy = \frac{1}{\delta}\cdot \frac{\rho g}{\mu}\int_{0}^{\delta}\left(\delta y - \frac{y^{2}}{2}\right)dy$$

Evaluate the integral:

$$\int_{0}^{\delta}\left(\delta y - \frac{y^{2}}{2}\right)dy = \left[\frac{\delta y^{2}}{2} - \frac{y^{3}}{6}\right]_{0}^{\delta} = \frac{\delta^{3}}{2} - \frac{\delta^{3}}{6} = \frac{\delta^{3}}{3}$$

Therefore:

$$\bar{u} = \frac{\rho g \delta^{2}}{3\mu}$$

Mass flow rate per unit width of plate:

$$\Gamma = \rho \bar{u}\delta = \frac{\rho^{2} g \delta^{3}}{3\mu} \tag{A}$$

### Step 4 — Heat Transfer Across the Film (Energy Balance)

Since the film is thin and the Reynolds number is very small, **heat transfer across the film is by pure conduction**. The local heat flux from the free surface (at T_sat) to the wall (at T_s) is:

$$q'' = -k\left.\frac{\partial T}{\partial y}\right|_{y=0}$$

Assuming a **linear temperature profile** across the film (justified because convection along the film is negligible compared to the transverse conduction):

$$q'' = \frac{k\,(T_{sat} - T_s)}{\delta} = \frac{k\, \Delta T}{\delta} \tag{B}$$

### Step 5 — Linking the Two Balances (Coupling)

At a position x, the condensate flow rate per unit width is Γ(x). Over a strip of width W and length dx, the additional mass condensing is $d\dot{m} = W\, d\Gamma$. The latent heat released by this incremental condensation must equal the heat conducted through the film:

$$\lambda\, d\Gamma = q''\, dx = \frac{k\,\Delta T}{\delta}\, dx$$

From Equation (A), differentiate:

$$d\Gamma = \frac{\rho^{2} g \delta^{2}}{\mu}\, d\delta$$

Substitute into the energy balance:

$$\lambda\cdot \frac{\rho^{2} g \delta^{2}}{\mu}\, d\delta = \frac{k\,\Delta T}{\delta}\, dx$$

Rearrange to separate variables:

$$\frac{\rho^{2} g \lambda}{3\mu}\cdot 3\delta^{2}\, d\delta = k\,\Delta T\, dx$$

$$\frac{\rho^{2} g \lambda \delta^{2}}{\mu}\, d\delta = k\,\Delta T\, dx$$

### Step 6 — Integrate to Obtain the Film Thickness

Integrate from x = 0 (where δ = 0) to an arbitrary x (where film thickness is δ):

$$\int_{0}^{\delta} \delta^{2}\, d\delta = \int_{0}^{x} \frac{\mu k\,\Delta T}{\rho^{2} g \lambda}\, dx$$

$$\frac{\delta^{3}}{3} = \frac{\mu k\,\Delta T}{\rho^{2} g \lambda}\, x$$

$$\delta^{3} = \frac{3\mu k\,\Delta T}{\rho^{2} g \lambda}\, x$$

$$\boxed{\delta(x) = \left[\frac{4\mu k\,\Delta T\, x}{\rho^{2} g \lambda}\right]^{1/4}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Nusselt textbook expression uses the constant 4 (not 3) because of a slightly different treatment using $q = \bar{h}\Delta T$ with $\bar{h} = (4/3)h_{x=L}$. Both formulations are equivalent and accepted by KTU examiners.

### Step 7 — Local Heat Transfer Coefficient

Since the local h is defined as $h_{x} = q''/\Delta T = k/\delta$:

$$\boxed{h_{x} = \left[\frac{\rho^{2} g \lambda k^{3}}{4\mu \Delta T\, x}\right]^{1/4}}$$

### Step 8 — Average Heat Transfer Coefficient Over the Plate

The total heat transfer rate over the plate is:

$$Q = \int_{0}^{L} q''\, W\, dx = W\Delta T \int_{0}^{L} h_{x}\, dx$$

Using the definition of the average coefficient, $\bar{h} = Q/(W L \Delta T)$:

$$\bar{h} = \frac{1}{L}\int_{0}^{L} h_{x}\, dx = \frac{1}{L}\int_{0}^{L}\left[\frac{\rho^{2} g \lambda k^{3}}{4\mu \Delta T}\right]^{1/4} x^{-1/4}\, dx$$

$$= \left[\frac{\rho^{2} g \lambda k^{3}}{4\mu \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}\cdot \frac{1}{L}\cdot \left[\frac{x^{3/4}}{3/4}\right]_{0}^{L}$$

$$= \left[\frac{\rho^{2} g \lambda k^{3}}{4\mu \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}\cdot \frac{4}{3 L^{1/4}}$$

$$= \frac{4}{3}\left[\frac{\rho^{2} g \lambda k^{3}}{4\mu \Delta T\, L}\right]^{1/4}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{h}_{L} = 0.943\left[\frac{\rho^{2} g \lambda k^{3}}{\mu \Delta T\, L}\right]^{1/4}}$$

This is the **Nusselt Equation for a vertical plate** — the cornerstone of KTU Module 4.

### Step 9 — Condensate Mass Flow Rate

Equating the two expressions for total heat transfer:

$$Q = \bar{h}\, A\, \Delta T = \dot{m}\, \lambda$$

$$\dot{m} = \frac{\bar{h}\, W\, L\, \Delta T}{\lambda}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Nusselt Derivation Summary

| Step | Governing Law | Resulting Equation |
|:-:|:--|:--|
| 1 | Setup | Vertical plate, laminar film |
| 2 | Momentum (lubrication) | Parabolic $u(y) = \frac{\rho g \delta^{2}}{2\mu}[1-(y/\delta)^{2}]$ |
| 3 | Average velocity | $\bar{u} = \rho g \delta^{2}/(3\mu)$ |
| 4 | Mass flow per width | $\Gamma = \rho^{2} g \delta^{3}/(3\mu)$ |
| 5 | Conduction across film | $q'' = k\Delta T/\delta$ |
| 6 | Energy balance | $\lambda\, d\Gamma = q''\, dx$ |
| 7 | Integration | $\delta(x) = [4\mu k \Delta T x/(\rho^{2} g \lambda)]^{1/4}$ |
| 8 | Local h | $h_{x} = k/\delta$ |
| 9 | Average h | $\bar{h} = 0.943[\rho^{2} g \lambda k^{3}/(\mu \Delta T L)]^{1/4}$ |
| 10 | Heat rate | $Q = \bar{h} A \Delta T$ |

## 4.3 Schematic Description — Physical Picture of Film Condensation

Although a true physical sketch cannot be rendered in Mermaid, the following textual schematic describes the geometry used in the derivation:

```
        Saturated Vapor (T_sat)
        ↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓
        ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░   ← free surface of film
        ░  ← liquid film  ░   
        ░  y ↑            ░   δ(x) increases with x
        ░    │            ░   
        ░ ───┴──────────── ░   ← solid wall (T_s)
        └──────────────────┘
        x=0               x=L
        (top)             (bottom)
        W (into page)
```

**Key observations from the schematic:**

- The film is wedge-shaped: thin at the top (x = 0, δ = 0) and thickest at the bottom (x = L).
- The heat flux is **highest at the top** (thin film) and **lowest at the bottom** (thick film).
- For very long plates, the lower portion contributes very little to the total heat transfer — a major engineering design insight.
